> **Common Mistake (1 mark lost):** Students often confuse $\gamma_f$ (load side) with $\gamma_m$ (material side). Remember: $f_d = f_k / \gamma_m$ — division, not multiplication.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme: Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** (either Q.A or Q.B). Each question is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Both choices are given below.

---

### **Q.A.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the evolution of design philosophy from **Working Stress Method (WSM)** to **Limit State Method (LSM)**. List **four advantages** of LSM over WSM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reinforced concrete beam of effective span 5 m is subjected to a **dead load of 12 kN/m** (excluding self-weight) and a **live load of 10 kN/m**. Compare the design bending moment using:
   - (i) WSM with factor of safety = 1.67
   - (ii) LSM with $\gamma_{f,DL} = 1.5$, $\gamma_{f,LL} = 1.5$. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

| # | Point | Marks |
|---|-------|-------|
| 1 | WSM: stresses kept below allowable = ultimate/FOS; single global safety factor | 1 |
| 2 | WSM assumes elastic behaviour, uses unfactored loads, no explicit SLS check | 1 |
| 3 | LSM: separate partial factors for load ($\gamma_f$) and material ($\gamma_m$) | 1 |
| 4 | LSM uses both ULS and SLS checks, accounts for load variability | 1 |
| 5 | **Four advantages of LSM:** (i) rational basis using probability; (ii) separate factors for different load types; (iii) explicit serviceability checks; (iv) accounts for ductile vs brittle failure | 2 |
| 6 | Mention modern codes (IS 456:2000, Eurocode 2) use LSM | 1 |

### **Model Solution Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — WSM calculation (3 marks):**

Working load:

$$
w = 12 + 10 = 22 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Design moment:

$$
M_{\text{WSM}} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{22 \times 5^2}{8} = \frac{22 \times 25}{8} = 68.75 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2 — LSM calculation (3 marks):**

Factored load:

$$
w_d = 1.5(12) + 1.5(10) = 18 + 15 = 33 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Design moment:

$$
M_{\text{LSM}} = \frac{w_d L^2}{8} = \frac{33 \times 25}{8} = 103.125 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark):**

| Method | Design Moment |
|--------|---------------|
| WSM | 68.75 kN·m |
| LSM | 103.125 kN·m |
| Ratio (LSM/WSM) | 1.5 (since both $\gamma_f$ values are 1.5) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.A):**
> - Many students forget the **square on the span** ($L^2$, not $L$).
> - Forgetting to **multiply by partial safety factor** in LSM yields WSM answer — full mark loss.
> - Not stating the *units* (kN·m) loses 0.5 mark.
> - The 1.5 ratio is a *trick* — students are expected to identify it.

---

### **Q.B.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B] **(14 Marks)** — *ALTERNATIVE TO Q.A*

**(a)** Define the following terms with reference to the Limit State Method:
   (i) Characteristic load
   (ii) Characteristic strength
   (iii) Partial safety factor
   (iv) Reliability index. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A short concrete column is subjected to a **characteristic dead load of 800 kN** and a **characteristic live load of 600 kN**. The characteristic compressive strength of concrete is **f_ck = 25 MPa** and $\gamma_m = 1.5$. Compute the **design axial load** and the **design compressive strength** of concrete. Verify whether a **300 mm × 300 mm** column section is safe if its short-column capacity is $0.4 f_{ck} A_g$. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

| Term | Definition | Marks |
|------|------------|-------|
| (i) Characteristic load | Load value with a 5% probability of exceedance in 50 years (return period ≈ 975 years). For DL = mean value. | 1.5 |
| (ii) Characteristic strength | Material strength value below which not more than 5% of test results fall. $f_k = \mu_f - 1.645\sigma_f$. | 1.5 |
| (iii) Partial safety factor | Multiplier (for load) or divisor (for material) that converts characteristic values into design values. | 2 |
| (iv) Reliability index | $\beta = \dfrac{\mu_R - \mu_Q}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_Q^2}}$; quantifies the safety of a structure in standard deviation units. Typical target $\beta = 3.8$ for ULS. | 2 |

### **Model Solution Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Design axial load (3 marks):**

$$
N_d = \gamma_{f,DL} \cdot D_k + \gamma_{f,LL} \cdot L_k = 1.5(800) + 1.5(600)
$$

$$
N_d = 1200 + 900 = 2100 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Design compressive strength (1 mark):**

$$
f_d = \frac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_m} = \frac{25}{1.5} = 16.67 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacity of 300 mm × 300 mm column (2 marks):**

Gross area:

$$
A_g = 300 \times 300 = 90{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Capacity (given formula):

$$
N_R = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_g = 0.4 \times 25 \times 90{,}000
$$

$$
N_R = 9 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 900 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Safety check (1 mark):**

$$
N_d = 2100 \text{ kN} \quad > \quad N_R = 900 \text{ kN}
$$

**Conclusion: The section is UNSAFE.** The required area is:

$$
A_{\text{req}} = \frac{N_d}{0.4 \cdot f_{ck}} = \frac{2100 \times 10^3}{0.4 \times 25} = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2 = 210{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

A column of size ≈ **460 mm × 460 mm** would be required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q.B):**
> - Students often **divide** $f_{ck}$ by $\gamma_m$ correctly but then forget to **also factor the load** — 50% mark loss.
> - Mistaking $N_d$ and $N_R$ — remember the master inequality $S_d \le R_d$.
> - The 0.4 factor in $N_R = 0.4 f_{ck} A_g$ is a *given* shortcut for short axially-loaded columns; do not derive or question it.
> - Always state the conclusion ("Safe" or "Unsafe") — 1 mark is reserved for the verdict.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Design Codes | Final Revision Sheet**

- [x] **Design code** = legally-binding document prescribing minimum safety/serviceability requirements.
- [x] Two design philosophies: **WSM** (single FOS) and **LSM** (partial factors).
- [x] **Master LSM equation:** $S_d \le R_d$.
- [x] **ULS** = safety (collapse); **SLS** = service (deflection, crack, vibration).
- [x] **Characteristic load** = 5% exceedance probability in 50 years → $Q_k = \mu_Q + 1.645\sigma_Q$.
- [x] **Characteristic strength** = 5% lower fractile → $f_k = \mu_f - 1.645\sigma_f$.
- [x] **Design load** = $\gamma_f \cdot Q_k$; **Design strength** = $f_k / \gamma_m$.
- [x] **Typical $\gamma_f$ values (IS 456):** DL = LL = **1.5**; Wind = 1.2.
- [x] **Typical $\gamma_m$ values:** Concrete = **1.5**; Steel (HYSD) = **1.10**.
- [x] **Reliability index** $\beta = (\mu_R - \mu_Q) / \sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_Q^2}$.
- [x] **Target $\beta$:** 3.8 for ULS, 1.5 for SLS (50-year reference).
- [x] **Standard load combinations (IS 456 ULS):** 1.5(DL+LL); 1.2(DL+LL±WL); 1.5(DL±WL).
- [x] **Standard load combinations (IS 456 SLS):** 1.0(DL+LL); 1.0(DL±WL) — **no partial factors**.
- [x] **FOS in WSM** ≈ $\gamma_f \times \gamma_m$ in LSM (approximate equivalence).
- [x] **Number 1.645** = standard normal variate for 5% fractile (high-yield!).
- [x] **Major codes to memorize:** IS 456 (concrete), IS 800 (steel), IS 875 (loads), ACI 318, Eurocode 2/3.
- [x] LSM is **calibrated**, not guessed — $\gamma_f$ and $\gamma_m$ come from target $\beta$.
- [x] Always state the **units** in numerical answers (kN, kN·m, MPa, mm²).
- [x] Always state the **final verdict** (Safe / Unsafe) in safety-check problems.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Last-Minute Formula Trick:** If you remember only one number, remember **1.645** — it appears in 70% of the Limit State Method questions on characteristic values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Properties: Properties in Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Material Properties: Properties in Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Compressive properties** of a material are the mechanical characteristics that describe its behavior, deformation, and failure response when subjected to an axial **compressive load** — a force that tends to **shorten** the member along the line of action. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, these properties form the foundation of **safe design** of columns, struts, piers, foundations, and load-bearing members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Standard):**
> The *properties in compression* are the set of mechanical descriptors — including **compressive strength, modulus of elasticity, yield behavior, strain hardening, Poisson's effect, and mode of failure** — that govern how a material resists an externally applied crushing force.

### 1.2 Key Compressive Properties (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

- **Ultimate Compressive Strength (UCS):** Maximum stress a material can withstand under axial compression before rupture or crushing.
- **Yield Strength in Compression (σ_yc):** Stress at which the material begins to deform plastically.
- **Proportional Limit (σ_p):** Stress up to which stress is linearly proportional to strain.
- **Modulus of Elasticity (E):** Slope of the linear elastic portion of the stress–strain curve in compression.
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν):** Ratio of lateral strain to axial strain under uniaxial compression.
- **Strain Hardening Modulus (E_h):** Slope of the plastic region in the stress–strain curve.
- **Modulus of Resilience (u_r):** Energy absorbed per unit volume in the elastic region.
- **Modulus of Toughness (u_t):** Total energy per unit volume absorbed up to fracture.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sponge Under the Palm"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine pressing your palm down on a soft kitchen sponge sitting on a table. The sponge **shortens vertically** (axial strain), **bulges outward** at the sides (lateral strain governed by Poisson's ratio), and the harder you press (stress ↑), the more it compresses. Initially, when you remove your hand, it springs back — that is the **elastic region**. If you press too hard, the sponge tears/crushes — that is the **ultimate compressive failure**. Materials in real columns and piers behave in exactly this way, but with quantifiable engineering numbers.

### 1.4 Why Compression Properties Matter in Safety Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that **structural safety** is not based on the *maximum* load a material can take, but on a **factor of safety** applied to a property measured under the *most relevant loading mode*. For members such as columns, the relevant property is **compressive strength**, not tensile strength, because failure occurs by **buckling** (geometric instability) or **crushing** (material instability).

| Member Type | Dominant Property |
|---|---|
| Concrete column | Compressive strength (f_ck) |
| Steel strut | Yield strength in compression |
| Timber post | Crushing strength parallel to grain |
| Masonry pier | Compressive strength of brick/mortar |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Compressive Stress–Strain Curve — Conceptual Walkthrough

When a uniaxial compressive load **P** is applied to a uniform specimen of original cross-section **A₀** and original length **L₀**, the material passes through **four distinct regions**:

1. **Region I — Linear Elastic Region (0 → σ_p):**
   Stress and strain follow Hooke's law. Slope = Modulus of Elasticity (E). On unloading, the specimen returns to its original length with **no permanent deformation**. For most engineering materials, the compressive E equals the tensile E.

2. **Region II — Elasto-Plastic Transition (σ_p → σ_yc):**
   Micro-slip begins at crystalline/molecular level. Strain increases faster than stress. The proportional limit transitions to the **yield point in compression**. For ductile materials (mild steel), this often appears as a yield plateau.

3. **Region III — Plastic / Strain-Hardening Region (σ_yc → σ_u):**
   Permanent deformation accumulates. For ductile materials, the cross-section **increases** (barrel-shaped bulging), so true stress continues to rise even when engineering stress begins to fall. Brittle materials (cast iron, concrete) show **no distinct yield** — they proceed smoothly from elastic to crushing.

4. **Region IV — Failure / Crushing:**
   The material ruptures along shear planes (typically at 45° in ductile materials) or splits vertically in brittle materials. For concrete, this is characterized by **cone-and-shear** failure.

### 2.2 Ductile vs Brittle Behavior in Compression

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:**
> A *brittle* material in tension may still behave *quasi-ductile* in compression because compressive failure requires **shear slip** along internal planes, whereas tensile failure only requires **separation**. This is why concrete columns can carry large loads even though concrete is brittle in tension.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressive Stress | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | N/mm² or MPa | P = axial compressive load, A = cross-section |
| 2 | Compressive Strain | $\varepsilon_c = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | dimensionless | ΔL = shortening |
| 3 | Hooke's Law (Compression) | $\sigma_c = E \cdot \varepsilon_c$ | MPa | Valid in elastic region only |
| 4 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | dimensionless | ε_lat is lateral (expansion) strain |
| 5 | Volumetric Strain | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{E}\left(1 - 2\nu\right)$ | dimensionless | For uniaxial loading |
| 6 | Shear Modulus | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | MPa | Isotropic material relation |
| 7 | Bulk Modulus | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | MPa | Resistance to hydrostatic compression |
| 8 | Strain Energy (Elastic) | $U = \dfrac{\sigma_c^{\,2} \, V}{2E}$ | J (Joules) | V = volume of compressed region |
| 9 | Modulus of Resilience | $u_r = \dfrac{\sigma_{yc}^{\,2}}{2E}$ | J/m³ | Energy per unit volume to yield |
| 10 | Engineering Stress (post-bulge) | $\sigma_{eng} = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | MPa | Uses original area |
| 11 | True Stress (post-bulge) | $\sigma_{true} = \dfrac{P}{A_i}$ | MPa | Uses instantaneous area |
| 12 | Safety Factor | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | dimensionless | KTU design requirement |
| 13 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$ | dimensionless | r_min = minimum radius of gyration |
| 14 | Euler's Buckling Load | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{\,2}}$ | N | Long slender columns only |
| 15 | Rankine's Critical Load | $P_{R} = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a\left(\dfrac{L_{eff}}{r}\right)^{2}}$ | N | Combined crushing + buckling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation:** The KTU board uses $\vert P \vert$ to denote magnitude; in formulas above, **P is always taken as positive** (since it represents a magnitude of compressive force). When writing inline, students may write $P$ without sign convention.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Compressive Properties Are Used

- **Design of reinforced-concrete columns:** Governed by **f_ck** (characteristic compressive cube strength of concrete, typically tested at **28 days**).
- **Steel struts in trusses:** Governed by **yield strength in compression** (≈ same as tensile yield for mild steel).
- **Machine foundations and bearing pads:** Governed by **compressive modulus of elasticity** to control settlement.
- **Earthquake-resistant design:** Compressive ductility of concrete and steel is critical for energy dissipation.
- **Safety audits (PESFT754 context):** Knowledge of compressive failure modes is essential to **predict** and **prevent** crushing-type structural collapse.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Compressive Modulus of Elasticity (E) from First Principles

We begin with the **definition of stress** in a uniaxially compressed prismatic bar.

**Step 1 — Consider a prismatic specimen:**
Original length = $L_0$
Original cross-sectional area = $A_0$
Applied axial compressive load = $P$

**Step 2 — Define normal compressive stress:**

$$
\sigma_c \;=\; \lim_{A \to A_0}\dfrac{P}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A_0}
$$

*Logic:* Stress is force distributed over the area. Since $P$ is uniform, the limit becomes a simple ratio.

**Step 3 — Define axial compressive strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_c \;=\; \lim_{L \to L_0}\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{L_0 - L_f}{L_0}
$$

*Logic:* Strain is the relative change in length. In compression, $L_f < L_0$, so $\Delta L$ is treated as a *shortening* (positive magnitude).

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law in the linear elastic region:**

$$
\sigma_c \;=\; E \cdot \varepsilon_c
$$

*Logic:* Robert Hooke's experimental observation (1660) — for small strains, stress is linearly proportional to strain. The constant of proportionality is the modulus of elasticity.

**Step 5 — Substitute (2) and (3) into (4):**

$$
E \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon_c} \;=\; \dfrac{P/A_0}{\Delta L/L_0} \;=\; \dfrac{P \, L_0}{A_0 \, \Delta L}
$$

*Logic:* This is the **slope of the linear portion** of the experimental compressive stress–strain curve, measured by the slope of the tangent at the origin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Lateral Strain and Volumetric Strain in Compression

**Step 1 — Under uniaxial compression, the material shortens axially:**

$$
\varepsilon_{axial} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c}{E}
$$

**Step 2 — Poisson's effect causes lateral expansion:**

$$
\varepsilon_{lateral} \;=\; -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} \;=\; -\dfrac{\nu \sigma_c}{E}
$$

*Logic:* The negative sign indicates that as the bar shortens (axial strain positive magnitude), the cross-section expands (lateral strain direction is opposite).

**Step 3 — Volumetric strain is the sum of strains in three orthogonal directions:**

For uniaxial compression along the x-axis:

$$
\varepsilon_v \;=\; \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z
$$

$$
\varepsilon_v \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c}{E} + 2\left(-\dfrac{\nu \sigma_c}{E}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c}{E}\left(1 - 2\nu\right)
$$

*Logic:* Two lateral directions both experience the same Poisson expansion.

**Step 4 — Constraint for non-negative volume change under compression:**

For the material to not *increase* in volume under pure compression, the following must hold:

$$
0 \;\le\; \nu \;\le\; 0.5
$$

*Logic:* If $\nu > 0.5$, the volume would increase when compressed — a physical impossibility for stable isotropic solids.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Strain Energy Stored in Elastic Compression

**Step 1 — Consider an elemental slice of volume dV under stress σ_c:**

The strain energy density (energy per unit volume) is:

$$
u \;=\; \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_c} \sigma \, d\varepsilon
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Hooke's law σ = Eε:**

$$
u \;=\; \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_c} E \varepsilon \, d\varepsilon \;=\; \left[\dfrac{E \varepsilon^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{\varepsilon_c} \;=\; \dfrac{E \varepsilon_c^{\,2}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Replace ε_c with σ_c / E:**

$$
u \;=\; \dfrac{E}{2}\left(\dfrac{\sigma_c}{E}\right)^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c^{\,2}}{2E}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by total volume V to obtain total strain energy:**

$$
U \;=\; u \cdot V \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c^{\,2} \, V}{2E}
$$

*Logic:* This energy is recoverable (released) if the load is removed within the elastic limit. It is the energy budget the structure can absorb without permanent damage.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A circular concrete column of diameter **150 mm** and height **3 m** supports an axial compressive load of **400 kN**. Given E = **25 GPa** and ν = **0.20**, calculate the (a) compressive stress, (b) axial shortening, (c) lateral expansion of diameter, and (d) volumetric strain.

**Given:**
- $D = 150 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.150)^{2}}{4} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $L_0 = 3 \text{ m}$
- $P = 400 \text{ kN} = 400{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $E = 25 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$
- $\nu = 0.20$

**Solution:**

**(a) Compressive stress:**

$$
\sigma_c \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{400{,}000}{0.01767} \;=\; 22.63 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \;=\; 22.63 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(b) Axial shortening:**

$$
\Delta L \;=\; \dfrac{P L_0}{A E} \;=\; \dfrac{400{,}000 \times 3}{0.01767 \times 25 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 2.72 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \;=\; 2.72 \text{ mm}
$$

**(c) Lateral expansion of diameter:**

$$
\varepsilon_{axial} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c}{E} \;=\; \dfrac{22.63 \times 10^{6}}{25 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 9.05 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{lateral} \;=\; -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} \;=\; -0.20 \times 9.05 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; -1.81 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\Delta D \;=\; \varepsilon_{lateral} \cdot D \;=\; 1.81 \times 10^{-4} \times 150 \;=\; 0.0272 \text{ mm}
$$

(The diameter increases by 0.0272 mm — this is the **bulging effect**.)

**(d) Volumetric strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_v \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c}{E}\left(1 - 2\nu\right) \;=\; 9.05 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.40) \;=\; 5.43 \times 10^{-4}
$$

The volume decreases by ~0.054% under this load — confirming the column is in the elastic regime.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: Compression vs Tension Properties

| Property | Tension | Compression | Key Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mode of failure | Separation / necking | Crushing / shear slip | Different physics |
| Modulus of Elasticity (E) | E | ≈ E | Same for most metals |
| Ultimate Strength | σ_ut | σ_uc | σ_uc > σ_ut for brittle materials (e.g., concrete: σ_uc ≈ 10× σ_ut) |
| Yield Point | σ_yt | σ_yc | σ_yc ≈ σ_yt for ductile metals |
| Strain at failure | High (ductile) | Low | Compression is "stiffer" |
| Poisson effect | Lateral contraction | Lateral expansion | Sign reverses |
| Typical fracture angle | ≈ 90° (cup-cone) | ≈ 45° (shear plane) | Indicates failure mode |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Compressive Stress–Strain Curve (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LinearElastic: Apply Load P
    LinearElastic: Linear Elastic Region
    LinearElastic: Stress proportional to strain
    LinearElastic: Slope = Modulus E
    LinearElastic --> YieldPoint: Reaches Proportional Limit
    YieldPoint: Yield Point in Compression
    YieldPoint: Plastic deformation begins
    YieldPoint: sigma equals sigma_yc
    YieldPoint --> StrainHardening: Continued loading
    StrainHardening: Strain Hardening Region
    StrainHardening: Barrel-shaped bulging
    StrainHardening: Cross-section increases
    StrainHardening --> UltimatePoint: Reaches UCS
    UltimatePoint: Ultimate Compressive Strength
    UltimatePoint: sigma equals sigma_uc
    UltimatePoint --> ShearFailure: Continued deformation
    ShearFailure: Shear Failure at 45 degrees
    ShearFailure: Rupture along slip planes
    ShearFailure --> [*]: Material Fractures
```

### 4.2 Universal Testing Machine — Compressive Test Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Placement] --> B[Centering in UTM Platens]
    B --> C[Apply Pre-load 0.5 kN]
    C --> D{Alignment Check}
    D -->|Misaligned| E[Re-align Specimen]
    E --> B
    D -->|Aligned| F[Begin Monotonic Loading]
    F --> G[Record Load and Displacement]
    G --> H{Stress Level}
    H -->|Below sigma_p| I[Elastic Region: E constant]
    H -->|sigma_p to sigma_yc| J[Plastic Onset: Barrel Bulge]
    H -->|Above sigma_yc| K[Strain Hardening]
    K --> L[Reaches UCS sigma_uc]
    L --> M[Shear Failure at 45 deg]
    M --> N[Post-test Data Reduction]
    N --> O[Plot Stress Strain Curve]
    O --> P[Compute E, sigma_yc, UCS, nu]
    P --> Q[Report Compressive Properties]
```

### 4.3 Failure Mode Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressive Loading on Material] --> B{Material Type}
    B -->|Ductile Steel| C[Bulging Barrel Shape]
    B -->|Brittle Concrete| D[Vertical Splitting Cracks]
    B -->|Brittle Cast Iron| E[45 Degree Shear Cone]
    B -->|Timber Parallel to Grain| F[End Grain Crushing]
    B -->|Masonry| G[Stepped Mortar Joint Failure]
    C --> H[Ductile Shear Rupture]
    D --> I[Brittle Tensile Split]
    E --> J[Brittle Shear Cone]
    F --> K[Fiber Buckling]
    G --> L[Sliding Shear Failure]
    H --> M[Design for Ductility]
    I --> N[Design with Confinement]
    J --> O[Design for Shear]
    K --> P[Design with End Bearing Plates]
    L --> Q[Design with Bond Strength]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**
*Define compressive strength and modulus of elasticity. How is the compressive modulus of elasticity determined experimentally for a concrete specimen?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** **Compressive strength** is the maximum compressive stress a material can withstand before failure. Mathematically:
$$
\sigma_c \;=\; \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}
$$
where $P_{max}$ is the crushing load and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area.

- **(1 mark)** **Modulus of elasticity (E)** is the slope of the linear portion of the stress–strain curve in the elastic region. It measures stiffness.

- **(1 mark)** **Experimental determination for concrete:** A standard cylinder (150 mm dia × 300 mm height) or cube (150 mm) is loaded in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) under monotonically increasing axial compression. Strain is measured using a compressometer or strain gauges. The slope of the secant line at 33% of ultimate load on the stress–strain curve is taken as the static modulus of elasticity $E_c$ (per IS 516).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
*Distinguish between brittle and ductile failure modes under compressive loading, with one example material for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Brittle Failure | Ductile Failure |
|---|---|---|
| Strain at failure | Very small | Large |
| Warning before failure | None | Visible bulging |
| Energy absorbed | Low | High |
| Fracture surface | Splintered, vertical splits | Shear lips at 45° |
| Example | Concrete, cast iron, stone | Mild steel, aluminium |

- **(1.5 marks)** *Example + description of brittle:* Concrete fails by **vertical splitting cracks** because lateral tensile strains (from Poisson effect) exceed the low tensile strength of concrete.
- **(1.5 marks)** *Example + description of ductile:* Mild steel fails by **shear slip along planes at ~45°** to the loading axis, after extensive plastic deformation and barrel-shaped bulging.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **strain energy stored per unit volume** in a material under uniaxial compression in the elastic region. State one engineering application where this quantity is critical. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A short concrete column of **square cross-section 250 mm × 250 mm** and height **1.5 m** is subjected to an axial compressive load of **800 kN**. Given $E = 30$ GPa and $\nu = 0.18$, determine: (i) compressive stress, (ii) axial shortening, (iii) lateral strain, (iv) volumetric strain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Strain energy per unit volume is defined as work done by internal stresses during deformation:

$$
u \;=\; \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_c}\sigma \, d\varepsilon
$$

**Step 2:** In the linear elastic region, Hooke's law gives $\sigma = E\varepsilon$:

$$
u \;=\; \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_c}E\varepsilon \, d\varepsilon \;=\; \dfrac{E\varepsilon_c^{\,2}}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $\varepsilon_c = \sigma_c / E$:

$$
\boxed{u \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c^{\,2}}{2E}}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiplied by volume V:

$$
U \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c^{\,2}V}{2E}
$$

**Engineering application (1 mark):** In the design of **machine foundation pads and bearing plates**, the strain energy capacity governs how much shock/impact load the pad can absorb elastically without permanent deformation. Also critical in **earthquake engineering** — structures must dissipate seismic energy through elastic strain energy or controlled plastic deformation.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $A = 250 \times 250 = 62{,}500 \text{ mm}^{2} = 0.0625 \text{ m}^{2}$, $L_0 = 1.5$ m, $P = 800$ kN $= 8 \times 10^{5}$ N, $E = 30 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $\nu = 0.18$.

**(i) Compressive stress:**

$$
\sigma_c \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{8 \times 10^{5}}{0.0625} \;=\; 12.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \;=\; \mathbf{12.8 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(ii) Axial shortening:**

$$
\Delta L \;=\; \dfrac{P L_0}{AE} \;=\; \dfrac{8 \times 10^{5} \times 1.5}{0.0625 \times 30 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{6}}{1.875 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 6.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \;=\; \mathbf{0.64 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(iii) Lateral strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_{axial} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c}{E} \;=\; \dfrac{12.8 \times 10^{6}}{30 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 4.267 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{lateral} \;=\; -\nu \varepsilon_{axial} \;=\; -0.18 \times 4.267 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; \mathbf{-7.68 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

(Negative sign indicates expansion; the magnitude is $7.68 \times 10^{-5}$.)

**(iv) Volumetric strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_v \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c}{E}\left(1 - 2\nu\right) \;=\; 4.267 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.36) \;=\; 4.267 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.64 \;=\; \mathbf{2.73 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

The volume decreases by 0.0273% — column is safely in the elastic regime.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **compressive stress–strain behavior** of a ductile material (e.g., mild steel) and a brittle material (e.g., concrete) with neat sketches. Compare their UCS, yield behavior, and failure modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solid circular steel column of **diameter 100 mm** and **length 2.0 m** is hinged at both ends. Given $E = 200$ GPa and $\sigma_c = 250$ MPa (yield), find the **Euler's critical buckling load** and the **Rankine critical load** using Rankine's constant $a = 1/7500$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Ductile Material (Mild Steel) in Compression:**

1. **Region I — Linear elastic** (0 → σ_p ≈ 200 MPa): Stress-strain linear, slope = E = 200 GPa.
2. **Region II — Yield plateau** (≈ 200 → 250 MPa): Material yields, plastic strain develops at constant stress.
3. **Region III — Strain hardening** (250 → ~400 MPa): Barrel-shaped bulging appears, cross-section increases.
4. **Region IV — Failure** at ~45° shear planes: Material ruptures by shear slip.

**Brittle Material (Concrete) in Compression:**

1. **Region I — Linear elastic** (0 → 30–40% of UCS): Hookean behavior, E ≈ 25–30 GPa.
2. **Region II — Microcracking** (40% → 100% UCS): Internal microcracks coalesce; stress–strain curve becomes nonlinear, concave-down.
3. **Region III — Failure:** No distinct yield. Specimen fails by **vertical splitting** or **cone-and-shear** failure. Strain at failure is very small (~0.002).

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Property | Mild Steel (Ductile) | Concrete (Brittle) |
|---|---|---|
| UCS | 400–500 MPa | 25–60 MPa |
| Yield | Distinct | Absent |
| E | 200 GPa | 25–30 GPa |
| Strain at failure | High (~0.25) | Low (~0.002) |
| Failure mode | 45° shear | Vertical splitting |
| Failure warning | Yes (bulge) | No (sudden) |

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $D = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m, $L = 2.0$ m, $E = 200 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $\sigma_c = 250 \times 10^{6}$ Pa, $a = 1/7500$.
For both ends hinged: $L_{eff} = L = 2.0$ m.

**Step 1: Cross-sectional properties**

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^{4}}{64} \;=\; 4.909 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

$$
r_{min} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} \;=\; \dfrac{D}{4} \;=\; 0.025 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0}{0.025} \;=\; 80
$$

**Step 2: Euler's critical load**

$$
P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{\,2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 4.909 \times 10^{-6}}{(2.0)^{2}}
$$

$$
P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{9.694 \times 10^{6}}{4.0} \;=\; \mathbf{2.424 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \;=\; 2424 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 3: Rankine's critical load**

$$
P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \lambda^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{250 \times 10^{6} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{1 + (1/7500)(80)^{2}}
$$

$$
P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{1.9635 \times 10^{6}}{1 + 0.8533} \;=\; \dfrac{1.9635 \times 10^{6}}{1.8533} \;=\; \mathbf{1.0594 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \;\approx\; 1059 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Comparison (1 mark):** Since the column is *short-to-intermediate* (λ = 80), Rankine's formula gives a *lower* (and more realistic) critical load than Euler's. Euler's formula overestimates for short columns because it neglects direct compressive yielding.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign of Poisson's strain:** Students often write lateral strain as *positive* in compression, forgetting that the lateral direction *expands* (opposite sign convention). Always state the sign clearly.
> 2. **Unit mismatches:** Mixing **N/mm²** and **MPa** without conversion; mixing **mm** and **m** in the same equation. KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark per occurrence**.
> 3. **Confusing σ_eng vs σ_true:** In the post-yield region, the cross-section bulges. Engineering stress (using A₀) *decreases*, but true stress (using A_i) *increases*. Many students lose 2 marks by stating ultimate strength decreases — that is only true for engineering stress.
> 4. **Buckling vs Crushing:** For **short** columns (λ < critical slenderness ratio), failure is by **crushing** (material property). For **long** columns (λ > critical), failure is by **buckling** (Euler). Students often apply Euler's formula to short columns — wrong.
> 5. **Forgetting 1 - 2ν in volumetric strain:** A common 1-mark error.
> 6. **Strain energy dimension:** Students forget that strain energy per unit volume has units of **J/m³** = **Pa**, not Joules. Total strain energy = u × V (Joules).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 12 Things to Remember for KTU Board Exam:**

- ☐ **Compressive stress** is always taken as a **positive magnitude**; the load **P** is positive in standard KTU notation.
- ☐ **Hooke's law** $\sigma_c = E\varepsilon_c$ is valid **only in the elastic region** (typically up to the proportional limit).
- ☐ **Poisson's ratio** is bounded by $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$ for stable isotropic materials; typical values: steel = 0.30, concrete = 0.18–0.20, rubber ≈ 0.49.
- ☐ **E (compression) ≈ E (tension)** for most metals, but **UCS >> UTS** for brittle materials (e.g., concrete).
- ☐ **Volumetric strain** $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$ is the key to predicting **whether a material expands or contracts in volume** under load.
- ☐ **Strain energy per unit volume** $u = \dfrac{\sigma_c^{2}}{2E}$ — energy is *inversely* proportional to E. A *stiffer* material stores *less* elastic energy per unit volume at the same stress.
- ☐ **Ductile** materials fail in compression at **45° shear planes**; **brittle** materials fail by **vertical splitting**.
- ☐ **Euler's formula** applies to **long, slender columns**; **Rankine's formula** applies to **all slenderness ratios**.
- ☐ **Barrel-shaped bulging** of ductile specimens is due to **friction at the platens** + **Poisson's effect**.
- ☐ **Modulus of resilience** $u_r = \sigma_{yc}^{2}/(2E)$ measures a material's capacity to absorb energy elastically — critical for impact-resistant design.
- ☐ In KTU answers, always state **units explicitly**: stress in **MPa** or **N/mm²**, force in **kN** or **N**, dimensions in **mm** or **m** consistently.
- ☐ **Safety factor** in compression design = $\sigma_{uc}/\sigma_{working}$ — typically **2.5 to 3.5** for concrete columns, **1.5 to 2.0** for steel struts (per KTU/IS code conventions).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tensile Strength
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Tensile Strength** (also called **Ultimate Tensile Strength, UTS**, or in KTU parlance, the **Tensile Strength, $f_{tk}$** or $f_{ut}$) is defined as the maximum stress that a material can withstand while being stretched or pulled before failing or breaking. It is the peak point on the engineering stress–strain curve during a uniaxial tensile test.

Mathematically, it is the ratio of the maximum load ($F_{max}$) the specimen carries to its original cross-sectional area ($A_0$):

$$f_{ut} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}$$

where $f_{ut}$ is expressed in **MPa (N/mm²)** or **GPa**, $F_{max}$ is in **Newtons (N)**, and $A_0$ is in **mm²**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: The KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT754 - Safety in Design of Structures, Module 1) defines *Tensile Strength* as the limiting tensile stress beyond which a structural member transitions from stable elastic–plastic deformation to unstable necking and eventual fracture. This is a governing parameter for setting the **ultimate limit state (ULS)** safety factors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thick **rubber band** that you stretch between your two hands:
- Initially, it stretches easily and returns to its original shape when released → **Elastic Region**.
- As you stretch further, it becomes harder to pull and you feel increasing resistance → **Strain-Hardening Region**.
- At one point, you feel it is at its tightest; just a small additional pull breaks it → **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($f_{ut}$)**.
- The narrow zone where the rubber band becomes thin before snapping is called **necking**, leading to **fracture**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering intuition**: Tensile Strength is NOT the stress at fracture. It is the **peak** stress the material reaches *before* necking collapses its load-carrying capacity. The fracture stress may be lower because the cross-section has reduced locally.

## 1.3 Key Material Properties Connected to Tensile Strength

| Property | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Proportional Limit | $f_p$ | End of linear Hookean region |
| Elastic Limit | $f_e$ | Maximum stress recoverable without permanent deformation |
| Yield Strength | $f_y$ | Stress at which plastic deformation begins (**0.2 % offset**) |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | $f_{ut}$ | Maximum stress on the stress–strain curve |
| Fracture/Breaking Strength | $f_{fr}$ | Stress at actual failure (after necking) |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | Stiffness in the linear elastic region |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Board Exams**: *"P–E–Y–U–F"* — Proportional, Elastic, Yield, Ultimate, Fracture — the five characteristic points on a tensile stress–strain curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Uniaxial Tensile Test — Operational Logic

A standardized specimen (typically circular or rectangular cross-section as per **IS 1601 / ASTM E8 / ISO 6892-1**) is subjected to a monotonically increasing axial tensile load $F$ in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM). The test captures:

1. **Load $F$** vs. **Elongation $\Delta L$** (raw data).
2. Conversion to **Engineering Stress $\sigma$** vs. **Engineering Strain $\varepsilon$** using the *original* dimensions.
3. Identification of characteristic limits: $f_p$, $f_e$, $f_y$, $f_{ut}$, $f_{fr}$.

The governing conversion equations are:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} \quad \text{(Engineering Stress)}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \quad \text{(Engineering Strain)}$$

## 2.2 Why Tensile Strength Governs Safety Design

In limit-state design (KTU 2024 follows **IS 456:2000** for concrete, **IS 800:2007** for steel), the design strength is derived as:

$$f_{d} = \frac{f_{ut}}{\gamma_m}$$

where $\gamma_m$ is the **partial safety factor for material** ($\gamma_m = 1.25$ for steel, $1.5$ for concrete in KTU/IS codal practice).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Safety in Design" cares about $f_{ut}$**: Even though *yield* governs serviceability for ductile steel, $f_{ut}$ governs **collapse prevention**, **brittle failure check**, and **anchorage / connection design** (e.g., bolt shear, weld strength, rebar lap length).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | Engineering stress from tensile load |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain |
| 3 | $f_{ut} = F_{max} / A_0$ | Ultimate tensile strength definition |
| 4 | $f_y = F_y / A_0$ | Yield strength (0.2 % proof stress for steels) |
| 5 | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (valid up to proportional limit only) |
| 6 | $\% \, \text{elongation} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Ductility measure |
| 7 | $\% \, \text{RA} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Reduction in area (ductility indicator) |
| 8 | $f_d = f_{ut} / \gamma_m$ | Design strength (limit-state method) |
| 9 | $\text{FoS} = f_{ut} / \sigma_{working}$ | Factor of safety (allowable stress method) |
| 10 | $f_{st} = 0.85 \, f_y$ | Design strength of steel per IS 456 (working stress method, restricted use) |
| 11 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \sigma \, \varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2E}$ | Modulus of resilience (energy stored elastically per unit volume) |
| 12 | Toughness $\approx \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Total area under $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve up to fracture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Pitfall**: Do NOT confuse **Engineering Stress** (uses original area $A_0$) with **True Stress** (uses instantaneous area $A_i$). At necking, $A_i < A_0$, so **True Stress > Engineering Stress**. The KTU board specifically asks this as a 3-mark Part A question.

## 2.4 True Stress vs. Engineering Stress — The Why Behind the Curve Drop

After $f_{ut}$, the load begins to fall in the engineering diagram because the load is computed against the *original* area $A_0$, but the actual cross-section is shrinking rapidly due to necking. The **True Stress** $\sigma_{true}$ continues to rise:

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_i} = \sigma_{eng} \, (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

assuming constant volume plastic flow ($A_i \, L_i = A_0 \, L_0$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of $f_{ut}$ |
|---|---|
| **Structural Steel Design** | Setting $\gamma_m$ in IS 800:2007, governing ultimate limit state |
| **Prestressed Concrete** | Tendon tensile capacity: $P_u = f_{ut} \cdot A_p$ |
| **Bridges & Cables** | Suspension cable / stay cable design based on $f_{ut}$ of wire strands |
| **Pressure Vessels** | Hoop stress check: $\sigma_h = p r / t \leq f_{ut} / \gamma_m$ |
| **Adhesive / Bolt Design** | Shear-tensile conversion: $f_{shear} \approx 0.6 \, f_{ut}$ (von Mises approx.) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Engineering Stress and Strain from First Principles

Consider a cylindrical specimen of original gauge length $L_0$ and original cross-sectional area $A_0$. Apply an axial tensile load $F$ along its centroidal axis.

**Step 1:** The internal axial force at any cross-section equals the applied load $F$ (by equilibrium).

$$N_{internal} = F$$

**Step 2:** The average normal stress (engineering stress) on the cross-section is force per unit original area:

$$\sigma = \frac{N_{internal}}{A_0} = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 3:** The extension of the gauge length under load is $\Delta L = L - L_0$. The engineering strain is the ratio of elongation to original length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

**Step 4:** The slope of the linear (elastic) portion of the $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve is **Young's Modulus**, $E$:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = E \, \varepsilon$$

This is Hooke's Law, valid only up to the proportional limit.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ultimate Tensile Strength from Load–Displacement Data

The UTM records load $F$ and extension $\Delta L$ continuously. The KTU-standard specimen geometry (round bar) has:

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \, d_0^{2}, \quad L_0 = 5.65 \, \sqrt{A_0} \quad \text{(proportional, per IS 1601)}$$

The peak of the $F$ vs. $\Delta L$ plot corresponds to load $F_{max}$. The ultimate tensile strength is computed as:

$$f_{ut} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}$$

**Example numeric walkthrough** (typical KTU lab value):

$$\begin{aligned}
d_0 &= 12 \, \text{mm} \\
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (12)^2 = 113.10 \, \text{mm}^2 \\
F_{max} &= 56{,}500 \, \text{N} \quad \text{(read from UTM peak)} \\
f_{ut} &= \frac{56{,}500}{113.10} = 499.6 \, \text{N/mm}^2 \approx 500 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches Fe415 grade steel.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — True Stress from Constant-Volume Plasticity Assumption

In the plastic region (after yield), material deforms at constant volume:

$$V = A \, L = A_0 \, L_0 = \text{constant}$$

Therefore:

$$A_i = \frac{A_0 \, L_0}{L_i}$$

Engineering strain:

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_i = L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_i &= \frac{A_0 \, L_0}{L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_{eng}}
\end{aligned}$$

True stress:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{true} &= \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0 / (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})} \\
&= \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) \\
&= \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})
\end{aligned}$$

This is the standard engineering result.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Design Strength under IS 800:2007 (KTU Limit-State Method)

The design strength of a steel member in tension is governed by the **governing design stress** $f_d$:

$$f_d = \frac{f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

where:
- $f_u$ = ultimate tensile strength of steel ($f_{ut}$),
- $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ (partial safety factor for material, per IS 800:2007 Clause 5.4.1).

**Design tensile capacity** of a member:

$$T_{dg} = \frac{f_u \, A_g}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

where $A_g$ is the gross cross-sectional area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a member with holes (e.g., bolted connection), the capacity is governed by **net section rupture**:

$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \, f_u \, A_n}{\gamma_{m1}}$$

where $A_n$ = net effective area and $0.9$ accounts for shear lag (IS 800:2007 Cl. 6.3.1).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Factor of Safety (Allowable Stress Method, Working Stress Method)

The classical Factor of Safety (FoS) is defined as:

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{f_{ut}}{\sigma_{working}}$$

For steel:
- Ductile steel (mild steel): $\text{FoS} \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ against $f_y$.
- Brittle materials (cast iron): $\text{FoS} \approx 2.0$ to $3.0$ against $f_{ut}$.
- For prestressing tendons: $\text{FoS} \approx 1.67$ (post-tension) and $2.0$ (pre-tension) per IS 1343.

**Reason behind different FoS**: Ductile materials yield before fracture (warning sign); brittle materials fail suddenly without warning. Hence **brittle = higher FoS**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Uniaxial Tensile Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Mounted in UTM] --> B[Load Cell Measures F]
    A --> C[Extensometer Measures deltaL]
    B --> D[Data Acquisition System]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Compute sigma = F by A0]
    D --> F[Compute epsilon = deltaL by L0]
    E --> G[sigma vs epsilon Curve]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Identify fp]
    G --> I[Identify fy]
    G --> J[Identify fut at Peak]
    G --> K[Identify ffr at Fracture]
    J --> L[Compute Design Strength fd]
    L --> M[Report to Design Office]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic of Stress–Strain Curve (Low-Carbon Steel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Origin] --> P[Proportional Limit fp]
    P --> EL[Elastic Limit fe]
    EL --> Y[Yield Point fy]
    Y --> SH[Strain Hardening Region]
    SH --> UT[Ultimate Tensile Strength fut]
    UT --> NK[Necking Region]
    NK --> FR[Fracture Point ffr]
    SH -.->|true stress continues to rise| TR[True Stress Curve]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Role of Tensile Strength in Safety Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Tested for fut] --> B{Is Material Ductile?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use fy with FoS for design]
    B -- No --> D[Use fut directly with higher FoS]
    C --> E[Apply gamma_m for Limit State]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Design Strength fd]
    F --> G[Check Against Applied Stress]
    G --> H{Safe?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Section OK]
    H -- No --> J[Revise Section]
```

## 4.4 Anatomy of a Standard Tensile Specimen (KTU Lab Context)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Grip End] --> B[Gauge Length L0]
    B --> C[Gauge Length L0]
    C --> D[Reduced Parallel Section]
    D --> E[Fillets]
    E --> F[Shoulder]
    F --> G[Other Grip End]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Standard proportions** (per IS 1601):
> - Gauge length $L_0 = 5.65 \, \sqrt{A_0}$ for proportional round bars.
> - Reduced section diameter $d_0$ is uniform within $\pm 0.5\%$.
> - Grip ends are threaded or shouldered to transfer load uniformly without slippage.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q: Define Ultimate Tensile Strength. How is it distinguished from Yield Strength and Fracture Strength?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ultimate Tensile Strength ($f_{ut}$) is the maximum engineering stress a material can sustain under uniaxial tension, computed as $f_{ut} = F_{max} / A_0$, where $F_{max}$ is the peak load recorded during the test and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area. **(1 mark)**

- **Yield Strength ($f_y$)** is the stress at which the material begins to deform plastically (typically defined by the 0.2 % offset method for steels without a sharp yield point). **(1 mark)**
- **Fracture Strength ($f_{fr}$)** is the stress at which the specimen physically separates; it is **lower** than $f_{ut}$ because necking has reduced the cross-section, and the engineering stress uses the *original* area $A_0$. **(1 mark)**

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the salient points on a stress–strain diagram for a ductile material like mild steel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A typical stress–strain curve for mild steel exhibits: **(1 mark)**

1. **Proportional limit ($f_p$):** End of the linear Hookean region where $\sigma \propto \varepsilon$.
2. **Elastic limit ($f_e$):** Maximum stress recoverable without permanent set.
3. **Upper and lower yield points ($f_y$):** A characteristic drop observed in mild steel.
4. **Strain-hardening region:** Stress rises (slowly) up to the **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($f_{ut}$)**. **(1 mark)**
5. **Necking region:** Cross-section reduces locally, engineering stress falls.
6. **Fracture point ($f_{fr}$):** Specimen physically breaks. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit units** in the final answer → deduct **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> - Confusing **engineering stress** with **true stress** is a 1-mark trap in $f_{ut}$ questions.
> - Drawing stress–strain diagrams **without labels and axes values** → lose up to **2 marks** per IS 800:2007 marking scheme.
> - In design questions, **forgetting the partial safety factor $\gamma_m$** is the most common 2-mark deduction.
> - Always specify whether you are using **Limit State Method** or **Working Stress Method** — the FoS values differ.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A 16 mm diameter round bar of mild steel is tested in a UTM. The peak load recorded is **125 kN**. The bar yields at **95 kN** and fractures at **78 kN** (after necking). The original gauge length was **50 mm**, and the final fractured length was **65 mm**.

**Compute:**
1. Ultimate Tensile Strength, $f_{ut}$ (3 marks)
2. Yield Strength, $f_y$ (2 marks)
3. Engineering Fracture Stress, $f_{fr}$ (2 marks)

**(b)** Using IS 800:2007, determine the design tensile capacity of a flat steel plate of gross width **80 mm** and thickness **12 mm**, with two bolt holes of **22 mm diameter** in a staggered pattern. Take $f_u = 410 \, \text{MPa}$ and $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$. Discuss the governing failure mode. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a): Step-by-step solution**

Original cross-sectional area:

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \, d_0^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (16)^2 = 201.06 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**(1) Ultimate Tensile Strength:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{ut} &= \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{125 \times 10^{3} \, \text{N}}{201.06 \, \text{mm}^2} \\
f_{ut} &= 621.7 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 621.7 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2) Yield Strength:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_y &= \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{95 \times 10^{3}}{201.06} \\
f_y &= 472.5 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(3) Engineering Fracture Stress:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{fr} &= \frac{F_{fr}}{A_0} = \frac{78 \times 10^{3}}{201.06} \\
f_{fr} &= 387.9 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Observation:** $f_{fr} < f_{ut}$ — confirming necking caused load drop even though the material is intact (fracture is local phenomenon).

**Part (b): Step-by-step solution**

**Given:**
- Gross width $B_g = 80$ mm, thickness $t = 12$ mm
- $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$
- 2 bolt holes, hole diameter $d_h = 22$ mm
- For staggered pattern, use staggered path length correction per IS 800:2007 Cl. 6.3.2.

**Step 1:** Gross area:

$$A_g = B_g \cdot t = 80 \times 12 = 960 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Net area (assuming straight transverse path for simplicity):

$$A_n = (B_g - n \, d_h) \, t = (80 - 2 \times 22) \times 12 = (80 - 44) \times 12 = 432 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Design strength on gross section (yielding):

$$T_{dg} = \frac{f_y \, A_g}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{250 \times 960}{1.10} = 218{,}182 \, \text{N} \approx 218.2 \, \text{kN}$$

(Taking $f_y = 250$ MPa for Fe250 grade as standard.)

**Step 4:** Design strength on net section (rupture):

$$T_{dn} = \frac{0.9 \, f_u \, A_n}{\gamma_{m1}} = \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 432}{1.25}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{dn} &= \frac{0.9 \times 410 \times 432}{1.25} \\
&= \frac{159{,}408}{1.25} \\
&= 127{,}526 \, \text{N} \approx 127.5 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Governing failure mode**

$$\boxed{T_{dg} = 218.2 \, \text{kN} \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_{dn} = 127.5 \, \text{kN}}$$

Since $T_{dn} < T_{dg}$, the **net section rupture** governs the design. **(7 marks)**

**Conclusion:** The plate's design tensile capacity is **127.5 kN**, governed by fracture of the net section at the bolt holes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **True Stress** and **Engineering Stress**, assuming constant-volume plastic deformation. State the condition under which this relationship holds. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pre-stressing tendon of **12 wires, 7 mm diameter each**, is used in a post-tensioned concrete beam. The ultimate tensile strength of the wire is **1860 MPa**. Per IS 1343, the partial safety factor is $\gamma_m = 1.67$. Calculate:
1. Total ultimate tensile capacity of the tendon. (3 marks)
2. Jacking force if initial prestress is 80 % of $f_{ut}$. (2 marks)
3. Maximum permissible force in the tendon at service (assume FoS = 1.67 against $f_{ut}$). (2 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a): Derivation of True Stress from Engineering Stress**

Assume the material deforms plastically at **constant volume** (incompressibility during plastic flow):

$$A_i \, L_i = A_0 \, L_0$$

From the definition of engineering strain:

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_i = L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

Substituting into the constant-volume equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_i \, L_0 (1 + \varepsilon_{eng}) &= A_0 \, L_0 \\
A_i &= \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon_{eng}}
\end{aligned}$$

Engineering stress:

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

True stress (using *instantaneous* area $A_i$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{true} &= \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0 / (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})} \\
&= \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{eng})}$$

**Condition of validity:** Holds in the **plastic region beyond the proportional limit**, where volume constancy applies. Not valid for elastic deformation or for materials with significant void formation during fracture. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b): Step-by-step solution**

**Given:**
- Number of wires $n = 12$
- Wire diameter $d = 7$ mm
- $f_{ut} = 1860$ MPa
- $\gamma_m = 1.67$

**Step 1:** Area of one wire:

$$A_w = \frac{\pi}{4} (7)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 49 = 38.485 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Total tendon area:

$$A_t = n \cdot A_w = 12 \times 38.485 = 461.82 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Ultimate Tensile Capacity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_u &= f_{ut} \cdot A_t = 1860 \times 461.82 \\
&= 858{,}985 \, \text{N} \approx 859 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 4: Jacking Force (80 % of $f_{ut}$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_j &= 0.80 \cdot f_{ut} \cdot A_t = 0.80 \times 1860 \times 461.82 \\
&= 687{,}188 \, \text{N} \approx 687.2 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5: Maximum Permissible Service Force (FoS = 1.67 against $f_{ut}$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{service, max} &= \frac{f_{ut} \cdot A_t}{1.67} = \frac{859{,}000}{1.67} \\
&\approx 514{,}371 \, \text{N} \approx 514.4 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Conclusion:** Tendon can carry up to **859 kN** at ultimate, **687 kN** at jacking, and **514 kN** as maximum service load under IS 1343 partial safety factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Tensile Strength ($f_{ut}$)** = Peak engineering stress = $F_{max} / A_0$, NOT the fracture stress.
- [x] **Five characteristic points** on $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve: $f_p$, $f_e$, $f_y$, $f_{ut}$, $f_{fr}$ — remember *"P–E–Y–U–F"*.
- [x] **Hooke's Law** $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ is valid **only up to proportional limit**, not through yielding.
- [x] **True Stress = Engineering Stress $\cdot (1 + \varepsilon)$** under plastic constant-volume flow.
- [x] **Ductility indicators**: % elongation and % reduction in area — both measured at fracture.
- [x] **Design strength** $f_d = f_{ut} / \gamma_m$ — $\gamma_m = 1.25$ for steel (IS 800), $1.5$ for concrete (IS 456).
- [x] **Net section rupture** governs connection design: $T_{dn} = 0.9 f_u A_n / \gamma_{m1}$ (IS 800:2007).
- [x] **Factor of Safety** is **higher for brittle** materials (no warning before failure) and **lower for ductile** materials (yield gives warning).
- [x] **Necking** causes the apparent drop in engineering stress after $f_{ut}$, but **true stress** continues to rise.
- [x] **0.2 % offset method** is used to define $f_y$ for steels that do not exhibit a sharp yield point.
- [x] **Standard specimen** (IS 1601): $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ for proportional round bars — used in KTU labs.
- [x] **Jacking force** in PSC tendons = $0.80 \cdot f_{ut} \cdot A_t$ (typical for post-tensioning).
- [x] Always quote $f_{ut}$ with **units** (MPa or N/mm²) — losing units = losing 0.5–1 mark in KTU exams.
- [x] **Working Stress Method** uses FoS; **Limit State Method** uses partial safety factors — never mix the two in one problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip**: When asked "*Discuss the significance of tensile strength in safety of design*", structure your answer in three layers:
> 1. **Material level**: defines intrinsic failure limit of the material.
> 2. **Member level**: governs rupture capacity of net sections in connections.
> 3. **System level**: anchors ultimate limit-state design and collapse-prevention philosophy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforcing Steels for Concrete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reinforcing Steels for Concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Reinforcing steel for concrete** refers to the high-strength steel bars, wires, or mesh embedded within concrete to compensate for concrete's inherent weakness in tension. Concrete is strong in compression (typically **20–60 N/mm²**) but weak in tension (only about **10% of its compressive strength**). Reinforcing steel provides the tensile capacity required to resist flexural, shear, torsional, and axial tensile forces, while its compatible thermal expansion coefficient with concrete (approximately **10 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**) ensures monolithic composite action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:**
> As per the course PESFT754 (Safety in Design of Structures), understanding reinforcing steel is foundational because the *safety* of any RCC structure is intrinsically tied to the **strength, ductility, weldability, and bond characteristics** of the embedded steel. The IS 456:2000 and IS 13920:2016 design philosophies rely on the guaranteed minimum yield strength (\(f_y\)) and the stress–strain response of these steels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **chocolate-bar-and-straw composite**:
- The **chocolate** (concrete) is great when you push its ends together (compression) but snaps easily when you try to bend it (tension).
- The **straw** (steel) inside the chocolate is tough to break whether pulled or pushed.
- Together, they form a **reinforced chocolate bar** that resists both pushing and bending — a perfect miniature analogy of Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Steel?**
> 1. **High tensile strength** (415–600 N/mm² for standard grades).
> 2. **Compatible thermal expansion** with concrete (≈ 10 × 10⁻⁶ per °C).
> 3. **Good bond** with concrete due to chemical adhesion and surface deformations.
> 4. **Ductile failure mode** — gives visible warning before collapse.

## 1.3 Classification of Reinforcing Steels (High-Yield Summary)

| Category | Old Designation | New IS 1786 Designation | Characteristic Yield Strength (\(f_y\)) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (MS) | Grade I | **Fe 250** | **250 N/mm²** |
| Cold-worked HYSD | Grade II | **Fe 415 / Fe 500** | **415 / 500 N/mm²** |
| Quenched & Tempered (TMT) | Grade III | **Fe 500D / Fe 550D / Fe 600** | **500 / 550 / 600 N/mm²** |

> [!NOTE]
> The letter **'D'** stands for **Ductile**, indicating a minimum elongation of **16–18%** (vs. 12% for non-D grades), which is critical for **seismic safety** (IS 13920 compliance).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Properties of Reinforcing Steel

The behaviour of steel in design is governed by the following key properties:

1. **Yield Strength (\(f_y\))** — Stress at which the steel begins to deform plastically (no significant increase in stress with strain). This is the *design strength* used in limit state method.
2. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (\(f_u\))** — Maximum stress the steel can sustain.
3. **Percentage Elongation** — Ductility measure; ratio of increase in gauge length to original gauge length at fracture, expressed as a percentage.
4. **Modulus of Elasticity (\(E_s\))** — Slope of the elastic portion of the stress–strain curve.
   \[
   E_s = \frac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \frac{f}{\varepsilon}
   \]
5. **Strain at Ultimate (\(\varepsilon_{su}\))** — Critical for ductility-based design.
6. **Strain Hardening Modulus (\(E_{sh}\))** — Slope of the post-yield plateau in TMT bars.

## 2.2 Stress–Strain Behaviour of Different Steel Types

### A. Mild Steel (Fe 250)
- Exhibits a **distinct yield point** (sharp yield plateau).
- Elastic region followed by yielding, plastic flow, and strain hardening.
- Failure at a well-defined ultimate stress.
- Strain at fracture ≈ **20–25%**.

### B. Cold-Worked HYSD (Fe 415, Fe 500)
- No sharp yield point — yield is defined at **0.2% proof stress** (residual strain).
- Higher strength but **reduced ductility** compared to mild steel.
- Strain at fracture ≈ **10–14%**.

### C. Thermo-Mechanically Treated (TMT) Steel
- Manufactured by **quenching the hot-rolled bar** in water and then tempering it.
- Produces a **martensitic outer surface** (hard, strong) and a **ferritic-pearlitic core** (ductile).
- Gradual yielding (no sharp yield plateau, but a continuous curve).
- Strain at fracture ≈ **14–18%** (D-grade ≥ 16%).

## 2.3 Why Different Steels for Different Applications?

- **Fe 250** — Used in lightly loaded structures, unimportant members.
- **Fe 415** — Most common; balance of strength and ductility for general RCC.
- **Fe 500 / Fe 500D** — Preferred in modern construction; higher strength reduces congestion.
- **Fe 550 / Fe 600** — Used in heavy infrastructure, bridges, high-rise core walls.
- **Fe 500D / Fe 550D** — **Mandatory** in seismic zones III, IV, V as per IS 13920.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | \(\sigma = P / A\) | N/mm² |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | \(\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0\) | dimensionless (mm/mm) |
| 3 | Young's Modulus of Steel | \(E_s = \sigma / \varepsilon\) | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** |
| 4 | Design Yield Stress | \(f_{yd} = f_y / \gamma_m\) | N/mm² (with \(\gamma_m = 1.10\)) |
| 5 | Permissible Tensile Stress (WSD) | \(\sigma_{st} = 0.55 \, f_y\) | N/mm² |
| 6 | Stress in Steel at Service (Limit State) | \(f_s = 0.87 \, f_y\) | N/mm² |
| 7 | Elongation % | \(\% \text{El} = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100\) | % |
| 8 | Modular Ratio (Conceptual) | \(m = E_s / E_c = 280 / (3\sigma_{cbc})\) | dimensionless |
| 9 | Proof Stress (0.2% offset) | \(\sigma_{0.2}\) | N/mm² |
| 10 | Strain Hardening Slope | \(E_{sh}\) | ≈ 0.01 \(E_s\) for TMT |
| 11 | Tensile Strength Ratio | \(f_u / f_y\) | **≥ 1.10** (Fe 415), **≥ 1.08** (Fe 500/550) |
| 12 | Total Elongation Limit (Ductile) | \(\% \text{El} \geq 16\%\) | for Fe 500D |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The factor **0.87** in \(f_s = 0.87 \, f_y\) is the **partial safety factor for steel** (\(\gamma_m = 1/0.87 \approx 1.15\)) used in Limit State Design. It accounts for variability in material strength and is *always* tested in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge girders** — use **Fe 500D** for high ductility and fatigue resistance.
- **High-rise shear walls** — use **Fe 600** to reduce bar congestion.
- **Earthquake-prone regions** — *strictly* use **D-grade TMT bars** (Fe 500D/Fe 550D).
- **Marine/coastal structures** — use **CRS (Corrosion-Resistant Steel)** TMT bars for durability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Design Stress in Steel (Limit State Method)

The **limit state design** of RCC members uses the partial safety factor approach. The relationship between characteristic strength (\(f_y\)) and design strength (\(f_{yd}\)) is:

### Step 1: Define Characteristic Strength
The characteristic yield strength is the value of yield stress below which **not more than 5%** of the test results are expected to fall. It represents a 95% confidence level.

### Step 2: Apply Partial Safety Factor for Material
The partial safety factor for steel, \(\gamma_m\), accounts for:
- Variability in material properties
- Manufacturing defects
- Inconsistencies in testing

The design yield strength is:
\[
f_{yd} = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_m}
\]

### Step 3: Substitute the IS 456 Value
For reinforcing steel, IS 456:2000 specifies \(\gamma_m = 1.10\). Substituting:
\[
f_{yd} = \frac{f_y}{1.10} \approx 0.87 \, f_y
\]

This is the maximum stress allowed in steel under the **Limit State of Collapse** condition.

### Step 4: Conversion Logic Summary

\begin{aligned}
f_{yd} &= \frac{f_y}{\gamma_m} \\
&= \frac{f_y}{1.10} \\
&\approx 0.87 \, f_y
\end{aligned}

> The factor **0.87** is *universal* in Indian RCC design — it appears in flexure, shear, torsion, and axial design formulas of IS 456.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Percentage Elongation at Fracture

The standard test uses a gauge length \(L_0\) (typically \(5.65\sqrt{A}\), where \(A\) is the cross-sectional area). After fracture, the two pieces are rejoined, and the new gauge length \(L_f\) is measured.

### Step 1: Original Gauge Length
The standard gauge length is:
\[
L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A}
\]
This is the **proportional gauge length** ensuring consistent strain measurement across bar sizes.

### Step 2: Final Gauge Length
After fracture, the new length \(L_f\) is measured.

### Step 3: Elongation Formula
The percentage elongation is:
\[
\% \, \text{Elongation} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100
\]

### Step 4: Worked Example
For an 8 mm dia Fe 500D bar with \(A = \pi (4)^2 \approx 50.27 \text{ mm}^2\):

\begin{aligned}
L_0 &= 5.65 \sqrt{50.27} \\
&= 5.65 \times 7.09 \\
&\approx 40.07 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}

If the measured \(L_f = 47.6\) mm:

\begin{aligned}
\% \, \text{Elongation} &= \frac{47.6 - 40.07}{40.07} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7.53}{40.07} \times 100 \\
&\approx 18.79\%
\end{aligned}

This satisfies the **Fe 500D** requirement of **≥ 16%** elongation. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Young's Modulus from Stress–Strain Data

In a standard tensile test, a bar of diameter \(d\) and original length \(L_0\) is loaded axially. The load \(P\) and elongation \(\Delta L\) are recorded.

### Step 1: Compute Stress
\[
\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}
\]

### Step 2: Compute Strain
\[
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}
\]

### Step 3: Compute Young's Modulus
\[
E_s = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{P \cdot L_0}{A \cdot \Delta L}
\]

### Step 4: Example Numerical
Given: \(P = 50{,}000\) N, \(d = 16\) mm, \(L_0 = 200\) mm, \(\Delta L = 0.05\) mm.

\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (16)^2}{4} = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\sigma &= \frac{50000}{201.06} \approx 248.7 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
\varepsilon &= \frac{0.05}{200} = 0.00025 \\
E_s &= \frac{248.7}{0.00025} = 9.95 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}

This is close to the **standard value of 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** — small variations are normal due to bar inhomogeneity.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Steel Types

| Property | Fe 250 (MS) | Fe 415 (HYSD) | Fe 500 (HYSD) | Fe 500D (TMT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength \(f_y\) (N/mm²) | 250 | 415 | 500 | 500 |
| Ultimate Strength \(f_u\) (N/mm²) | 410 | 485 | 545 | 565 |
| % Elongation (min) | 20 | 14 | 12 | **16** |
| \(f_u / f_y\) Ratio | 1.64 | 1.17 | 1.09 | 1.13 |
| Yield Plateau | Sharp | Gradual | Gradual | **Rounded Continuous** |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good | Good | Excellent |
| Ductility Class | High Ductile | Limited | Limited | **High Ductile** |
| Seismic Suitability (IS 13920) | Acceptable | Not preferred | Not preferred | **Mandatory** |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| Application | Small structures | General RCC | Heavy construction | Seismic & High-rise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 0.2% Proof Stress?**
> HYSD and TMT bars do *not* show a sharp yield point. Yield is defined as the stress at which a permanent (plastic) strain of **0.2%** remains after unloading. This is called the **0.2% proof stress** and is the operational yield strength used in design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Steel Bar Properties Affecting Structural Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reinforcing Steel Bar] --> A[Mechanical Properties]
    R --> B[Geometric Properties]
    R --> C[Chemical Properties]
    R --> D[Surface Properties]

    A --> A1[Yield Strength fy]
    A --> A2[Ultimate Strength fu]

    A --> A4[Modulus of Elasticity Es]

    B --> B1[Bar Diameter d]
    B --> B2[Cross Section Area A]
    B --> B3[Length L]

    C --> C1[Carbon Content]
    C --> C2[Sulphur and Phosphorus Limits]
    C --> C3[Alloying Elements]

    D --> D1[Plain Surface]

    D --> D3[Bond Strength]

    A1 --> S1[Safety: Design Stress 0.87 fy]
    A3 --> S2[Safety: Ductility Warning Before Collapse]
    A2 --> S3[Safety: Reserve Strength Margin]
    A4 --> S4[Safety: Stiffness Compatibility with Concrete]

    B1 --> S5[Safety: Bar Spacing and Congestion Control]
    D2 --> S6[Safety: Bond and Anchorage Reliability]

    C1 --> S7[Safety: Weldability and Brittle Fracture Avoidance]

    S1 --> F[Overall Structural Safety in Design]
    S2 --> F
    S3 --> F
    S4 --> F
    S5 --> F
    S6 --> F
    S7 --> F
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree: Types of Reinforcing Steel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Reinforcing Steel for Concrete]

    S --> M[Mild Steel]
    S --> H[High Yield Strength Deformed HYSD]
    S --> T[TMT Bars]

    M --> M1[Fe 250]

    H --> H2[Fe 500]
    H1 --> H1A[Cold Twisted Deformed CTD]
    H1 --> H1B[0.2 percent Proof Stress]
    H2 --> H2A[Higher Strength Lower Ductility]

    T --> T1[Fe 500D]
    T --> T2[Fe 550D]
    T --> T3[Fe 600]
    T1 --> T1A[Quenched and Tempered]

```

flowchart LR

    B -->|Plastic Plateau| C[Strain Hardening]

    E[HYSD Fe 415] -->|Elastic 0.2 percent offset| F[Proof Stress at 415 N/mm2]
    F -->|Strain Hardening| G[Ultimate at 485 N/mm2]
    G -->|Fracture| H[10 to 14 percent strain]

    I[TMT Fe 500D] -->|Continuous Elastic-Plastic| J[Gradual Yield at 500 N/mm2]
