# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions**

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
**Define the term "characteristic yield strength" of reinforcing steel as per IS 1786. Why is this value used in design rather than the actual yield strength of a particular bar?**

**Model Answer:**
The **characteristic yield strength (\(f_y\))** of reinforcing steel is the value of the yield stress below which **not more than 5%** of the test results of a representative sample are expected to fall. In statistical terms, it is the **5% fractile value** of the yield strength distribution, ensuring a 95% confidence that actual material strength will be at least this value.

It is used in design (not the actual yield strength) because:
1. Material properties vary from batch to batch.
2. The design must guarantee safety for *all* bars, not just one.
3. It provides a uniform, code-compliant reference value for **all** design calculations.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, 3 Marks]**
**Differentiate between Mild Steel (Fe 250) and TMT (Fe 500D) bars based on (i) manufacturing process, (ii) stress-strain behaviour, and (iii) suitable applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Fe 250 (Mild Steel) | Fe 500D (TMT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Hot-rolled, no further treatment | Hot-rolled, then **quenched** in water and **tempered** |
| **Microstructure** | Uniform ferrite-pearlite | **Hard martensitic outer shell + ductile ferritic-pearlitic core** |
| **Yield Point** | **Sharp** yield plateau | **Gradual** rounded yield (no sharp point) |
| **Ductility** | **High** (≥ 20% elongation) | **Controlled** (≥ 16% elongation, D-grade) |
| **Application** | Small/lightly loaded members, ties | **Seismic structures, high-rise, heavy infrastructure** |

---

## **Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **manufacturing process of TMT bars**. With a neat stress-strain sketch, describe why TMT bars are preferred in seismic design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 16 mm diameter HYSD bar of grade Fe 500 is tested in tension. The yield load recorded is **98 kN** and the ultimate load is **118 kN**. The bar fractured at a gauge length of **127 mm**, original gauge length being **108 mm**. Verify whether the bar conforms to IS 1786 specifications for Fe 500.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — TMT Manufacturing and Seismic Suitability [7 Marks]

**1. Manufacturing Process of TMT Bars:**
TMT (Thermo-Mechanically Treated) bars are produced by a controlled 3-stage process:
1. **Hot Rolling:** Steel billets at 1100–1250 °C are passed through a sequence of rolling stands to achieve the required diameter and shape with ribs/deformations.
2. **Quenching (Rapid Cooling):** The hot-rolled bar immediately passes through a **water-spray cooling system** (withdrawal rate 30–80 °C/s). This produces a **martensitic (hard) outer surface layer**, while the **core remains austenitic** (soft, ductile).
3. **Self-Tempering:** Heat flows from the hot core back to the cooled surface, tempering the martensite to **tempered martensite** — giving an optimal combination of hardness and toughness.

**2. Microstructure:**
- **Outer surface** → tempered martensite (hard, strong, fatigue-resistant)
- **Core** → ferrite-pearlite (ductile, weldable)

**3. Stress-Strain Behaviour:**
- No sharp yield point — **continuous rounded yield curve**.
- Higher yield strength (≥ 500 N/mm²).
- High uniform elongation (≥ 16% for D-grade).
- Excellent **strain-hardening** capacity.

**4. Why Preferred in Seismic Design:**
- **High ductility** allows energy dissipation under cyclic loading.
- The **ductile core** prevents brittle fracture during earthquakes.
- IS 13920:2016 mandates **Fe 500D** or higher in Zones III, IV, and V.
- Provides **visible warning** (large deflections) before failure.

#### Part (b) — Verification of Fe 500 Conformance [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Diameter \(d = 16\) mm
- Yield load \(P_y = 98\) kN = 98,000 N
- Ultimate load \(P_u = 118\) kN = 118,000 N
- Original gauge length \(L_0 = 108\) mm
- Final gauge length \(L_f = 127\) mm

**Step 1: Cross-Sectional Area**

\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (16)^2}{4} \\
&= \frac{3.1416 \times 256}{4} = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}

**Step 2: Yield Strength (\(f_y\))**

\begin{aligned}
f_y &= \frac{P_y}{A} = \frac{98000}{201.06} \\
&= 487.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}

For Fe 500, IS 1786 requires **0.2% proof stress ≥ 500 N/mm²**. Here, \(f_y = 487.4\) N/mm² → **Below 500 N/mm²** ✗

**Step 3: Ultimate Tensile Strength (\(f_u\))**

\begin{aligned}
f_u &= \frac{P_u}{A} = \frac{118000}{201.06} \\
&= 586.9 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}

IS 1786 requires \(f_u \geq 1.08 \times 500 = 540\) N/mm² for Fe 500. Here, \(f_u = 586.9\) N/mm² → **Satisfies** ✓

**Step 4: Percentage Elongation**

\begin{aligned}
\% \text{El} &= \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{127 - 108}{108} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{19}{108} \times 100 \\
&= 17.59\%
\end{aligned}

IS 1786 requires **≥ 12%** for Fe 500 (and ≥ 16% for Fe 500D). Here, %El = 17.59% → **Satisfies Fe 500D** ✓

**Step 5: Conclusion**
| Property | Required for Fe 500 | Obtained | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength | ≥ 500 N/mm² | 487.4 N/mm² | **Fails** ✗ |
| Ultimate Strength | ≥ 540 N/mm² | 586.9 N/mm² | Passes ✓ |
| Elongation | ≥ 12% | 17.59% | Passes ✓ |

**Verdict: The bar does NOT conform to Fe 500** (yield strength is below 500 N/mm²). However, since elongation is high, it *may* conform to **Fe 415** specification.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **mechanical properties of reinforcing steel** that are critical for ensuring safety in RCC design. How do IS 456 and IS 13920 use these properties?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 12 mm dia Fe 415 bar of length **2.5 m** is subjected to a tensile load of **45 kN**. Calculate:
1. The stress in the bar.
2. The elongation, taking \(E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5\) N/mm².
3. Verify the bar's safety if the design strength is \(0.87 \, f_y\).

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Mechanical Properties for Safety [7 Marks]

The safety of RCC structures depends critically on the following properties of steel:

1. **Yield Strength (\(f_y\))** — Defines the *maximum permissible design stress* (\(0.87 f_y\)). IS 456 uses \(f_y\) for flexural, shear, axial, and torsion design. IS 13920 specifies **higher grades (Fe 500D, Fe 550D)** for seismic zones.

2. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (\(f_u\))** — Indicates the **reserve strength** beyond yield. The ratio \(f_u / f_y\) (≥ 1.08 for Fe 500) ensures **sufficient plastic deformation capacity** before fracture.

3. **Ductility (Elongation %)** — Provides **visible warning** (cracking, deflection) before failure. IS 13920 mandates **≥ 16% elongation** (D-grade bars) for seismic ductility.

4. **Modulus of Elasticity (\(E_s\))** — Used in serviceability (deflection, crack-width) calculations. Standard value: **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²**.

5. **Weldability** — Critical for prefabricated reinforcement cages. Carbon content < 0.30% ensures weldability.

6. **Bond Strength** — Surface ribs/deformations provide mechanical interlock with concrete. Higher bond → better anchorage → safer connections.

7. **Fatigue Resistance** — Important for bridges and dynamic loads.

**How IS Codes Use These:**
- **IS 456:2000** uses \(f_y\) for design (Limit State), and prescribes 0.87 \(f_y\) for stress in steel.
- **IS 13920:2016** (Ductile Detailing) enforces **Fe 500D minimum**, additional stirrups, anchorage length, and capacity design.

#### Part (b) — Numerical on Fe 415 Bar [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- \(d = 12\) mm
- \(L = 2.5\) m = 2500 mm
- \(P = 45\) kN = 45,000 N
- \(f_y = 415\) N/mm² (Fe 415)
- \(E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5\) N/mm²

**Step 1: Cross-Sectional Area**

\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi (12)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 144}{4} = 113.10 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}

**Step 2: Stress in the Bar**

\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{P}{A} = \frac{45000}{113.10} \\
&= 397.88 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}

**Step 3: Strain in the Bar**

\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{\sigma}{E_s} = \frac{397.88}{2.0 \times 10^5} \\
&= 1.9894 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4: Elongation**

\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= \varepsilon \times L_0 = 1.9894 \times 10^{-3} \times 2500 \\
&= 4.97 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5: Safety Verification**
Design strength of Fe 415:
\[
f_{yd} = 0.87 \times 415 = 361.05 \text{ N/mm}^2
\]

Actual stress = **397.88 N/mm²** > **361.05 N/mm²**

**Conclusion:** The bar is **UNSAFE** — the applied stress exceeds the design strength. The bar will yield before reaching the applied load of 45 kN.

**Maximum safe load:**
\[
P_{\text{safe}} = f_{yd} \times A = 361.05 \times 113.10 = 40{,}831 \text{ N} \approx 40.83 \text{ kN}
\]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing characteristic strength with design strength** — many students use \(f_y\) directly instead of \(0.87 f_y\) in design checks. This leads to **unsafe conclusions** and **2–3 mark deductions**.
> 2. **Mixing up WSD and LSD factors** — in Working Stress Design, \(\sigma_{st} = 0.55 f_y\); in Limit State Design, it is \(0.87 f_y\). Wrong factor = full marks lost.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — load in kN, area in mm², stress in N/mm². A 1-unit error can cascade and lose 5+ marks.
> 4. **In stress-strain diagrams, NOT labelling axes** — both axes must be marked with units (N/mm² on Y-axis, mm/mm on X-axis).
> 5. **Forgetting the role of partial safety factor (\(\gamma_m = 1.10\))** in deriving the 0.87 factor — examiners expect this derivation.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- [x] Reinforcing steel compensates for concrete's tensile weakness; chosen for **high strength, ductility, weldability, and bond**.
- [x] **Standard grades per IS 1786:** Fe 250, Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 500D, Fe 550, Fe 550D, Fe 600.
- [x] **Design stress in steel** (Limit State): \(\sigma_s = 0.87 f_y\), derived from \(\gamma_m = 1.10\).
- [x] **Permissible stress** (WSD): \(\sigma_{st} = 0.55 f_y\).
- [x] **Young's Modulus \(E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5\) N/mm²** (universal for all steel grades).
- [x] **HYSD and TMT bars** have **no sharp yield point** — yield is defined by **0.2% proof stress**.
- [x] **TMT bars** have a **martensitic surface + ferritic-pearlitic core** — best for seismic safety.
- [x] **D-grade bars** (Fe 500D, Fe 550D) require **≥ 16% elongation** — **mandatory in IS 13920**.
- [x] **Characteristic yield strength** = 5% fractile (95% confidence).
- [x] **Elongation formula:** \(\% \text{El} = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100\).
- [x] **Standard gauge length:** \(L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A}\).
- [x] **Tensile-to-yield ratio** \(f_u / f_y \geq 1.08\) (Fe 500), 1.10 (Fe 415).
- [x] **IS 456:2000** governs general RCC; **IS 13920:2016** governs ductile seismic detailing.
- [x] **TMT = Quenching + Self-Tempering** — unique manufacturing that gives high strength + high ductility.
- [x] **Safety hierarchy:** Yield > Reserve > Ductility > Warning before collapse.
- [x] **Always apply \(\gamma_m\) in design** — never use raw \(f_y\) for limit state checks.

topics.title: Flexural Design of R.C. Beams: Bending of Homogeneous Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flexural Design of R.C. Beams: Bending of Homogeneous Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flexural Design of Reinforced Cement Concrete (R.C.) Beams** is the systematic structural engineering process of proportioning the cross-section of a concrete beam, along with its embedded steel reinforcement, such that it safely resists **bending moments** and **shear forces** induced by applied loads, while satisfying the limit states of **safety** (strength) and **serviceability** (deflection, cracking) as codified by IS 456:2000 and SP-16 design charts.

The foundational prerequisite for flexural design of R.C. beams is the classical **Bending of Homogeneous Beams** (also known as the **Simple Bending Theory** or **Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory**). This theory analyzes a prismatic, isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic beam subjected to pure bending (constant bending moment with zero shear) and predicts the linear distribution of bending stresses across the depth of the cross-section.

The governing relationship of homogeneous beam bending is universally expressed as:

$$\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$

where **M** = Bending moment at the section (N·mm), **I** = Second moment of area about the Neutral Axis (mm⁴), **σ** = Bending (flexural) stress at a fibre located distance **y** from the Neutral Axis (N/mm² or MPa), **E** = Young's Modulus of Elasticity of the homogeneous material (N/mm²), and **R** = Radius of curvature of the deflected beam axis (mm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 establishes the *classical homogeneous beam theory* as the analytical foundation upon which the *transformed section concept* for R.C. beams (transforming steel into equivalent concrete) is later built. Mastery of the M/I = σ/y = E/R relationship is **non-negotiable** for Part A and Part B examination success.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Board Intuition

Imagine a **diving board** projecting over the edge of a swimming pool. When a diver stands at its free end, the board bends downward. Visualize slicing the board with a vertical cut at any section:

- The **top fibres** of the board are **stretched (in tension)** — they become longer than their original length.
- The **bottom fibres** are **squeezed (in compression)** — they become shorter than their original length.
- Somewhere between, there exists a layer of fibres whose length remains **unchanged** — this is the **Neutral Axis (N.A.)**.

The farther a fibre lies from the Neutral Axis, the greater its deformation. Because the board is made of one uniform material (wood = homogeneous), stress is directly proportional to strain via Hooke's Law (σ = Eε). Consequently, **stress also varies linearly with distance y from the Neutral Axis**: zero at the N.A. and maximum at the extreme outer fibres.

A bookshelf loaded with heavy books behaves identically — top fibres stretch, bottom fibres compress. The same equations describe both, because they are both "homogeneous beams under flexure."

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus (E) for structural steel**: **E_s = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa**
- **Young's Modulus (E) for concrete (IS 456, short-term)**: **E_c = 5000√(f_ck) N/mm²**
- **Modular Ratio (m)**: m = **E_s / E_c** (typically 8 to 13 depending on f_ck grade)
- **Standard unit of bending stress σ**: **N/mm² = MPa**
- **Standard unit of bending moment M**: **N·mm (SI), kN·m (practical)**
- **Standard unit of second moment of area I**: **mm⁴**

> [!NOTE]
> The word **Homogeneous** in the section title is critical: it implies a *single, uniform material* throughout the cross-section. R.C. beams are **heterogeneous** (concrete + steel), which is why in Module 2 the **Transformed Section Method** will convert the heterogeneous section into an equivalent homogeneous one using the modular ratio **m**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Simple Bending Theory

The classical bending equation is derived under the following **six explicit assumptions**:

1. **Homogeneous Material:** The beam material has identical elastic properties at every point in its volume. A single value of Young's Modulus **E** governs the entire cross-section.
2. **Isotropic Behaviour:** The material exhibits the same elastic properties in all directions at any given point.
3. **Linearly Elastic Material:** The material obeys Hooke's Law perfectly (σ = Eε) up to the proportional limit. No yielding occurs.
4. **Plane Sections Remain Plane (Hypothesis of Navier):** Cross-sections that are initially flat and perpendicular to the neutral axis of the beam *remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis* after bending. They do not warp.
5. **Stress is Uniaxial:** Fibres are assumed to experience only pure longitudinal (axial) stress — σ_x — caused by bending. Transverse stresses (σ_y, σ_z) and shear stresses (τ_xy) are neglected. This is valid only for **pure bending** (constant M, zero V).
6. **Self-equilibrium of Stresses:** The internal stress distribution over the cross-section must statically balance the externally applied bending moment M and axial force N (zero N for pure bending).

### 2.2 Why a Linear Strain Profile Exists (Geometric Logic)

Consider a short segment of the beam of original length **dx** subjected to bending:

- After deformation, fibres above the N.A. elongate; fibres below the N.A. shorten.
- Due to the **plane sections remain plane** assumption, the cross-section rotates by a small angle **dθ** about the Neutral Axis.
- A fibre at distance **y** from the N.A. undergoes a change in length of **y · dθ**.
- The longitudinal strain in that fibre is therefore:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\text{change in length}}{\text{original length}} = \dfrac{y \, d\theta}{dx}$$

Since the curvature κ = 1/R = dθ/dx, we obtain:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$$

This is a **purely geometric** result — it depends on kinematics alone, not on the material. Hence the strain profile is **linear in y** for *any* material, homogeneous or heterogeneous. This is the geometric foundation of beam theory.

### 2.3 Why Stress is Linear (Constitutive Logic)

Applying Hooke's Law (σ = Eε) to the geometric strain result:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R}$$

For a homogeneous beam, **E is constant** across the section, so **σ also varies linearly with y**. Maximum compressive stress occurs at the extreme top fibre (y = +y_max), and maximum tensile stress at the extreme bottom fibre (y = −y_max). Both have equal magnitude when the N.A. passes through the centroid.

### 2.4 Equivalence of the Three Ratios (M/I = σ/y = E/R)

The three ratios in the master equation are unified because they represent three simultaneous, consistent physical conditions at the same cross-section:

- **σ/y** = ratio of fibre stress to its distance from N.A. (force/geometry result).
- **E/R** = ratio of material stiffness to curvature (constitutive result).
- **M/I** = ratio of applied bending moment to the geometric second moment of area (equilibrium result).

All three must be equal at every point — this is the mathematical statement that geometry, material, and statics are simultaneously satisfied.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Standard Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---------|------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | σ/y = M/I | Bending stress at any fibre | N/mm² | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | σ/y = E/R | Curvature-stress relation | N/mm², mm | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | M/I = E/R | Moment-curvature relation | N·mm, mm⁴, N/mm² | ★★★★ |
| 4 | σ_max = M · y_max / I | Maximum bending stress | N/mm² | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | Z = I / y_max | Section Modulus | mm³ | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | σ_max = M / Z | Compact form of max stress | N/mm² | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | ε = y / R | Strain at distance y | dimensionless | ★★★★ |
| 8 | R = E·I / M | Radius of curvature | mm | ★★★★ |
| 9 | EI | Flexural Rigidity | N·mm² | ★★★★★ |
| 10 | 1/R = M / (EI) | Curvature κ | 1/mm | ★★★★ |
| 11 | M = σ_b · Z | Design equation (allowable stress) | N·mm | ★★★★★ |
| 12 | Z_required = M / σ_allowable | Sizing the beam | mm³ | ★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Utility:** The Section Modulus **Z** is the single most important geometric property in beam design. It encapsulates both the cross-sectional geometry (I, y_max) and appears directly in the design equation **M = σ · Z**. Doubling Z (e.g., by increasing depth) doubles the moment capacity without any change in material — this is why **beams are deepened, not widened**, to resist bending.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Steel Girders:** Z is used directly to size I-sections (ISMB, ISMC) per IS 800:2007 (working stress method) and IS 800:2007 LSM.
- **Machine Shafts:** Diameter is selected using σ_max = 32M/(πd³) derived from M/I = σ/y for a solid circular section.
- **Timber Joists, Railway Sleepers:** Section modulus tables (Z-tables) are used by practising civil engineers for quick sizing.
- **Mechanical Springs (Leaf Springs):** The curvature R changes continuously as load varies, and E·I determines the spring stiffness k.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Bending Equation σ/y = M/I = E/R

**Setup:** Consider a small element of a beam of length **dx**, originally straight and prismatic. The cross-section is symmetric about a vertical plane (the plane of loading). Let the **Neutral Axis (N.A.)** lie within the cross-section at the centroid C. Let **y** be the vertical coordinate measured from the N.A. (positive upward).

**Step 1 — Consider a fibre at distance y from the N.A.**

The fibre originally has length dx. After bending, the cross-section rotates by a small angle dθ about the N.A. The deformed length of the fibre becomes (R − y)·dθ, where R is the radius of curvature of the Neutral Axis.

> **Conversion Logic:** A fibre below the N.A. (y negative) lies at a shorter distance (R − y > R when y < 0), so it lies on the *inner* arc of curvature and shortens; a fibre above the N.A. (y positive) lies on the *outer* arc and lengthens.

**Step 2 — Compute the change in length.**

Original length of fibre = dx = R·dθ (since the N.A. has length R·dθ).

Deformed length of fibre = (R − y)·dθ.

Change in length (elongation) = Deformed − Original:

$$\delta L = (R - y)d\theta - R \, d\theta = -y \, d\theta$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The negative sign is consistent with the sign convention: positive y (above N.A.) gives negative elongation (shortening? No — wait — correction below).

**Step 2 (Corrected Sign Convention)**

If positive y is taken **upward** and the beam sags downward (concave-up bending of the segment when looking at the deformed shape between two sagging moments), then fibres *above* the N.A. **shorten** (compression) and fibres *below* the N.A. **lengthen** (tension). Using a consistent convention where y is positive *downward* (standard structural convention):

$$\delta L = y \, d\theta$$

The fibre at distance y below N.A. elongates by y·dθ.

**Step 3 — Compute the longitudinal strain.**

By definition:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\delta L}{\text{original length}} = \dfrac{y \, d\theta}{R \, d\theta} = \dfrac{y}{R}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Both numerator and denominator contain the same infinitesimal dθ, which cancels. The result depends only on y and R.

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}} \quad \text{...(Geometric / Kinematic Result)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law (Constitutive Relation).**

For a linearly elastic homogeneous material:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** For a homogeneous beam, E is constant; hence σ is linear in y.

$$\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{E \, y}{R}} \quad \text{...(Constitutive Result, linear in y)} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Static Equilibrium of the Cross-Section.**

The internal stresses σ over the cross-section must produce a couple equal and opposite to the externally applied bending moment M.

(a) **Axial Force Equilibrium (Pure Bending ⇒ ΣF_x = 0):**

$$\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0$$

Substituting σ = E·y/R:

$$\dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0 \implies \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

This proves that the **Neutral Axis must pass through the Centroid of the Cross-Section** (first moment of area about N.A. is zero by definition of centroid). This is a critical derived conclusion.

(b) **Moment Equilibrium (ΣM = M):**

The moment of the stress σ about the N.A. equals M:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$

Substituting σ = E·y/R:

$$M = \int_A \dfrac{E \, y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

By definition, $\int_A y^2 dA = I$, the **second moment of area** about the N.A.

$$M = \dfrac{E \, I}{R}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The integral $\int_A y^2 dA$ defines the second moment of area I — the geometric property of the cross-section.

$$\boxed{M = \dfrac{E \, I}{R}} \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the Three Relations.**

From Equation 1: $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$

From Equation 2: $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$

Equating the right-hand sides:

$$\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$

This is the **Unified Bending Equation**, valid for any homogeneous, linearly elastic beam under pure bending:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Bending Stress and Section Modulus

The maximum bending stress occurs at the fibre farthest from the Neutral Axis. If y_top and y_bot are the distances from N.A. to the extreme top and bottom fibres respectively, then:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I}$$

Defining the **Section Modulus**:

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Z is purely a geometric property (mm³). The design equation M = σ_allowable · Z shows that for a given allowable stress and applied moment, the required Z is known.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A simply supported beam of span **L = 4 m** carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **w = 20 kN/m** over its entire span. The beam has a rectangular cross-section of **breadth b = 200 mm** and **depth d = 400 mm**. Determine the **maximum bending stress** developed in the beam. Take E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² for steel.

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment:**

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{20 \times 4^2}{8} = 40 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{max} = 40 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus of Rectangle:**

$$I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12} = \dfrac{200 \times 400^3}{12} = 1.0667 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$y_{max} = \dfrac{d}{2} = 200 \text{ mm}$$

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{1.0667 \times 10^9}{200} = 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Z = I/y_max captures all the geometry; the unit mm⁴ / mm = mm³.

**Step 3 — Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6} = 7.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (MPa)}$$

**Step 4 — Radius of Curvature at the Section of Maximum Moment:**

$$R = \dfrac{E \, I}{M} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5 \times 1.0667 \times 10^9}{40 \times 10^6} = 5.33 \times 10^6 \text{ mm} = 5.33 \text{ km}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The very large radius (kilometres!) confirms the engineer’s intuition that beam deflections are tiny compared to the span.

**Step 5 — Curvature κ:**

$$\kappa = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^6}{2.0 \times 10^5 \times 1.0667 \times 10^9} = 1.875 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Bending Derivation

The following flow chart maps the logical sequence in which the three pillars of beam theory — **Geometry**, **Constitutive Law**, and **Equilibrium** — combine to produce the master equation M/I = σ/y = E/R.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Beam of length L, cross-section A, subjected to bending moment M] --> B[Step 1: Apply Plane Sections Remain Plane assumption]
    B --> C[Fibre at distance y from N.A. changes length by y . dtheta]
    C --> D[Strain eps = y divided by R]
    D --> E[Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law sigma = E . eps]
    E --> F[Stress sigma = E . y divided by R]
    F --> G[Step 3: Apply Force Equilibrium]
    G --> H[Integral of sigma dA over area = 0]
    H --> I[Result: N.A. passes through Centroid of section]
    I --> J[Step 4: Apply Moment Equilibrium]
    J --> K[M = Integral of sigma . y dA over area]
    K --> L[M = E . I divided by R]
    L --> M[Combine all three results]
    M --> N[Master Equation: M over I = sigma over y = E over R]
    N --> O[Step 5: Define Section Modulus Z = I divided by y_max]
    O --> P[Final Design Equation: sigma_max = M divided by Z]
    P --> Q[End: Beam can be sized against allowable stress]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Stress and Strain Distribution in a Homogeneous Beam Under Pure Bending

The following diagram shows the **linear strain distribution** and the **linear stress distribution** across the depth of a rectangular homogeneous beam in pure bending. Compression acts on the top fibre, tension on the bottom fibre, with zero stress/strain at the Neutral Axis (centroid).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph crosssection["Beam Cross-Section (depth d, width b)"]
        T["Top extreme fibre y = plus d by 2"] -->|Compression sigma_c| NA["Neutral Axis y = 0 sigma = 0"]
        NA -->|Tension sigma_t| B["Bottom extreme fibre y = minus d by 2"]
    end

    subgraph stressdiagram["Linear Stress Diagram (Triangular)"]
        S1["Top: sigma_c max compressive"] --> S2["NA: sigma = 0"]
        S2 --> S3["Bottom: sigma_t max tensile"]
    end

    subgraph straindiagram["Linear Strain Diagram (Triangular)"]
        E1["Top: eps_c max compressive"] --> E2["NA: eps = 0"]
        E2 --> E3["Bottom: eps_t max tensile"]
    end

    crosssection --> stressdiagram
    crosssection --> straindiagram
```

### 4.3 Logical Mapping of the Three Ratios to the Three Pillars of Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph pillar1["Geometry Kinematics"]
        P1["Strain eps = y by R"]
    end
    subgraph pillar2["Constitutive Material Law"]
        P2["Stress sigma = E . eps"]
    end
    subgraph pillar3["Equilibrium Statics"]
        P3["Moment M = Integral of sigma . y dA = E . I divided by R"]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> master["Unified: M over I = sigma over y = E over R"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** In an answer sheet sketch, always show the stress diagram as a **triangle** (zero at N.A., linear at extremes). Annotate the compression zone clearly above the N.A. and the tension zone below. Examiners award easy marks for a clean, labelled figure even in purely analytical answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

State the **assumptions** made in the theory of simple bending of homogeneous beams.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The theory of simple bending is based on the following six assumptions:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous** — uniform elastic properties throughout its volume.
2. The beam material is **isotropic** — same elastic properties in all directions.
3. The material is **linearly elastic** and obeys Hooke's Law up to the proportional limit.
4. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (Navier's hypothesis).
5. The beam is initially **straight** and prismatic.
6. **Self-equilibrium** of stresses exists — the resultant stress system over any cross-section reduces to a couple equal to the applied bending moment.

(Any 6 valid points ⇒ 3 marks; 4 points ⇒ 2 marks; less than 4 ⇒ 0–1 mark.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Define **Section Modulus (Z)** of a beam cross-section and write its significance in flexural design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Section Modulus of a beam cross-section, with respect to the Neutral Axis, is defined as the ratio of the second moment of area (I) about the Neutral Axis to the distance (y_max) of the extreme fibre from the Neutral Axis:

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}} \quad \text{(units: mm}^3\text{)}$$

**Significance in Flexural Design (1.5 marks):**

- The maximum bending stress is given by $\sigma_{max} = M/Z$.
- For a given allowable stress σ_allow and applied moment M, the **required section modulus** is: $Z_{required} = M / \sigma_{allow}$.
- This permits the engineer to *directly select* a standard rolled section (I-beam, channel) from a steel section handbook by reading off its Z value, without separately computing I.
- **Doubling the depth d of a rectangular section increases Z by 4 times** (Z ∝ d²), so deepening is far more efficient than widening (Z ∝ b).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** A cast iron beam of rectangular cross-section **120 mm wide × 240 mm deep** is simply supported over a span of **6 m** and carries a point load of **30 kN** at the centre. The permissible stresses in tension and compression are **σ_t = 30 MPa** and **σ_c = 60 MPa** respectively. Using bending theory of homogeneous beams:

**(i)** Determine the **maximum bending moment** and the **position of the Neutral Axis (N.A.)**.

**(ii)** Calculate the **maximum tensile and compressive stresses** in the beam and verify whether the beam is safe under the given permissible limits.

**(b)** For the same beam cross-section, determine the **radius of curvature R** at the section of maximum moment. Take **E = 1.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** for cast iron.

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a)(i) — Maximum Bending Moment**

The maximum bending moment for a simply supported beam with a central point load:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4} = \dfrac{30 \times 6}{4} = 45 \text{ kN·m} = 45 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Part (a)(i) — Position of Neutral Axis**

For an *unsymmetrical permissible stress* beam (cast iron), the N.A. does NOT pass through the centroid. Let y₁ = distance from N.A. to top extreme fibre (compression side), and y₂ = distance from N.A. to bottom extreme fibre (tension side).

Given: b = 120 mm, total depth d = 240 mm, so y₁ + y₂ = 240 mm.

The bending stress varies linearly: $\sigma = M \cdot y / I$.

Maximum compressive stress (at top, y = y₁):

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{M \cdot y_1}{I}$$

Maximum tensile stress (at bottom, y = y₂):

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{M \cdot y_2}{I}$$

Dividing:

$$\dfrac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_t} = \dfrac{y_1}{y_2} = \dfrac{60}{30} = 2$$

So y₁ = 2·y₂. Combined with y₁ + y₂ = 240:

$$2y_2 + y_2 = 240 \implies y_2 = 80 \text{ mm}, \quad y_1 = 160 \text{ mm}$$

**N.A. is located 80 mm above the bottom extreme fibre (or 160 mm below the top fibre).**

**Part (a)(ii) — Maximum Stresses**

Compute the second moment of area about the N.A. (note: N.A. is not the centroid, so the parallel-axis theorem is needed):

$$I_{NA} = \dfrac{b \cdot y_1^3}{3} + \dfrac{b \cdot y_2^3}{3} = \dfrac{b}{3}(y_1^3 + y_2^3)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The standard formula for I of a rectangle about an axis at its base is bd³/3. Here we split the depth into two rectangles y₁ (above N.A.) and y₂ (below N.A.) — the moment of inertia about the N.A. is the sum of the individual I values about the N.A. as base.

$$I_{NA} = \dfrac{120}{3}(160^3 + 80^3) = 40 \times (4.096 \times 10^6 + 0.512 \times 10^6) = 40 \times 4.608 \times 10^6$$

$$I_{NA} = 1.8432 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

Maximum compressive stress:

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{M \cdot y_1}{I_{NA}} = \dfrac{45 \times 10^6 \times 160}{1.8432 \times 10^8} = 39.06 \text{ MPa}$$

Maximum tensile stress:

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{M \cdot y_2}{I_{NA}} = \dfrac{45 \times 10^6 \times 80}{1.8432 \times 10^8} = 19.53 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification:**

- σ_c = 39.06 MPa < σ_c,permissible = 60 MPa → **Safe in compression** ✓
- σ_t = 19.53 MPa < σ_t,permissible = 30 MPa → **Safe in tension** ✓

**The beam is SAFE under both permissible stress limits.**

**Part (b) — Radius of Curvature**

From M/I = E/R:

$$R = \dfrac{E \cdot I_{NA}}{M} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5 \times 1.8432 \times 10^8}{45 \times 10^6}$$

$$R = \dfrac{1.8432 \times 10^{13}}{4.5 \times 10^7} = 4.096 \times 10^5 \text{ mm} = 409.6 \text{ m}$$

**R ≈ 410 m.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive the **bending equation M/I = σ/y = E/R** for a homogeneous beam under pure bending, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported timber beam of span **3.5 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **8 kN/m** (inclusive of self-weight). The cross-section is rectangular, **150 mm wide × 250 mm deep**. The permissible bending stress in timber is **σ_allow = 12 N/mm²**. Take **E = 1.2 × 10⁴ N/mm²** for timber.

**(i)** Compute the **maximum bending moment** and the **required section modulus** Z_required.

**(ii)** Verify whether the given cross-section is **adequate**. If not, suggest a **revised depth** keeping width constant. Also compute the **maximum deflection** of the beam. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**

1. Homogeneous and isotropic material.
2. Linearly elastic — Hooke's Law valid.
3. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the Neutral Axis (Navier's hypothesis).
4. Self-equilibrating stresses; pure bending (constant M, V = 0).
5. Beam initially straight and prismatic.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

*Step 1 — Geometric strain:*

Consider a fibre at distance y from N.A. Original length = dx. After bending, length changes by y·dθ (where dθ is the rotation of the cross-section about N.A.).

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{y \, d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{y}{R}$$

*Step 2 — Constitutive law (Hooke):*

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \dfrac{E \, y}{R}$$

*Step 3 — Axial equilibrium gives N.A. through centroid:*

$$\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0 \implies \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

*Step 4 — Moment equilibrium:*

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \dfrac{E \, I}{R}$$

*Step 5 — Combining:*

$$\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R} \quad \blacksquare$$

(Full mathematical steps of derivation as detailed in **Section 3.1** above carry 5 marks.)

**Part (b)(i) — Maximum Moment and Required Z**

Maximum moment for simply supported beam under UDL:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{8 \times 3.5^2}{8} = 12.25 \text{ kN·m} = 12.25 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

Required Section Modulus:

$$Z_{required} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{12.25 \times 10^6}{12} = 1.0208 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Verification and Revised Depth**

Provided Section Modulus of rectangular cross-section:

$$Z_{provided} = \dfrac{bd^2}{6} = \dfrac{150 \times 250^2}{6} = 1.5625 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

Since Z_provided (1.5625 × 10⁶) > Z_required (1.0208 × 10⁶), **the cross-section is ADEQUATE** ✓.

**Maximum Deflection:**

For simply supported beam under UDL:

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \, w L^4}{384 \, EI}$$

where I = bd³/12 = 150 × 250³/12 = 1.953 × 10⁸ mm⁴. L = 3500 mm, w = 8 N/mm.

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \times 8 \times 3500^4}{384 \times 1.2 \times 10^4 \times 1.953 \times 10^8}$$

Numerator: 5 × 8 × 1.5006 × 10¹⁴ = 6.0025 × 10¹⁵

Denominator: 384 × 1.2 × 10⁴ × 1.953 × 10⁸ = 9.0006 × 10¹⁴

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{6.0025 \times 10^{15}}{9.0006 \times 10^{14}} \approx 6.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Check serviceability (IS 456 limit):** For spans without finishes, δ_allowable ≈ L/350 = 3500/350 = **10 mm**. Since 6.67 mm < 10 mm, the beam **satisfies the serviceability deflection limit** ✓.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Bending-of-Homogeneous-Beams Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Mixing kN·m and N·mm in the same calculation. Always convert to **N·mm** before plugging into $\sigma = M/Z$ because Z is in mm³ and σ comes out in N/mm² (MPa).
> 2. **N.A. Assumption Error:** Students often assume the N.A. lies at the centroid *automatically*. It does **only when the beam material is symmetric in tension and compression** (e.g., steel, concrete — but concrete is poor in tension). For cast iron (unsymmetrical permissible stresses), the N.A. **shifts away from the centroid toward the stronger (compression) side**.
> 3. **Sign-Convention Confusion:** Forgetting that the N.A. must lie where strain = 0, not where moment = 0. Some students confuse N.A. with the point of zero shear force — they are entirely different concepts.
> 4. **I vs Z Confusion:** Mixing up the second moment of area **I** (mm⁴) and the section modulus **Z** (mm³). Use I when you want stress at an *arbitrary* fibre (σ = My/I); use Z only for the *extreme* fibre (σ = M/Z).
> 5. **Plane Sections Assumption Violation:** Applying σ = M·y/I to a beam near a concentrated load (where the plane-sections assumption fails due to local stress concentration) — the equation is valid only for sections where **pure bending** prevails (V ≈ 0).
> 6. **Forgetting to compute y_max separately for each fibre:** When y_top ≠ y_bot (e.g., unsymmetric section like a T-beam, or unsymmetrical permissible stresses), always use the respective y for that fibre's stress calculation.
> 7. **Parallel-Axis Theorem Missed:** When the N.A. is *not* at the centroid (unsymmetrical permissible stress case), you cannot use I_centroid directly. Compute I about the actual N.A. using the additivity of rectangles or the parallel-axis theorem.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation:** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ — the single most tested formula in Module 1.
- **Three Pillars:** Geometry (ε = y/R), Constitutive (σ = Eε), Equilibrium (M = ∫σ·y dA = EI/R). All three must hold simultaneously.
- **N.A. Position:** Always passes through the **centroid** for a homogeneous beam with *symmetric* permissible stresses (steel, aluminium, timber in WSM); **shifts toward the stronger side** for unsymmetric cases (cast iron).
- **Section Modulus Z = I/y_max** encapsulates geometry; appears in **M = σ·Z**, the working-stress design equation.
- **Flexural Rigidity EI** governs curvature: κ = 1/R = M/(EI). Doubling E or I halves the curvature for the same moment.
- **Doubling depth d increases Z four-fold** for a rectangular section (since Z ∝ bd²/6). Hence deepening the section is the most efficient way to increase moment capacity without changing material.
- **Maximum stress σ_max = M/Z** governs strength; **maximum deflection** governs serviceability — both must be checked in design.
- **Pure Bending Limit:** All derivations assume V = 0; near supports and concentrated loads, transverse shear stresses exist and plane-sections assumption breaks down.
- **Heterogeneous Beam Link:** For R.C. beams, the transformed section concept (next module) replaces E·I with the equivalent homogeneous-section EI using modular ratio m = E_s/E_c.
- **Units Discipline:** M in N·mm, I in mm⁴, σ in N/mm², E in N/mm², y in mm, R in mm — consistency prevents arithmetic disaster.
- **IS Code Linkage:** IS 456:2000 governs R.C. flexural design; IS 800:2007 governs steel beams — both are founded on this same homogeneous-beam theory.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforced Concrete Beam Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reinforced Concrete Beam Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** and **IS 456:2000 / ACI 318-19** standards, the behaviour of a **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) flexural member** under transverse loading is defined as the *coupled mechanical response of two fundamentally different materials — concrete (brittle, strong in compression) and steel (ductile, strong in tension) — acting monolithically as a composite section to resist bending moments and shear forces.*

The structural safety of an RC beam is governed by a tri-phasic response:
- **Uncracked (Elastic) Phase** — Both materials remain below their proportional limits.
- **Cracked (Service) Phase** — Concrete in tension zone fractures; steel carries tensile force.
- **Ultimate (Failure) Phase** — Either steel yields (ductile) or concrete crushes (brittle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 focus is to understand *why* an under-reinforced section is mandated in **Limit State Design (LSD)**. The behaviour of RC beams is the very foundation on which the partial safety factor philosophy (γ_m for material, γ_f for load) is built.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tug-of-War With Two Different People" Model

Imagine two friends tied back-to-back playing **tug-of-war** against a heavy load:
- **Friend A (Concrete)** — Strong but gets injured (cracks) if pulled too hard. Great at *pushing* against the load.
- **Friend B (Steel Reinforcement)** — Flexible, can stretch significantly without breaking. Great at *pulling* against the load.

The rope (load) tries to bend the team forward. Friend A pushes back from the front (compression), Friend B pulls from the back (tension). They only succeed as a team if **Friend B is given just enough strength** so that when Friend A finally gives out (crushes), Friend B has already stretched enough to warn us (ductile failure with visible deflection).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** A simple lintel above a door. When you place a load (brickwork) on it, the bottom fibres try to stretch (tension) and the top fibres get squeezed (compression). Plain concrete alone would crack at the bottom — but the steel bar embedded there stretches with the concrete and carries the tension. This *composite action* is the essence of RC behaviour.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete | $E_c$ | $5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ **MPa** | IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.1 |
| Modulus of Elasticity of Steel | $E_s$ | **200,000 MPa (2 × 10⁵ MPa)** | IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.2 |
| Modular Ratio | $m$ | $E_s / E_c$ (≈ **13** for M20) | IS 456 Cl. B-1.4 |
| Characteristic Strength of Concrete | $f_{ck}$ | M20, M25, M30... **MPa** | IS 456 Cl. 2.2 |
| Characteristic Strength of Steel | $f_y$ | Fe415 = **415 MPa**, Fe500 = **500 MPa**, Fe550 = **550 MPa** | IS 456 Cl. 2.2 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Stages of Flexural Behaviour (KTU High-Yield Topic)

#### **Stage I — Uncracked Elastic Stage**
- Entire cross-section is effective; both concrete and steel participate in resisting tension.
- Strain distribution is **linear** (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis of plane sections remaining plane).
- Valid for: $M < M_{cr}$ where $M_{cr}$ is the **cracking moment**.
- Stress in concrete tensile fibre: $f_t = \dfrac{M \cdot y_t}{I_g}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this stage matter for safety design?** This stage governs **deflection at service loads** and is the basis for *uncracked section* checks in working stress design.

#### **Stage II — Cracked Elastic Stage**
- Concrete in tension zone has cracked; tensile strength of concrete is neglected.
- Only the **transformed/composite section** (steel area × modular ratio *m* added to compression concrete area) resists moment.
- Strain distribution remains **linear**, but the **neutral axis shifts upward** (since tension area is reduced).

$$
I_{cr} = \dfrac{b \cdot x^3}{3} + m \cdot A_{st} \cdot (d - x)^2
$$

#### **Stage III — Ultimate / Failure Stage**
- Steel reaches yield strain $\varepsilon_y$ or concrete reaches ultimate strain $\varepsilon_{cu}$ (≈ **0.0035** as per IS 456).
- **Non-linear** material behaviour.
- This is the stage used in **Limit State of Collapse (Flexure)** design.

### 2.2 Under-Reinforced vs Over-Reinforced — The Heart of Safety Philosophy

| Parameter | Under-Reinforced Section | Over-Reinforced Section |
|---|---|---|
| **Steel Content** | $A_{st} < A_{st,balanced}$ | $A_{st} > A_{st,balanced}$ |
| **Failure Mode** | Steel yields first (tensile failure) | Concrete crushes first (compression failure) |
| **Failure Nature** | **Ductile** (warning via large deflection & cracking) | **Brittle** (sudden, catastrophic, no warning) |
| **Preferred in Design?** | ✅ **YES — Mandated by IS 456** | ❌ **NO — Code prohibits** |
| **Analogy** | Rope snaps slowly, giving time to react | Glass rod snaps with a loud crack |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** A 14-mark question is almost always asked on *"Why is an under-reinforced section preferred over a balanced section? Explain with reference to IS 456 philosophy."*

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the *first column* — these equations are worth 60% of marks in any RC beam problem.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I_g}$ | Bending stress in concrete (uncracked) | MPa |
| 2 | $M_{cr} = \dfrac{f_{cr} \cdot I_g}{y_t}$ | Cracking moment of section | kN·m |
| 3 | $\dfrac{b \cdot x^2}{2} = m \cdot A_{st} \cdot (d - x)$ | NA depth *x* (cracked elastic stage) | mm |
| 4 | $I_{cr} = \dfrac{b \cdot x^3}{3} + m \cdot A_{st} \cdot (d - x)^2$ | Cracked moment of inertia | mm⁴ |
| 5 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \cdot \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d} \cdot \left(1 - 0.42 \cdot \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d}\right) \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2$ | Limiting moment of resistance (IS 456) | N·mm |
| 6 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2$ (for Fe415) | Simplified form for Fe415, $\sigma_{st} = 0.87f_y$ | N·mm |
| 7 | $A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85}{f_y} \cdot b \cdot d$ | Minimum tension steel (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1) | mm² |
| 8 | $\dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d} = 0.48$ for Fe415; **0.46** for Fe500 | Limiting NA depth ratio (IS 456 Annex G) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$ | Ultimate crushing strain of concrete (IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.3) | — |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{f_y}{E_s} = \dfrac{415}{2 \times 10^5} \approx 0.00207$ | Yield strain of Fe415 steel | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Tip for Students:** Notice that no vertical bar `\|` was used inside the table — absolute values and conditional symbols were rendered using $\vert$ and $\le$ to preserve markdown compatibility.

### 2.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Practice

Understanding RC beam behaviour is the *bedrock* of:
- **Earthquake-resistant design** — Ductility (from under-reinforced behaviour) is the sole source of energy dissipation in RC frames during seismic events.
- **Bridge girder design** — Over-reinforced girders have caused collapses like the *Morandi Bridge, Genoa (2018)*.
- **Deflection control** — Cracked moment of inertia (Stage II) is used in serviceability checks per **IS 456 Cl. 23.2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Case Study:** The 1995 *Hyundai Department Store collapse in Seoul* was partly attributed to brittle failure of over-reinforced transfer beams. This single event reshaped the Korean design code to mandate ductile detailing — a direct lesson from RC beam behaviour theory.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Neutral Axis Depth in Cracked Elastic Section (Working Stress Method)

**Starting Premise:** At any horizontal cross-section, the internal moment $M$ is resisted by a couple formed by the compressive force $C$ in concrete and the tensile force $T$ in steel.

**Step 1 — Convert steel into equivalent concrete using modular ratio**

$$
A_{st,\text{equiv}} = m \cdot A_{st}
$$

This is the *transformation principle* — the steel area is increased by factor $m$ so that strain compatibility is preserved (both concrete-equivalent and steel experience the same strain at a given depth).

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium of internal forces**

For the cracked section, taking moments of the compression and tension areas about the neutral axis:

$$
\text{Compression force, } C = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \sigma_c \cdot b \cdot x
$$

$$
\text{Tension force, } T = \sigma_{st} \cdot A_{st}
$$

For equilibrium: $C = T$

**Step 3 — Equate strains using modular ratio**

Since the strains are linear and proportional to distance from NA:

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_c}{x} = \dfrac{\sigma_{st}}{(d - x)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{m}
$$

This gives:

$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{st}}{m} \cdot \dfrac{x}{(d - x)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into equilibrium equation**

$$
\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{st}}{m} \cdot \dfrac{x}{(d - x)} \cdot b \cdot x = \sigma_{st} \cdot A_{st}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\sigma_{st}$ from both sides**

$$
\dfrac{b \cdot x^2}{2 \cdot m \cdot (d - x)} = A_{st}
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-multiply to obtain the standard quadratic form**

$$
b \cdot x^2 = 2 \cdot m \cdot A_{st} \cdot (d - x)
$$

$$
\boxed{b \cdot x^2 + 2 \cdot m \cdot A_{st} \cdot x - 2 \cdot m \cdot A_{st} \cdot d = 0}
$$

This is the **fundamental quadratic equation** for the depth of neutral axis in a singly-reinforced rectangular beam under WSM.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Moment of Resistance of an Under-Reinforced Section (LSM)

**Given:** Singly-reinforced rectangular beam, width $b$, effective depth $d$, area of tension steel $A_{st}$, concrete grade $f_{ck}$, steel grade $f_y$.

**Step 1 — Define design strengths with partial safety factors**

IS 456 mandates:

$$
f_d = 0.45 \cdot f_{ck} \quad \text{(design compressive stress of concrete)}
$$

$$
f_{yd} = 0.87 \cdot f_y \quad \text{(design yield stress of steel)}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate compressive and tensile forces at ultimate state**

Using the rectangular stress block (IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3):

$$
C_u = 0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_u
$$

$$
T_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st}
$$

Equating $C_u = T_u$:

$$
0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $x_u$ (depth of NA at ultimate state)**

$$
x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st}}{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the moment equilibrium about the compression face**

The lever arm is $z = d - 0.42 \cdot x_u$ (IS 456 rectangular stress block simplification):

$$
M_u = T_u \cdot z = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st} \cdot \left(d - 0.42 \cdot x_u\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $x_u$ to get $M_u$ in terms of reinforcement ratio**

After substituting and simplifying:

$$
M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st} \cdot d \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{A_{st} \cdot f_y}{b \cdot d \cdot f_{ck}}\right]
$$

This is the **general moment of resistance equation** for a singly-reinforced rectangular section per IS 456.

**Step 6 — Derive the limiting moment of resistance**

For Fe415, $x_{u,max} = 0.48 \cdot d$:

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \cdot \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d} \cdot \left(1 - 0.42 \cdot \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d}\right) \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2
$$

Substituting $\dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d} = 0.48$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.36 \cdot 0.48 \cdot (1 - 0.42 \cdot 0.48) \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 \\
&= 0.1728 \cdot (1 - 0.2016) \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 \\
&= 0.1728 \cdot 0.7984 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 \\
&= 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 \quad \text{(for Fe415, IS 456)}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** An RC beam, 250 mm wide × 500 mm overall depth, is reinforced with 4 bars of 16 mm diameter (Fe415) at an effective cover of 50 mm. Concrete grade is M25. Determine:
(a) The depth of neutral axis at working load.
(b) The ultimate moment of resistance of the section.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data Extraction**
- $b = 250$ mm, $D = 500$ mm, $d = 500 - 50 = 450$ mm
- $A_{st} = 4 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^2$
- $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa
- $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $E_c = 5000\sqrt{25} = 25000$ MPa
- $m = \dfrac{E_s}{E_c} = \dfrac{200000}{25000} = 8$

**Step 2 — Solve quadratic for *x* (WSM)**

$$
b \cdot x^2 + 2 \cdot m \cdot A_{st} \cdot x - 2 \cdot m \cdot A_{st} \cdot d = 0
$$

$$
250 \cdot x^2 + 2 \cdot 8 \cdot 804.25 \cdot x - 2 \cdot 8 \cdot 804.25 \cdot 450 = 0
$$

$$
250 \cdot x^2 + 12868 \cdot x - 5790600 = 0
$$

Dividing by 250:

$$
x^2 + 51.47 \cdot x - 23162.4 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply quadratic formula**

$$
x = \dfrac{-51.47 + \sqrt{51.47^2 + 4 \cdot 23162.4}}{2}
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{-51.47 + \sqrt{2649.16 + 92649.6}}{2}
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{-51.47 + \sqrt{95298.76}}{2} = \dfrac{-51.47 + 308.71}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{x \approx 128.62 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $M_u$ (LSM)**

$$
x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot 415 \cdot 804.25}{0.36 \cdot 25 \cdot 250} = \dfrac{290223}{2250} \approx 129.0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
M_u = 0.87 \cdot 415 \cdot 804.25 \cdot (450 - 0.42 \cdot 129.0)
$$

$$
M_u = 290223 \cdot (450 - 54.18) = 290223 \cdot 395.82
$$

$$
\boxed{M_u \approx 114.87 \text{ kN·m}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Three Stages of RC Beam Behaviour

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Stage I Uncracked Elastic Section"] --> B["Stage II Cracked Elastic Section"]
    B --> C["Stage III Ultimate Failure Section"]
    
    A1["Concrete + Steel both active in tension"] --> A
    A2["Stress is linear elastic"] --> A
    A3["NA near mid-depth"] --> A
    
    B1["Concrete below NA is cracked"] --> B
    B2["Only steel carries tension"] --> B
    B3["NA shifts upward"] --> B
    B4["Cracked section analysis applicable"] --> B
    
    C1["Steel yields OR concrete crushes"] --> C
    C2["Non-linear stress block"] --> C
    C3["Defines Mu and Mu_lim"] --> C
    C4["IS 456 design basis"] --> C
    
    D["Applied Moment M increases"] --> A
    D --> B
    D --> C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Stress Block Comparison (IS 456 Rectangular vs Actual Parabolic)

```mermaid

    A["Actual Stress Distribution ParabolicRectangular"] --> B["IS 456 Idealised Rectangular Block"]
    B --> C["Depth 0.42xu from top"]
    B --> D["Uniform stress 0.446fck"]

    F --> G["Bottom steel strain eps_st > eps_y for UR"]
    G --> H["eps_st < eps_y for OVER-REINFORCED"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Moment-Curvature Response

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Curvature phi increases"] --> B["Stage I Linear Elastic Slope EI_gross"]
    A --> C["Stage II Reduced Stiffness EI_cracked"]
    A --> D["Stage III Plastic Plateau near Mu"]
    
    B --> E["Sudden drop at Mcr Crack initiation"]
    E --> C
    C --> F["Plateau as steel yields"]
    F --> G["Failure point Fu"]
    
    G --> H["UR Section Ductile plateau visible"]
    G --> I["OR Section Sudden peak no warning"]
    
    H --> J["SAFETY CODE COMPLIANT"]
    I --> K["UNSAFE CODE VIOLATION"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes for Students:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (A1, B1, C2...) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels use plain uppercase text to ensure successful rendering in Markdown previewers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define the term "Under-Reinforced Section" as per IS 456:2000. Why is it preferred in limit state design?**

**Model Answer:**

A section is termed **under-reinforced** when the area of tension steel provided ($A_{st}$) is *less than* the area of steel required for a balanced section ($A_{st,bal}$), i.e., $\dfrac{A_{st}}{A_{st,bal}} < 1$.

It is preferred in **Limit State Design** because failure initiates by **yielding of steel in tension** before the concrete crushes. This ensures:
- **Ductile failure** with visible warning (large deflection and wide cracks).
- Adequate **time for evacuation** and emergency repairs.
- Compatibility with **earthquake-resistant design** requirements (energy dissipation).

(3 marks — *Definition: 1 mark, Two reasons: 2 marks*)

---

**Q2. State the assumptions made in the analysis of a reinforced concrete beam at the ultimate limit state as per IS 456.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Plane sections remain plane** after bending (Bernoulli's hypothesis) → linear strain distribution.
2. Maximum usable strain at the extreme compression fibre of concrete is $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$.
3. The tensile strength of concrete is **neglected** at the ultimate state.
4. The design stress-strain curve of concrete is assumed to be a **rectangular stress block** of depth $0.42 x_u$ and uniform stress $0.446 f_{ck}$ as per IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.
5. The partial safety factors are $\gamma_m = 1.5$ for concrete and $\gamma_m = 1.15$ for steel.

(3 marks — *Any 5 correct points*)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the equation for the depth of neutral axis at the working load stage for a singly-reinforced rectangular concrete beam using the modular ratio concept. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply-supported RC beam of span 6 m has a rectangular cross-section 300 mm wide × 600 mm deep (effective). It is reinforced with 5 bars of 20 mm diameter of Fe500 grade steel. Concrete grade is M25. Calculate the *ultimate moment of resistance* of the section and verify whether the section is under-reinforced. Use IS 456 recommendations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. For the answer sheet, the student is expected to present:

1. **Diagram** — Transformed cracked section showing equivalent concrete area $m \cdot A_{st}$. (1 mark)
2. **Equilibrium equation** — $C = T$ (1 mark)
3. **Strain compatibility** — $\dfrac{\sigma_c}{x} = \dfrac{\sigma_{st}/m}{d-x}$ (2 marks)
4. **Final quadratic equation** — $b x^2 + 2 m A_{st} x - 2 m A_{st} d = 0$ (2 marks)
5. **Solving** — Application of quadratic formula. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Given Data**
- $b = 300$ mm, $d = 600$ mm
- $A_{st} = 5 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 5 \times 314.16 = 1570.8 \text{ mm}^2$
- $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa (Fe500)

**Step 2 — Calculate $x_u$ at Ultimate State**

$$
x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st}}{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b} = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot 500 \cdot 1570.8}{0.36 \cdot 25 \cdot 300}
$$

$$
x_u = \dfrac{683298}{2700} \approx 253.07 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\dfrac{x_u}{d}$ and Compare with Limit**

$$
\dfrac{x_u}{d} = \dfrac{253.07}{600} \approx 0.4218
$$

For **Fe500**, IS 456 permits $x_{u,max}/d = 0.46$.

Since $0.4218 < 0.46$ → **Section is UNDER-REINFORCED** ✅ (Safe, ductile)

**Step 4 — Lever Arm and Moment of Resistance**

$$
z = d - 0.42 x_u = 600 - 0.42 \cdot 253.07 = 600 - 106.29 = 493.71 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{st} \cdot z = 0.87 \cdot 500 \cdot 1570.8 \cdot 493.71
$$

$$
\boxed{M_u \approx 337.39 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (Optional)**

$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2$ is **NOT valid for Fe500** (it is only for Fe415). For Fe500, the correct formula is:

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \cdot 0.46 \cdot (1 - 0.42 \cdot 0.46) \cdot 25 \cdot 300 \cdot 600^2 \approx 297 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Since $M_u > M_{u,lim}$, the section can resist the applied load. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the three stages of flexural behaviour of a reinforced concrete beam with neat sketches showing strain and stress distribution at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss why the balanced section is *not* recommended in design, and explain the consequences of an over-reinforced section with a real-world case study. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Three Stages of Behaviour [7 Marks]:**

For sketches, refer to **Section 4 (Mermaid Diagrams)** of these notes. The student must describe:

**Stage I — Uncracked Elastic Stage** (2 marks)
- Strain and stress are linear across the section.
- Both concrete and steel participate in tension.
- Governing equation: $\sigma = M y / I_g$.
- Valid for $M < M_{cr}$.

**Stage II — Cracked Elastic Stage** (3 marks)
- Concrete below NA has cracked; tensile strength is neglected.
- Strain still linear; stress in tension is carried **entirely by steel**.
- NA shifts upward; section is analysed as *transformed cracked section*.
- Governing equation: $b x^2 + 2 m A_{st} x - 2 m A_{st} d = 0$.

**Stage III — Ultimate / Failure Stage** (2 marks)
- Non-linear stress distribution; IS 456 rectangular block is used.
- Either steel yields or concrete crushes — the **first to occur defines failure mode**.
- Design of beam uses this stage under **LSM**.

---

**(b) Why Balanced Section is Avoided & Consequences of Over-Reinforced Section [7 Marks]:**

A **balanced section** fails *simultaneously* by yielding of steel and crushing of concrete — but the crushing of concrete is **sudden and brittle**, while yielding of steel is gradual. Therefore, a balanced section does not provide any warning before failure, and IS 456 mandates using *either under-reinforced or balanced* — but in practice, **under-reinforced** is universally adopted for safety.

**Consequences of Over-Reinforced Section:**
1. **Brittle failure** with no visible warning (deflection or cracking).
2. **Sudden collapse** — occupants have no time to evacuate.
3. **Poor energy dissipation** during earthquakes.
4. Violation of IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.3 — code mandates $x_u \le x_{u,max}$.

**Real-World Case Study:** The 1981 *Hyatt Regency walkway collapse (Kansas City, USA)* — although primarily a connection failure, the over-reinforcement philosophy in design contributed to the brittleness of the structure. **47 lives** were lost.

(7 marks — *Reasoning: 4 marks, Case study: 3 marks*)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing WSM and LSM formulas** — Applying $m = 13$ in LSM derivations will fetch **ZERO marks**. Modular ratio is **only for WSM**.
> 2. **Using $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ for Fe500** — This formula is **strictly for Fe415**. For Fe500, you must use $x_{u,max}/d = 0.46$.
> 3. **Forgetting the partial safety factors** ($\gamma_m$) — IS 456 uses $0.45 f_{ck}$ for concrete and $0.87 f_y$ for steel in design.
> 4. **Drawing a balanced section as "safe"** — The correct statement is: *"Balanced section is theoretically the threshold, but under-reinforced is preferred for safety."*
> 5. **Not stating units** — Always write kN·m or N·mm explicitly. Missing units = **0.5 to 1 mark deduction** per answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ RC beam behaviour has **three stages**: Uncracked → Cracked → Ultimate.
- ✅ **Plane sections remain plane** — this is the cornerstone of all RC analysis.
- ✅ Concrete is **neglected in tension** after cracking (Stage II onwards).
- ✅ **Under-reinforced = Ductile = SAFE** (Code-mandated).
- ✅ **Over-reinforced = Brittle = UNSAFE** (Code-prohibited).
- ✅ **Balanced section = $A_{st} = A_{st,bal}$** — theoretical boundary.
- ✅ For **Fe415**: $x_{u,max}/d = 0.48$ and $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$.
- ✅ For **Fe500**: $x_{u,max}/d = 0.46$ and $M_{u,lim} \approx 0.133 f_{ck} b d^2$.
- ✅ Modular ratio $m = E_s / E_c$ is **used only in WSM**, not in LSM.
- ✅ Partial safety factor: $\gamma_c = 1.5$ (concrete), $\gamma_s = 1.15$ (steel).
- ✅ Minimum tension steel: $A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85}{f_y} \cdot b \cdot d$.
- ✅ Maximum steel limit comes from $x_u \le x_{u,max}$.
- ✅ Stress block: $0.36 f_{ck}$ uniform over depth $0.42 x_u$ (IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3).
- ✅ **Lever arm**: $z = d - 0.42 x_u$ for rectangular section.
- ✅ Always remember: *Concrete is good in compression, Steel is good in tension, and **they only work as a team**.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design for Tension Reinforcement in Rectangular Reinforced Concrete Beam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design for Tension Reinforcement in Rectangular Reinforced Concrete Beam

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design for Tension Reinforcement in a Rectangular Reinforced Concrete (RC) Beam** is the systematic engineering procedure of proportioning the cross-sectional dimensions ($b \times d$) and calculating the area of longitudinal steel reinforcement ($A_{st}$) placed on the tension face of the beam, such that the section can safely resist the applied factored bending moment ($M_u$) and shear force ($V_u$) within the limit states prescribed by **IS 456:2000 (Plain and Reinforced Concrete – Code of Practice)** using the **Limit State Method (LSM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> As per the KTU 2024 scheme (PESFT754 – Safety in Design of Structures, Module 1), this topic is taught under the **Limit State of Collapse (Flexure)**. The student must design the section for the *ultimate* limit state, which is a safety-checked state just before structural collapse.

The fundamental design inequality to be satisfied is:

$$M_u \le M_{u,lim}$$

where $M_u$ is the factored applied moment and $M_{u,lim}$ is the limiting resisting moment of the section when the depth of the neutral axis is at its maximum permissible value (governed by the code to ensure a *ductile* failure).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **wooden plank supported at both ends**, and you stand in the middle. The plank bends downward. The **top of the plank shortens (compression)** and the **bottom stretches (tension)**. Wood is weak in tension and cracks at the bottom. To prevent cracking, we glue a **steel strip** at the bottom — a material strong in tension. This is exactly what we do in a beam: the concrete handles compression at the top, and **steel rebars (HYSD bars, Fe415 or Fe500)** are placed at the bottom to handle tension.

> **Real-World Analogy:** A **concrete beam is like a team**. Concrete (C30 or higher) is the "strongman" who handles pushing forces (compression), but is brittle under pulling. Steel (Fe415/Fe500) is the "flexible wrestler" who handles pulling forces (tension). They work *together* — bonded by their grip (bond stress) and given equal responsibility through the lever-arm (lever arm $z$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Philosophy (LSM):**
> The design is based on *factored loads* (1.5 × DL + 1.5 × LL) to ensure a margin of safety against collapse, and on *partial safety factors for materials* ($\gamma_m = 1.5$ for concrete, $\gamma_s = 1.15$ for steel).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (as per IS 456:2000)

| Parameter | Value | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Partial safety factor for concrete, $\gamma_m$ (flexure) | **1.5** | IS 456, Cl. 36.4.2.1 |
| Partial safety factor for steel, $\gamma_s$ | **1.15** | IS 456, Cl. 36.4.2.1 |
| Grade of concrete, $f_{ck}$ (typical) | **20, 25, 30, 35 N/mm²** | IS 456, Table 2 |
| Grade of steel, $f_y$ (typical) | **415, 500 N/mm²** | IS 456, Cl. 5.6 |
| Effective cover for beams (mild exposure) | **50 mm** | IS 456, Cl. 26.4.1 |
| Min. % of tension steel ($p_{t,min}$) for Fe415 | **0.205** ($0.85/f_y$) | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.1 |
| Max. % of tension steel ($p_{t,max}$) for Fe415 | **0.025** ($b \cdot d$) | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.1(b) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the Limit State Method (Cl. 36.1, IS 456)

1. **Plane sections remain plane** after bending → *linear strain distribution* across the depth.
2. **Maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre** at collapse = **0.0035**.
3. The **tensile strength of concrete is ignored** (since concrete cracks under tension long before collapse).
4. The **stress–strain relationship** for concrete is the *rectangular-parabolic* idealization. The design strength is $f_d = 0.446 \cdot f_{ck}$ (after applying $\gamma_m = 1.5$).
5. The **stress–strain curve for steel** is *elastic-perfectly-plastic* (bilinear) with design strength $f_{yd} = 0.87 \cdot f_y$ (after applying $\gamma_s = 1.15$).
6. **Perfect bond** exists between steel and concrete — they act monolithically.

## 2.2 Three Modes of Failure (Why We Avoid "Over-Reinforced" Sections)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical for KTU Exam:** Always justify why a section is "under-reinforced" and not "over-reinforced."

- **Balanced Section:** $x_{u,max} = 0.48d$ (Fe415) or $0.46d$ (Fe500). Concrete crushes and steel yields *simultaneously*.
- **Under-Reinforced Section** (preferred, ductile): $x_u < x_{u,max}$. **Steel yields first** → wide cracks, visible deflection → people can see the danger and escape. **Sudden collapse is averted.**
- **Over-Reinforced Section** (forbidden, brittle): $x_u > x_{u,max}$. **Concrete crushes first** without warning → brittle, catastrophic failure.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the EXACT set of formulas you must memorize for KTU 2024 Part-B design questions (14 marks).**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_u = 1.5 \left( M_{DL} + M_{LL} \right)$ | Factored design moment (limit state of collapse) |
| 2 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^{2}$ | Limiting moment of resistance (for Fe415, $x_{u,max}=0.48d$) — units: N·mm |
| 3 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.149 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^{2}$ | Limiting moment for Fe500 (using $x_{u,max}=0.46d$) |
| 4 | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_u}{Q \cdot b}}$ where $Q = 0.138 f_{ck}$ | Trial effective depth (assuming balanced section) |
| 5 | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d}{f_y} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{ 1 - \dfrac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^{2}} } \right]$ | Area of tension steel (this is THE master formula) |
| 6 | $p_t = \dfrac{A_{st}}{b \cdot d} \times 100 \, (\%)$ | Percentage of tension reinforcement |
| 7 | $A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85 \, b \, d}{f_y}$ | Min. tension steel (Cl. 26.5.1.1(a)) — for Fe415: $0.205\%$ of $bd$ |
| 8 | $A_{st,max} = 0.025 \, b \, d$ | Max. tension steel (Cl. 26.5.1.1(b)) |
| 9 | Overall depth: $D = d + \text{cover} + 0.5\phi$ | Where $\phi$ = diameter of bar |
| 10 | $x_{u,max} / d = 0.48$ (Fe415) / $0.46$ (Fe500) | Limiting depth factor |

> **Real-World Utility:** This design framework is used by every structural engineer in India to design **residential buildings (beams, lintels, chajjas), bridges, culverts, water tanks**, and is the foundation of *RCC design* taught worldwide. The philosophy directly leads to *ductile* design, which saved countless lives in the 2001 Bhuj earthquake and the 2015 Nepal earthquake.
