**Differential equation** with the same form, but boundary conditions are:

- $x = 0$ (fixed): $y = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$.
- $x = L$ (pinned): $y = 0$.

Using $y = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx)$:

From $y(0) = 0$: $B = 0$.
From $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\big|_{x=0} = 0$: $Ak = 0$, so $A = 0$ (trivial).

The resolution requires using the **slope-deflection approach** with two halves. Following the standard energy / equilibrium analysis, the transcendental equation reduces to:

$$\tan(kL) = kL$$

The smallest non-zero root of this transcendental equation is:

$$kL \approx 4.4934 \approx \dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Hence:**

$$P_{cr} = (kL)^2 \cdot \dfrac{EI}{L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{(\sqrt{2} L)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{2 L^2}$$

**Effective Length:**

$$L_e = \sqrt{2} \cdot L \approx 0.707 L$$

This confirms the table entry: $k \approx 0.7$, $L_e = L / \sqrt{2}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Table — All End Conditions

| End Condition | Characteristic Equation | Root $kL$ | Effective Length $L_e$ | Critical Load $P_{cr}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Pinned–Pinned | $\sin(kL) = 0$ | $\pi$ | $L$ | $\pi^2 EI / L^2$ |
| Fixed–Fixed | $\sin(kL/2) = 0$ | $2\pi$ | $L/2$ | $4\pi^2 EI / L^2$ |
| Fixed–Free | $\cos(kL) = 0$ | $\pi/2$ | $2L$ | $\pi^2 EI / (4L^2)$ |
| Fixed–Pinned | $\tan(kL) = kL$ | $\approx 4.493$ | $\approx 0.707L$ | $\pi^2 EI / (2L^2)$ |

## 3.5 Derivation of Limiting Slenderness Ratio

At the boundary between elastic Euler buckling and inelastic (Johnson) buckling, the **Euler stress equals the yield stress**:

$$\sigma_{cr} = f_y$$

Euler's critical stress is:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$

Setting them equal:

$$\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda_{cr}^2} = f_y$$

Solving:

$$\lambda_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{f_y}}$$

**Numerical value for Fe410 steel** ($E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $f_y = 410$ MPa):

$$\lambda_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{410}}$$

$$\lambda_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.974 \times 10^6}{410}}$$

$$\lambda_{cr} = \sqrt{4815.6}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{cr} \approx 83.8}$$

For **Fe250** ($f_y = 250$ MPa):

$$\lambda_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{250}} = \sqrt{7895.7} \approx 88.8$$

These limits appear directly in **IS 800:2007 Table 7** and are central to the KTU examination.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Column Behaviour Classification Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the *logical decision sequence* a designer follows when assessing an axially compressed member. Note that all node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeB`) to comply with Mermaid syntax safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["AXIAL COMPRESSION MEMBER RECEIVED FOR DESIGN"]
    nodeB["CALCULATE SLENDERNESS RATIO LAMBDA = Le DIVIDED BY r"]
    nodeC{"IS LAMBDA LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO LAMBDA_cr = 83.8 FOR Fe410"}
    nodeD["LONG COLUMN : GOVERNED BY EULER BUCKLING"]
    nodeE["APPLY P_cr = pi SQUARED E I DIVIDED BY Le SQUARED"]
    nodeF{"IS LAMBDA LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO LAMBDA_p = 118.6"}
    nodeG["INTERMEDIATE COLUMN : JOHNSON PARABOLIC FORMULA"]
    nodeH["APPLY IS 800 PERRY ROBERTSON EQUATION FOR f_cd"]
    nodeI["SHORT COLUMN : GOVERNED BY CRUSHING"]
    nodeJ["APPLY P_d = f_y A DIVIDED BY gamma_m0"]
    nodeK["FINAL SAFE DESIGN LOAD CAPACITY P_d COMPUTED"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeF
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeG
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Effective Length Selection (End Conditions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["COLUMN WITH UNKNOWN END CONDITIONS"]
    nodeB{"END CONDITION TYPE"}
    nodeC["PINNED PINNED : Le = L : k = 1.0"]
    nodeD["FIXED FIXED : Le = L OVER 2 : k = 0.5"]
    nodeE["FIXED FREE : Le = 2L : k = 2.0"]
    nodeF["FIXED PINNED : Le = L OVER ROOT 2 : k = 0.7"]
    nodeG["USE Le IN EULER FORMULA P_cr = pi SQUARED E I OVER Le SQUARED"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- BOTH PINNED --> nodeC
    nodeB -- BOTH FIXED --> nodeD
    nodeB -- FIXED AND FREE --> nodeE
    nodeB -- FIXED AND PINNED --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Stress Behaviour vs Slenderness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INCREASING SLENDERNESS RATIO LAMBDA ON X AXIS"]
    nodeB["STRESS ON Y AXIS"]
    nodeC["SHORT COLUMN REGION : LAMBDA LESS THAN 40 : STRESS EQUALS f_y"]
    nodeD["INTERMEDIATE COLUMN : LAMBDA 40 TO 84 : STRESS DROPS PER JOHNSON CURVE"]
    nodeE["LONG COLUMN : LAMBDA GREATER THAN 84 : STRESS FOLLOWS EULER HYPERBOLA pi SQUARED E OVER LAMBDA SQUARED"]
    nodeF["LIMITING POINT : LAMBDA_cr = 83.8 : TRANSITION FROM JOHNSON TO EULER"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeB
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Design Procedure as per IS 800:2007

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STEP 1 : ASSUME A TRIAL SECTION ISMB OR ISMC OR ISWB"]
    nodeB["STEP 2 : COMPUTE A, I, r FOR THE SECTION"]
    nodeC["STEP 3 : COMPUTE Le BASED ON END CONDITIONS"]
    nodeD["STEP 4 : COMPUTE LAMBDA = Le DIVIDED BY r MINIMUM ACROSS BOTH AXES"]
    nodeE["STEP 5 : IDENTIFY BUCKLING CLASS a b c OR d FROM IS 800 TABLE 10"]
    nodeF["STEP 6 : READ f_cd FROM IS 800 TABLE 9 BASED ON LAMBDA AND BUCKLING CLASS"]
    nodeG["STEP 7 : COMPUTE P_d = f_cd TIMES A"]
    nodeH{"STEP 8 : IS P_d GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO APPLIED FACTORED LOAD P_u"}
    nodeI["SECTION SAFE : PROVIDE END CONNECTIONS AND STIFFENERS"]
    nodeJ["REVISE SECTION : TRY LARGER SIZE OR ADD LATERAL SUPPORT"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeI
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Distinguish between crushing failure and buckling failure in axially loaded compression members. Under what conditions does each mode govern the design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Crushing Failure:**
Crushing is a *material* failure where the compressive stress in the column reaches the yield stress $f_y$ of the material. It occurs without any lateral deflection of the member. The failure is governed by the cross-sectional area and the yield strength:

$$P_c = f_y \times A$$

**Buckling Failure:**
Buckling is a *stability* failure where the column suddenly deflects laterally at a load much below the crushing load. It occurs in slender members and is governed by the flexural rigidity $EI$ and the effective length $L_e$:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$$

**Conditions of Governance:**
- **Short, stocky columns** (low $\lambda$): Crushing governs.
- **Long, slender columns** (high $\lambda$): Buckling governs.
- **Intermediate columns:** A combination; governed by Johnson's or Perry-Robertson formula.

The transition is at $\lambda = \lambda_{cr} \approx 83.8$ for Fe410 steel.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Define slenderness ratio. What are the limiting values of slenderness ratio as per IS 800:2007 for (a) compression members resisting lateral loads, and (b) compression members not resisting lateral loads?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Slenderness ratio is the ratio of effective length to the radius of gyration of the cross-section:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r}$$

where $r = \sqrt{I/A}$.

**IS 800:2007 Limits:**
- **(a) Members resisting lateral loads:** $\lambda \le 180$
- **(b) Members not resisting lateral loads:** $\lambda \le 250$
- **(c) For tension members (for reference):** $\lambda \le 400$

These limits ensure that the member does not undergo excessive lateral flexibility or accidental lateral loads before the intended failure mode.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**OR Question A (a) — 7 Marks:**
A steel column of effective length 4.5 m is made of ISHB 300 @ 576 N/m. The section properties are: $A = 73.4 \text{ cm}^2$, $r_{min} = 5.36$ cm, $f_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa. Check whether the column will fail by yielding or by buckling. Take $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Slenderness Ratio:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \dfrac{4500 \text{ mm}}{53.6 \text{ mm}} = 83.96$$

**Step 2 — Compute Limiting Slenderness:**

$$\lambda_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{f_y}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{250}}$$

$$\lambda_{cr} = \sqrt{7895.7} \approx 88.86$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

Since $\lambda = 83.96 < \lambda_{cr} = 88.86$, the column falls in the **intermediate / inelastic range**. Therefore, buckling will occur **before** pure yielding. We must check the actual compressive stress against IS 800 design strength.

**Step 4 — Crushing Capacity:**

$$P_c = \dfrac{f_y \times A}{\gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{250 \times 7340}{1.10} = 1,668,182 \text{ N} \approx 1668 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Euler Buckling Load:**

$$I_{min} = A \cdot r_{min}^2 = 73.4 \times 5.36^2 = 73.4 \times 28.73 = 2109 \text{ cm}^4$$

Converting: $I = 2109 \text{ cm}^4 = 2109 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4 = 2.109 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$.

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2.109 \times 10^7}{(4500)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 4.218 \times 10^{12}}{2.025 \times 10^7}$$

$$P_{cr} = \pi^2 \times 208,300$$

$$P_{cr} \approx 9.87 \times 208,300 \approx 2,055,921 \text{ N} \approx 2056 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

Since $P_{cr} = 2056 \text{ kN} < P_c = 1668 \text{ kN}$ … wait, $P_{cr} > P_c$ here. The actual critical load is governed by the **smaller** of the two values. Since $P_c = 1668 \text{ kN}$ is smaller, the column will fail by **yielding (crushing)** before elastic buckling — though it is close to the transition.

(Note: This example is illustrative; in real IS 800 design, the Perry-Robertson formula gives the actual design strength.)

---

**OR Question A (b) — 7 Marks:**
A 3 m long strut of a roof truss has both ends pinned. The cross-section is a single angle 60 mm × 60 mm × 6 mm. Determine the Euler's buckling load. Given: $A = 6.91 \text{ cm}^2$, $I_{min} = 23.5 \text{ cm}^4$, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective Length (Both Ends Pinned):**

$$L_e = L = 3 \text{ m} = 3000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum Moment of Inertia:**

$$I_{min} = 23.5 \text{ cm}^4 = 23.5 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4 = 2.35 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's Formula:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L_e^{\,2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2.35 \times 10^5}{(3000)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 4.70 \times 10^{10}}{9 \times 10^6}$$

$$P_{cr} = \pi^2 \times 5222.22$$

$$P_{cr} \approx 9.8696 \times 5222.22$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 51,544 \text{ N} \approx 51.54 \text{ kN}}$$

This is the Euler buckling load for the given angle section strut.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Question B (a) — 7 Marks:**
A column of effective length 3.6 m consists of two channels ISJC 200 placed back-to-back with a clear gap of 100 mm between webs. Compute the safe load carrying capacity of the column if $f_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, and the section properties are: $A = 22.5 \text{ cm}^2$ (per channel), $I_{xx} = 1380 \text{ cm}^4$, $I_{yy} = 76.3 \text{ cm}^4$ (about own centroidal axis), $C_{yy} = 2.05$ cm, $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Area:**

$$A_{total} = 2 \times 22.5 = 45 \text{ cm}^2 = 4500 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia about X-X Axis (Built-up Section):**

Since the channels are placed back-to-back symmetrically, the x-x axis remains the common centroidal axis:

$$I_{xx,total} = 2 \times 1380 = 2760 \text{ cm}^4 = 2.76 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Moment of Inertia about Y-Y Axis (using parallel axis theorem):**

Distance between centroid of channel and overall centroid of built-up section:

$$d = \dfrac{100}{2} + C_{yy} = 50 + 20.5 = 70.5 \text{ mm} = 7.05 \text{ cm}$$

Wait — note that $C_{yy} = 2.05$ cm is the centroidal distance of channel. The total distance from web face to overall centroid is half the gap plus $C_{yy}$:

$$d = \dfrac{100}{2} + 20.5 = 70.5 \text{ mm} = 7.05 \text{ cm}$$

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \times \left[ I_{yy,own} + A \cdot d^2 \right]$$

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \times \left[ 76.3 + 22.5 \times (7.05)^2 \right]$$

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \times \left[ 76.3 + 22.5 \times 49.7 \right]$$

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \times \left[ 76.3 + 1118.25 \right]$$

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \times 1194.55 = 2389.1 \text{ cm}^4$$

**Step 4 — Identify Governing Axis:**

Compare $I_{xx} = 2760 \text{ cm}^4$ vs $I_{yy} = 2389.1 \text{ cm}^4$.

Governing axis is **y-y** (minimum $I$): $I_{min} = 2389.1 \text{ cm}^4 = 2.389 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$.

**Step 5 — Slenderness Ratio:**

$$r_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A_{total}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2389.1}{45}} = \sqrt{53.09} = 7.29 \text{ cm} = 72.9 \text{ mm}$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \dfrac{3600}{72.9} = 49.38$$

**Step 6 — Limiting Slenderness:**

$$\lambda_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{250}} \approx 88.86$$

Since $\lambda = 49.38 < 88.86$, this is an **intermediate column**.

**Step 7 — Compute Euler Stress:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{(49.38)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{1.974 \times 10^6}{2438.4} \approx 809.6 \text{ MPa}$$

But since $f_y = 250$ MPa is the maximum, the actual stress at yielding governs.

**Step 8 — Apply Johnson's Formula (conservative estimate):**

$$\sigma_{c,allow} = f_y \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\lambda^2}{2 \lambda_{cr}^2} \right] = 250 \left[ 1 - \dfrac{(49.38)^2}{2 \times (88.86)^2} \right]$$

$$\sigma_{c,allow} = 250 \left[ 1 - \dfrac{2438.4}{15791.4} \right]$$

$$\sigma_{c,allow} = 250 \times [1 - 0.1544] = 250 \times 0.8456 = 211.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 9 — Safe Load Capacity:**

$$P_{safe} = \dfrac{\sigma_{c,allow} \times A_{total}}{\gamma_{m0}} = \dfrac{211.4 \times 4500}{1.10}$$

$$P_{safe} = \dfrac{951,300}{1.10}$$

$$\boxed{P_{safe} \approx 864,818 \text{ N} \approx 865 \text{ kN}}$$

---

**Question B (b) — 7 Marks:**
A hollow circular cast iron column of external diameter 200 mm and internal diameter 150 mm is used as a column with one end fixed and the other end free. The length is 3 m. Find the Euler's crippling load. Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ MPa.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective Length (Fixed-Free):**

$$L_e = 2L = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ m} = 6000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia (Hollow Circular Section):**

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left( D^4 - d^4 \right)$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left( 200^4 - 150^4 \right)$$

$$200^4 = 1.6 \times 10^9$$

$$150^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^8$$

$$D^4 - d^4 = 1.6 \times 10^9 - 5.0625 \times 10^8 = 1.09375 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \times 1.09375 \times 10^9$$

$$I = \dfrac{3.4359 \times 10^9}{64} = 5.368 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's Formula:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 5.368 \times 10^7}{(6000)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 6.4416 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^7}$$

$$P_{cr} = \pi^2 \times 178,933$$

$$P_{cr} \approx 9.8696 \times 178,933$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 1,765,920 \text{ N} \approx 1766 \text{ kN}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Effective length confusion:** Mixing up $L_e$ with actual length $L$. For "one end fixed and other free," students often write $L_e = L$, losing 2–3 marks instantly. Correct: $L_e = 2L$.
> 2. **Using wrong axis:** Failing to compute $r_{min}$ across *both* axes and identify the governing (weak) axis.
> 3. **Forgetting $\gamma_{m0}$:** Quoting $P_{cr}$ directly as the safe load. Safe design load is always $P_{cr} / \gamma_{m0}$ or derived from $f_{cd}$ (IS 800 design stress).
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing cm and mm in $I$, $r$, $L$. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unit mismatches.
> 5. **Not classifying the column:** Skipping the check of $\lambda$ vs $\lambda_{cr}$. The examiner wants explicit classification (short/intermediate/long).
> 6. **Forgetting to take minimum $I$ for asymmetric sections:** For single angles, use the smaller principal axis value of $I$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

- **Axial compression** is governed by two distinct failure modes: **crushing** (material, for short columns) and **buckling** (stability, for long columns).
- **Euler's Critical Load:** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ — applies only to *long, elastic* columns.
- **Effective Length Factors:** Pinned-Pinned: **1.0**, Fixed-Fixed: **0.5**, Fixed-Free: **2.0**, Fixed-Pinned: **≈ 0.7**.
- **Slenderness Ratio:** $\lambda = L_e / r$ — single most important design parameter for compression members.
- **Limiting Slenderness:** $\lambda_{cr} = \sqrt{\pi^2 E / f_y}$ ≈ **83.8 for Fe410**, **88.8 for Fe250**.
- **Short column** ($\lambda \le \lambda_p$): Use crushing strength $f_y \cdot A / \gamma_{m0}$.
- **Long column** ($\lambda > \lambda_{cr}$): Use Euler's formula.
- **Intermediate column** ($\lambda_p < \lambda \le \lambda_{cr}$): Use **Johnson's parabolic formula** or **IS 800 Perry-Robertson equation**.
- **IS 800:2007 Slenderness Limits:** **180** (with lateral loads), **250** (without lateral loads).
- **Partial Safety Factors:** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ for material, $\gamma_f = 1.50$ for loads.
- **Built-up Sections:** Use parallel axis theorem to compute $I$ about the built-up centroidal axis.
- **Single angles & tees:** Use the smaller principal axis for $I_{min}$.
- **Always classify the column first** before selecting a formula — this is a 2-mark question in KTU exams if skipped.
- **Always take minimum $r$** (weakest axis) for design — failure always initiates about the weakest axis.
- **Factored load approach:** Use $P_u = 1.5 (DL + LL)$ and ensure $P_d \ge P_u$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lateral Ties and Spirals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lateral Ties and Spirals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Lateral ties** (also called **transverse reinforcement**, **stirrups**, or **hoops**) are closed-loop steel bars placed perpendicular (or at an angle) to the longitudinal reinforcement in a reinforced concrete (RCC) compression member, such as a column. Their primary engineering function is to provide **lateral confinement** to the concrete core and to **prevent the buckling of longitudinal bars** under axial compression.

A **spiral** (or **helical reinforcement**) is a continuous helical coil of steel bar wound around the longitudinal bars of a circular or polygonal column. It functions identically to lateral ties but provides a more uniform and continuous confining pressure around the concrete core, dramatically enhancing the ductility and load-carrying capacity of the column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PESFT754 – Module 2):**
> The course explicitly covers lateral ties and spirals under the broader umbrella of **"Safety Provisions in Reinforced Concrete Members"**. Students must be able to differentiate between the two, justify their selection for a given column design, and compute spacing, diameter, and volumetric ratios as per **IS 456:2000** and **SP-16**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a bundle of uncooked spaghetti sticks standing upright on a table. If you press down on the top, the sticks will immediately splay outward and buckle. Now imagine tying a rubber band tightly around the bundle every few centimeters — the sticks are now held together, the buckling is delayed, and they can carry significantly more load before failing.

**This is exactly what lateral ties and spirals do inside an RCC column:**

- The **longitudinal bars** = the spaghetti sticks (resist axial compression).
- The **concrete** = the binding that holds them in place, but it has very low tensile strength.
- The **lateral ties / spirals** = the rubber bands (provide lateral confinement, prevent buckling, and improve ductility).

Without lateral reinforcement, a slender concrete column fails in a **sudden, brittle, and catastrophic** manner. With adequate ties or spirals, the column exhibits **ductile failure** — a critical safety requirement in seismic and blast-resistant design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Highlighted:**
> - **Minimum yield strength of tie steel (fy):** typically **415 MPa** or **500 MPa** (Fe415 / Fe500) in Indian practice.
> - **Diameter of ties:** NOT less than **1/4** of the diameter of the largest longitudinal bar, and NOT less than **6 mm** (per IS 456:2000, Cl. 26.5.3.2).
> - **Pitch of spirals:** NOT more than **75 mm**, NOT less than **25 mm**, and NOT more than **1/6** of the core diameter (per IS 456:2000, Cl. 26.5.3.3).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Lateral Ties and Spirals Needed? — The Engineering "Why"

A concrete column under axial compression is subjected to a complex triaxial stress state. The transverse reinforcement performs **four critical safety functions**, each of which directly affects the **factor of safety** of the structure:

### Function 1: Prevention of Buckling of Longitudinal Bars
- Longitudinal bars are slender elements under compression.
- Without lateral support, they would buckle outward at stresses far below their yield strength.
- **How ties help:** They act as **intermediate supports**, reducing the effective buckling length of the longitudinal bars.

### Function 2: Confinement of Concrete Core
- Concrete is strong in compression but weak in tension.
- When a column is loaded, the concrete tends to expand laterally (Poisson effect).
- Ties/spirals resist this expansion, creating a **triaxial state of stress** that dramatically increases the effective compressive strength of the core concrete.

### Function 3: Shear Resistance
- In columns subjected to bending and axial load (eccentric loading), **shear forces** develop.
- Ties act as **shear reinforcement**, similar to stirrups in beams, resisting diagonal tension cracks.

### Function 4: Restraint Against Bar Splaying During Seismic / Cyclic Loading
- During earthquakes, columns experience **reversal of moments**, causing bars to alternate between tension and compression.
- Well-detailed ties prevent bars from **splaying outward** and maintain the integrity of the joint — a non-negotiable requirement per **IS 13920:2016** (Ductile Detailing).

## 2.2 Types of Lateral Reinforcement — A Taxonomy

| Type | Shape | Typical Use | Confinement Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Tie (Open)** | Single closed loop | Light columns, minor members | Low |
| **Double Tie (Cross-tie)** | Two overlapping loops | Columns with ≥ 6 longitudinal bars | Medium |
| **Lateral Hoops** | Continuous overlapping hoops | Seismic / ductile columns | High |
| **Spiral (Helical)** | Continuous helix | Circular columns, high-stress zones | **Very High** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (IS 456:2000 + SP-16)

| Quantity | Formula / Provision | Code Reference | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Min. diameter of lateral tie, $\phi_{tie}$ | $\phi_{tie} \geq \dfrac{\phi_{l,max}}{4}$ AND $\phi_{tie} \geq 6\text{ mm}$ | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2 | mm |
| Pitch of lateral ties (s) | $s \leq \text{least of } (B, 16\phi_{l,min}, 300\text{ mm})$ | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2(b) | mm |
| Pitch of spiral (s) | $s \leq \text{least of } (75\text{ mm}, D_c/6, 25\text{ mm})$ $\leq s$ | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3 | mm |
| Volumetric ratio of spiral, $\rho_s$ | $\rho_s = \dfrac{0.36 (A_g/A_k - 1) \cdot f_{ck}}{f_y}$ (for enhanced strength) | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3 | — |
| Equivalent lateral confinement pressure, $f_l$ | $f_l = \dfrac{2 f_y A_{sp}}{D_c s}$ (ACI 318) | ACI 318 Cl. 25.7.3.3 | MPa |
| Enhanced concrete strength (confined), $f_{cc}'$ | $f_{cc}' = f_c' + k_1 f_l$ (Mander model) | Mander et al. 1988 | MPa |
| Ultimate axial capacity (spiral column), $P_u$ | $P_u = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$ | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3 | kN |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $\phi_{l,max}$ = diameter of the largest longitudinal bar
> - $\phi_{l,min}$ = diameter of the smallest longitudinal bar
> - $B$ = least lateral dimension of the column
> - $D_c$ = diameter of the core (center-to-center of spiral)
> - $A_g$ = gross cross-sectional area
> - $A_k$ = core area (inside spiral)
> - $A_{sp}$ = cross-sectional area of spiral bar
> - $A_c$ = net area of concrete
> - $A_{sc}$ = area of longitudinal steel
> - $f_{ck}$ = characteristic compressive strength of concrete
> - $f_y$ = yield strength of steel

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-rise buildings:** Lateral ties enable columns to resist wind and seismic lateral loads without brittle failure.
- **Bridges and flyovers:** Spiral columns in piers provide the ductility needed to absorb impact and vibration loads.
- **Nuclear containment structures:** The enhanced ductility from spirals is a critical safety barrier against blast loading.
- **Offshore platforms and chimneys:** Spirals provide fatigue resistance under cyclic wave/wind loading.
- **Post-earthquake forensics:** Columns that lacked adequate lateral ties are the most common cause of progressive collapse — a direct lesson from the **1994 Northridge** and **2001 Bhuj** earthquakes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Ratio of Spiral Reinforcement

The governing requirement per **IS 456:2000, Cl. 26.5.3.3** is that the spiral shall be so designed that it provides adequate confinement to ensure that the additional strength of the confined core is not less than the loss of strength in the outer concrete shell (which spalls off at ultimate load).

**Step 1: Equate the loss of strength in the outer shell to the gain in strength of the confined core.**

Loss in strength (outer shell):
$$
\Delta P_{loss} = 0.85 f_{ck} (A_g - A_k)
$$

Gain in strength (confined core, from the Mander/IS approach):
$$
\Delta P_{gain} = 0.85 f_{ck} A_k \cdot (k \cdot \rho_s \cdot f_y / f_{ck})
$$

**Step 2: Setting $\Delta P_{loss} = \Delta P_{gain}$ and solving for $\rho_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.85 f_{ck} (A_g - A_k) &= 0.85 f_{ck} A_k \cdot \left(\frac{k \cdot \rho_s \cdot f_y}{f_{ck}}\right) \\
(A_g - A_k) &= A_k \cdot \left(\frac{k \cdot \rho_s \cdot f_y}{f_{ck}}\right) \\
\frac{A_g}{A_k} - 1 &= \frac{k \cdot \rho_s \cdot f_y}{f_{ck}} \\
\rho_s &= \frac{0.36 \left(\dfrac{A_g}{A_k} - 1\right) f_{ck}}{f_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **0.36** emerges from the calibration constant $k$ used in the IS code (empirically derived to match test data on spiral columns by Richart, Brandzaeg, and Brown in 1929).

**Step 3: Express $\rho_s$ geometrically in terms of the spiral bar area $A_{sp}$, core diameter $D_c$, and pitch $s$:**

$$
\rho_s = \frac{\text{Volume of spiral steel}}{\text{Volume of confined core}} = \frac{A_{sp} \cdot \pi D_c}{(\pi D_c^2 / 4) \cdot s} = \frac{4 A_{sp}}{D_c \cdot s}
$$

**Step 4: Equating the two expressions for $\rho_s$ and solving for pitch $s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4 A_{sp}}{D_c \cdot s} &= \frac{0.36 \left(\dfrac{A_g}{A_k} - 1\right) f_{ck}}{f_y} \\
s &= \frac{4 A_{sp} \cdot f_y}{0.36 \left(\dfrac{A_g}{A_k} - 1\right) f_{ck} \cdot D_c} \\
s &= \frac{11.1 \cdot A_{sp} \cdot f_y}{\left(\dfrac{A_g}{A_k} - 1\right) f_{ck} \cdot D_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the **maximum permissible pitch** of the spiral for the desired confinement level.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Confinement Pressure (ACI 318 Approach)

When a spiral with pitch $s$ and bar area $A_{sp}$ confines a concrete core of diameter $D_c$, the **lateral confining pressure** $f_l$ is derived from a free-body equilibrium of a horizontal slice of the spiral.

**Step 1: Consider the hoop tension in one turn of the spiral (length $\pi D_c$):**

$$
T = f_y \cdot A_{sp}
$$

**Step 2: This tension acts radially inward. Resolving it into a uniform pressure over the core cross-section:**

The spiral exerts a radially inward force per unit height of column equal to $2 f_y A_{sp} / s$ (factor of 2 accounts for two sides of the spiral resisting outward pressure).

**Step 3: Divide by the core diameter to get confining pressure:**

$$
f_l = \frac{2 f_y A_{sp}}{D_c \cdot s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental confinement equation** used universally in reinforced concrete design. It forms the basis of the Mander confined-concrete model and the ACI 318 column design provisions.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Pitch of Spiral for a Circular Column

**Given:**
- Column diameter (overall): $D = 500$ mm
- Clear cover: $40$ mm
- Longitudinal bars: $8$ nos., $20$ mm dia
- Spiral bar: $10$ mm dia
- $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa

**Step 1: Compute gross and core areas.**

Core diameter: $D_c = 500 - 2(40) - 10 = 410$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_g &= \frac{\pi}{4} (500)^2 = 196{,}350 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_k &= \frac{\pi}{4} (410)^2 = 132{,}025 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the required volumetric ratio of spiral.**

$$
\rho_s = \frac{0.36 \left(\dfrac{196{,}350}{132{,}025} - 1\right) \times 25}{415}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{A_g}{A_k} &= 1.4872 \\
\rho_s &= \frac{0.36 \times 0.4872 \times 25}{415} = \frac{4.3848}{415} = 0.01057
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute spiral bar area.**

$$
A_{sp} = \frac{\pi}{4} (10)^2 = 78.54 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4: Solve for pitch $s$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{4 A_{sp}}{D_c \cdot \rho_s} = \frac{4 \times 78.54}{410 \times 0.01057} \\
s &= \frac{314.16}{4.334} = 72.49 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply IS 456 limits.**

- $s \leq 75$ mm ✓
- $s \leq D_c/6 = 410/6 = 68.33$ mm ✗
- $s \geq 25$ mm ✓

**Governing value: $s = 65$ mm** (rounded down to a practical value satisfying all limits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world design tip:** Engineers always round **down** the pitch to a practical value (e.g., 60 mm or 65 mm) to provide an additional factor of safety on confinement.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Lateral Tie Detailing Decision Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **engineer's decision-making process** when selecting and detailing lateral ties vs. spirals in a reinforced concrete column. This complements the textual analysis in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Column Design] --> B{Column Shape?}
    B -- Circular --> C[Use SPIRAL Reinforcement]
    B -- Rectangular --> D[Use LATERAL TIES / HOOPS]
    C --> E{Is Column Seismic?}
    D --> F{Is Column Seismic?}
    E -- Yes --> G[Apply IS 13920:2016 Ductile Detailing]
    E -- No --> H[Apply IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3 Spiral Provisions]
    F -- Yes --> G
    F -- No --> I[Apply IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2 Tie Provisions]
    G --> J[Check Spacing Limits]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Spacing Satisfies All Code Limits?}
    K -- No --> L[Reduce Pitch / Add Extra Ties]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[Check Diameter of Tie / Spiral Bar]
    M --> N{Diameter Satisfies Min. Limit?}
    N -- No --> O[Increase Bar Diameter]
    O --> M
    N -- Yes --> P[Check Volumetric Ratio rho_s]
    P --> Q{rho_s Satisfies Code Min.?}
    Q -- No --> R[Increase Spiral Bar Size or Reduce Pitch]
    R --> P
    Q -- Yes --> S[Final Detailing Drawing]
    S --> T[End: Design Complete]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic: Column Tie Patterns

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **typology of lateral tie arrangements** based on the number and arrangement of longitudinal bars. This is essential for visualizing how ties and cross-ties interact with longitudinal bars.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[4 Longitudinal Bars]
        A1[Corner Bar 1]
        A2[Corner Bar 2]
        A3[Corner Bar 3]
        A4[Corner Bar 4]
        T1[Single Closed Tie]
        A1 --- T1
        T1 --- A2
        T1 --- A3
        T1 --- A4
    end
    subgraph B[6 to 8 Longitudinal Bars]
        B1[Corner Bars x4]
        B2[Middle Bars x2 to 4]
        DT1[Outer Perimeter Tie]
        DT2[Inner Cross Tie]
        B1 --- DT1
        B2 --- DT2
        DT1 --- DT2
    end
    subgraph C[More Than 8 Bars - Seismic Zones]
        C1[Multiple Bars in Core]
        C2[Special Confining Hoop per IS 13920]
        C3[Supplementary Cross Ties]
        C1 --- C2
        C1 --- C3
        C2 --- C3
    end
```

## 4.3 Engineering Schematic: Spiral Column Anatomy (Text-Based Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SC[Spiral Column Assembly]
        SC1[Outer Concrete Shell - Unconfined Cover Region]
        SC2[Spiral Reinforcement - Continuous Helix]
        SC3[Longitudinal Bars - 6 to 12 nos. Around Perimeter]
        SC4[Confined Concrete Core - Triaxial Stress State]
        SC5[Inner Core Diameter Dc]
    end
    SC1 --> SC2
    SC2 --> SC3
    SC2 --> SC4
    SC4 --> SC5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - The **outer concrete shell** (cover region) typically spalls off at ultimate load — this is why we only count the **core** in strength calculations for spiral columns.
> - The **confined core** carries a higher effective stress because of the triaxial state induced by the spiral.
> - The **longitudinal bars** are restrained from buckling by the continuous spiral, allowing them to develop their full yield strength.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between lateral ties and spiral reinforcement in RCC columns. State the IS code provision for minimum diameter of lateral ties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lateral Ties | Spiral Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Discrete closed loops (hoops) | Continuous helical coil |
| Application | Rectangular / square columns | Circular / polygonal columns |
| Confinement | Discontinuous (at tie spacing) | Continuous (uniform) |
| Ductility | Moderate | High |

**IS 456:2000 Cl. 26.5.3.2 provision:** The diameter of lateral ties shall be **NOT less than 1/4 of the diameter of the largest longitudinal bar**, and in **no case less than 6 mm**.

*(1 mark – definition; 1 mark – difference; 1 mark – IS provision)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is the function of lateral ties in a reinforced concrete column? List any two IS 456 code limits for the pitch of lateral ties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Functions of lateral ties:**
1. Prevent buckling of longitudinal bars.
2. Provide lateral confinement to the concrete core.
3. Resist shear forces in the column.

**IS 456 limits on pitch of lateral ties (Cl. 26.5.3.2b):**
1. Pitch shall **NOT exceed the least lateral dimension of the column**.
2. Pitch shall **NOT exceed 16 times the diameter of the smallest longitudinal bar**.
3. Pitch shall **NOT exceed 300 mm**.

*(1 mark – function; 1 mark – first limit; 1 mark – second limit)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**A short rectangular RCC column of size 400 mm × 500 mm is reinforced with 8 longitudinal bars of 20 mm diameter. The column is designed for axial load only (non-seismic). Design the lateral tie system as per IS 456:2000. Assume Fe415 steel and clear cover of 40 mm.**

#### (a) Determine the diameter and pitch of the lateral ties. (7 marks)

**Step 1: Diameter of tie bar.**

$$
\phi_{tie} \geq \frac{\phi_{l,max}}{4} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

But $\phi_{tie} \geq 6$ mm (governing).

**∴ Provide 8 mm diameter ties.**

**Step 2: Pitch of lateral tie.**

Least lateral dimension of column = 400 mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &\leq B = 400 \text{ mm} \\
s &\leq 16 \phi_{l,min} = 16 \times 20 = 320 \text{ mm} \\
s &\leq 300 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Governing pitch: $s = 300$ mm.**

**However**, since 8 longitudinal bars are present, **cross-ties** must be provided to restrain the middle bars (per IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2c).

**∴ Provide 8 mm dia. ties @ 300 mm c/c, with cross-ties in alternate spacings.**

#### (b) Sketch the cross-section showing the tie arrangement and justify the placement of cross-ties. (7 marks)

**Cross-Section Layout:**

- 4 corner bars + 4 intermediate bars (2 on each 400 mm face, 2 on each 500 mm face).
- One **outer perimeter tie** (8 mm dia) passing through all 8 bars in a rectangular pattern.
- Two **cross-ties** (8 mm dia), each connecting a pair of intermediate bars on opposite faces of the column.

**Justification:**
- Per **IS 456:2000 Cl. 26.5.3.2(c)**: "Every alternate longitudinal bar (or group of bars) shall be restrained by a lateral tie."
- The corner bars are restrained by the perimeter tie.
- The intermediate bars (which would otherwise buckle outward) are restrained by the cross-ties.
- In seismic zones (per IS 13920), **every** longitudinal bar must be restrained by a hoop leg or cross-tie at every level — this is satisfied here.

**Sketch (in description form):**

```
        o----------o          o = Longitudinal bar (20 mm dia)
        |          |          | = Perimeter tie (8 mm dia)
        |  +---o---+          + = Cross-tie (8 mm dia)
        |  |   |   |
        o--+---o---o
        |  |   |   |
        |  +---o---+
        |          |
        o----------o
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**A circular spiral column of 500 mm overall diameter is reinforced with 8 longitudinal bars of 20 mm diameter and a 10 mm dia. spiral. Concrete grade is M25 and steel is Fe415. Design the pitch of the spiral as per IS 456:2000. Take clear cover = 40 mm.**

#### (a) Compute the required volumetric ratio of spiral reinforcement. (7 marks)

**Step 1: Core diameter.**

$$
D_c = 500 - 2(40) - 10 = 410 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Gross and core areas.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_g &= \frac{\pi}{4}(500)^2 = 196{,}350 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_k &= \frac{\pi}{4}(410)^2 = 132{,}025 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Volumetric ratio from IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_s &= \frac{0.36 (A_g/A_k - 1) f_{ck}}{f_y} \\
\rho_s &= \frac{0.36 \times (1.4872 - 1) \times 25}{415} \\
\rho_s &= \frac{0.36 \times 0.4872 \times 25}{415} = 0.01057
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Determine the pitch of the spiral and verify against IS 456 limits. (7 marks)

**Step 1: Pitch from volumetric ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{4 A_{sp}}{D_c \cdot \rho_s} \\
A_{sp} &= \frac{\pi}{4}(10)^2 = 78.54 \text{ mm}^2 \\
s &= \frac{4 \times 78.54}{410 \times 0.01057} = \frac{314.16}{4.334} = 72.49 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply IS 456 limits.**

| Limit | Value | Computed Pitch | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s \leq 75$ mm | 75 mm | 72.49 mm | ✓ OK |
| $s \leq D_c/6$ | 68.33 mm | 72.49 mm | ✗ FAILS |
| $s \geq 25$ mm | 25 mm | 72.49 mm | ✓ OK |

**Governing value: $s = 65$ mm** (reduced to satisfy $D_c/6$ limit).

**∴ Provide 10 mm dia. spiral @ 65 mm pitch.**

**Additional check – Seismic detailing (if required per IS 13920):**
- In seismic zones, pitch at ends (over a length of $l_0$ from face of joint) shall be **$s/2 = 32$ mm** (rounded to 30 mm).
- Length of confinement zone: $l_0 \geq \text{greater of } (B, L/6, 450 \text{ mm})$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake 1 (2 marks lost):** Students often forget to apply the $D_c/6$ limit when designing spirals. This is the most frequent deduction.
> 2. **Common mistake 2 (2 marks lost):** Confusing **clear cover** with **effective cover**. The spiral diameter $D_c$ is measured **center-to-center** of the spiral bar, NOT outer-to-outer.
> 3. **Common mistake 3 (1 mark lost):** Not mentioning the role of spiral in **confinement** — students often only talk about "holding bars" without referencing the triaxial stress state.
> 4. **Common mistake 4 (3 marks lost in 14-mark questions):** Skipping the **volumetric ratio check** for spiral columns. IS 456 makes this MANDATORY, and the answer is incomplete without it.
> 5. **Common mistake 5 (1 mark lost):** In cross-tie questions, students draw ties but fail to **justify the cross-tie pattern** as per Cl. 26.5.3.2(c).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lateral Ties and Spirals**

### Definition & Purpose
- ⭐ Lateral ties and spirals are **transverse reinforcement** in RCC columns.
- ⭐ Their **four functions**: (1) prevent buckling of longitudinal bars, (2) confine concrete core, (3) resist shear, (4) maintain integrity under cyclic/seismic loads.

### Lateral Ties (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2)
- ⭐ Min. diameter: $\phi_{tie} \geq \phi_{l,max}/4$ **AND** $\phi_{tie} \geq 6$ mm.
- ⭐ Max. pitch: $s \leq \min(B, 16\phi_{l,min}, 300 \text{ mm})$.
- ⭐ Every **alternate** longitudinal bar (or bundle) must be laterally restrained.
- ⭐ Ties shall be **anchored** with 135° hooks in seismic zones (IS 13920).

### Spirals (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.3)
- ⭐ Min. diameter: **6 mm** (typical 8–12 mm).
- ⭐ Pitch: $25 \text{ mm} \leq s \leq \min(75 \text{ mm}, D_c/6)$.
- ⭐ Volumetric ratio: $\rho_s \geq \dfrac{0.36 (A_g/A_k - 1) f_{ck}}{f_y}$.
- ⭐ Spirals must be **continuous** from top to bottom of column with proper laps.

### Code References
- ⭐ **IS 456:2000** – General RCC design.
- ⭐ **IS 13920:2016** – Ductile seismic detailing.
- ⭐ **SP-16** – Design tables (for quick pitch/spacing selection).
- ⭐ **ACI 318** – For international / reference purposes (confinement pressure formula).

### Engineering Judgment
- ⭐ **Always round down pitch** to a practical value (50, 60, 65, 75 mm).
- ⭐ **Spirals** give better ductility and are preferred in **seismic zones** and **high-stress columns**.
- ⭐ **Tied columns** are simpler to construct and used in most ordinary buildings.
- ⭐ Failure to provide adequate lateral ties was the #1 cause of column failures in the **2001 Bhuj earthquake** — a real-world lesson in the importance of this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression Plus Bending in Rectangular Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compression Plus Bending in Rectangular Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Compression Plus Bending in Rectangular Columns** refers to the structural condition where a reinforced concrete (RCC) rectangular column is simultaneously subjected to:
1. An **axial compressive load (P)** acting along its longitudinal centroidal axis.
2. A **bending moment (M)** acting about one or both of its principal cross-sectional axes (uniaxial or biaxial bending).

This loading combination produces a **non-uniform linear stress distribution** across the cross-section, governed by the principle of **superposition** of direct compressive stress and flexural (bending) stress. Such columns are technically classified as **Beam-Columns** or **Eccentrically Loaded Columns** because the moment can be equivalently represented as an axial load applied at an **eccentricity** $e$ from the centroid, where $M = P \cdot e$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** As per IS 456:2000 (Clause 25.4), all compression members shall be designed for the **minimum eccentricity** $e_{min} = \dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$, regardless of whether a calculated moment exists. This ensures no column is treated as purely axially loaded.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Stool Standing on a Tilted Floor Analogy:**
Imagine a heavy wooden stool (representing the column) standing on a perfectly flat floor. If you push straight down on the seat (pure axial load), the stress distributes **uniformly** on all four legs. Now imagine the floor tilts slightly — the stool tends to topple, but you keep it from falling by pushing down diagonally. One pair of legs now carries **more load** than the other.

**In structural terms:**
- The **axial push** = axial compression $P$.
- The **tilt correction force** = the bending moment $M$.
- One face of the column experiences **higher compressive stress**, the opposite face experiences **lower compressive stress** (and possibly tension if the eccentricity is large).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples:**
> - Edge columns of a building frame subjected to gravity loads from beams framing into one side.
> - Columns supporting crane girders (horizontal thrust creates moment).
> - Columns in portal frames resisting lateral wind or seismic forces.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (IS 456:2000) |
|---|---|
| Partial Safety Factor for Concrete ($\gamma_m$) | **1.5** |
| Partial Safety Factor for Steel ($\gamma_s$) | **1.15** |
| Characteristic Strength $f_{ck}$ | **20, 25, 30, 35, 40 MPa** |
| Characteristic Strength $f_y$ | **500 MPa** (Fe500) |
| Min. Eccentricity $e_{min}$ | $\frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \geq 20$ mm |
| Max. Longitudinal Steel | **6%** of gross cross-section |
| Min. Longitudinal Steel | **0.8%** of gross cross-section |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Stress Equation (Elastic Analysis)

For a rectangular column of gross dimensions $b \times D$ subjected to axial load $P$ and uniaxial bending moment $M$ about the axis perpendicular to dimension $D$, the combined stress at any fiber is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M}{Z}$$

where:
- $A = b \cdot D$ = gross cross-sectional area
- $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot D^2}{6}$ = section modulus (elastic)
- $+$ sign = stress on the **more compressed face**
- $-$ sign = stress on the **less compressed (or tensile) face**

### Physical Interpretation of Stresses
1. **Direct compressive stress** $P/A$ is **uniformly distributed** across the section.
2. **Flexural stress** $M/Z$ varies **linearly** from a maximum compressive value on one edge to an equal-magnitude tensile value on the opposite edge.
3. The **superposition** yields a **trapezoidal** or **triangular** stress block depending on eccentricity.

---

## 2.2 The Concept of Kern (Core) of a Section

The **Kern** is the region within the cross-section where the application of an axial compressive load produces **no tensile stress** anywhere in the section.

For a **rectangular section** $b \times D$:
- Bending about the major axis (depth $D$): $\text{Kern limit} = \dfrac{D}{6}$
- Bending about the minor axis (width $b$): $\text{Kern limit} = \dfrac{b}{6}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Middle-Third Rule:** For a rectangular section, the Kern occupies the **middle third** of the depth. If eccentricity $e \leq D/6$, the entire section remains in compression. If $e > D/6$, tension develops and the uncracked elastic analysis becomes invalid (cracked section analysis or ultimate strength design is used).

---

## 2.3 IS 456 Design Philosophy for Combined Loading

The design of columns under compression plus bending follows the **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** method, not elastic working stress. The procedure is:

1. Calculate the **design axial load** $P_u = 1.5 \cdot P$ and design moment $M_u = 1.5 \cdot M$ (factored loads).
2. Compute **equivalent eccentricity** $e = \dfrac{M_u}{P_u}$.
3. If $e < e_{min}$, design as axially loaded column with $e = e_{min}$.
4. Otherwise, apply **SP-16 Design Charts** or the **interaction formula** approach.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} \pm \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Elastic combined stress (working stress method) |
| 2 | $e = \dfrac{M}{P}$ | Equivalent eccentricity from moment and axial load |
| 3 | $e_{min} = \dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$ | IS 456 minimum eccentricity |
| 4 | $P_u = 1.5 \cdot P$ and $M_u = 1.5 \cdot M$ | Factored (design) loads at ULS |
| 5 | $\text{Kern limit (rectangular)} = \dfrac{D}{6}$ | Middle-third boundary for no tension |
| 6 | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | Axial capacity of short column (pure compression, IS 456 Cl. 39.3) |
| 7 | $\dfrac{P_u}{P_{uz}} + \dfrac{M_u}{M_{uz}} \leq 1.0$ | Linear interaction check (approximate) |
| 8 | $p_g = \dfrac{A_{sc}}{bD}$ | Percentage of longitudinal steel |
| 9 | $A_{c,\text{required}} = \dfrac{P_u}{0.4 f_{ck} + 0.67 p_g (f_y - 0.4 f_{ck})}$ | Area required for given axial load |
| 10 | $\text{Min } p_g = 0.8\%$ (for columns) | IS 456 minimum steel requirement |
| 11 | $\text{Max } p_g = 6\%$ (gross section) | IS 456 maximum steel cap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoidance Tip:** In the markdown table above, the formula for equivalent eccentricity $e = M/P$ uses the division symbol instead of the vertical pipe $\vert$ to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The combined compression-bending analysis is essential for:

- **Building Frame Columns:** Subjected to gravity loads + wind/seismic moments.
- **Bridge Piers:** Combined axial load from superstructure + bending from eccentric live loads or wind.
- **Retaining Wall Stem Walls:** Axial load from surcharge + bending from earth pressure.
- **Industrial Columns:** Supporting cranes, conveyor systems, or asymmetric equipment.
- **Offshore Structures:** Subjected to wave-induced moments combined with self-weight.

The accurate prediction of stress distribution prevents **tension cracking**, **buckling**, and **progressive collapse** — critical for the **Safety in Design** philosophy emphasized in the PESFT754 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Elastic Combined Stress Equation

**Starting Point:** Consider a rectangular column cross-section $b \times D$.

### Step 1 — Direct Compressive Stress
When an axial compressive force $P$ is applied at the **geometric centroid (G)** of the section, the stress is uniformly distributed:

$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{b \cdot D}$$

This stress is **compressive** (negative) and **constant across the entire cross-section**.

### Step 2 — Pure Bending Stress
When a bending moment $M$ is applied about the centroidal axis, the stress varies **linearly**:

$$\sigma_{bending} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where:
- $y$ = distance from the neutral axis (centroid)
- $I = \dfrac{b \cdot D^3}{12}$ = second moment of area about the bending axis

The **maximum bending stress** at the extreme fiber ($y = D/2$) is:

$$\sigma_{bending,\max} = \frac{M \cdot (D/2)}{b D^3 / 12} = \frac{6M}{b D^2} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where the elastic section modulus is $Z = \dfrac{b D^2}{6}$.

### Step 3 — Superposition Principle
Since both stresses act simultaneously on the same elastic body, and assuming the material remains in the elastic range, the **principle of superposition** is valid:

$$\sigma_{combined} = \sigma_{axial} + \sigma_{bending}$$

For the **extreme compressive fiber** (where both stresses are compressive):

$$\sigma_{\max,comp} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{M}{Z}$$

For the **extreme tensile (or lesser-compressive) fiber** (where bending stress is tensile):

$$\sigma_{\min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{M}{Z}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Eccentricity Form
Since $M = P \cdot e$ for an eccentrically applied load at distance $e$ from the centroid:

$$\sigma_{\max,comp} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{D}\right)$$

$$\sigma_{\min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{D}\right)$$

### Step 5 — No-Tension Condition (Kern Derivation)
Setting $\sigma_{\min} \geq 0$ (i.e., no tension anywhere):

$$\frac{P}{A}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{D}\right) \geq 0$$

Since $P/A > 0$ (compression):

$$1 - \frac{6e}{D} \geq 0 \implies e \leq \frac{D}{6}$$

This proves the **middle-third rule**: the load must lie within the middle third of the cross-section (depth $D/3$) to avoid tension in an uncracked elastic rectangular section.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of IS 456 Interaction Formula (Simplified)

For a short tied column under combined axial load and uniaxial bending, IS 456 allows the following approximate interaction equation when eccentricity is small:

$$\frac{P_u}{P_{uz}} + \frac{M_u}{M_{uz,\text{balanced}}} \leq 1.0$$

### Step 1 — Pure Axial Capacity
For a short column with helical binding (or tied column), the ultimate axial capacity is:

$$P_{uz} = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$$

where:
- $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$ = net concrete area
- $A_g = b \cdot D$ = gross area
- $A_{sc}$ = total area of longitudinal steel

### Step 2 — Pure Flexural Capacity
The ultimate moment capacity (when the column is treated as a beam under pure flexure, with axial force = 0) is computed using standard flexural design equations:

$$M_{uz} = 0.87 f_y A_{st} \cdot d \cdot \left(1 - \frac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Interaction Check
For a given $P_u$ and $M_u$, the column is safe if:

$$\frac{P_u}{P_{uz}} \leq 1.0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{M_u}{M_{uz}} \leq 1.0$$

And the combined interaction satisfies the **axial-moment interaction curve** (typically obtained from **SP-16 design charts** for rectangular columns).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A short rectangular RCC column of size $300 \text{ mm} \times 400 \text{ mm}$ (b × D) is subjected to:
- Service axial load $P = 800 \text{ kN}$
- Service bending moment $M = 60 \text{ kN·m}$ (about the major axis)
- Concrete grade: M25 ($f_{ck} = 25 \text{ MPa}$)
- Steel grade: Fe500 ($f_y = 500 \text{ MPa}$)
- Unsupported length $L = 3.0 \text{ m}$

**Find:** Whether the stresses remain safe under working stress conditions.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate gross area:

$$A = b \cdot D = 300 \times 400 = 120000 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.12 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate section modulus:

$$Z = \frac{b \cdot D^2}{6} = \frac{300 \times 400^2}{6} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3 = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Calculate direct compressive stress:

$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{800 \times 10^3}{120000} = 6.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate maximum bending stress:

$$\sigma_{bending} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^6} = 7.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate combined stresses at extreme fibers:

$$\sigma_{\max,comp} = 6.667 + 7.5 = 14.167 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{\min} = 6.667 - 7.5 = -0.833 \text{ MPa (tension)}$$

**Step 6:** Check minimum eccentricity compliance:

$$e_{min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} = \frac{3000}{500} + \frac{400}{30} = 6 + 13.33 = 19.33 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt $e_{min} = 20 \text{ mm}$ (governing).

Actual eccentricity $e = \dfrac{M}{P} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6}{800 \times 10^3} = 75 \text{ mm} > e_{min}$. ✓ OK

**Step 7:** Check kern compliance:

$$\frac{D}{6} = \frac{400}{6} = 66.67 \text{ mm}$$

Since $e = 75 \text{ mm} > 66.67 \text{ mm}$, tension develops, confirming our Step 5 calculation. **Working stress method is invalid for tension evaluation; must use ULS design with SP-16 charts.**

**Step 8:** Apply partial safety factors for ULS:

$$P_u = 1.5 \times 800 = 1200 \text{ kN}$$

$$M_u = 1.5 \times 60 = 90 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 9:** Use SP-16 chart or iterative design to find $A_{sc}$. Final design would typically yield $A_{sc} \approx 1800 \text{ mm}^2$ (6 bars of 20 mm $\phi$), corresponding to $p_g \approx 1.5\%$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress Distribution Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["AXIAL LOAD P"]:::axial --> nodeB["Uniform Compressive Stress P/A"]
    nodeC["BENDING MOMENT M"]:::bending --> nodeD["Linear Flexural Stress M.Z / y"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["SUPERPOSITION PRINCIPLE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF{"ECCENTRICITY CHECK"}
    nodeF -- "e less than D/6" --> nodeG["TRAPEZOIDAL STRESS BLOCK\nFull Section in Compression"]
    nodeF -- "e equals D/6" --> nodeH["TRIANGULAR STRESS BLOCK\nZero Stress at One Edge"]
    nodeF -- "e greater than D/6" --> nodeI["TRAPEZOID WITH TENSION\nCracked Section Analysis Required"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["IS 456 ULS DESIGN\nUsing SP-16 Charts"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Final Design:\nLongitudinal Steel + Lateral Ties"]
    classDef axial fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bending fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Cross-Section Stress Profile Schematic (Text Representation)

```
   BENDING COMPRESSION SIDE (+M)
   ┌─────────────────────────┐
   │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │  ← σ_max = P/A + M/Z  (HIGH COMPRESSION)
   │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │
   │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │  ← Neutral Axis (σ = 0 if e = D/6)
   │ ▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒ │  ← σ_min = P/A - M/Z
   └─────────────────────────┘
   BENDING TENSION SIDE (-M)
   
   LEGEND:
   ▓ = Maximum compressive stress zone
   ░ = Neutral axis region (zero stress)
   ▒ = Tensile or reduced compressive zone
```

## 4.3 Design Decision Flowchart for Combined Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["STEP 1: Calculate Service Loads\nP and M"] --> startB["STEP 2: Apply Load Factors\nPu = 1.5P, Mu = 1.5M"]
    startB --> startC["STEP 3: Compute Eccentricity\ne = Mu / Pu"]
    startC --> startD{"e less than e_min?"}
    startD -- "Yes" --> startE["Use e = e_min\nDesign as Axially Loaded"]
    startD -- "No" --> startF["STEP 4: Apply SP-16 Charts\nfor Rectangular Column"]
    startF --> startG["STEP 5: Read Values of\nPu / (fck b D) and Mu / (fck b D squared)"]
    startG --> startH["STEP 6: Determine Required\nPercentage of Steel pg"]
    startH --> startI["STEP 7: Check pg between\n0.8 percent and 6 percent"]
    startI -- "Within limits" --> startJ["STEP 8: Design Lateral Ties\nIS 456 Clause 25.5"]
    startI -- "Exceeds 6 percent" --> startK["STEP 9: Increase Section Size\nand Re-design"]
    startJ --> endZ["SAFE DESIGN ACHIEVED"]
    startK --> startF
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef terminal fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class startA,startB,startC,startE,startF,startG,startH,startJ,startK process
    class endZ terminal
```

## 4.4 Kern of Rectangular Section (Schematic)

```
   KERN REGION (MIDDLE THIRD) - LOAD APPLIED HERE = NO TENSION
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                                             │
   │         ┌─────────────────┐                 │
   │         │    e ≤ D/6      │                 │
   │         │   ░░░░░░░░░░░   │ ← KERN CORE    │
   │         │   ░░░░░░░░░░░   │   (Middle 1/3) │
   │         └─────────────────┘                 │
   │                                             │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   
   Neutral Axis (N.A.) at centroid
```
