**Step 1 — Sample size per "set".** A set consists of 6 cubes, of which 3 are tested at 7 days and 3 at 28 days.

**Step 2 — Frequency rule (Clause 15.1.1 of IS 456).**

$$
N_{sets} = \max\left(\left\lceil \frac{V}{30} \right\rceil,\ 1 \text{ set per day}\right)
$$

where $V$ = total volume of concrete in m³, and $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling function.

**Step 3 — Acceptance over 4 consecutive samples.**

For any group of 4 consecutive samples, the mean strength must satisfy:

$$
\bar{f}_4 \geq f_{ck} + 4\ \text{N/mm}^2
$$

and *every* individual result must satisfy:

$$
f_i \geq f_{ck} - 3\ \text{N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Statistical origin (brief).** The 1.65 multiplier in the characteristic strength definition assumes a normal distribution of concrete strength with 5% defective probability. The 4 N/mm² allowance and -3 N/mm² individual tolerance in the 4-sample rule provide a moving-window check consistent with this probabilistic basis, balancing safety and economy.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Specification vs. Code vs. Drawing

| Dimension | **Code (e.g., IS 456)** | **Specification** | **Drawing** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | National body (BIS) | Designer / Owner / CPWD | Designer |
| **Nature** | Mandatory minimum | Prescriptive/Performance | Graphical |
| **Question answered** | *How strong must it be?* | *How must it be built?* | *What does it look like?* |
| **Conflict resolution** | Supremacy of code | Specification yields to code | Drawing yields to specification if conflicting |
| **Frequency of change** | Decades | Per project | Per revision |
| **Safety impact** | Defines the **target** safety | Defines the **process** safety | Defines the **geometric** safety |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Control Documents in Structural Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[National Building Code of India] --> B[IS Codes e.g. IS 456, IS 800]
    A --> C[CPWD Specifications]
    A --> D[MoRTH Specifications]
    B --> E[Project Specific Design Basis Report]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Detailed Structural Drawings]
    E --> G[Technical Specifications Document]
    F --> H[Construction Stage]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Quality Control and Testing]
    I --> J[As Built Documentation]
    J --> K[Handover and Life Cycle Safety]

    nodeA[National Building Code of India]
    nodeB[IS Codes IS 456 and IS 800]
    nodeC[CPWD Specifications]
    nodeD[MoRTH Specifications]
    nodeE[Project Design Basis Report]
    nodeF[Structural Drawings]
    nodeG[Technical Specifications]
    nodeH[Construction Stage]
    nodeI[Quality Control and Testing]
    nodeJ[As Built Documentation]
    nodeK[Handover and Lifecycle Safety]
```

### 4.2 Specification Document Structure (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SPEC[Technical Specification Document] --> GCC[General Conditions of Contract]
    SPEC --> SCC[Special Conditions of Contract]
    SPEC --> GTS[General Technical Specifications]
    SPEC --> DTS[Detailed Technical Specifications]
    SPEC --> AML[Approved Makes List]
    SPEC --> QAQC[QA QC and Testing Schedule]
    SPEC --> SAF[Safety and OHS Specifications]

    GTS --> MAT[Material Specifications]
    GTS --> WORK[Workmanship Specifications]
    GTS --> TOL[Tolerance Specifications]

    DTS --> EW[Earthwork Clause]
    DTS --> CONC[Concrete Clause]
    DTS --> STL[Steel Clause]
    DTS --> FIN[Finishes Clause]

    QAQC --> CT[Cube Test Schedule]
    QAQC --> ST[Slump Test]
    QAQC --> NDT[Non Destructive Testing]

    SAF --> PPE[Personal Protective Equipment]
    SAF --> SCAFF[Scaffolding Rules]
    SAF --> HW[Hot Work Permit]

    nodeSpec[Technical Specification Document]
    nodeGcc[General Conditions of Contract]
    nodeScc[Special Conditions of Contract]
    nodeGts[General Technical Specifications]
    nodeDts[Detailed Technical Specifications]
    nodeAml[Approved Makes List]
    nodeQaqc[QA QC and Testing Schedule]
    nodeSaf[Safety and OHS Specifications]
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic: Prescriptive vs. Performance Specification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Is the construction method mature and standardized?} -->|Yes| P[Prescriptive Specification]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the desired outcome clearly measurable?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| PERF[Performance Specification]
    Q2 -->|No| H[Hybrid Specification]

    P --> A1[Define how to build it]
    PERF --> A2[Define what to achieve]
    H --> A3[Define outcome plus critical method constraints]

    nodeQ1[Is the construction method mature and standardized]
    nodeQ2[Is the desired outcome clearly measurable]
    nodeP[Prescriptive Specification]
    nodePerf[Performance Specification]
    nodeH[Hybrid Specification]
    nodeA1[Define how to build it]
    nodeA2[Define what to achieve]
    nodeA3[Define outcome plus critical method constraints]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, May 2024]**
*Define the term "Specification" as used in structural engineering. How does it differ from a "Code"?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Specification** is a detailed written document that prescribes the quality, type, workmanship, and testing requirements for materials and construction processes in a project. It is project-specific and binding on the contractor.
A **Code** (e.g., IS 456), in contrast, is a national standard that sets minimum safety and design requirements applicable across all projects. The code defines *what* must be achieved, while the specification defines *how* it must be achieved. In the hierarchy of project documents, the code has supremacy over the specification.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Dec 2023]**
*What is a "Performance Specification"? Give one example from concrete construction.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Performance Specification** specifies the *desired end-result* of construction, leaving the contractor free to choose the method, materials mix, and procedure, provided the outcome meets the stated criteria. It encourages innovation and contractor expertise.
**Example:** Specifying that "Concrete shall achieve a characteristic compressive strength of 30 N/mm² at 28 days" without dictating the cement brand, mix proportion, or mixing time. Compliance is verified through cube tests and statistical acceptance per IS 456.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model 2024]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of specifications used in structural engineering. Discuss the relative advantages of **prescriptive** versus **performance** specifications with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the role of **Approved Makes List (AML)** in specifications. Discuss why material specifications are the *first line of safety defense* in structural construction. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification and Comparison (7 Marks):**
Specifications may be classified on four bases:
1. **By Scope:** General vs. Detailed specifications.
2. **By Method:** Prescriptive (Method) vs. Performance (End-Result).
3. **By Issuing Authority:** Standard (CPWD/MoRTH), Project-Specific, Manufacturer's.
4. **By Trade:** Civil, Electrical, Mechanical, Fire-fighting, etc.

Comparison Table:

| Aspect | Prescriptive Specification | Performance Specification |
|---|---|---|
| Defines | *How* to do the work | *What* result to achieve |
| Method freedom | Restricted to specified method | Free to choose |
| Innovation | Discouraged | Encouraged |
| Quality verification | Process audits | End-product testing |
| Risk of non-compliance | Low (if method followed) | Higher (outcome-based) |
| Best suited to | Conventional, well-known works | Complex or specialized works |
| Example | "Mix concrete for 3 minutes in drum mixer" | "Concrete must attain 30 N/mm² in 28 days" |

A **hybrid specification** combines both — it specifies performance outcomes *and* imposes method constraints on critical safety processes (e.g., pile foundations often need this approach).

**(b) Approved Makes List and Material Specifications (7 Marks):**
The **AML** is a vetted catalogue of proprietary products (cement brands, rebar manufacturers, admixture suppliers, waterproofing systems) that have been pre-approved by the owner/consultant for use on the project. Its role in safety includes:
- Preventing use of sub-standard or counterfeit materials.
- Ensuring consistency of quality across the project lifetime.
- Reducing approval delays at site.

Material specifications form the *first line of safety defense* because:
1. Even a perfectly designed structure fails if the materials used do not match the assumed properties (e.g., assumed $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm² but actual cement delivers only 20 N/mm²).
2. Material tests (cube tests, tensile tests on rebar) provide the empirical evidence that the designer's safety assumptions are realized in practice.
3. Material specifications are *verifiable* — they can be tested, sampled, and rejected — whereas workmanship is harder to reverse.

The chain **Code → Material Spec → Workmanship Spec → Test Spec** represents the layers of safety assurance in a typical Indian structural project.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model 2024, Alternative Set]**

**(a)** What is a **Specification Compliance Index (SCI)**? Derive the SCI for a concrete element with the following site data and interpret the result. *(7 Marks)*

> *Site data:* $f_{ck}$ = 25 N/mm²; Achieved $\bar{f}_{28}$ = 27.5 N/mm²; Specified slump = 75 mm; Achieved slump = 60 mm; Specified w/c = 0.50; Actual w/c = 0.55; Specified cover = 50 mm; Actual cover = 42 mm; Specified curing = 14 days; Actual curing = 10 days. *Use weights:* $w_c = 0.40$, $w_s = 0.10$, $w_w = 0.25$, $w_{cv} = 0.15$, $w_{cu} = 0.10$.

**(b)** Discuss the importance of **testing specifications** (cube tests, slump tests, NDT) in ensuring structural safety. How does IS 456 define the acceptance criterion for 4 consecutive cube samples? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of SCI (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — Compute individual ratios:

$$
R_c = \frac{27.5}{25} = 1.10, \quad R_s = \frac{60}{75} = 0.80
$$

$$
R_w = \frac{0.50}{0.55} = 0.909, \quad R_{cv} = \frac{42}{50} = 0.84, \quad R_{cu} = \frac{10}{14} = 0.714
$$

Step 2 — Apply weights and sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SCI &= (0.40)(1.10) + (0.10)(0.80) + (0.25)(0.909) + (0.15)(0.84) + (0.10)(0.714) \\
    &= 0.440 + 0.080 + 0.227 + 0.126 + 0.071 \\
    &= 0.944
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Apply acceptance rule:

- $SCI = 0.944 < 1.00$ → **Non-compliant on aggregate**.
- $R_{cu} = 0.714 < 0.85$ → **Weakest-link failure** (curing is severely deficient).
- $R_{cv} = 0.84 < 0.85$ → **Weakest-link failure** (cover is also inadequate).

**Interpretation:** The element must be **rejected / reworked**. The contractor should be instructed to (i) extend curing to the full 14 days, (ii) apply remedial cover enhancement (e.g., protective plaster / coating) since cover is non-replaceable without jacketing. A re-test after corrective action is mandatory.

**(b) Testing Specifications and IS 456 Acceptance (7 Marks):**

Testing specifications are the *empirical proof* of safety. They translate design intent into as-built reality and create a legally auditable quality trail. Key tests include:

- **Slump Test (IS 1199):** Verifies workability at the point of placement.
- **Cube Test (IS 516):** Verifies compressive strength at 7 and 28 days.
- **NDT (Rebound Hammer, UPV, Core Cutting — IS 13311):** Verifies in-situ strength and detects voids/honeycombs.
- **Cover Meter Survey (IS 14654):** Verifies rebar positioning and cover.
- **Half-Cell Potential (ASTM C876):** Assesses corrosion risk in RCC.

**IS 456 Acceptance Criterion (Clause 16.1):** For any group of **4 consecutive samples**, the acceptance is two-fold:

1. **Mean strength condition:** The average strength of the 4 samples must be:

$$
\bar{f}_4 \geq f_{ck} + 4\ \text{N/mm}^2
$$

2. **Individual strength condition:** Each individual sample must be:

$$
f_i \geq f_{ck} - 3\ \text{N/mm}^2
$$

Both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously. If either fails, the concrete represented by the samples is deemed non-conforming, and remedial action (or rejection of the affected pour) is required. The "+4 / -3" rule is calibrated to the same 1.65σ confidence assumed in the definition of $f_{ck}$ and ensures the 5% defective probability is respected in the moving average.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (KTU Examiner's Eye):**
> 1. **Conflating Specifications with Codes:** Students often write "IS 456 is a specification." It is a **Code**. Specifications are *project documents* that *cite* IS 456 as their basis. This single confusion has cost students up to 2 marks per question historically.
> 2. **Ignoring the "Weakest-Link" rule:** In the SCI question, students compute the aggregate index and forget to check the individual $R_i \geq 0.85$ condition. Always apply **both** checks.
> 3. **Wrong formula notation:** Writing $f_{ck}$ as $f^{'}_c$ (ACI notation) in an IS-based answer. KTU expects IS 456 notation strictly.
> 4. **No units in the final answer:** Always write N/mm² (not MPa without conversion) and kg/m³ (not kg) for compliance.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention curing duration and method:** In concrete safety questions, omitting curing (a specification parameter) is a frequent deduction trigger.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Specification** = written, project-specific, prescriptive/performance document. **Code** = national, mandatory-minimum standard.
- Specifications are **contractually binding**, codes are **statutorily binding**.
- Two main types: **Prescriptive (Method)** and **Performance (End-Result)** — hybrid is common in critical works.
- Standard issuing bodies in India: **CPWD, MoRTH, BIS, Indian Railways**.
- **AML (Approved Makes List)** prevents use of sub-standard proprietary products.
- **Material Specs** = first line of safety defense.
- **Workmanship Specs** ensure on-site execution matches design assumptions.
- **Testing Specs** = cube (IS 516), slump (IS 1199), NDT (IS 13311), cover meter.
- **SCI (Specification Compliance Index)** aggregates compliance; $SCI \geq 1.00$ AND every $R_i \geq 0.85$.
- **IS 456 acceptance:** $\bar{f}_4 \geq f_{ck} + 4$ **AND** $f_i \geq f_{ck} - 3$ (N/mm²).
- **Cover tolerance:** $\pm 10$ mm generally; cover is the most violated specification on Indian sites.
- **Curing duration:** 7 days (OPC), 10–14 days (blended cement) per IS 456 — curing is *cheapest* but *most skipped* safety activity.
- Document hierarchy: **NBC → IS Code → Project Spec → Drawing → Construction**.
- Conflict resolution rule: **Code > Specification > Drawing** (in case of conflict).
- Frequency of cube testing: 1 set per 30 m³ **or** 1 set per day, whichever is more.
- Performance specs are *innovation-friendly*; Prescriptive specs are *process-safe*.
- Specifications are the *only* document that gives the contractor a *method* to follow — code alone is silent on *how*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structural Fasteners: Types of Riveted and Bolted Joints
Strength of a Riveted Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strength of a Riveted Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔩 Strength of a Riveted Joint — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **riveted joint** is a permanent, non-detachable mechanical fastening used to join two or more structural members (plates, angles, or rolled sections) by inserting a ductile metal pin called a **rivet** through pre-punched or drilled holes, and then deforming the protruding tail end (called the manufactured head) on the opposite side to form a second head, thereby clamping the plates together by residual clamping force and bearing pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard**
> According to the KTU 2024 Scheme for *PESFT754 – Safety in Design of Structures*, the **strength of a riveted joint** is defined as the **least of the following four ultimate failure loads** that the joint can sustain before failure:
> 1. Tearing of the plate between rivet holes
> 2. Shearing of the rivets
> 3. Bearing (crushing) of the rivet on the plate hole
> 4. Tearing of the plate at the edge (edge shear / edge failure)

The **"Strength of a Riveted Joint"** is therefore a comparative study of the load-carrying capacity of the joint against each of these failure modes, with the joint's true strength being governed by the **weakest mode (minimum of all)**.

## 1.2 Types of Riveted Joints (KTU Syllabus Classification)

| Joint Type | Description | Load Path |
|------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Lap Joint (Single Shear)** | Plates overlap; rivet in single shear | Eccentric → Bending moment |
| **Butt Joint with Single Cover Plate** | Plates butt against each other; covered by one strap; rivets in single shear | More concentric |
| **Butt Joint with Double Cover Plate** | Plates butt against each other; covered by two straps; rivets in double shear | Most concentric & strongest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **double cover plate butt joint**, because of symmetry, there is **no eccentric load**; the rivets are in **double shear**, and hence the joint has the **highest efficiency**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bookmark" Effect

Imagine you have **two sheets of paper** that you want to glue together. You punch holes along the seam and pass thick thread through them, knotting at the back — this knot prevents the paper from pulling apart, much like the manufactured head of a rivet. Now, imagine pulling the two papers apart:

- If the **thread snaps** → that is **rivet shear failure**.
- If the **paper around the hole tears** → that is **plate tearing failure**.
- If the **paper is crushed at the hole edge** → that is **bearing (crushing) failure**.

The **strength of the joint** is simply how hard you can pull before **any one** of these three things happens first. The earliest failure governs the design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rivet vs. Bolt:** A **rivet** is installed hot (hot riveting) and is permanent. A **bolt** is tightened cold with a nut and is detachable. The strength analysis, however, is **identical** for both.

## 1.4 Standard Notations (To Be Used Throughout)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|--------|----------|--------------------|
| $d$ | Nominal diameter of rivet | mm |
| $t$ | Thickness of plate (thinner plate in case of unequal plates) | mm |
| $p$ | Pitch of rivets (centre-to-centre spacing along load direction) | mm |
| $\sigma_t$ | Permissible / ultimate tensile stress of plate material | N/mm² (MPa) |
| $\tau$ | Permissible / ultimate shear stress of rivet material | N/mm² (MPa) |
| $\sigma_b$ | Permissible / ultimate bearing/crushing stress of rivet (or plate, whichever is lower) | N/mm² (MPa) |
| $n$ | Number of rivets per pitch length | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Failure Modes — Logic Behind Each

### Mode 1: Tearing of the Plate Between Rivet Holes (Tensile Failure)

When a tensile load $P$ is applied to the joint, the plate material **between two consecutive rivet holes** is in pure tension. The net resisting cross-section is reduced because of the holes.

- The gross width of plate considered = pitch $p$
- Material removed by hole = diameter $d$
- **Net resisting width** $= (p - d)$
- **Net resisting area** $= (p - d) \times t$

> The force required to tear the plate across this net section is the **tearing strength**.

### Mode 2: Shearing of the Rivet (Shear Failure)

When the plate tries to slide, the rivet acts as a shear pin across the joint plane.

- Cross-sectional area of one rivet $= \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$
- If there are $n$ rivets per pitch length, total shear area $= n \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$
- In a **single shear** (lap joint), this area is fully engaged.
- In a **double shear** (butt joint with double straps), the area is **doubled** because the rivet is sheared at two planes.

### Mode 3: Bearing / Crushing Failure of the Rivet on the Plate

The rivet presses against the **cylindrical surface** of the hole wall. This contact area, called the **projected bearing area**, is:
$$A_b = d \times t$$

- For $n$ rivets, total projected bearing area $= n \times d \times t$

> The sheet may fail by **tearing outwards** (called **shear-out** or **edge shear**) if the edge distance is insufficient, but in the standard KTU analysis, bearing is governed by the projected area $d \cdot t$.

### Mode 4: Edge Shear / Tearing at the Edge

If the rivet is too close to the edge of the plate, the plate material between the hole and the edge may shear out. The KTU empirical rule mandates a minimum edge distance of **$1.5d$ to $2d$** to prevent this.

## 2.2 ⚡ KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These four formulas are the absolute core of any KTU question on this topic. Memorize them with their variables and units.**

| # | Failure Mode | Formula (per pitch length) | Variable Meaning |
|---|--------------|---------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Tearing strength of plate** | $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Net area × tensile stress |
| 2 | **Shearing strength of rivet (single shear)** | $P_s = n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$ | Rivet area × shear stress |
| 3 | **Shearing strength of rivet (double shear)** | $P_{s,2} = 2n \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$ | Twice the single-shear capacity |
| 4 | **Bearing strength of rivet** | $P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ | Projected area × bearing stress |
| 5 | **Strength of joint** | $P_{joint} = \min(P_t,\ P_s,\ P_b)$ | The lowest governs |
| 6 | **Strength of solid (un-riveted) plate** | $P_{solid} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ | Gross area × tensile stress |
| 7 | **Efficiency of joint** $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} \times 100\ \%$ | Always $\le 100\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL: Table Formatting Rule** — For absolute-value or conditional terms in any formula (e.g., $\vert \sigma_t \vert$), use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

## 2.3 Efficiency of a Riveted Joint — The Designer's Metric

The **efficiency** $\eta$ is the **ratio of the strength of the jointed plate to the strength of an un-riveted (solid) plate of the same dimensions**, expressed as a percentage. It is the **most asked concept** in KTU examinations on this topic.

$$ \eta = \frac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} = \frac{\min(P_t,\,P_s,\,P_b)}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\ \% $$

> A well-designed lap joint typically has $\eta \approx 60$–$70\%$, while a double-strap butt joint can reach $\eta \approx 80$–$90\%$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure vessels and boilers**: Riveted seams were historically used in railway boilers; design efficiency governed by these very formulas.
- **Bridge girders and truss members**: Older IBR (Indian Boiler Regulation) and IS:800 (now IS:800:2007 — bolted/welded) code revisions stemmed from these classical analyses.
- **Aircraft fuselage (historical)**: Aluminum-alloy riveted joints are still analysed using the same shearing-tearing-bearing triad.
- **Modern relevance**: Although welding and high-strength bolts have largely replaced riveting, **the failure-mode analysis is identical and forms the basis of bolted joint design** taught in Machine Design (KTU ME streams).

## 2.5 Design Philosophy — Why We Use a "Factor of Safety"

In ultimate-strength analysis (used in KTU 2024 Scheme problems unless specified), we use the **ultimate stresses** $\sigma_{tu}, \tau_u, \sigma_{bu}$ and then divide by a **Factor of Safety (FoS)** — typically **2.0 to 2.5** — to obtain permissible stresses. The joint must satisfy:

$$ P_{applied} \le \frac{P_{joint,\,ultimate}}{FoS} $$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tearing Strength of the Plate (Per Pitch Length)

**Setup:** Consider two adjacent rivets separated by pitch $p$ along a plate of thickness $t$. A tensile load $P_t$ acts along the plate's length.

**Step 1 — Identify the critical cross-section.**
The plate is weakened at the location of the rivet hole. The critical section is the **line passing through the rivet hole centre**, perpendicular to the load.

**Step 2 — Compute the net resisting width.**
At this section, the full width $p$ is reduced by the hole diameter $d$.
$$ \text{Net width} = p - d $$

**Step 3 — Compute the net resisting area.**
$$ A_{net} = (p - d) \times t $$

**Step 4 — Apply the basic stress equation.**
Using the relationship $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P_t}{A_{net}}$, solve for the tearing load:
$$ P_t = A_{net} \times \sigma_t $$

Substituting:
$$ \boxed{P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} $$

> **Logic note:** $p$ appears because the resisting area per pitch length is what governs the load transferred per unit pitch.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shearing Strength of Rivet (Single Shear)

**Setup:** A single rivet passes through a lap joint; the upper plate tends to slide over the lower plate, shearing the rivet **at one cross-section** (single plane).

**Step 1 — Identify shear plane area.**
The rivet is circular, so its cross-sectional area is:
$$ A_{rivet} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} $$

**Step 2 — Account for multiple rivets per pitch.**
If $n$ rivets are arranged in a row along one pitch, total shear-resisting area:
$$ A_{total} = n \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} $$

**Step 3 — Apply the shear equation.**
$$ P_s = A_{total} \times \tau $$

Substituting:
$$ \boxed{P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau} $$

**For double shear** (double-strap butt joint), the rivet is sheared across **two planes**, so:
$$ \boxed{P_{s,2} = 2n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bearing Strength of the Rivet

**Setup:** When the rivet is loaded, the cylindrical surface of the rivet presses against the curved surface of the hole. The **projected area** onto the failure plane is a rectangle of dimensions $d \times t$.

**Step 1 — Define the projected bearing area per rivet.**
$$ A_{b,\text{ per rivet}} = d \times t $$

**Step 2 — Total bearing area for $n$ rivets.**
$$ A_{b,\text{ total}} = n \cdot d \cdot t $$

**Step 3 — Apply the bearing stress equation.**
$$ P_b = A_{b,\text{ total}} \times \sigma_b $$

Substituting:
$$ \boxed{P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b} $$

> **Why the "projected" area?** When a cylinder presses on a plate, the actual contact is along a curve, but the failure mechanism effectively uses the projection $d \cdot t$ — this is an **empirical simplification** universally adopted in KTU/IS codes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Efficiency Formula

**Step 1 — Strength of the un-riveted (solid) plate of the same dimensions.**
The gross resisting area is $p \cdot t$, so:
$$ P_{solid} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t $$

**Step 2 — Strength of the actual riveted joint.**
$$ P_{joint} = \min(P_t,\, P_s,\, P_b) $$

**Step 3 — Define efficiency as the ratio.**
By definition:
$$ \eta = \frac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} $$

Substituting the formulas:
$$ \eta = \frac{\min\!\left[(p-d)\,t\,\sigma_t,\;\; n\,\tfrac{\pi}{4}\,d^{2}\,\tau,\;\; n\,d\,t\,\sigma_b\right]}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\ \% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The efficiency can never exceed 100 %. It is, by construction, less than 1.** This is a fundamental physical constraint — punching holes always weakens the plate.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Worked Example (Full Derivation)

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> A single-riveted lap joint connects two steel plates each of thickness $t = 12$ mm. The rivet diameter is $d = 24$ mm, pitch $p = 60$ mm. Permissible stresses are: $\sigma_t = 120$ MPa, $\tau = 90$ MPa, $\sigma_b = 150$ MPa. One rivet per pitch ($n=1$). Find the strength and efficiency.

**Step 1 — Tearing strength of plate.**
$$ P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t $$

$$ P_t = (60 - 24) \times 12 \times 120 $$

$$ P_t = 36 \times 12 \times 120 $$

$$ P_t = 432 \times 120 $$

$$ P_t = 51\,840\ \text{N} = 51.84\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 2 — Shearing strength of rivet (single shear).**
$$ P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau $$

$$ P_s = 1 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (24)^2 \times 90 $$

$$ P_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 576 \times 90 $$

$$ P_s = 452.39 \times 90 $$

$$ P_s = 40\,715.04\ \text{N} \approx 40.72\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 3 — Bearing strength of rivet.**
$$ P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b $$

$$ P_b = 1 \times 24 \times 12 \times 150 $$

$$ P_b = 288 \times 150 $$

$$ P_b = 43\,200\ \text{N} = 43.20\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 4 — Strength of joint (minimum).**
$$ P_{joint} = \min(51.84,\ 40.72,\ 43.20)\ \text{kN} $$

$$ \boxed{P_{joint} = 40.72\ \text{kN}\ \text{(governed by rivet shear)}} $$

**Step 5 — Strength of solid plate.**
$$ P_{solid} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t $$

$$ P_{solid} = 60 \times 12 \times 120 $$

$$ P_{solid} = 86\,400\ \text{N} = 86.40\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 6 — Efficiency.**
$$ \eta = \frac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} \times 100\ \% $$

$$ \eta = \frac{40.72}{86.40} \times 100\ \% $$

$$ \boxed{\eta \approx 47.13\ \%} $$

> **Interpretation:** Only **47 %** of the parent plate's strength is being utilized by this joint — a typical value for a poorly designed single-riveted lap joint. A **double-strap butt joint** would have raised this to **~75–80 %**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Failure Mode Decision Flow

> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision tree** an engineer follows when computing joint strength. It replaces the physical drawing of a riveted plate (which is impractical to render in Mermaid syntax) with a high-density functional flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Riveted Joint Geometry Given] --> B[Identify Joint Type]
    B --> B1[Lap Joint - Single Shear]
    B --> B2[Butt Joint Single Strap - Single Shear]
    B --> B3[Butt Joint Double Strap - Double Shear]

    B1 --> C[Compute Four Failure Loads]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C

    C --> D1[Pt equals p minus d times t times sigma_t]
    C --> D2[Ps equals n times pi by 4 times d squared times tau]
    C --> D3[Pb equals n times d times t times sigma_b]
    C --> D4[Check Edge Shear - min edge distance 1.5d]

    D1 --> E[Take Minimum of Pt Ps Pb]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F{Ps less than Pt AND Ps less than Pb?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Strength governed by Rivet Shear]
    F -- No --> H{Compare Pt and Pb}

    H -- Pt smaller --> I[Strength governed by Plate Tearing]
    H -- Pb smaller --> J[Strength governed by Bearing]

    G --> K[Compute Efficiency: Pjoint over Psolid times 100]
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L[End: Joint Designed]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Strength Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lap Joint Single Shear] -->|eta approx 60 to 70 percent| B[Joint Strength]
    C[Butt Joint Single Strap] -->|eta approx 65 to 75 percent| B
    D[Butt Joint Double Strap] -->|eta approx 80 to 90 percent| B
    B --> E{Select Joint with Minimum Material for Required Strength}
```

## 4.3 Physical Layout Schematic (Text-Based)

For a single-riveted **lap joint**, the rivet is denoted by `O` and the load $P$ by arrows:

```
  P  →  ┌──────●──────●──────●──────┐   ← Upper plate (thickness t)
         │      O      O      O      │       Rivets in single shear
  P  →  └──────●──────●──────●──────┘   ← Lower plate
                ↑      ↑      ↑
              pitch  pitch  pitch
                ←─── p ──→
```

For a **double-strap butt joint**, the rivets are sheared in **two planes** (double shear):

```
  P  →  ┌──────●──────●──────●──────┐   ← Upper plate
         │      O      O      O      │   ← Upper strap
  P  →  ┌══════════════════════════┐   ← Joint line
         │      O      O      O      │   ← Lower strap
  P  →  └──────●──────●──────●──────┘   ← Lower plate
```

> Each rivet now has **two shear planes** — hence $P_{s,2} = 2 P_s$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1] [CO3, BTL: Remember]**
> **Q1. List the four possible failure modes of a riveted joint.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four failure modes of a riveted joint are:
1. **Tearing of the plate** between consecutive rivet holes along the line of least resistance (tensile failure).
2. **Shearing of the rivet** across the joint plane (single or double shear depending on joint type).
3. **Bearing / crushing failure** of the rivet on the plate hole wall (projected-area compression).
4. **Edge shear / tearing of plate at the edge** if edge distance is insufficient.

The joint strength is governed by the **minimum** of these four loads.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2] [CO3, BTL: Understand]**
> **Q2. Define the efficiency of a riveted joint. What is its typical range for a double-strap butt joint?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Efficiency** $\eta$ of a riveted joint is defined as the ratio of the **strength of the riveted joint** to the **strength of an un-riveted (solid) plate** of the same dimensions, expressed as a percentage:

$$ \eta = \frac{P_{joint}}{P_{solid}} \times 100\ \% $$

For a **double-strap butt joint**, efficiency typically ranges between **80 % and 90 %**, which is why it is the preferred configuration for high-strength applications such as pressure vessels and bridge girders.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Q3A] [CO3, BTL: Apply / Analyse]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expressions for **tearing strength of plate**, **shearing strength of rivet**, and **bearing strength of rivet** in a single-riveted lap joint. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-riveted lap joint connects two mild steel plates of thickness 16 mm. The rivet diameter is 28 mm, pitch is 70 mm. The ultimate stresses are: $\sigma_{tu} = 420$ MPa, $\tau_u = 320$ MPa, $\sigma_{bu} = 560$ MPa. Number of rivets per pitch $n = 1$. Find the **ultimate strength** of the joint and its **efficiency**.

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Assumptions:**
- The load is uniformly distributed among all rivets of a pitch.
- The rivet fills the hole completely (no slip).
- The plate is stressed uniformly in tension across the net section.
- Bearing stress is assumed uniform over the **projected area** $d \cdot t$.
- Shear failure of the rivet occurs on a plane perpendicular to the load.

**Derivation 1 — Tearing Strength of Plate $P_t$** (2 marks):
The net resisting area at the rivet hole = $(p - d) \cdot t$. The load required to tear the plate is:
$$ P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t $$

**Derivation 2 — Shearing Strength of Rivet $P_s$** (2.5 marks):
Each rivet has shear area $\frac{\pi}{4}d^2$. For $n$ rivets per pitch, total shear area is $n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$. The shear load is:
$$ P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau $$

**Derivation 3 — Bearing Strength of Rivet $P_b$** (2.5 marks):
The projected bearing area per rivet = $d \cdot t$. For $n$ rivets, total bearing area = $n \cdot d \cdot t$. The bearing load is:
$$ P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b $$

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $t = 16$ mm, $d = 28$ mm, $p = 70$ mm, $n = 1$, $\sigma_{tu} = 420$ MPa, $\tau_u = 320$ MPa, $\sigma_{bu} = 560$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Tearing strength $P_t$** (2 marks):
$$ P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{tu} $$
$$ P_t = (70 - 28) \times 16 \times 420 $$
$$ P_t = 42 \times 16 \times 420 = 282\,240\ \text{N} = 282.24\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 2 — Shearing strength $P_s$** (2 marks):
$$ P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau_u $$
$$ P_s = 1 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 28^2 \times 320 $$
$$ P_s = 0.7854 \times 784 \times 320 = 196\,940\ \text{N} \approx 196.94\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 3 — Bearing strength $P_b$** (2 marks):
$$ P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{bu} $$
$$ P_b = 1 \times 28 \times 16 \times 560 $$
$$ P_b = 448 \times 560 = 250\,880\ \text{N} = 250.88\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 4 — Strength of joint = minimum** (0.5 mark):
$$ P_{joint} = \min(282.24,\ 196.94,\ 250.88)\ \text{kN} = \mathbf{196.94\ \text{kN}} \quad \text{(governed by rivet shear)} $$

**Step 5 — Efficiency** (0.5 mark):
$$ P_{solid} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{tu} = 70 \times 16 \times 420 = 470\,400\ \text{N} = 470.4\ \text{kN} $$
$$ \eta = \frac{196.94}{470.4} \times 100\ \% = \mathbf{41.87\ \%} $$

> **Final Answer:** Joint strength = **196.94 kN**, Efficiency = **41.87 %**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Q3B — Internal Choice] [CO3, BTL: Apply / Analyse]**

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **three types of riveted joints**. Compare their efficiencies. Why is a double-strap butt joint preferred for high-pressure applications?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A double-riveted double-strap butt joint is used to connect two plates of thickness 18 mm. Rivet diameter $d = 25$ mm, pitch $p = 80$ mm, with **2 rivets per pitch** arranged in **zig-zag (chain) staggering**. The ultimate stresses are: $\sigma_{tu} = 410$ MPa, $\tau_u = 300$ MPa, $\sigma_{bu} = 540$ MPa. Determine the **ultimate strength of the joint** and the **efficiency**.

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Type 1 — Lap Joint (Single Shear)** (2 marks):
The plates overlap each other. The rivet is sheared at a single plane. There is an **eccentric load path**, which induces a bending moment in addition to shear. Efficiency is the lowest (~60–70%).

**Type 2 — Butt Joint with Single Cover Strap (Single Shear)** (2 marks):
The two main plates butt against each other and are joined by a single cover strap. The rivet is still in single shear, but the load path is more concentric. Efficiency is moderate (~65–75%).

**Type 3 — Butt Joint with Double Cover Strap (Double Shear)** (2.5 marks):
The butt joint is covered by **two straps** (one on each side). Each rivet is sheared at **two planes**, doubling the shear capacity. The load path is **fully concentric**, eliminating the bending moment. Efficiency is the highest (~80–90%).

**Why preferred for high-pressure applications** (0.5 mark):
A double-strap butt joint has (i) **double shear** rivets (higher shear capacity), (ii) **concentric loading** (no bending moment), and (iii) **higher efficiency**, making it ideal for pressure vessels, boilers, and bridge trusses.

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $t = 18$ mm, $d = 25$ mm, $p = 80$ mm, $n = 2$ rivets per pitch, double-strap (double shear), $\sigma_{tu} = 410$ MPa, $\tau_u = 300$ MPa, $\sigma_{bu} = 540$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Tearing strength $P_t$** (2 marks):
$$ P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{tu} = (80 - 25) \times 18 \times 410 = 55 \times 18 \times 410 = 405\,900\ \text{N} = 405.9\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 2 — Shearing strength in double shear $P_{s,2}$** (2.5 marks):
$$ P_{s,2} = 2n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot \tau_u = 2 \times 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 25^2 \times 300 $$
$$ P_{s,2} = 4 \times 0.7854 \times 625 \times 300 = 589.05 \times 300 = 176\,715\ \text{N} \approx 176.72\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 3 — Bearing strength $P_b$** (2 marks):
$$ P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{bu} = 2 \times 25 \times 18 \times 540 = 900 \times 540 = 486\,000\ \text{N} = 486.0\ \text{kN} $$

**Step 4 — Strength of joint = minimum** (0.5 mark):
$$ P_{joint} = \min(405.9,\ 176.72,\ 486.0)\ \text{kN} = \mathbf{176.72\ \text{kN}} \quad \text{(governed by double shear)} $$

> Note that **double shear** (even with $n=2$ rivets) gives less than **single shear with $n=4$** — this is because in a double-shear butt joint, each rivet has only half the load per shear plane; the formula correctly doubles the area. The student should be **careful with the factor of 2**.

> **Final Answer:** Joint strength = **176.72 kN**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in double shear.** In a double-strap butt joint, the rivet is sheared at **two planes**. Students often write $P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$ and lose **2–3 marks** instantly.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong $t$ for bearing.** When two unequal plates are joined, use the **thinner plate's thickness** for the bearing area $d \cdot t$, since the thinner plate yields first.
>
> 3. **Forgetting that $P_{solid} = p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ (gross area)** for efficiency calculation. Students often use $(p - d) \cdot t$ and end up with $\eta > 100\%$, which is **physically impossible** and immediately marked wrong.
>
> 4. **Ignoring edge distance.** If the problem specifies an edge distance less than **$1.5d$**, you must also check the **edge shear** failure mode, which has its own formula.
>
> 5. **Unit errors.** Always express the final answer in **kN or N** — mixing mm with metres is a common arithmetic blunder.
>
> 6. **Selecting the WRONG minimum.** Always compare $P_t$, $P_s$, $P_b$ explicitly before declaring the minimum. Examiners deduct a full mark for skipping the comparison.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Bullet-Point Memory Aid)**

- ✅ A **riveted joint's strength** is the **minimum of three ultimate loads**: $P_t$ (tensile tearing), $P_s$ (rivet shear), $P_b$ (bearing).
- ✅ **Tearing:** $P_t = (p - d) \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t$ — net area × tensile stress.
- ✅ **Shearing (single):** $P_s = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$ — rivet area × shear stress.
- ✅ **Shearing (double):** $P_{s,2} = 2n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \tau$ — **multiply by 2 for double-strap butt joints**.
- ✅ **Bearing:** $P_b = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$ — **projected area** $d \cdot t$, not $\frac{\pi d^2}{4}$.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{joint}}{p \cdot t \cdot \sigma_t} \times 100\ \%$ — uses **gross area** in denominator.
- ✅ Typical efficiencies: **Lap joint ≈ 60–70 %**; **Butt single-strap ≈ 65–75 %**; **Butt double-strap ≈ 80–90 %**.
- ✅ **Edge distance** must be $\ge 1.5d$ to $2d$ to prevent edge shear.
- ✅ For **unequal plates** in bearing, use the **thinner plate thickness** $t$.
- ✅ **Failure of joint is always the LEAST of the four failure loads** — never average them, never take the maximum.
- ✅ **Factor of Safety (FoS)** = 2.0 to 2.5 for static loads; divide ultimate load by FoS to get the **safe working load**.
- ✅ The KTU 2024 Scheme tests this topic at **CO3 (Apply) / BTL-3** level, often as a 14-mark Part-B problem with sub-parts (a) derivation + (b) numerical.
- ✅ **Uniaxial Euler column, eccentric loading, and riveted joint strength** together form the **"core triad"** of Module 3 in PESFT754.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Riveted Joints for Axially Loaded Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Riveted Joints for Axially Loaded Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **riveted joint** is a *permanent, non-detachable* mechanical fastening arrangement in which hot- or cold-driven cylindrical shanks (called **rivets**) are passed through pre-punched or drilled holes in the members to be joined, and the protruding tail end is deformed plastically (upset) to form a *second head*, thereby locking the plates together through the combined action of **shear, bearing, and friction** between the rivet shank and the hole wall.

For **axially loaded members**, the principal force acts along the centroidal axis of the connected plates, producing uniform tensile stress across the net cross-section. The design problem, therefore, reduces to ensuring that *under the working load* — and after applying the **Factor of Safety (FoS)** stipulated in IS 800:2007 (general construction in steel) — **no failure mode** of the joint is violated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Design of riveted joints for axially loaded members is treated as a *deterministic strength-of-materials problem* working with permissible (working) stresses, since the course objectives emphasize **safety concepts** rather than LRFD limit-state calibration.

### 1.2 The Three Governing Strengths of a Riveted Joint

For any rivet connecting two plates loaded in tension, three independent strength criteria must be satisfied simultaneously:

| Criterion | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|------------------|
| **Shear Strength of Rivet (P_s)** | Resistance to the rivet being cut (sheared) across its cross-section by the relative sliding of the plates. |
| **Bearing Strength of Rivet (P_b)** | Resistance to the rivet shank crushing (bearing) against the hole wall on the loaded side. |
| **Tearing Strength of Plate (P_t)** | Resistance of the plate metal between successive rivet holes (or between hole and edge) to tensile rupture. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have two thick cardboard strips that you want to join end-to-end so that they can pull against each other (think of a paper-bridge for a school project). You punch matching holes and push paper fasteners (or split pins) through them. The strip of cardboard *between* two holes is the weakest spot — if you pull too hard, it tears there first. The fastener itself can also *snap across* its cross-section, or it can *crush the surrounding paper*. A riveted steel joint works exactly the same way, but with hot-driven steel rivets instead of paper fasteners.

The job of the design engineer is to choose:
- The **diameter (d)** of the rivet,
- The **pitch (p)** — the centre-to-centre spacing of rivets in the direction of load,
- The **gauge (g)** — the transverse spacing between rows,
- The **edge distance (e)** — the distance from the rivet centre to the plate edge,

such that **all three of the failure-mode strengths are ≥ the design force**, with an acceptable margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Philosophy Connection:** A *safe* riveted joint is not merely one that is "strong" — it is one in which the **probabilities of all possible failure modes are simultaneously acceptably low** under the factored load. This multi-mode safety check is the conceptual seed of modern *limit-state design* in IS 800:2007.

### 1.4 Classification of Riveted Joints (Quick Reference)

- **Lap Joint** — plates overlap; rivets in **single shear**.
- **Butt Joint with Single Cover Plate** — plates butt against each other, covered by one splice plate; rivets on each side in **single shear**.
- **Butt Joint with Double Cover Plates** — two splice plates (one each side); rivets in **double shear**, doubling the shear capacity per rivet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions Underlying Classical Rivet Design (Working-Stress Method)

For *axially loaded* tension members, the classical analysis assumes:

1. The tensile load is **uniformly distributed** among all the rivets connecting the member at a given section.
2. The tensile stress is **uniformly distributed** across the *net* area of the plate (gross area minus rivet-hole area).
3. **No combined stresses** (no bending, torsion, or eccentric load path) are considered.
4. The **rivet hole is considered as a pre-existing hole** with diameter $d_h = d + \text{clearance} \approx d + 1.5\,\text{mm}$ for design; for KTU board problems, **d_h = d + 1.5 mm** is conventionally used, unless otherwise stated.
5. Stress concentration around the hole is **neglected** (a known approximation).

### 2.2 The Three Governing Strengths — Detailed Mechanics

#### (a) Shearing of the Rivet (P_s)

When two plates slide relative to each other, the rivet cross-section resists with shear stress $\tau_v$.

**Single Shear (Lap Joint):**

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2 \,\tau_v$$

**Double Shear (Butt Joint with Double Cover Plates):**

$$P_s = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2 \,\tau_v = \frac{\pi}{2}\,d^2 \,\tau_v$$

#### (b) Crushing / Bearing of the Rivet Hole (P_b)

The projected area of the rivet on the plate surface resists bearing:

$$P_b = d \times t \times \sigma_b$$

where $t$ is the **thickness of the thinner plate** (or the *sum* of cover plate thicknesses, whichever is smaller relative to main plate thickness in butt joints), and $\sigma_b$ is the **permissible bearing stress**.

#### (c) Tearing of the Plate between Rivets (P_t)

Along a zig-zag or straight fracture line through the holes, the resisting area is the *net* plate area:

$$P_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t$$

For a zig-zag line (more critical), with chain of $n$ rivets at transverse gauge $g$:

$$P_t = \left(p\,n - d\,n + \sum \text{transverse deductions}\right)\,t\,\sigma_t$$

For a *straight* line through $n$ rivets in a row:

$$P_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t \quad \text{(per pitch length)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas** — they account for > 80% of the marks in any KTU Part B question on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit Convention |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Shear strength of one rivet (single) | $P_s = (\pi/4)\,d^2\,\tau_v$ | N (with d in mm, τ in MPa) |
| 2 | Shear strength of one rivet (double) | $P_s = (\pi/2)\,d^2\,\tau_v$ | N |
| 3 | Bearing strength of one rivet | $P_b = d\,t\,\sigma_b$ | N |
| 4 | Tearing strength per pitch (straight) | $P_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t$ | N |
| 5 | Tearing strength (zig-zag, per pitch) | $P_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t + \text{gauge terms}$ | N |
| 6 | Strength of joint | $P = \min(P_{s,\text{total}},\;P_{b,\text{total}},\;P_{t,\text{min}})$ | N |
| 7 | Strength of plate (gross) | $P_g = b\,t\,\sigma_t$ | N |
| 8 | **Efficiency of joint** | $\eta = \dfrac{P}{P_g}\times 100\,\%$ | % |
| 9 | Unwin's formula for pitch | $p = 0.286\,d + 6\,t\,\text{ (in mm)}$ | mm |
| 10 | Min. pitch (IS 800) | $p_{\min} = 2.5\,d$ | mm |
| 11 | Max. pitch (tension member) | $p_{\max} = \min(32\,t,\,300\,\text{mm})$ | mm |
| 12 | Min. edge distance | $e_{\min} = 1.5\,d_h$ (or as per IS 800 Table) | mm |
| 13 | Rivet hole diameter | $d_h = d + 1.5\,\text{mm}$ (board convention) | mm |
| 14 | Rivet value (strength of one rivet) | $R_v = \min(P_s,\;P_b)$ | N |
| 15 | Number of rivets per pitch length | $n = \dfrac{P_g - P_t}{R_v}$ (when governing tear) | — |

**Standard Permissible Stresses (typically used in KTU problems):**

| Stress | Symbol | Typical Value (Mild Steel) |
|--------|--------|----------------------------|
| Tensile stress in plate | $\sigma_t$ | **150 MPa** |
| Shear stress in rivet | $\tau_v$ | **100 MPa** |
| Bearing stress on rivet/plate | $\sigma_b$ | **300 MPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> If a specific problem statement provides *different* permissible stresses, those always override the values above. Always quote the problem-given values in the answer script.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although *high-strength bolted connections* (friction-grip bolts of grades 8.8, 10.9) and *welded connections* have largely displaced rivets in modern structural steelwork (post-1960s), riveted connections are still:

- **Mandated by certain codes** for railway bridges, overhead cranes, and historical monument restoration (because rivets fail in a *ductile, gradual* manner — a safety virtue).
- **Essential for hot-metal applications** (boilers, pressure vessels, forgework) where field welding is impractical.
- **Pedagogically critical** — they are the *cleanest* vehicle for teaching the multi-mode safety check that underpins modern limit-state philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Strength of a Riveted Lap Joint (n Rivets in a Row)

**Given:** Plate width $b$, thickness $t$, rivet diameter $d$, pitch $p$ (constant), single shear.

**Step 1 — Strength Against Tearing of Plate (per pitch length).**

The plate tears along two lines of rivets. Net width resisting tension = $b - n_1 d_h$, where $n_1$ is the number of rivet holes on the critical section. For a *single chain* of $n$ rivets in one row, per pitch length only one hole is deducted:

$$P_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t$$

> **Logic:** The gross cross-section at a pitch length carries force $p\,t\,\sigma_t$, but a hole of diameter $d$ removes area $d\,t$, leaving $(p-d)\,t$ of effective net material.

**Step 2 — Total Tearing Strength of the Joint (across full width).**

If there are $m$ rows at gauge $g$, and a *straight fracture* path goes through all rows:

$$P_{t,\text{total}} = (b - m\,d)\,t\,\sigma_t$$

If a *zig-zag* path is more critical (gauge-wise it is shorter but deducts the same number of holes):

$$P_{t,\text{zz}} = (b - m\,d)\,t\,\sigma_t - \sum (g - d)\,t\,\sigma_t / \text{(number of offsets)}$$

For KTU problems with **single chain (one row)**, this reduces to:

$$P_{t,\text{total}} = (b - d)\,t\,\sigma_t$$

**Step 3 — Strength Against Shearing of Rivets.**

With $n$ rivets per pitch length, all sharing the load equally:

$$P_{s,\text{total}} = n \times \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\tau_v$$

**Step 4 — Strength Against Bearing.**

$$P_{b,\text{total}} = n \times d \times t \times \sigma_b$$

**Step 5 — Joint Strength.**

$$P = \min(P_{t,\text{total}},\; P_{s,\text{total}},\; P_{b,\text{total}})$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P}{P_g} \times 100\,\% \quad \text{where} \quad P_g = b\,t\,\sigma_t$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Board Style (Full Solution)

**Problem Statement:** A lap joint is used to connect two mild-steel plates, each **80 mm wide** and **12 mm thick**, using **rivets of 20 mm diameter** at a uniform pitch of **50 mm** in a single row. The permissible stresses are $\sigma_t = 150$ MPa, $\tau_v = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_b = 300$ MPa. Determine: (a) the strength of the joint per pitch length, and (b) the efficiency of the joint.

#### (a) Strength of the Joint Per Pitch Length

**Step 1 — Tearing strength per pitch length:**

$$P_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t$$

Substituting $p = 50$ mm, $d = 20$ mm, $t = 12$ mm, $\sigma_t = 150$ MPa:

$$P_t = (50 - 20)\times 12 \times 150$$

$$P_t = 30 \times 12 \times 150$$

$$P_t = 54{,}000\,\text{N} = 54\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Shear strength of one rivet (single shear):**

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\tau_v = \frac{\pi}{4}\times (20)^2 \times 100$$

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4}\times 400 \times 100 = 31{,}416\,\text{N} \approx 31.42\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Bearing strength of one rivet:**

$$P_b = d\,t\,\sigma_b = 20\times 12\times 300 = 72{,}000\,\text{N} = 72\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Rivet value (strength of one rivet):**

$$R_v = \min(P_s,\; P_b) = \min(31.42,\; 72) = 31.42\,\text{kN}$$

> **Logic:** The rivet is *stronger in bearing* than in shear, so shear governs.

**Step 5 — Strength per pitch length (governed by tearing, since 1 rivet transfers 31.42 kN and tearing can carry 54 kN):**

$$P = \min(P_t,\; n R_v) = \min(54,\; 31.42) = 31.42\,\text{kN}$$

> **Logic:** **Shearing of the rivet is the critical failure mode** — the joint fails by the rivet snapping before the plate tears.

#### (b) Efficiency of the Joint

**Step 6 — Gross strength of plate per pitch length:**

$$P_g = b\,t\,\sigma_t$$

But efficiency is defined for the **full plate width**, so per pitch length the gross resisting force is proportional to $b$:

$$P_g = 80 \times 12 \times 150 = 1{,}44{,}000\,\text{N} = 144\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P}{P_g}\times 100 = \frac{31.42}{144}\times 100$$

$$\eta = 21.82\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only ~22% of the parent metal's capacity is utilized — a typical riveted joint is *highly inefficient*. This is precisely why engineers move to welded or high-strength bolted connections.

### 3.3 Design of Pitch — Analytical Matrix

**Given:** A tension member of width $b$ and thickness $t$ carrying design load $P$, with rivet diameter $d$ and permissible stresses $\sigma_t, \tau_v, \sigma_b$. Find the **pitch $p$** such that all three failure modes have *equal* safety (i.e., the joint is *balanced* — highest efficiency).

**Condition 1 — Equate Tearing and Shear (per pitch length, with $n$ rivets):**

$$(p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t = n \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\tau_v$$

Solving for $p$:

$$p = \frac{n\,\pi\,d^2\,\tau_v}{4\,t\,\sigma_t} + d$$

**Condition 2 — Equate Tearing and Bearing:**

$$(p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t = n \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_b$$

Solving for $p$:

$$p = \frac{n\,d\,\sigma_b}{\sigma_t} + d$$

**Condition 3 — Code-Defined Limits on Pitch:**

$$p_{\min} = 2.5\,d, \quad p_{\max} = \min(32\,t,\; 300\,\text{mm}) \quad \text{(in tension)}$$

The **design pitch** is the largest value among the three conditions that is *consistent with* the code limits. This is the key **analytical trade-off** that KTU examiners test.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Pitch Design

**Problem:** A tension member $100\,\text{mm} \times 10\,\text{mm}$ carries $P = 90$ kN. Design the pitch of 16 mm diameter rivets, with $\sigma_t = 150$ MPa, $\tau_v = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_b = 300$ MPa. Use single chain, single shear.

**Step 1 — Strength of one rivet in shear:**

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4}\times 16^2 \times 100 = 20{,}106\,\text{N} \approx 20.11\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Strength of one rivet in bearing:**

$$P_b = 16\times 10\times 300 = 48{,}000\,\text{N} = 48\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Rivet value:**

$$R_v = 20.11\,\text{kN (shear governs)}$$

**Step 4 — Number of rivets required:**

$$n = \frac{P}{R_v} = \frac{90}{20.11} \approx 4.48 \;\Rightarrow\; 5\,\text{rivets (round up)}$$

**Step 5 — Pitch from Tearing Equating Shear (using $n = 1$ for per-pitch design):**

$$p = \frac{1\cdot\pi\cdot 16^2\cdot 100}{4\cdot 10\cdot 150} + 16 = \frac{25{,}133}{6000} + 16$$

$$p = 4.19 + 16 = 20.19\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Pitch from Tearing Equating Bearing:**

$$p = \frac{1\times 16\times 300}{150} + 16 = 32 + 16 = 48\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 7 — Apply Code Limits:**

$$p_{\min} = 2.5\times 16 = 40\,\text{mm}, \quad p_{\max} = \min(320,\;300) = 300\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 8 — Adopt Maximum Permissible Pitch (highest efficiency):**

$$\boxed{p = 48\,\text{mm (governed by bearing-tearing equality, capped by } p_{\min})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Even though the tearing-shear equality gave a smaller pitch (20 mm), it violates $p_{\min} = 40$ mm. The *code-mandated* minimum controls. This is a classic KTU trap — always check code limits.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid is used to render *functional block diagrams* that capture the analytical structure of riveted-joint design and the failure-mode hierarchy. Physical cross-sectional sketches of rivets are intentionally substituted with structured analytical blocks for clarity and to remain within the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguards.

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Riveted Joints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Riveted Joints]
    nodeB[Lap Joint]
    nodeC[Butt Joint]
    nodeB1[Single Shear]
    nodeB2[Load Eccentric Causes Bending]
    nodeC1[Single Cover Plate]
    nodeC2[Double Cover Plates]
    nodeC1A[Single Shear]
    nodeC2A[Double Shear Double Capacity]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeB1
    nodeB --> nodeB2
    nodeC --> nodeC1
    nodeC --> nodeC2
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1A
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Failure Mode Hierarchy & Strength Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Joint Subjected to Axial Load P]
    nodeT[Tearing of Plate]
    nodeS[Shearing of Rivet]
    nodeB[Bearing on Rivet Hole]
    nodeA1[Compute Pt per pitch length]
    nodeA2[Compute Ps per rivet]
    nodeA3[Compute Pb per rivet]
    nodeV[Rivet Value Rv equals min of Ps and Pb]
    nodeN[Number of Rivets n equals P divided by Rv]
    nodePJ[Joint Strength P equals min of Pt and n times Rv]
    nodeEff[Efficiency eta equals P divided by Pg times 100 percent]
    nodeStart --> nodeT
    nodeStart --> nodeS
    nodeStart --> nodeB
    nodeT --> nodeA1
    nodeS --> nodeA2
    nodeB --> nodeA3
    nodeA2 --> nodeV
    nodeA3 --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeN
    nodeA1 --> nodePJ
    nodeN --> nodePJ
    nodePJ --> nodeEff
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Design of Pitch — Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeD[Given P, b, t, d, sigmaT, tauV, sigmaB]
    nodeD1[Compute Ps per rivet]
    nodeD2[Compute Pb per rivet]
    nodeD3[Rivet value Rv equals min of Ps Pb]
    nodeD4[Number of rivets n equals P divided by Rv]
    nodeD5[Pitch from tearing equals shear]
    nodeD6[Pitch from tearing equals bearing]
    nodeD7[Apply code limits pmin and pmax]
    nodeD8[Adopt maximum permissible pitch]
    nodeD --> nodeD1
    nodeD --> nodeD2
    nodeD1 --> nodeD3
    nodeD2 --> nodeD3
    nodeD3 --> nodeD4
    nodeD4 --> nodeD5
    nodeD4 --> nodeD6
    nodeD5 --> nodeD7
    nodeD6 --> nodeD7
    nodeD7 --> nodeD8
```

### 4.4 Schematic Layout of a Riveted Lap Joint (Analytical View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Main Plate Left]
    nodeP2[Main Plate Right]
    nodeR1[Rivet 1 at pitch p]
    nodeR2[Rivet 2 at pitch p]
    nodeR3[Rivet 3 at pitch p]
    nodeEd[Edge distance e]
    nodeP1 --> nodeR1
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3
    nodeR3 --> nodeP2
    nodeP1 --> nodeEd
```

**Caption:** Schematic of a single-chain lap joint showing edge distance $e$, uniform pitch $p$, and three consecutive rivets transmitting axial force through shear across the lap interface.

### 4.5 Failure Cross-Sections (Analytical Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeF1[Failure Mode 1: Shear of Rivet]
    nodeF2[Failure Mode 2: Bearing on Hole Wall]
    nodeF3[Failure Mode 3: Tearing of Plate]
    nodeF4[Section across rivet diameter]
    nodeF5[Projected area d times t]
    nodeF6[Section through rivet hole net area]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF4
    nodeF2 --> nodeF5
    nodeF3 --> nodeF6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "efficiency of a riveted joint" and state its mathematical expression. Why is a high efficiency desirable from a safety-economy standpoint?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **efficiency of a riveted joint** is defined as the ratio of the *strength of the joint* to the *strength of the solid (un-riveted) plate* of the same dimensions, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{joint}}}{P_{\text{solid plate}}} \times 100\,\%$$

A high efficiency means the joint utilizes a greater proportion of the parent metal's capacity. From a **safety-economy standpoint**, a high-efficiency joint carries a larger fraction of the design load for the same amount of steel, reducing material wastage while still maintaining the required factor of safety on the critical failure mode.

*[Examiner note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for safety-economy interpretation.]*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List the three modes of failure of a riveted joint subjected to axial tension, and write the expression for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Failure Mode | Expression |
|---|--------------|------------|
| 1 | Shearing of the rivet (single shear) | $P_s = (\pi/4)\,d^2\,\tau_v$ |
| 2 | Crushing / Bearing of rivet on hole wall | $P_b = d\,t\,\sigma_b$ |
| 3 | Tearing of the plate between rivet holes | $P_t = (p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t$ |

*[Examiner note: 1 mark for each correctly listed failure mode with formula. Naming without formula gets 0.5 per item.]*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A mild-steel tie-bar $120\,\text{mm}$ wide and $16\,\text{mm}$ thick is to be connected to a gusset plate using a lap joint with **20 mm diameter rivets** at a uniform pitch in a single chain. The permissible stresses are: $\sigma_t = 150$ MPa, $\tau_v = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_b = 300$ MPa. The bar is required to carry an axial pull of **180 kN**.

**(a)** Determine the *number of rivets required* and the *pitch* of the rivets.
**(b)** Determine the *efficiency* of the joint so designed.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Number of Rivets and Pitch (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Shear strength of one rivet (single shear):**

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2\,\tau_v = \frac{\pi}{4}\times (20)^2 \times 100 = 31{,}416\,\text{N} \approx 31.42\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Bearing strength of one rivet:**

$$P_b = d\,t\,\sigma_b = 20 \times 16 \times 300 = 96{,}000\,\text{N} = 96\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Rivet value:**

$$R_v = \min(31.42,\; 96) = 31.42\,\text{kN (shear governs)}$$

**Step 4 — Number of rivets:**

$$n = \frac{P}{R_v} = \frac{180}{31.42} = 5.73 \;\Rightarrow\; 6\,\text{rivets}$$

**Step 5 — Pitch (equating tearing to shear, per pitch length, $n=1$):**

$$(p - d)\,t\,\sigma_t = R_v \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (p - 20)\times 16\times 150 = 31{,}420$$

$$p - 20 = \frac{31{,}420}{2400} = 13.09 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p = 33.09\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Code limits:**

$$p_{\min} = 2.5\,d = 50\,\text{mm} \quad \text{(controls)}$$

$$p_{\max} = \min(32\,t,\, 300) = \min(512,\;300) = 300\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 7 — Adopt:**

$$\boxed{p = 50\,\text{mm}}$$

> **Logic:** The derived pitch (33 mm) violates $p_{\min}$, so the code limit governs.

**(b) Efficiency of the Joint (7 marks)**

**Step 8 — Total tearing strength of plate across full width (1 row of 6 rivets):**

$$P_t = (b - d)\,t\,\sigma_t = (120 - 20)\times 16\times 150 = 240{,}000\,\text{N} = 240\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 9 — Total rivet shear strength:**

$$P_s = 6 \times 31.42 = 188.5\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 10 — Joint strength:**

$$P = \min(P_t,\; nR_v) = \min(240,\; 188.5) = 188.5\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 11 — Gross strength of solid plate:**

$$P_g = b\,t\,\sigma_t = 120\times 16\times 150 = 288{,}000\,\text{N} = 288\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 12 — Efficiency:**

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{188.5}{288}\times 100 = 65.45\,\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently use $d_h = d + 1.5$ mm (= 21.5 mm) for *all* hole-related deductions, but for **tearing-strength** calculations, the convention in KTU board problems is to use **$d$** (the *nominal* rivet diameter) unless the problem explicitly states to use $d_h$. Always quote your assumption in the first line of the answer. Mismatched $d$ vs $d_h$ can swing the answer by 5–8%.

---

**Question B (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

Two mild-steel plates each **100 mm wide** and **10 mm thick** are connected by a **butt joint with double cover plates**, using **18 mm diameter rivets** in double shear at a uniform pitch in **two rows** (zig-zag arrangement). The gauge between rows is **40 mm**. Permissible stresses: $\sigma_t = 150$ MPa, $\tau_v = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_b = 300$ MPa.

**(a)** Compute the *strength of the joint per pitch length* along the critical fracture line.
**(b)** Determine the *efficiency of the joint* and comment on whether the design satisfies the IS 800 requirement of minimum efficiency.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Strength Per Pitch Length (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Shear strength of one rivet (double shear):**

$$P_s = \frac{\pi}{2}\,d^2\,\tau_v = \frac{\pi}{2}\times (18)^2 \times 100 = 50{,}894\,\text{N} \approx 50.89\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Bearing strength of one rivet:**

$$P_b = d\,t\,\sigma_b = 18\times 10\times 300 = 54{,}000\,\text{N} = 54\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Rivet value:**

$$R_v = \min(50.89,\; 54) = 50.89\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Critical fracture line: zig-zag through two rows.**

In one pitch length, two rivet holes are deducted. Net effective width for tearing:

$$b_{\text{net}} = b - 2d + \frac{(g - d)^2}{4g} \quad \text{(zig-zag empirical form)}$$

$$b_{\text{net}} = 100 - 2(18) + \frac{(40 - 18)^2}{4\times 40} = 100 - 36 + \frac{484}{160} = 64 + 3.025 = 67.025\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Tearing strength:**

$$P_t = b_{\text{net}}\,t\,\sigma_t = 67.025 \times 10 \times 150 = 100{,}538\,\text{N} \approx 100.54\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 6 — Two rivets per pitch length:**

$$nR_v = 2 \times 50.89 = 101.78\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 7 — Strength per pitch length:**

$$P = \min(P_t,\; nR_v) = \min(100.54,\; 101.78) = 100.54\,\text{kN}$$

> **Logic:** Tearing just barely governs — the joint is *almost balanced* (high efficiency expected).

**(b) Efficiency and Code Check (7 marks)**

**Step 8 — Gross strength:**

$$P_g = b\,t\,\sigma_t = 100\times 10\times 150 = 150{,}000\,\text{N} = 150\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 9 — Efficiency:**

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{100.54}{150}\times 100 = 67.03\,\%}$$

**Step 10 — IS 800 Comment:**

IS 800:2007 (clause 10.4.3) recommends that the *design efficiency* of a connection be **not less than 60%** for primary tension members. The computed 67% satisfies this requirement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In a **double-cover-plate butt joint**, students often forget to *double* the shear capacity and end up computing only single-shear strength — losing 7 marks instantly. Also, for *bearing*, always use the *thinner* of (main plate) vs (sum of cover plates). In symmetric double-cover design, cover plates together are *thicker* than main plate, so **main plate thickness governs**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- **Three failure modes** — *always check all three*; the joint strength is the **minimum** of the three, not the maximum.
- **Rivet value** $R_v = \min(P_s, P_b)$ — the strength of *one rivet*; the number of rivets = $P/R_v$ rounded **up** to the next integer.
- **Tearing strength** uses **net area** — deduct rivet-hole diameter at each critical cross-section. For zig-zag fracture lines, add the gauge-offset terms.
- **Pitch limits** — $p_{\min} = 2.5\,d$, $p_{\max} = \min(32\,t,\; 300\,\text{mm})$ in tension. The *adopted* pitch is the **largest** value that satisfies all conditions (highest efficiency), but never below $p_{\min}$ or above $p_{\max}$.
- **Double shear = double shear capacity**, but **bearing is unchanged** because it depends on the plate thickness, not the shear planes.
- **Efficiency is a fraction**, not the absolute load — always compute against the *gross* strength of the *solid* plate.
- **Always state the assumption** for hole diameter ($d$ vs $d_h = d + 1.5$ mm) on the first line of your answer.
- **Work in consistent units** — typically N and mm, giving stresses in MPa (which is N/mm²).
- **Show all three computations** ($P_s, P_b, P_t$) even if one obviously governs — *partial marking* in KTU rewards each correct sub-calculation.
- **Safety philosophy link** — riveted joints are *deterministic, working-stress* design, but the **multi-mode check** is the *conceptual ancestor* of modern limit-state philosophy (each "permissible stress" = yield/ultimate divided by a partial FoS).
- **Unwin's empirical formula** $p = 0.286 d + 6 t$ (in mm) is a quick *initial estimate*; always verify with the analytical derivation above.
- **Edge distance** $e_{\min} = 1.5 d_h$ — used to prevent the plate from tearing towards its free edge; commonly omitted in KTU single-row problems but essential for multi-row design.
- **Memorize the standard permissible stresses** (150 / 100 / 300 MPa for $\sigma_t / \tau_v / \sigma_b$); they are the *default* unless a problem overrides them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welded Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Welded Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **welded joint** is a permanent, non-boltable, non-detachable fusion connection between two or more structural steel members, achieved by localized melting of the base metal (parent metal) with or without the addition of a filler material, such that the atoms of the joined parts coalesce to form a continuous monolithic metallic bond. In the context of structural safety (PESFT754), welded joints are governed by the dual requirements of **(a) Strength Adequacy** — the weld metal and heat-affected zone (HAZ) must transfer the applied load without exceeding the design strength — and **(b) Ductility & Toughness** — the joint must deform plastically under overload and resist brittle fracture at low temperatures or under dynamic loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PESFT754 – Module 3):**
> Welded joints form the *third* major class of structural connections (after riveted and bolted joints). Design must satisfy **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel – Limit State Method)** with reference to **IS 816:1969** (welding procedure) and **IS 2062:2011** (parent metal grade).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine two pieces of chocolate being melted together at their touching edge. The molten chocolate solidifies into one continuous piece — you can no longer see where one chocolate ended and the other began. A **weld is exactly this** in steel: an electric arc (or gas flame) melts the edges of the plates together, and the molten metal fuses into a single unified member.

- **Butt weld** = "Glue two chocolate pieces edge-to-edge and let them merge into one slab" → carries full tension/compression across the joint.
- **Fillet weld** = "Melt a triangular bead of chocolate in the corner between two pieces" → like a triangular glue fillet, transfers load via **shear** along the throat.
- **Plug/Slot weld** = "Pour chocolate into a hole cut in one piece resting on another" → used mainly to prevent lap joint buckling or as a sealant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** In *bolted/riveted joints* the load is transferred by **bearing and shear of fasteners**. In *welded joints* the load is transferred by **shear of the weld throat** (fillet welds) or by **direct tension/compression in the parent metal** (butt welds). This is the conceptual pivot students often miss in KTU exams.

### 1.3 Classification of Welded Joints (Syllabus Mandatory)

| Class | Sub-Type | Load Transfer Mechanism | Typical Use |
|-------|----------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **Butt Weld** | Square, V, U, J, X, K-prep | Direct tension/compression or shear | Column splices, tension members |
| **Fillet Weld** | Transverse, Longitudinal, Inclined, End/return | Shear through throat | Lap joints, T-joints, bracket connections |
| **Plug / Slot Weld** | Circular hole / elongated slot | Shear in the plug or surface | Stiffeners, sealing lap joints |
| **Edge / Seam Weld** | — | Shear along edge | Sheet metal, cold-formed sections |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Filler metal density:** ≈ **7850 kg/m³** (≈ structural steel).
- **Weld metal ultimate tensile strength (E250 electrodes):** $f_{u,\text{electrode}} \geq \mathbf{410 \text{ MPa}}$.
- **Reduction factor for long welds (IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.5.7.1):** $\beta_{l,w} = \mathbf{1.2 - 0.2 L_w \le 1.0}$ where $L_w$ is in metres, applicable when $L_w > 150\,t_t$.
- **Effective length of fillet weld** = actual length − **2 × (weld size)** to account for end craters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fillet Weld Geometry — The "Right Triangle" of Design

A fillet weld is idealized as a **45° isosceles right triangle** in cross-section (when both plates are of equal thickness and the weld is equilateral).

Let:
- $s$ = **leg size** of the weld (mm)
- $t_t$ = **effective throat thickness** (mm)
- $l_w$ = **effective length** of weld (mm)

For an **equilateral fillet weld** (legs at 90° to each other and at 45° to the direction of the load):

$$
t_t = 0.707 \, s
$$

This is the **shortest distance** from the weld root (intersection of the two parent surfaces) to the weld face (hypotenuse). It is the **failure plane** along which the weld is assumed to shear off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 0.707?** Geometry: in a right isosceles triangle, hypotenuse $= s\sqrt{2}$, and the altitude to the hypotenuse $= s/\sqrt{2} = s \times 0.7071$.

### 2.2 Types of Stresses in Fillet Welds (KTU Repeated Concept)

1. **Shear stress (q):** Acts on the throat plane, parallel to the load. For a fillet weld aligned parallel to the load, this is the **only stress**.
2. **Direct / Axial stress ($f_a$):** When the line of action of the load is **perpendicular** to the weld length (transverse fillet weld), the throat plane is inclined at 45° to the load, producing:
   - Normal stress on throat: $\sigma_w = \dfrac{P \sin 45°}{t_t \cdot l_w} = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, t_t \, l_w}$
   - Shear stress on throat: $\tau_w = \dfrac{P \cos 45°}{t_t \cdot l_w} = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, t_t \, l_w}$
3. **Combined stress (f):** A vector sum applicable to **inclined fillet welds**:

$$
f = \sqrt{f_a^{\,2} + q^{\,2}}
$$

This combined stress must satisfy:

$$
f \le f_{wd}
$$

where $f_{wd}$ = **design strength of the fillet weld** (MPa).

### 2.3 Design Strength of Fillet Weld (IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.5.7)

The **design strength of a fillet weld** per unit length is the **smaller** of:

**(a) Shear capacity of weld metal:**

$$
f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mw}} \cdot t_t
$$

**(b) Shear capacity of base (parent) metal:**

$$
f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_{u,\text{base}}}{\sqrt{3} \, \gamma_{mb}} \cdot t_t
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Value (Typical) |
|--------|---------|------------------|
| $f_{uw}$ | Ultimate tensile strength of weld electrode | **410 MPa** (E250) |
| $f_{u,\text{base}}$ | Ultimate tensile strength of parent metal | **410 MPa** (E250) |
| $\gamma_{mw}$ | Partial safety factor for weld metal | **1.25** (shop) / **1.50** (field) |
| $\gamma_{mb}$ | Partial safety factor for base metal | **1.25** |
| $t_t$ | Effective throat thickness | $0.707\,s$ mm |

Substituting typical values for E250 grade, shop welding:

$$
f_{wd} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} \cdot 0.707\,s = 0.342\,f_{uw} \cdot s \approx 140\,s \text{ (N/mm per mm leg size)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory trick for KTU:** Strength per mm leg size per mm length ≈ **0.146 × f_u** in shop welding. So a **6 mm fillet weld** of E250 grade carries ≈ **360 N/mm of length**.

### 2.4 Minimum and Maximum Size of Fillet Welds (IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.5.3 & 10.5.4)

- **Minimum size ($s_{\min}$):**

$$
s_{\min} = 3 \text{ mm} \quad \text{for } t \le 10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
s_{\min} = 5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{for } 10 < t \le 20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
s_{\min} = 6 \text{ mm} \quad \text{for } 20 < t \le 32 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
s_{\min} = \dfrac{t - 1}{2} \text{ for } t > 32 \text{ mm, rounded up to next higher even mm}
$$

- **Maximum size ($s_{\max}$):**
  - Along edge of plate ≤ 6 mm thick: $s_{\max} = t$
