  - Along edge of plate > 6 mm thick: $s_{\max} = t - 1.5$ mm

### 2.5 Effective Length of Fillet Weld

$$
l_{w,\text{eff}} = L_{\text{actual}} - 2s
$$

(Subtract 2 × weld size to remove the defective start/end crater regions.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Clause check (Cl. 10.5.7.1):** If $l_{w,\text{eff}} \le 100\,s$, no reduction. If $l_{w,\text{eff}} > 150\,t_t$, apply long-weld reduction factor $\beta_{l,w}$.

### 2.6 Strength of Butt Welds (IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.5.8)

A butt weld is assumed to have the **same strength as the parent metal** when:
- The weld metal has matching or over-matching strength,
- The weld is fully penetrated, and
- Both surfaces are ground flush or tested by NDT.

Design strength of butt weld:

$$
T_{db} = \dfrac{f_y \, t \, l_w}{\gamma_{mb}} \quad \text{(for compression)}
$$

$$
T_{db} = \dfrac{f_u \, t \, l_w}{\gamma_{mb}} \quad \text{(for tension)}
$$

If incomplete penetration, only **effective throat thickness** $t_t < t$ is used.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $t_t = 0.707\,s$ | Effective throat from leg size (equilateral fillet) |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{P}{0.707\,s\,l_w}$ | Shear stress in parallel (longitudinal) fillet weld |
| 3 | $f = \sqrt{f_a^2 + q^2}$ | Combined stress in inclined fillet weld |
| 4 | $f_{wd,\text{weld}} = \dfrac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}} \cdot 0.707\,s$ | Weld metal strength per unit length |
| 5 | $f_{wd,\text{base}} = \dfrac{f_{u}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mb}} \cdot 0.707\,s$ | Base metal strength per unit length |
| 6 | $R_{wd} = f_{wd} \cdot l_w$ | Total weld capacity (governed by lower of 4 or 5) |
| 7 | $\beta_{l,w} = 1.2 - 0.2\,L_w$ ($L_w$ in m) | Long-weld reduction ($L_w > 150\,t_t$) |
| 8 | $l_{w,\text{eff}} = L - 2s$ | Effective length = actual − 2 × crater |
| 9 | $T_{db} = \dfrac{f_u\,t\,l_w}{\gamma_{mb}}$ | Butt weld capacity (tension) |
| 10 | $s_{\min}$ from table above | Cl. 10.5.3 minimum leg size |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridges:** Butt-welded splices in main girders; fillet welds for stiffeners and diaphragms.
- **High-rise buildings:** Welded column splices, beam-to-column end-plate connections (often fillet + partial-penetration butt).
- **Pressure vessels & penstocks:** 100% full-penetration butt welds with radiographic (RT) or ultrasonic (UT) testing.
- **Industrial structures:** Crane gantry girders, transmission towers — fillet welds for bracket-type connections.

The **safety philosophy** here ties directly into PESFT754: the weld is only as safe as its **inspection regime** (visual, NDT) and the **procedural qualification** of the welder (per IS 7318 / ASME Sec IX). A weld that *looks* good can be unsafe if it contains **undercut, porosity, lack of fusion, or slag inclusion** — these are KTU favourite viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throat Thickness $t_t$ of an Equilateral Fillet Weld

**Statement:** For a fillet weld of leg size $s$ deposited between two perpendicular plates, derive the effective throat thickness $t_t$ assuming an isosceles right-triangle cross-section.

**Step 1:** Set up geometry. The two legs of the weld triangle are each of length $s$ and meet at 90°. The hypotenuse is the visible weld face.

$$
\text{Hypotenuse} = \sqrt{s^2 + s^2} = s\sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 2:** The effective throat $t_t$ is the perpendicular distance from the root of the triangle (intersection of the two parent plates) to the hypotenuse.

For an isosceles right triangle, this altitude equals half the hypotenuse:

$$
t_t = \dfrac{s\sqrt{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerical simplification:

$$
t_t = \dfrac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{1.4142}{2}\,s = 0.7071\,s
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_t \approx 0.707\,s\,}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The throat is **30% smaller** than the visible leg. This is why a 6 mm leg weld has only ≈ 4.24 mm of load-carrying depth — a common source of KTU exam marks lost.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Strength of Parallel (Longitudinal) Fillet Weld per Unit Length

**Statement:** A plate of thickness $t$ is connected to a gusset using two parallel fillet welds of leg size $s$ and length $l_w$. Derive the average shear stress in the weld throat and the safe load per unit length.

**Step 1:** The applied tensile force $P$ is transferred into the gusset through **two** weld lines. Each weld line carries $P/2$.

**Step 2:** The failure plane is the throat of each weld. Area of throat per weld line:

$$
A_t = t_t \cdot l_w = 0.707\,s \cdot l_w
$$

**Step 3:** Average shear stress on the throat:

$$
q = \dfrac{P/2}{0.707\,s\,l_w}
$$

**Step 4:** Equating to design shear strength of weld $\tau_{wd}$:

$$
\dfrac{P}{2 \cdot 0.707\,s\,l_w} \le \tau_{wd}
$$

**Step 5:** Solving for safe load $P$:

$$
P_{\text{safe}} = 2 \times 0.707\,s\,l_w \times \tau_{wd} = 1.414\,s\,l_w\,\tau_{wd}
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $\tau_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}}$ (E250, shop):

$$
\tau_{wd} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
P_{\text{safe}} = 1.414 \times s \times l_w \times 189.4 \approx 268\,s\,l_w \text{ (N)}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{safe, parallel}} = 1.414\,s\,l_w\,\tau_{wd}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Stress in an Inclined Fillet Weld

**Statement:** A fillet weld is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the line of action of the load. Derive the normal and shear stress components on the throat plane and the equivalent combined stress.

**Step 1:** The throat plane is at 45° to the weld leg. Therefore, relative to the load axis, the throat normal makes an angle of $45° + \theta$ with the load (for a weld in the "compression" orientation) or $45° - \theta$ for the alternative orientation. We adopt the KTU convention:

**Step 2:** Resolve the load $P$ along the throat normal ($\sigma_w$) and along the throat plane ($\tau_w$):

$$
\sigma_w = \dfrac{P \sin\theta}{0.707\,s\,l_w}
$$

$$
\tau_w = \dfrac{P \cos\theta}{0.707\,s\,l_w}
$$

**Step 3:** The **equivalent combined stress** $f$ on the throat (per IS 800, Cl. 10.5.7.1.1):

$$
f = \sqrt{\sigma_w^{\,2} + \tau_w^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and simplify:

$$
f = \dfrac{P}{0.707\,s\,l_w}\sqrt{\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta} = \dfrac{P}{0.707\,s\,l_w}
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the **combined stress limit** is:

$$
\dfrac{P}{0.707\,s\,l_w} \le f_{wd}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-intuitive result (favourite KTU viva trap!):** The combined stress $f$ is *independent* of $\theta$ — the angle of inclination does **not** change the equivalent stress on the throat. However, the *individual* components $\sigma_w$ and $\tau_w$ vary. The practical implication: an inclined weld is **NOT** stronger than a transverse weld per unit throat area — the design strength governs either way.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Design of Bracket Welded to a Column

**Problem statement (KTU typical):** A bracket carrying a factored load of $P_u = 250$ kN is welded to a column flange using two transverse fillet welds (one on each side of the bracket), each of length $l_w$. The bracket plate is 12 mm thick (E250). Design the weld size.

**Step 1:** Identify the load per weld (two welds share the load symmetrically):

$$
P_{\text{each}} = \dfrac{250}{2} = 125 \text{ kN} = 125\,000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2:** Assume a trial weld size. From minimum size rule, $s_{\min} = 5$ mm (for 12 mm plate, $10 < t \le 20$). Try $s = 8$ mm.

**Step 3:** Throat thickness:

$$
t_t = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.66 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Required effective length per weld:

$$
l_w = \dfrac{P_{\text{each}}}{0.707\,s \cdot f_{wd}}
$$

With $f_{wd,\text{weld}} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4$ MPa (weld metal governing).

$$
l_w = \dfrac{125\,000}{0.707 \times 8 \times 189.4} = \dfrac{125\,000}{1071.6} = 116.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Actual length to be provided (to allow for craters):

$$
L_{\text{actual}} = l_w + 2s = 116.6 + 2(8) = 132.6 \text{ mm} \approx 135 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Verify base metal does not govern. Base metal shear strength:

$$
\tau_{bd} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa (same as weld for E250)}
$$

So **weld metal governs** (since $f_{u,\text{base}} = f_{uw}$ here, neither governs the other strictly — design is OK).

**Step 7:** Final check — length-to-throat ratio for long-weld reduction:

$$
L_w = 0.1356 \text{ m}, \quad 150\,t_t = 150 \times 5.66 = 849 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $L_w \ll 150\,t_t$, **no reduction needed**.

**Final Design:**

$$
\boxed{\,s = 8 \text{ mm, } L = 135 \text{ mm (each side) for E250 bracket, } P_u = 250 \text{ kN}\,}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Design Decision Table

| Situation | Governing Strength | Equation to Use | KTU Test Tip |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|--------------|
| Parallel fillet weld (lap joint) | Weld metal shear | $P = 1.414\,s\,l_w\,\tau_{wd}$ | Remember factor 1.414, not 1.0 |
| Transverse fillet weld | Weld metal shear on inclined throat | $P = 0.707\,s\,l_w\,(f_{uw}/\gamma_{mw})$ | Strength is 0.707× of parallel, but component is normal |
| Butt weld, full penetration | Base metal | $P = (f_u/\gamma_{mb}) \cdot t \cdot l_w$ | Treat like parent metal |
| Long fillet weld ($l > 150\,t_t$) | Reduced strength | Multiply by $\beta_{l,w}$ | Many students forget this |
| Weld in tension (e.g., end return) | Weld metal normal stress | $\sigma \le f_{wd}$ | Return welds are restricted by Cl. 10.5.8 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Welded Joints

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Welded Joints] --> B[Butt Weld]
    A --> C[Fillet Weld]
    A --> D[Plug and Slot Weld]
    A --> E[Edge and Seam Weld]

    B --> B1[Square Groove]
    B --> B2[V Groove]
    B --> B3[U Groove]
    B --> B4[J Groove]
    B --> B5[Bevel Groove]

    C --> C1[Transverse Fillet]
    C --> C2[Longitudinal Fillet]
    C --> C3[Inclined Fillet]
    C --> C4[End Return Weld]

    D --> D1[Circular Plug Weld]
    D --> D2[Elongated Slot Weld]

    E --> E1[Edge Flange Weld]
    E --> E2[Seam Weld]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Fillet Weld Geometry

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Parent Plate 1] --> W1[Weld Leg s]
    P2[Parent Plate 2] --> W1
    W1 --> T[Effective Throat 0.707 s]
    W1 --> F[Weld Face Hypotenuse]
    W1 --> R[Weld Root]
    T --> S[Shear Failure Plane]
    S --> L[Effective Length lw]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Design Flow for Fillet Weld

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Given Load Pu and Plate Thickness t] --> S1[Select Weld Size s greater than or equal to smin]
    S1 --> S2[Check smax for Edge Condition]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Throat tt equals 0.707 s]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Weld Metal Strength fwd]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Base Metal Strength fbd]
    S5 --> S6[Governing Strength fwd is minimum of both]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Required Length lw equals Pu divided by fwd]
    S7 --> S8{Lw greater than 150 tt?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Apply Long Weld Reduction]
    S8 -->|No| S10[Adopt Actual Length L equals lw plus 2s]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> End[Final Design s and L]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Weld Inspection and Safety Hierarchy (PESFT754 Context)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Design Strength Check IS 800 Cl 10.5] --> L2[Welder Qualification IS 7318]
    L2 --> L3[Procedure Qualification ASME Sec IX]
    L3 --> L4[In Process Inspection Visual]
    L4 --> L5[Non Destructive Testing NDT]
    L5 --> L6[Ultrasonic Testing UT]
    L5 --> L7[Radiographic Testing RT]
    L5 --> L8[Magnetic Particle Testing MT]
    L5 --> L9[Dye Penetrant Testing DP]
    L6 --> L10[Acceptance and Sign Off]
    L7 --> L10
    L8 --> L10
    L9 --> L10
    L1 -.-> L10
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model)] *Define a fillet weld. With a neat sketch, show the leg size and effective throat thickness. Why is the effective throat taken as 0.707 × leg size?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A fillet weld is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces meeting at an angle between 0° and 145°. The **leg size $s$** is the shorter of the two legs of the right triangle formed. The **effective throat thickness $t_t$** is the perpendicular distance from the root to the face (hypotenuse).

**Sketch:**

> [Plate 1]──┐
>           ╲╲
>            ╲╲  ← weld face (hypotenuse = $s\sqrt{2}$)
>             ╲╲
>              ╲╲  ← effective throat $t_t$
> [Plate 2]──────┘
>             root

For an isosceles right triangle, the altitude to the hypotenuse equals half the hypotenuse:

$$
t_t = \dfrac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707\,s
$$

This throat represents the shortest path through which the weld will shear under load and is the plane used in design calculations. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023 (Model)] *State the minimum and maximum sizes of fillet welds as per IS 800:2007. Why are these limits specified?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Minimum size** (Cl. 10.5.3) — to ensure adequate heat input and prevent cracking due to rapid cooling:

| Plate thickness $t$ (mm) | $s_{\min}$ (mm) |
|--------------------------|------------------|
| $t \le 10$ | 3 |
| $10 < t \le 20$ | 5 |
| $20 < t \le 32$ | 6 |
| $t > 32$ | $(t-1)/2$ rounded up |

**Maximum size** (Cl. 10.5.4) — to prevent melting of the parent plate edge:
- For plates ≤ 6 mm thick: $s_{\max} = t$
- For plates > 6 mm thick: $s_{\max} = t - 1.5$ mm

These limits ensure the weld has sufficient strength without distorting or burning through the parent metal. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** *Answer one full question from the choice. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *A tie member of 120 mm × 12 mm plate (Fe 410 grade) is to be connected to a gusset plate of 12 mm thickness using two parallel fillet welds. The member carries a factored tensile load of 360 kN. Design the welds. Take $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$.*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Material properties:**
- $f_u = 410$ MPa (Fe 410 = E250 equivalent per IS 2062).
- Design shear strength of weld:

$$
\tau_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}} = \dfrac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Weld size:**
- Plate thickness $t = 12$ mm → $s_{\min} = 5$ mm, $s_{\max} = t - 1.5 = 10.5$ mm.
- Adopt $s = 6$ mm.

**Step 3 — Throat:**

$$
t_t = 0.707 \times 6 = 4.243 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Strength per unit length of weld:**

$$
R_{wd} = 0.707 \times s \times \tau_{wd} = 0.707 \times 6 \times 189.4 = 803 \text{ N/mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Total length required (two welds):**

$$
l_{w,\text{total}} = \dfrac{P_u}{R_{wd}} = \dfrac{360\,000}{803} = 448.3 \text{ mm}
$$

Each weld: $l_w = 448.3/2 = 224.2$ mm.

**Step 6 — Actual length with craters:**

$$
L = 224.2 + 2s = 224.2 + 12 = 236.2 \approx 240 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Checks:**
- $150\,t_t = 150 \times 4.243 = 636$ mm. Since $L = 240$ mm < 636 mm, no long-weld reduction.
- Provide **end returns** of $2s = 12$ mm to prevent crater cracking.
- $s = 6$ mm $\ge s_{\min} = 5$ mm ✓
- $s = 6$ mm $\le s_{\max} = 10.5$ mm ✓

**Final Design:** Two parallel fillet welds of **$s = 6$ mm leg size, $L = 240$ mm length each**, shop-welded. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *A bracket welded to a column flange carries a factored load of 280 kN. The bracket is 14 mm thick. Design a transverse fillet weld connection. Comment on the orientation effect.*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Assumptions:**
- Two transverse welds (one each side of bracket).
- E250 grade steel, shop welding.

**Step 2 — Load per weld:**

$$
P_{\text{each}} = \dfrac{280}{2} = 140 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — For transverse fillet weld, the throat is inclined at 45° to the load. The combined stress equation (IS 800 Cl. 10.5.7.1.1) is:**

$$
f = \sqrt{f_a^2 + q^2} = \dfrac{P}{0.707\,s\,l_w} \le f_{wd}
$$

**Step 4 — $f_{wd}$ (weld metal, E250, shop):**

$$
f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}} = 189.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Trial $s$: $s_{\min}$ for 14 mm plate = 5 mm. Try $s = 8$ mm.**

$$
t_t = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.66 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Required length per weld:**

$$
l_w = \dfrac{P}{0.707\,s\,f_{wd}} = \dfrac{140\,000}{0.707 \times 8 \times 189.4} = \dfrac{140\,000}{1071.6} = 130.6 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L = 130.6 + 2s = 146.6 \approx 150 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Orientation comment:**

For a **transverse** fillet weld, the throat is at 45° to the load, producing **both normal and shear stress** on the throat. The vector-sum gives the same combined stress as a parallel weld of identical size and length, but the **individual components** differ (transverse → 50% normal, 50% shear; parallel → 100% shear). Therefore, **per unit length, both orientations have the same design strength**, contrary to the popular (but wrong) notion that transverse welds are stronger.

**Final Design:** Two transverse welds, **$s = 8$ mm, $L = 150$ mm each**, with 8 mm end returns. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2022] *Two plates, each 200 mm wide × 16 mm thick (Fe 410), are to be joined by a full-penetration butt weld and subjected to a factored tensile load of 800 kN. Check the adequacy of the joint. Take $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$.*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Net area of the plate at the weld (full-penetration, no reduction in thickness):**

$$
A_g = 200 \times 16 = 3200 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Design tensile strength of butt weld (matching the parent metal):**

$$
T_{db} = \dfrac{f_u \cdot A_g}{\gamma_{mb}} = \dfrac{410 \times 3200}{1.25} = 1\,049\,600 \text{ N} = 1049.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Check against applied load:**

$$
T_{db} = 1049.6 \text{ kN} > P_u = 800 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_u}{T_{db}} = \dfrac{800}{1049.6} = 76.2\%
$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** Joint is adequate with **≈24% reserve capacity**. No reduction in area required since it is a full-penetration butt weld with matching electrode. The weld metal strength and parent metal strength are equal, so the joint efficiency is 100% for the matched case.

**Conclusion:** The butt-welded joint is **safe and adequate** for the given loading. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2022] *Discuss the various types of stresses induced in a fillet weld under different orientations. Show that the combined stress is independent of the angle of inclination.*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — When the line of action of the load makes an angle $\theta$ with the weld length (inclined fillet weld), the throat plane (inclined at 45° to the weld leg) is subjected to:**

- **Normal stress** $f_a$ (perpendicular to throat):

$$
f_a = \dfrac{P \sin\theta}{0.707\,s\,l_w}
$$

- **Shear stress** $q$ (along the throat plane):

$$
q = \dfrac{P \cos\theta}{0.707\,s\,l_w}
$$

**Step 2 — IS 800 (Cl. 10.5.7.1.1) prescribes the equivalent combined stress:**

$$
f = \sqrt{f_a^2 + q^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**

$$
f = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{P\sin\theta}{0.707\,s\,l_w}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{P\cos\theta}{0.707\,s\,l_w}\right)^2}
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{P}{0.707\,s\,l_w} \sqrt{\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Applying the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$:**

$$
\boxed{\,f = \dfrac{P}{0.707\,s\,l_w}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
- The combined stress $f$ is **independent of $\theta$**.
- However, the **individual** normal and shear components change with $\theta$.
- At $\theta = 0°$ (parallel): pure shear, $q = P/(0.707\,s\,l_w)$, $f_a = 0$.
- At $\theta = 90°$ (transverse): $f_a = q = P/(0.707\,s\,l_w)$.
- The **design check** $f \le f_{wd}$ remains the same regardless of orientation.

**Engineering implication:** A welder cannot increase capacity by inclining the weld — design must satisfy the same strength criterion. However, the **ductility demand** differs: transverse welds are stiffer and attract more load by virtue of their lower deformation, but the code treats them equally. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make (lose 1–3 marks per mistake):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 0.707** for throat thickness → overestimates strength by 41% → unsafe design → **MAJOR deduction**.
> 2. **Not subtracting $2s$ for crater length** → overestimates effective length → over-rates the weld → **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Confusing $\gamma_{mw}$ (1.25 shop / 1.50 field)** — if the problem states "site welding" and you use 1.25, you **over-rate** the weld by 20% → **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Omitting the long-weld reduction** for $l_w > 150\,t_t$ → up to **20% loss in capacity** missed → **2-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Using $f_y$ instead of $f_u$** for weld design (weld is governed by ultimate strength, not yield) → **3-mark deduction**.
> 6. **In transverse vs. parallel comparison**, stating that transverse welds are "stronger" — this is a **misconception**; per IS 800, the combined strength is identical per unit throat area → **2-mark deduction**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PESFT754 – Welded Joints**

- [x] A **fillet weld** is a triangular cross-section weld; **effective throat** $t_t = 0.707\,s$.
- [x] A **butt weld** is edge-to-edge fusion; full-penetration butt welds are as strong as parent metal.
- [x] Design strength of **weld metal**: $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_{uw}}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mw}}$.
- [x] Design strength of **base metal**: $f_{bd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3}\,\gamma_{mb}}$.
- [x] The **governing strength** is the **lower** of weld and base metal strength.
- [x] **Effective length** = actual length − $2s$ (for craters).
- [x] **Minimum weld size** depends on plate thickness (3/5/6 mm or $(t-1)/2$).
- [x] **Maximum weld size** at plate edge: $s_{\max} = t$ (if $t \le 6$ mm) or $t - 1.5$ mm (if $t > 6$ mm).
- [x] **Combined stress** in fillet weld: $f = \sqrt{f_a^2 + q^2}$, **independent of $\theta$**.
- [x] **Long-weld reduction**: $l_w > 150\,t_t$ → multiply capacity by $\beta_{l,w} = 1.2 - 0.2 L_w$ ($L_w$ in m).
- [x] **Partial safety factors**: $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop), $1.50$ (field); $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$.
- [x] **End returns** of $2s$ are recommended on transverse fillet welds to prevent crater cracking.
- [x] **NDT methods** (UT, RT, MT, DP) are integral to *safety verification* in PESFT754.
- [x] **Weld metal E250** = $f_u \ge 410$ MPa; matching electrode is mandatory for full-strength butt welds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Welded Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of Welded Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **welded joint** is defined as a **permanent, monolithic joint** formed by establishing a **metallurgical bond** between two or more pieces of base metal through the application of heat, pressure, or both — with or without the addition of a filler material — such that the joint achieves **continuity of atomic structure** across the interface.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PESFT754), welded joints are classified as a primary category of **permanent fastening** in structural steel design (per **IS 816:1969** and **IS 9595:1996**), alongside the more recent **IS 2062:2011** standards for weldable structural steel. The fusion process melts the edges of the parent metal (and filler, if used), and upon solidification the joint behaves as a **continuous, homogeneous material** rather than a mechanical interlock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** Welded joints are covered under the unit dealing with **permanent connections** in steel structures. The expected level of treatment per KTU's Course Outcome **CO3** is: *"Analyse the behaviour and design of welded and bolted connections in steel structures."*

The principal weld types you must know for the KTU board exam are:
- **Fillet Weld** (most common — triangular cross-section in the gap between two members)
- **Butt Weld** (full penetration groove weld joining two members in the same plane)
- **Plug / Slot Weld** (used for lap joints, secondary load transfer)
- **Spot / Seam Weld** (resistance welding for sheet metal)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

Imagine you have **two pieces of chocolate bar**. There are two ways to join them:

**Method A — The "Rivet/Bolt" Way (Mechanical Joint):** You drill a hole, push a metal pin through, and crimp the end. The two pieces stay together, but if you look closely, the joint is still **two separate pieces** held by an external fastener. There is a stress concentration at the hole, and the joint can slip or loosen.

**Method B — The "Weld" Way (Metallurgical Joint):** You gently heat the contact edges until they melt, push them together, and let them cool. The two pieces have now **become one single piece**. There is no fastener, no hole, no separate component — just a continuous body of metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for safety design:** Because a welded joint is *continuous material* rather than a mechanical interlock, it has **no inherent weak point** like a rivet hole. However, this same property means that **defects in the weld itself** (porosity, cracks, lack of fusion) become the weak points — which is why **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing)** is critical in welded structure safety.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Codal References (KTU High-Priority)

| Parameter | Standard Value | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Fillet weld minimum size | **6 mm** (for plates ≤ 20 mm thick) | IS 816:1969 |
| Maximum effective throat thickness | **0.7 × size of weld** | IS 816:1969 |
| Design strength of weld | **f_wd = f_u / (√3 × γ_mw)** | IS 800:2007, Cl. 9.5.7 |
| Partial safety factor (γ_mw) | **1.25** (shop), **1.50** (field) | IS 800:2007 |
| Permissible shear in fillet weld | **102 MPa** (shop), 84 MPa (field) | IS 800:2007, Table 9.4 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Welds Behave the Way They Do

To understand the advantages and disadvantages of welded joints at a deep level, we must understand the **physics of the fusion zone**. When an arc or gas flame heats the parent metal above its melting point (~1500°C for mild steel), four distinct zones are formed:

1. **Fusion Zone (FZ)** — The actual weld metal where filler + parent metal have melted and solidified.
2. **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — Parent metal that did not melt but underwent microstructural change due to heat.
3. **Weld Interface** — The boundary between FZ and HAZ.
4. **Unaffected Parent Metal** — Material far from the weld, retaining original properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **HAZ** is the **single most important concept** in safety design of welded joints. It is the zone where the original properties of the steel are *altered*. Hardening, softening, grain growth, or embrittlement can occur depending on cooling rate. **Safety-critical designs mandate pre-heating and post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) to control HAZ properties.**

## 2.2 Structural Logic — Advantages Breakdown

### A. Advantages of Welded Joints

1. **Permanent and Rigid Connection**
   - Welds provide **full continuity** — the joint behaves as a monolithic structure.
   - Higher **stiffness** compared to riveted/bolted joints because there is no slip plane.

2. **Weight Economy**
   - No gusset plates, splice plates, or angles required (in many cases).
   - **Weight savings of 10–20%** compared to riveted equivalents.
   - Direct material saving → **lighter structure** → reduced foundation loads.

3. **Airtight and Watertight Joints**
   - Welds are **leak-proof** because there is no interstitial gap.
   - Critical for **pressure vessels, water tanks, pipelines, boilers** — applications where bolted joints fail.

4. **Design Flexibility**
   - Can join **any shape**: plates, sheets, tubes, rolled sections, castings.
   - Can join **dissimilar metals** (with proper filler selection).
   - Can join **thin to thick sections** easily.

5. **No Hole Weakening**
   - In bolted/riveted joints, holes reduce the net effective area. Welds require **no holes**, so the **full cross-section remains effective**.

6. **Smooth Aesthetic Profile**
   - Welds can be ground flush — important for **architectural steel** and fatigue-sensitive structures.

7. **Faster Fabrication & Economy at Scale**
   - For mass production, welding is **faster and cheaper** than drilling and riveting.

8. **Higher Strength-to-Weight Ratio**
   - The fused zone can develop strength **equal to or exceeding** the parent metal (especially for butt welds with full penetration).

### B. Disadvantages of Welded Joints

1. **Skilled Labour Dependency**
   - Welding quality is **operator-dependent**. A poorly executed weld is a safety hazard.
   - Requires **certified welders** (per IS 7310 / ASME Section IX).

2. **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) — Metallurgical Degradation**
   - The HAZ suffers **grain coarsening, hardening, or temper embrittlement**.
   - May have **lower toughness, ductility, and fatigue life** than parent metal.

3. **Residual Stresses and Distortion**
   - Non-uniform heating and cooling induce **locked-in residual stresses**.
   - Causes **warping, shrinkage, and dimensional distortion** — affects tolerances.

4. **Difficult Quality Inspection**
   - Internal defects (porosity, slag inclusion, lack of fusion, undercuts) are **not visible to the eye**.
   - Requires **NDT methods** (ultrasonic, radiographic, magnetic particle, dye penetrant).

5. **Brittle Fracture Risk**
   - Welds can act as **notch-like stress concentrators**.
   - At low temperatures, brittle fracture can initiate from weld defects → **catastrophic failure**.

6. **Fatigue Sensitivity**
   - Welded joints have **lower fatigue life** than parent metal due to weld toe stress concentration and surface profile.
   - Fatigue category classification (per IS 800:2007 Table 26) penalizes welded joints.

7. **Irreversibility / Non-Dismantlable**
   - Welded joints **cannot be easily dismantled** for repair or replacement.
   - In contrast, bolted joints can be undone.

8. **Equipment Cost & Safety Hazards**
   - Initial investment in **welding machines, electrodes, gas, fume extraction** is high.
   - Hazards: **arc radiation, toxic fumes, fire risk, electric shock**.

9. **Pre-heating & PWHT Required**
   - For thick sections or high-carbon steels, **pre-heating before welding** and **post-weld heat treatment** are mandatory — adds time and cost.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Design shear strength of weld | $f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}$ | MPa | IS 800 Cl. 9.5.7 |
| Strength of fillet weld per unit length | $R_{dw} = 0.7 \cdot s \cdot f_{wd}$ | N/mm | Where $s$ = weld size |
| Minimum weld size (plate ≤ 20 mm) | $s_{min} = 6 \text{ mm}$ | mm | IS 816 |
| Effective throat (fillet) | $t_e = 0.7 \cdot s$ | mm | Geometric relation |
| Effective length requirement | $l_{eff} \geq 4 \cdot s$ | mm | IS 800 Cl. 10.5.4.1 |
| Net effective area (welded, no holes) | $A_{eff} = A_g$ | mm² | No reduction unlike bolts |
| Fatigue stress range (weld toe) | $\Delta f = f_{max} - f_{min}$ | MPa | For cyclic loading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The formula $R_{dw} = 0.7 \cdot s \cdot f_{wd}$ is the **single most tested equation** in KTU board exams for welded joint design. Memorize it along with $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop) and $1.50$ (field).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

Welded joints are the backbone of:
- **High-rise steel buildings** (beam-column moment connections)
- **Bridges** (box girders, orthotropic decks)
- **Pressure vessels and boilers** (ASME Code)
- **Offshore platforms** (API 2A standards)
- **Railway and ship structures**

The **safety design imperative** is that while welded joints are *structurally efficient*, their **failure mode is brittle and sudden** (unlike ductile yielding of bolted joints). Hence **quality control, NDT, and codal compliance** are non-negotiable in safety engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Throat Thickness of a Fillet Weld

This derivation explains **why** the factor **0.7** appears in the design strength formula — a frequent KTU question.

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

A fillet weld of **size $s$** is deposited at the corner of two plates meeting at right angles (typical case). The cross-section of the weld is an **isosceles right-angled triangle** with the two equal legs of length $s$.

The **throat thickness** $t$ is the **perpendicular distance** from the root of the weld to the hypotenuse (the face of the weld).

### Step 2: Apply Pythagoras Theorem

The hypotenuse $h$ of the isosceles right triangle is given by:

$$
h = \sqrt{s^{2} + s^{2}} = \sqrt{2 \, s^{2}} = s \sqrt{2}
$$

### Step 3: Throat as Altitude on Hypotenuse

For a right-angled triangle, the altitude $t$ to the hypotenuse is given by:

$$
t = \dfrac{\text{product of legs}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \dfrac{s \cdot s}{s \sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 4: Rationalise the Denominator

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$
t = \dfrac{s \sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \, s
$$

### Step 5: Adopt the Codal Value

For design simplicity, IS 800:2007 adopts:

$$
t_e = 0.7 \, s
$$

where $t_e$ is the **effective throat thickness** used in strength calculations.

### Step 6: Final Design Strength Expression

$$
R_{dw} = f_{wd} \cdot t_e = f_{wd} \cdot (0.7 \, s)
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{dw} = 0.7 \, s \cdot f_{wd} \;\;[N/mm \text{ per mm length}]}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A lap joint between two 10 mm plates carries a factored tensile load of **250 kN**. Fillet welds are provided on both sides. If shop welding is used with $f_u = 410$ MPa, find the required weld size and length.

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**
Factored load $F = 250{,}000$ N, $f_u = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop).

**Step 2: Compute design shear strength.**

$$
f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}
$$

$$
f_{wd} = \dfrac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = \dfrac{410}{2.165} \approx 189.4 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Assume trial weld size.**
For 10 mm plate, minimum $s = 6$ mm (but check maximum — for 10 mm plate, $s_{max} = 10 - 1.5 = 8.5$ mm, considering 1.5 mm gap root). Adopt **$s = 8$ mm** for safety.

**Step 4: Effective throat.**

$$
t_e = 0.7 \times 8 = 5.6 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5: Strength per mm length (one weld).**

$$
R_{dw,1} = 5.6 \times 189.4 \approx 1060.6 \; \text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 6: Total strength with welds on both sides.**

$$
R_{dw,total} = 2 \times 1060.6 = 2121.3 \; \text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 7: Required effective length.**

$$
l_{eff} = \dfrac{F}{R_{dw,total}} = \dfrac{250{,}000}{2121.3} \approx 117.9 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 8: Actual length provided.**

Allowing 2 × $s = 16$ mm for end craters:

$$
l_{actual} = 117.9 + 16 = 133.9 \;\text{mm}, \quad \text{say } 140 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 9: Check $l_{eff} \geq 4 s$.**

$$
4s = 32 \;\text{mm} \ll 117.9 \;\text{mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Provide } s = 8 \text{ mm}, \; l_{actual} = 140 \text{ mm on each side (both faces of lap)}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Weld vs Bolt vs Rivet

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam table**. Memorize the comparative reasoning.

| Criterion | Welded Joint | Bolted Joint | Riveted Joint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joint type | Permanent, monolithic | Temporary, detachable | Permanent, mechanical |
| Strength efficiency | Up to 100% (full penetration butt) | 80–85% (due to holes) | 70–80% (due to holes) |
| Weight | **Lightest** (no splice plates) | Heavier (plates + bolts) | Heaviest (plates + rivets) |
| Skill required | High (certified welder) | Moderate | High (hot riveting) |
| Inspection ease | Difficult (NDT needed) | **Easiest** (visual) | Moderate |
| Dismantlability | **Not possible** | **Possible** | Difficult |
| Fatigue resistance | **Lower** (stress conc. at toe) | Higher | Moderate |
| Brittle fracture risk | **High** | Lower (ductile) | Moderate |
| Equipment cost | High | Low | Moderate |
| Best application | Bridges, pressure vessels, towers | Temporary structures, maintenance | Old bridges, boilers (legacy) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Welded Joint Classification & Behaviour Flow

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of welded joints: their classification, behaviour under load, and associated safety considerations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["WELDED JOINT SYSTEM"] --> nodeB["CLASSIFICATION"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["LOAD BEHAVIOUR"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["SAFETY CONTROL"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["FILLET WELD"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["BUTT WELD"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["PLUG SLOT WELD"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["SPOT SEAM WELD"]

    nodeB1 --> nodeB1A["Most common in steel structures"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1B["Triangular cross section"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1C["Effective throat equals 0.7 times size"]

    nodeB2 --> nodeB2A["Full penetration possible"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2B["Strength equals parent metal"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB2C["Used in primary tension members"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["STATIC LOAD"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["FATIGUE LOAD"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["IMPACT LOAD"]

    nodeC1 --> nodeC1A["Design by IS 800 Cl 9"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2A["Fatigue category per IS 800 Table 26"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2B["Stress range governs design"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3A["Charpy toughness required at HAZ"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["PRE HEATING CONTROL"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["NDT INSPECTION"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["PWHT FOR RELIEF"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["WELD QUALIFICATION"]

    nodeD1 --> nodeD1A["Slows cooling rate, prevents HAZ hardening"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2A["Ultrasonic and Radiographic testing"]
    nodeD3 --> nodeD3A["Reduces residual stress and distortion"]
    nodeD4 --> nodeD4A["Welder and procedure qualification per IS 7310"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Safety Assessment of Welded Joints

When direct physical schematics are too complex, this **block-level flow** captures the **decision logic** an engineer follows during safety design:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA["DESIGN INPUT: Load, Material, Plate Thickness"] --> procB["STEP 1: SELECT WELD TYPE"]
    procB --> procC["STEP 2: COMPUTE DESIGN STRENGTH fw d"]
    procC --> procD["STEP 3: SIZE THE WELD"]
    procD --> procE["STEP 4: CHECK DEFECTS AND HAZ"]
    procE --> procF{"IS NDT PASS?"}
    procF -- YES --> outG["ACCEPT JOINT"]
    procF -- NO --> procH["REPAIR OR REJECT"]
    procH --> procE

    inpA --> noteA["Factored Load per IS 800"]
    procB --> noteB["Fillet for lap, Butt for tension"]
    procC --> noteC["Use gamma mw = 1.25 shop or 1.50 field"]
    procD --> noteD["Effective throat = 0.7 s"]
    procE --> noteE["Check porosity, undercut, lack of fusion"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flow mirrors the **decision sequence a KTU examiner expects** in a 14-mark design problem. The loop `procE → procF → procH → procE` represents the **iterative quality control cycle** that mirrors real-world fabrication QA/QC protocols.

## 4.3 HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) Sub-Zone Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pa["PARENT METAL UNAFFECTED"] --> h1["HAZ SUB ZONE 1: GRAIN GROWTH"]
    h1 --> h2["HAZ SUB ZONE 2: NORMALISED"]
    h2 --> h3["HAZ SUB ZONE 3: PARTIALLY TRANSFORMED"]
    h3 --> wf["WELD FUSION ZONE FZ"]
    wf --> h3b["HAZ SUB ZONE 3 MIRROR"]
    h3b --> h2b["HAZ SUB ZONE 2 MIRROR"]
    h2b --> h1b["HAZ SUB ZONE 1 MIRROR"]
    h1b --> pa2["PARENT METAL UNAFFECTED"]

    pa --> notePA["Original grain structure intact"]
    h1 --> noteH1["Temperature above 1100 degree C"]
    h1 --> noteH1B["Coarse grains, low toughness"]
    h2 --> noteH2["Temperature 900 to 1100 degree C"]
    h2 --> noteH2B["Refined grain, good strength"]
    h3 --> noteH3["Temperature 700 to 900 degree C"]
    h3 --> noteH3B["Mixed microstructure, brittle"]
    wf --> noteWF["Fully melted and resolidified"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram is high-yield:** KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify that **failure most likely initiates in the HAZ**, not in the weld metal itself. The coarse-grain HAZ is the weakest link and dictates the **fracture-critical member** designation in safety audits.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List any four advantages of welded joints over riveted joints."**

**Model Answer (4 × 1 mark presentation format expected):**

1. **Higher strength-to-weight ratio:** Welded joints do not require holes for fasteners; hence the full cross-sectional area of the member remains effective, giving higher efficiency.

2. **Greater rigidity:** Welded joints provide monolithic continuity with no slip plane, resulting in stiffer behaviour compared to riveted joints.

3. **Airtight and watertight:** Welds are leak-proof, making them suitable for pressure vessels, storage tanks, and pipelines — applications where riveted joints fail.

4. **Weight and material economy:** Elimination of splice plates, gussets, and rivet heads reduces overall weight by approximately 10–20%, lowering foundation loads.

5. *(Optional 5th point for extra credit)* **Design flexibility:** Welds can join any shape, thickness, or even dissimilar metals, whereas rivets are restricted to standard hole patterns.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State two disadvantages of welded joints related to inspection and quality."**

**Model Answer (2 × 1.5 marks):**

1. **Difficulty in inspection:** Internal weld defects such as porosity, slag inclusion, lack of fusion, and undercuts are not visible to the naked eye. Quality assessment requires specialised **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** methods like ultrasonic testing (UT), radiographic testing (RT), magnetic particle testing (MPT), or dye penetrant testing (DPT), all of which add cost, time, and require qualified personnel.

2. **Skilled labour dependency:** Welding is highly operator-sensitive. The quality of the joint depends directly on the welder's skill, electrode handling, travel speed, and arc length. A poorly executed weld is a **latent safety hazard** that may not be apparent until failure occurs. IS 7310 mandates welder qualification testing for structural applications.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Module 3 carries internal choice. **Solve EITHER Question A OR Question B.** Both are below — choose one in the actual exam.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Two plates of 12 mm thickness are joined by a lap joint using parallel fillet welds. The joint carries a factored tensile load of 320 kN. The plates are of Fe 410 grade steel ($f_u = 410$ MPa). Shop welding is used. Design the weld size and length.**

#### **Part (a): Compute design strength and select weld size** [7 marks]

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — List known data.**

$$
\text{Plate thickness} = 12 \;\text{mm}, \quad F = 320{,}000 \;\text{N}, \quad f_u = 410 \;\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\gamma_{mw} = 1.25 \;\text{(shop welding)}
$$

**Step 2 — Design shear strength of weld.**

$$
f_{wd} = \dfrac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \dfrac{410}{1.732 \times 1.25} = \dfrac{410}{2.165}
$$

$$
f_{wd} \approx 189.4 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine permissible weld size range.**

Minimum size (IS 816, for plate 10–20 mm thick):

$$
s_{min} = 6 \;\text{mm}
$$

Maximum size (limiting fusion beyond plate edge by 1.5 mm):

$$
s_{max} = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Trial weld size.**

Adopt **$s = 10$ mm** for economy.

**Step 5 — Effective throat.**

$$
t_e = 0.7 \times s = 0.7 \times 10 = 7 \;\text{mm}
$$

#### **Part (b): Determine weld length and check codal provisions** [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Strength per mm length per weld.**

$$
R_{dw,1} = t_e \cdot f_{wd} = 7 \times 189.4 = 1325.8 \;\text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Total strength (welds on both sides of lap).**

$$
R_{dw,total} = 2 \times 1325.8 = 2651.6 \;\text{N/mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Required effective length.**

$$
l_{eff,req} = \dfrac{F}{R_{dw,total}} = \dfrac{320{,}000}{2651.6} \approx 120.7 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual weld length per side (add 2s for end craters).**

$$
l_{actual} = 120.7 + 2 \times 10 = 140.7 \;\text{mm} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{provide } l = 145 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Check minimum effective length clause.**

$$
l_{eff,req} = 120.7 \;\text{mm} \geq 4s = 40 \;\text{mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Final design.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Provide } s = 10 \;\text{mm},\;\; l = 145 \;\text{mm} \text{ on each face of the lap joint.}}
$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Compare welded and riveted joints under the following heads: (a) Strength efficiency and weight, (b) Inspection, fatigue life, and dismantlability. State which joint is preferred for (i) pressure vessels and (ii) temporary structures, with justification.**

#### **Part (a): Strength Efficiency and Weight** [7 marks]

**Strength Efficiency:**

- **Welded joints** can achieve **100% efficiency** in full-penetration butt welds because no material is removed (no holes). The full cross-section remains effective.
- **Riveted joints** suffer **net section reduction** due to rivet holes. With multiple rows of rivets, efficiency drops to **70–80%** due to combined stress effects (shear lag and stress concentration).

**Weight:**

- Welded joints are **10–20% lighter** than riveted equivalents because:
  - No gusset/splice plates in many cases.
  - No rivet heads.
  - No need for additional packing materials.
- Riveted joints require **splice plates, gussets, and rivets**, all of which add dead weight and increase foundation loads.

#### **Part (b): Inspection, Fatigue Life, Dismantlability & Application** [7 marks]

**Inspection:**

- Welded joints require **NDT (UT, RT, MPT, DPT)** for defect detection — costly and time-consuming.
- Riveted joints can be inspected **visually** for loose rivets, cracks, and deformation — simpler and cheaper.

**Fatigue Life:**

- Welded joints have **lower fatigue life** because weld toes act as **notch-like stress concentrators**. IS 800:2007 assigns **lower fatigue categories** (e.g., 36 MPa for fillet welds vs. higher values for parent metal).
- Riveted joints have **relatively higher fatigue life** because the rivet clamp force distributes stress more evenly, though fretting corrosion can occur.

**Dismantlability:**

- Welded joints are **permanent** and cannot be dismantled without cutting — unsuitable for temporary structures.
- Riveted joints (though not as easily as bolted) can be **drilled out** for dismantling with moderate effort.

**Application Justification:**

- **(i) Pressure vessels → Welded joints preferred.** Airtight/watertight nature, higher strength efficiency, smoother profile (no leak paths), and lighter weight are critical for high-pressure service.
- **(ii) Temporary structures → Riveted joints preferred.** Although bolted is even better, riveted offers ease of dismantling compared to welded. Welds are unsuitable due to non-reversibility.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add 2s** for end crater deduction when computing actual weld length. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Using $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ in shop conditions** — should be **1.25**. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Not checking the $l_{eff} \geq 4s$ clause** — mandatory under IS 800:2007 Cl. 10.5.4.1. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing 0.7 factor**: Some students incorrectly use $0.707$ or $1/\sqrt{2}$ in design. The **codal value is exactly 0.7**. (-0.5 mark)
> 5. **Not stating the HAZ effect** in advantage/disadvantage answers — a high-weightage conceptual point. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Writing "weld is always better"** — examiners expect a **balanced comparative answer** with specific applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Welded joint = permanent, monolithic, metallurgical bond** formed by fusion.
- [x] **Effective throat of fillet weld = 0.7 × size** (from $s/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707 s$).
- [x] **Design shear strength:** $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw})$.
- [x] **Partial safety factor:** $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ (shop) or **1.50** (field/site).
- [x] **Minimum weld size:** 6 mm for plates ≤ 20 mm (IS 816:1969).
- [x] **Strength per mm length:** $R_{dw} = 0.7 \cdot s \cdot f_{wd}$.
- [x] **Minimum effective length:** $l_{eff} \geq 4s$ — non-negotiable codal clause.
- [x] **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** is the weakest link — coarse grains, low toughness.
- [x] **Welded joints are NOT preferred where fatigue, brittle fracture, or dismantling is critical.**
- [x] **Welded joints ARE preferred for pressure vessels, water-tightness, weight-critical, and aesthetic structures.**
- [x] **NDT is mandatory** for safety-critical welded joints.
- [x] **Weight saving** vs. riveted joints: **10–20%**.
- [x] **Key disadvantages to memorize:** HAZ degradation, residual stress/distortion, skilled labour, NDT cost, fatigue weakness, irreversible.
- [x] **Key advantages to memorize:** high efficiency, airtight, rigid, lightweight, design flexibility, no hole weakening.
- [x] **Fillet weld = lap/T-connections; Butt weld = in-plane tension members.**
- [x] **Pre-heating + PWHT** are mandatory for thick/high-carbon steel welds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark design question on welded joints, your answer should contain: **(1) Data extraction, (2) Selection of $\gamma_{mw}$, (3) Computation of $f_{wd}$, (4) Trial weld size with $s_{min}$/$s_{max}$ check, (5) Effective throat, (6) Strength calculation, (7) Length computation with $2s$ crater addition, (8) Final $l_{eff} \geq 4s$ verification, (9) Codal reference (IS 800:2007 + IS 816:1969).** Missing any two steps typically costs 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Welds and Their Symbols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Welds and Their Symbols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Welds and Welding Symbols

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weld (per AWS A2.4 / IS 813:1986):** A weld is a localized coalescence of metals or non-metals produced by heating the materials to the welding temperature, with or without the application of pressure, and with or without the use of filler metal, to form a permanent structural joint.

A **welding symbol** is a standardized graphical language used on engineering drawings to communicate, in a concise manner, the **type**, **size**, **length**, **location**, **extent**, and **finishing requirements** of a weld to the fabricator. The system adopted worldwide is governed by the **American Welding Society (AWS) Standard A2.4** and in India by **IS 813:1986 — "Code of Practice for Preparation of Welding Symbols on Drawings"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme (PESFT754), Module 3 emphasizes that engineers must *interpret and specify* welds on structural drawings with safety and reliability in view — incorrect symbology leads to misinterpretation at the fabrication shop, which is a leading cause of structural failure during service.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" of a Welder

Imagine a tailor sewing two pieces of cloth. To instruct another tailor, you could write a long paragraph — *"Sew along the edge for 3 cm using a running stitch, lock the end, and trim the loose thread."* That is messy. Instead, tailor's drawings use short pictorial **symbols** that any tailor instantly recognizes. **Welding symbols work exactly the same way.** They are the universal "shorthand recipe" that converts engineering intent into a fabricator's action — without ambiguity.

## 1.3 Why Welding Symbols Matter in Design Safety

- They eliminate **verbal misinterpretation** at the shop floor.
- They standardize communication across **international projects**.
- They carry **mandatory legal and contractual** value on engineering drawings.
- They ensure compliance with **IS 800:2007 / IS 816:1989** structural welding requirements.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metric:** In India, structural welds are governed by **IS 816:1989** (welding procedures) and **IS 800:2007** (general construction in steel). The symbol system itself is governed by **IS 813:1986** and **AWS A2.4:2012**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Welds (Master Framework)

Welds are classified along **three** independent axes:

| Axis | Categories |
|---|---|
| By **Joint Type** | Butt, Lap, Tee, Corner, Edge |
| By **Weld Geometry** | Fillet, Groove (Square, V, Bevel, U, J), Plug, Slot, Spot, Seam, Surfacing |
| By **Process** | Shielded Metal Arc (SMAW), Gas Metal Arc (GMAW), Flux Cored Arc (FCAW), Gas Tungsten Arc (GTAW), Submerged Arc (SAW), Electroslag (ESW) |

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Major Weld Types

### 2.2.1 Fillet Weld (Most Common in Structural Work)

A **fillet weld** is approximately **triangular in cross-section**, joining two surfaces meeting at an angle (commonly 90°). It does not require edge preparation of the parent plates.

**Theoretical Logic Steps:**
- Fillet welds are deposited in a **concave, convex, or flat profile**.
- The **leg size (s)** is the shortest distance from the weld toe to the weld face.
- The **throat thickness (t)** = $0.707 \times s$ for a 90° equal-leg fillet.
- Per **IS 800:2007**, the **effective throat thickness** is taken as **$0.7 \times s$** for design strength calculations (accounting for partial penetration profile).

### 2.2.2 Groove Welds (Full-Penetration Family)

A **groove weld** is deposited in a gap between two prepared edges. Strength depends on penetration into the parent metal.

| Sub-Type | Edge Preparation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Square Butt Groove** | No bevel; gap $\leq 6$ mm | Thin plates up to 6 mm |
| **Single-V Groove** | Both edges bevelled forming a V | Plates 6–20 mm thick |
| **Double-V Groove** | V on both sides | Plates $> 20$ mm thick |
| **Single-Bevel Groove** | One edge bevelled | Tee / Corner joints |
| **Double-Bevel Groove** | Both edges bevelled (one side each) | Heavy Tee joints |
| **Single-U Groove** | Both edges concavely machined | Critical thick sections |
| **Single-J Groove** | One edge J-shaped | Costly; thick tee joints |
| **Backing / Spacer Groove** | With permanent or temporary backing | One-side welding |

### 2.2.3 Plug and Slot Welds

- **Plug weld:** A circular hole (or slot) is filled with weld metal. Used to transmit shear in lap joints.
- **Slot weld:** Same concept, but with elongated slot. Per IS 800, slot length $\leq 10 \times$ plate thickness.

### 2.2.4 Spot and Seam Welds (Resistance Welding Family)

- **Spot weld:** Localized coalescence between two overlapping sheets produced by electrode pressure and current.
- **Seam weld:** A continuous series of overlapping spot welds made by roller electrodes.

### 2.2.5 Surfacing Weld

A weld deposited on a surface to build up dimensions or restore worn parts (hard-facing).

## 2.3 Welding Symbol Architecture (IS 813 / AWS A2.4)

Every welding symbol consists of **three core elements**:

1. **Reference Line** — Horizontal baseline drawn left-to-right.
2. **Arrow Line** — A leader pointing to the joint, with an arrowhead.
3. **Weld Symbol** — Placed on the reference line.
4. **Tail** — Optional, holds supplementary information (process, specification).

### 2.3.1 Placement Rules (Where the Symbol Goes)

| Weld Location | Position on Reference Line |
|---|---|
| **Arrow side** of joint (the side the arrow points to) | **Below** the reference line |
| **Other side** of joint (opposite) | **Above** the reference line |
| **Both sides** | Both above and below the reference line |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Below = Arrow side; Above = Other side."* — This single rule is the most tested fact in KTU examinations.

### 2.3.2 Supplementary Symbols (Add-Ons)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Convex contour | $\frown$ — convex fillet |
| Concave contour | $\smile$ — concave fillet |
| Flush contour | Straight line — flush finish |
| "C" letter | Chip finish |
| "G" letter | Grind finish |
| "M" letter | Machine finish |
| "R" letter | Roll finish |

### 2.3.3 The Eight Standard Weld Symbol Forms (Quick Reference)

| Symbol Form | Type | Below Ref Line | Above Ref Line |
|---|---|---|---|
| Triangle (▷) | Fillet | Arrow-side fillet | Other-side fillet |
| Square (⊐) | Square groove | Arrow-side square | Other-side square |
| V (∨) | V-groove | Arrow-side V | Other-side V |
| Bevel (>) | Bevel groove | Arrow-side bevel | Other-side bevel |
| U (∪) | U-groove | Arrow-side U | Other-side U |
| J (⊃) | J-groove | Arrow-side J | Other-side J |
| Circle (○) | Plug / Spot | Arrow-side plug | Other-side plug |
| Two parallel lines | Seam | Arrow-side seam | Other-side seam |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Reference | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effective throat of fillet | $t_e = 0.7 \, s$ | IS 800:2007 Cl.10.4.2 | mm |
| Actual throat (90°) | $t = 0.707 \, s$ | Geometry | mm |
| Weld size vs plate thickness | $s \le t_{\text{plate}} - 1.5\,\text{mm}$ | IS 800:2007 | mm |
| Min fillet size (mm) | $3$ (for $t \le 10$), $5$ (for $10 < t \le 20$), $6$ (for $20 < t \le 32$), $8$ (for $t > 32$) | IS 800 Table 21 | mm |
| Design strength of fillet | $f_{wd} = f_{uw} / \gamma_{mw}$ | IS 800:2007 | N/mm² |
| Strength of fillet per mm | $F = 0.7 \, s \, f_{uw} / \gamma_{mw}$ | IS 800:2007 | N/mm |
| $\gamma_{mw}$ (partial safety factor) | $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ (shop), $1.70$ (field) | IS 800:2007 | — |
| Plug hole diameter | $d \ge (t + 8)\,\text{mm}$; $d \le 2.5 \, t_{\min}$ | IS 800:2007 | mm |
| Slot length max | $L \le 10 \, t$ | IS 800:2007 | mm |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Civil structures:** Welds carry shear and tension in built-up columns, plate girders, and trusses.
- **Mechanical pressure vessels:** Groove welds ensure full penetration and leak-tightness.
- **Bridges (IR, NHAI):** Fillet and groove welds govern fatigue life of girders.
- **Failure mode studied in safety:** Improperly specified welds cause **brittle fracture**, **fatigue cracks**, and **lamellar tearing** — the core reason this topic is taught under *Safety in Design*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Effective Throat Thickness

The **effective throat** $t_e$ is the design dimension through which load is assumed to transfer across a fillet weld. For a **90° equal-leg fillet weld**, the geometry yields the relationship between leg size $s$ and throat $t$.

**Step 1 — Draw the geometric triangle.** The fillet weld cross-section is an isosceles right triangle with each leg of length $s$.

**Step 2 — Apply Pythagoras Theorem.** Hypotenuse (actual throat):
$$L^2 = s^2 + s^2 = 2 s^2$$

**Step 3 — Take square root.**
$$L = \sqrt{2 \, s^2} = s \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 4 — Express numerically.**
$$t_{\text{actual}} = \frac{s \sqrt{2}}{1} = 1.4142 \, s$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the reciprocal form.**
$$t_{\text{actual}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071 \, s$$

Therefore, the geometric throat thickness is:
$$t = 0.707 \, s$$

**Step 6 — Apply IS 800:2007 reduction factor.** To account for imperfect penetration and surface profile, IS 800 reduces the design throat to **0.7 s** (not 0.707 s).

$$
t_e = 0.7 \, s
$$

**Step 7 — Worked numerical example.** For a fillet weld with leg size $s = 10$ mm:

$$
t_e = 0.7 \times 10 = 7.0 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_{\text{actual}} = 0.707 \times 10 = 7.07 \text{ mm}
$$

The reduction (7.07 → 7.0 mm) is the **safety margin** mandated by the code.

## 3.2 Derivation of Fillet Weld Strength per Unit Length

**Step 1 — State the governing formula** (per IS 800:2007, Cl.10.4.7):
$$f_{wd} = \frac{f_{uw}}{\gamma_{mw}}$$

**Step 2 — Define each term.**

- $f_{uw}$ = ultimate strength of weld metal (MPa)
- $\gamma_{mw}$ = partial safety factor for weld metal

**Step 3 — Compute strength per unit length of weld.**
$$F = t_e \times f_{wd} = (0.7 \, s) \times \frac{f_{uw}}{\gamma_{mw}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**
$$
F = \frac{0.7 \, s \, f_{uw}}{\gamma_{mw}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values** for a common case: $s = 8$ mm, $f_{uw} = 410$ MPa, $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ (shop weld):
$$
F = \frac{0.7 \times 8 \times 410}{1.50} = \frac{2296}{1.50}
$$

$$
F = 1530.67 \text{ N/mm} \approx 1.53 \text{ kN/mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kN/m.**
$$
F_{\text{per metre}} = 1530.67 \times 1000 \times 10^{-3} = 1530.67 \text{ kN/m}
$$

## 3.3 Minimum Fillet Size Derivation (from IS 800 Table 21)

The minimum size is governed by the thicker plate joined, to ensure the weld cools slowly enough to avoid brittleness.

**Boundary conditions:**

$$
s_{\min} = \begin{cases}
3 \text{ mm}, & t \le 10 \text{ mm} \\
5 \text{ mm}, & 10 < t \le 20 \text{ mm} \\
6 \text{ mm}, & 20 < t \le 32 \text{ mm} \\
8 \text{ mm}, & t > 32 \text{ mm}
\end{cases}
$$

**Derivation logic:** As plate thickness increases, heat dissipates faster into the surrounding mass. A larger weld cross-section prevents the cooling rate from exceeding the critical rate that produces martensite (a brittle phase). The discrete jumps in $s_{\min}$ are empirically calibrated from weld cooling experiments.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Weld Selection

| Parameter | Fillet | Square Groove | V-Groove | U-Groove | Plug / Slot |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Edge prep | None | None | Bevelling | Machining | Drilling |
| Plate thickness | All | $\le 6$ mm | 6–20 mm | $> 20$ mm | All |
| Weld metal volume | Moderate | Low | High | Moderate | Low |
| Cost factor | Low | Very low | Moderate | High | Low |
| Stress distribution | Shear-bias | Direct tension | Direct tension | Direct tension | Local shear |
| Inspection difficulty | Easy | Easy | Moderate | Hard | Easy |
| Common use | Bracing, stiffeners | Thin sheets | Pressure vessels | Heavy shafts | Lap joints |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Reading a Complex Welding Symbol

**Given drawing annotation:** A weld symbol showing a triangle below the reference line, the digit "8", a length of "150", and a pitch of "100", with "G" inside the tail.

**Interpretation:**
- **Triangle below** = Fillet weld on the arrow side.
- **"8"** = Leg size $s = 8$ mm.
- **"150"** = Length of each weld segment = 150 mm.
- **"100"** = Pitch (centre-to-centre distance between segments) = 100 mm.
- **"G" in tail** = Grind finish required.

**Result:** *Intermittent fillet weld, 8 mm leg, 150 mm long, 100 mm pitch, ground flush, on the arrow side of the joint.*

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *length* with *pitch*. **Length = size of each weld run; Pitch = centre-to-centre spacing.** A classic 3-mark trap.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
