KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: Define a tension splice. State any two situations where a tension splice is unavoidable in structural steel design.** **Model Answer:** A tension splice is a connection provided to join two structural tension members end-to-end so that the tensile force is transmitted from one member to the other without any loss of load-carrying capacity. Two unavoidable situations: 1. **Length limitation of stock material** — Standard rolled plates/sections are limited to 6–12 m; longer members must be spliced. 2. **Transportation constraints** — Long members cannot be transported in one piece and must be fabricated and joined at site. *(Acceptable: change in cross-section, construction joints, replacement/retrofitting.)* **[3 marks]** --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Differentiate between a bearing-type bolted splice and a friction-grip (HSFG) bolted splice.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Bearing-type | HSFG (Friction-grip) | |---|---|---| | **Load transfer** | Through bolt shear and bearing | Through friction between clamped plates | | **Bolt pretension** | Not required | Mandatory (proof load) | | **Slip permitted** | Yes, slip can occur at service load | No slip at service load | | **Bolt grade** | 4.6 / 8.8 (ordinary) | 8.8 / 10.9 / 12.9 (high-strength) | | **Surface prep** | Not critical | Critical (μ_f depends on surface) | | **Application** | Light/medium buildings | Bridges, heavy industrial | **[3 marks]** --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **Part B Module questions carry 14 marks** with internal choice. **Two alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are provided.** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** A tension member of a truss consists of a plate **200 mm × 16 mm** of grade **Fe 410**. The plate is to be connected at site using a double-cover-plate bolted splice with **M20 bolts of grade 4.6**. The member carries a factored tensile force of **T = 600 kN** at the splice location. **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Design the splice — calculate the bolt value, number of bolts, and cover plate thickness. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Check the splice for (i) net section rupture of main plate, and (ii) block shear failure. --- #### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a): Design of Bolted Splice** **Given data:** - Main plate: $b = 200$ mm, $t = 16$ mm, Fe 410 ($f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_u = 410$ MPa) - Bolts: M20 grade 4.6 ($f_{ub} = 400$ MPa), $d_0 = 21.5$ mm - Factored force: $T = 600$ kN - $\gamma_{mb} = 1.25$, $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ **Step 1: Cover Plate Thickness** $$t_c = 0.6 \times t = 0.6 \times 16 = 9.6 \text{ mm}$$ Provide cover plates of **10 mm thickness**, with width $b_c = 200$ mm. **Step 2: Bolt Shear Strength (Double Shear)** Nominal bolt area: $A_{nb} = 0.78 \times \pi \times 20^2 / 4 = 245.04 \text{ mm}^2$ $$V_{\text{dsb, shear}} = \frac{2 \cdot f_{ub} \cdot A_{nb}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mb}} = \frac{2 \times 400 \times 245.04}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25 \times 10^3} = 90.55 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3: Bolt Bearing Strength** Take $k_b = 0.50$ (standard pitch and edge distance). Cover plate thickness governs: $$V_{\text{dsb, bearing}} = \frac{2.5 \cdot k_b \cdot d \cdot t_c \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.50 \times 20 \times 10 \times 410}{1.25 \times 10^3} = 82.0 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4: Bolt Value** $$V_{\text{dsb}} = \min(90.55,\ 82.0) = 82.0 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5: Number of Bolts** $$n = \frac{600}{82.0} = 7.32 \rightarrow \text{Provide } 8 \text{ bolts (4 on each side)}$$ **Step 6: Arrangement** - Edge distance: $e = 1.5 \times 21.5 = 32.25$ mm → Provide **35 mm** - Pitch: $p = 2.5 \times 20 = 50$ mm → Provide **60 mm** - Gauge between rows: **60 mm** **[7 marks]** --- **Part (b): Net Section Rupture + Block Shear Check** **(i) Net Section Rupture of Main Plate** Failure path passes through **one transverse hole**: $$A_n = (200 - 21.5) \times 16 = 178.5 \times 16 = 2856 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$A_n / A_g = 2856 / 3200 = 0.893$$ From IS 800 Table 11 (interpolation): $$\alpha \approx 0.72$$ $$T_{\text{dn}} = \frac{0.9 \cdot \alpha \cdot A_n \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{m1}} = \frac{0.9 \times 0.72 \times 2856 \times 410}{1.25 \times 10^3} = 606.86 \text{ kN}$$ Since $T_{\text{dn}} = 606.86$ kN $> T = 600$ kN → **SAFE** ✓ **(ii) Block Shear Check** Shear plane length: $L_v = 60 + 60 = 120$ mm Tension plane length: $L_t = 35 - 21.5/2 = 24.25$ mm $$A_{vg} = 2 \times 120 \times 16 = 3840 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$A_{tn} = 24.25 \times 16 = 388 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$T_{\text{db1}} = \frac{A_{vg} f_y}{\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0}} + \frac{0.9 A_{tn} f_u}{\gamma_{m1}}$$ $$T_{\text{db1}} = \frac{3840 \times 250}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10 \times 10^3} + \frac{0.9 \times 388 \times 410}{1.25 \times 10^3}$$ $$T_{\text{db1}} = \frac{960{,}000}{1905.26} + \frac{143{,}172}{1250} = 503.86 + 114.54 = 618.40 \text{ kN}$$ $$A_{vn} = 2 \times (60 - 21.5/2) \times 16 = 2 \times 49.25 \times 16 = 1576 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$A_{tg} = 35 \times 16 = 560 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$T_{\text{db2}} = \frac{0.9 A_{vn} f_u}{\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m1}} + \frac{A_{tg} f_y}{\gamma_{m0}}$$ $$T_{\text{db2}} = \frac{0.9 \times 1576 \times 410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.25 \times 10^3} + \frac{560 \times 250}{1.10 \times 10^3}$$ $$T_{\text{db2}} = \frac{581{,}544}{2165.06} + \frac{140{,}000}{1100} = 268.60 + 127.27 = 395.87 \text{ kN}$$ Block shear strength = $\min(618.40,\ 395.87) = 395.87$ kN **⚠️ CRITICAL OBSERVATION:** Block shear strength (395.87 kN) < Applied force (600 kN) → **DESIGN FAILS in block shear!** **Remedial action:** - Increase edge distance (e) to 50 mm → improves block shear - Reduce pitch to 50 mm → reduces shear path - Use larger bolt diameter (M24) → fewer bolts needed → longer pitch possible **Revised Design (after correction):** Provide edge distance = 50 mm and pitch = 50 mm: $L_v = 50 + 50 = 100$ mm $L_t = 50 - 21.5/2 = 39.25$ mm $A_{vg} = 2 \times 100 \times 16 = 3200 \text{ mm}^2$ $A_{tn} = 39.25 \times 16 = 628 \text{ mm}^2$ $$T_{\text{db1}} = \frac{3200 \times 250}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10 \times 10^3} + \frac{0.9 \times 628 \times 410}{1.25 \times 10^3} = 419.91 + 185.36 = 605.27 \text{ kN}$$ Now block shear ≈ 605 kN > 600 kN ✓ **[7 marks]** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > Most students compute only **bolt shear and bearing**, forgetting the **block shear check**. Block shear failure is the **#1 reason for tension splice failures in real structures**. Always verify $T_{db} \geq T_{applied}$ before finalising the design. **Loss of up to 4 marks** if missed. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]** A factory-fabricated tension member of grade **Fe 410** is to be spliced using a **complete penetration butt weld**. The cross-section is **200 mm × 16 mm** and the applied factored tensile force is **T = 540 kN**. **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the design strength of the butt-welded splice and state whether it is adequate. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If field conditions necessitate a **fillet-welded splice** using side fillets of size **10 mm**, calculate the **weld length required on each side** of the splice. --- #### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a): Butt Welded Splice** For a **complete penetration butt weld** in Fe 410 plate: $$f_{\text{weld}} = f_{\text{parent metal}} = f_u = 410 \text{ MPa}$$ (Strength of butt weld = strength of weaker of base metal and weld metal; both are Fe 410.) Design strength of splice: $$T_{\text{d, butt}} = \frac{A_g \cdot f_u}{\gamma_{mw}}$$ For **shop weld**: $\gamma_{mw} = 1.25$ $$T_{\text{d, butt, shop}} = \frac{200 \times 16 \times 410}{1.25 \times 10^3} = \frac{1{,}312{,}000}{1250} = 1049.6 \text{ kN}$$ For **field weld**: $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ $$T_{\text{d, butt, field}} = \frac{200 \times 16 \times 410}{1.50 \times 10^3} = \frac{1{,}312{,}000}{1500} = 874.67 \text{ kN}$$ In both cases: $$T_{\text{d, butt}} \gg T_{\text{applied}} = 540 \text{ kN} \rightarrow \textbf{ADEQUATE ✓}$$ **Conclusion:** The butt weld splice is **fully adequate** for both shop and field conditions. No additional reinforcement is needed. **[7 marks]** --- **Part (b): Fillet Welded Splice (Side Fillet)** Given: Fillet weld size $s = 10$ mm, grade Fe 410 ($f_u = 410$ MPa), field welded. **Step 1: Effective Throat Thickness** $$t_t = 0.7 \cdot s = 0.7 \times 10 = 7 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Design Strength of Fillet Weld per unit length** Field weld: $\gamma_{mw} = 1.50$ $$f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}} = \frac{410}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.50} = \frac{410}{2.598} = 157.78 \text{ MPa}$$ Strength per mm length: $$f_{\text{wd, per mm}} = t_t \cdot f_{wd} = 7 \times 157.78 = 1104.5 \text{ N/mm} = 1.1045 \text{ kN/mm}$$ **Step 3: Required Total Weld Length** Total weld (on both sides of splice): $$l_{w,\text{total}} = \frac{T}{f_{\text{wd, per mm}}} = \frac{540}{1.1045} = 488.91 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4: Weld Length on Each Side** $$l_w = \frac{488.91}{2} = 244.45 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{Provide } 250 \text{ mm per side}$$ **Step 5: Effective Length Check** Effective length = actual length − 2 × weld size (for overrun): $$l_{\text{eff}} = 250 - 2 \times 10 = 230 \text{ mm}$$ Re-check: $$T_{\text{provided}} = 2 \times 230 \times 1.1045 = 508.07 \text{ kN} < 540 \text{ kN}$$ **⚠️ Insufficient!** Increase weld length. **Revised length:** $$l_{\text{eff, required}} = \frac{540}{2 \times 1.1045} = 244.45 \text{ mm}$$ $$l_w = 244.45 + 2 \times 10 = 264.45 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{Provide } 270 \text{ mm per side}$$ Maximum weld length per IS 800 Cl. 10.5.4.1: $$l_{w,\text{max}} = 100 \cdot t_t = 100 \times 7 = 700 \text{ mm}$$ Since 270 mm < 700 mm → **Acceptable** ✓ **Final Weld Specification:** - Weld size $s = 10$ mm - Weld length per side = **270 mm** - Use **two side fillets** (one on each side of the splice) - Effective length = 250 mm per side **[7 marks]** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Weld Length:** > 1. **Forgetting to deduct 2× weld size** for overrun → loses 2 marks. > 2. **Confusing field vs shop γ_mw** → wrong partial safety factor → complete failure of design. > 3. **Not checking $l_{w,\text{max}} = 100 t_t$ limit** → invalid design. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Master Warning Summary > [!WARNING] > **FATAL ERRORS That Cause Full Mark Deductions in Tension Splice Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting block shear check** (most common, 4 marks lost). > 2. **Using cover plate thickness less than t/2** — causes net section rupture in cover plate before main plate yields. > 3. **Wrong partial safety factor for HSFG bolts** ($\gamma_{mb} = 1.10$ for HSFG, **not 1.25**). > 4. **Confusing shop vs field welds** — $\gamma_{mw}$ differs (1.25 vs 1.50). > 5. **Incorrect bolt shear planes** — single shear vs double shear changes calculation by factor of 2. > 6. **Not rounding up bolt number** to even integer for symmetric splice. > 7. **Applying α = 0.9 for <4 bolts case** — this is incorrect; use α only when ≥4 bolts. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — TENSION SPLICE** ### Core Definitions - ☐ Tension splice = end-to-end connection of tension members to transfer axial tensile force. - ☐ Splice design force = max(factored tension at splice, design strength of member at splice). - ☐ Splice is preferred at locations of **minimum moment / low stress**. ### Three Splice Types - ☐ **Bearing-type bolted** (most common for buildings). - ☐ **HSFG bolted** (for bridges, dynamic loads). - ☐ **Welded** (factory-controlled; butt weld ideal). ### Design Formulae (Memorise) - ☐ $T_{dg} = A_g f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ (gross yielding) - ☐ $T_{dn} = 0.9 \alpha A_n f_u / \gamma_{m1}$ (net section rupture, ≥4 bolts) - ☐ $T_{db} = \min(T_{db1}, T_{db2})$ (block shear) - ☐ $V_{dsb} = \min(V_{dsb,shear}, V_{dsb,bearing})$ (bolt value) - ☐ $V_{dsf} = \mu_f n_e K_h F_0 / \gamma_{mb}$ (HSFG slip) - ☐ $f_{wd} = f_u / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{mw})$ (weld strength) ### Partial Safety Factors - ☐ γ_m0 = 1.10 | γ_m1 = 1.25 | γ_mb = 1.25 (bearing) / 1.10 (HSFG) | γ_mw = 1.25 (shop) / 1.50 (field) ### Spacing & Detailing Rules - ☐ Edge distance: $e_{\min} = 1.5 d_0$ (machine cut), $1.7 d_0$ (rolled) - ☐ Pitch: $p_{\min} = 2.5 d$ | $p_{\max} = 32t$ or 300 mm - ☐ Cover plate thickness: $t_c \geq t/2$ (use 0.6t practically) - ☐ Weld effective length: $l_{\text{eff}} = l_{\text{actual}} - 2s$ ### Critical Failure Modes (Always Check) - ☐ **Net section rupture** (main plate AND cover plate) - ☐ **Block shear** (often overlooked) - ☐ **Bolt shear & bearing** - ☐ **Edge tear-out** ### KTU Quick Memory Hook > **"S-A-N-B" — Splice Always Needs Block shear check"** > **S**hear in bolts > **A**dequate cover plates > **N**et section rupture > **B**lock shear --- *End of Module 4 — Tension Splice Notes* *PESFT754 — Safety in Design of Structures | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Compression Member: Strength of an Axially Loaded Compression Member
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compression Member: Strength of an Axially Loaded Compression Member

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **axially loaded compression member** (commonly called a **column** or **strut**) is a structural member subjected to a compressive force acting along its centroidal longitudinal axis. Unlike a tension member where failure occurs by yielding or rupture of the material, the strength of a compression member is governed primarily by its **stability against buckling** rather than by its crushing strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Definition (KTU Expected Wording):**
> "An axially loaded compression member is a structural element in which the resultant of the external loads acts parallel to the longitudinal axis and produces compressive stresses throughout the cross-section. Its design strength is governed by the **Euler buckling load**, **Rankine-Gordon load**, or the **Perry-Robertson buckling curves** of IS 800, depending on the **slenderness ratio** of the member."

The **strength of an axially loaded compression member** is defined as the maximum axial compressive load that the member can sustain before failure, where failure may occur by any of the following modes:

1. **Flexural (Euler) Buckling** — Sudden lateral deflection of a slender member.
2. **Local Buckling** — Localized plate buckling in thin sections.
3. **Crushing / Yielding** — Material failure in short, stocky members.
4. **Combined Bending and Axial Load (Beam-Column Behaviour)** — Eccentric loading effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Strength ≠ Yield Strength. For compression members, strength = minimum of {Crushing strength, Buckling strength}. This is a frequent 2-mark question.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **thin plastic ruler vertically** on a table and pressing down on the top with your palm:

- If the ruler is **very short (stocky)** → it simply crushes or yields at the base. (Material failure)
- If the ruler is **moderately long** → it bends slightly but you can still increase the load a little before it fails.
- If the ruler is **very long and slender** → even the slightest push causes it to suddenly **bow sideways** with a snap — this is **buckling**, not crushing.

This is the essence of compression member design: **a longer member is weaker, not because its material changed, but because of geometry-induced instability.**

| Real-World Analogy | Compression Member Concept |
|---|---|
| Length of the ruler | **Effective length (L_e)** of the column |
| Thickness / cross-section of the ruler | **Moment of Inertia (I) / Radius of Gyration (r)** |
| Force required to bend the ruler | **Euler Critical Load (P_cr)** |
| Direction in which the ruler bends most easily | **Axis of least flexural rigidity (weakest axis)** |

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus for Structural Steel:** **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200 GPa)**
- **Yield Stress for Mild Steel (Fe 410):** **f_y = 250 N/mm²**
- **Buckling Classes in IS 800:** **a, b, c, d** (with 'a' being the strongest)
- **Reference yield stress (f_yb):** The minimum specified yield stress of the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PESFT754 – Module 4):**
> This topic is the foundation for the **'Secant Formula', 'Perry-Robertson Formula', 'Beam-Column Behaviour'** and **'Design of Compression Members'** that follow in subsequent modules. Master this first.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Classification of Compression Members

Compression members are classified based on their **slenderness ratio (λ)**, which dictates the governing failure mode.

**Slenderness Ratio:** $$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r}$$

where L_e = effective length, r = radius of gyration = √(I/A).

| Class | Slenderness Ratio (λ) | Governing Failure Mode | Governing Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Long Columns** | λ ≥ λ_c (critical) | Elastic Flexural Buckling | **Euler's Formula** |
| **Intermediate Columns** | Intermediate range | Inelastic Buckling | **Rankine-Gordon / Johnson's Formula** |
| **Short Columns** | λ small (≤ ~20 for steel) | Crushing / Yielding | **f_y × A (Yielding)** |

The **critical slenderness ratio** (λ_c) separating Euler from Rankine regime is:

$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{f_y}}$$

For Fe 410 (E = 2×10⁵, f_y = 250):
$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{250}} \approx 88.86$$

---

### 2.2 Effective Length (L_e) — End Condition Theory

The effective length L_e depends on the **rotational and translational restraints** at the column ends. It is expressed as:

$$L_e = K \cdot L$$

where **K** is the **effective length factor** depending on end conditions.

| End Condition (in one plane) | Theoretical K | Effective Length |
|---|---|---|
| Both ends hinged (pinned) | **1.0** | L |
| Both ends fixed | **0.5** | 0.5 L |
| One end fixed, other end free (cantilever) | **2.0** | 2.0 L |
| One end fixed, other end hinged | **0.7** (≈ 0.65) | 0.7 L |
| One end fixed, other end guided (translation prevented) | **1.0** | 1.0 L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why K matters for KTU exam:** IS 800 Table 11 and the design tables you will use later in the semester rely on L_e. Always convert actual length L to effective length L_e first.

---

### 2.3 Euler's Theory of Column Buckling (The Foundation)

**Assumptions of Euler's Theory:**
1. The column is perfectly straight, initially unloaded.
2. The load is purely axial and applied through the centroid.
3. Material is perfectly elastic, homogeneous, isotropic.
4. Self-weight is neglected.
5. End conditions are ideal (theoretical cases).
6. Buckling occurs in the plane of least flexural rigidity.

**The Euler Critical Buckling Load:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^{\,2}}$$

**Equivalent Critical Stress:**

$$f_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E}{\lambda^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Using actual length L instead of effective length L_e in the Euler formula. IS 800 / KTU questions always require **L_e** (or K·L).

---

### 2.4 Rankine-Gordon Formula (For Intermediate Columns)

Empirically bridges the gap between Euler (long) and crushing (short) regimes:

$$\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{P_{cr}} + \frac{1}{P_c}$$

$$P_R = \frac{P_c}{1 + a \left(\frac{L_e}{r}\right)^2} = \frac{P_c}{1 + a \, \lambda^2}$$

where:
- **P_c** = Crushing load = f_c · A
- **a** = Rankine's constant (empirical, depends on material)
- **λ** = slenderness ratio = L_e / r

**Rankine's Critical Stress:**

$$f_R = \frac{f_c}{1 + a \, \lambda^2}$$

> For mild steel, IS code uses a ≈ 1/7500 to 1/9000.

---

### 2.5 The Perry-Robertson Approach (Used in IS 800)

IS 800:2007 (and the 2024 aligned revisions) uses the **Perry-Robertson formula** modified with multiple column curves (a, b, c, d) to account for **initial imperfections (out-of-straightness)** and **residual stresses**.

The **stress reduction factor χ** (chi) is:

$$\chi = \frac{f_{cd}}{f_y} \leq 1.0$$

where f_cd = design compressive stress.

$$\chi = \frac{1}{\phi_L + \sqrt{\phi_L^{\,2} - \lambda_0^{\,2}}} \leq 1.0$$

with:
$$\phi_L = 0.5\left[1 + \alpha(\lambda_0 - 0.2) + \lambda_0^{\,2}\right]$$

$$\lambda_0 = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{f_{cc}}} = \frac{\lambda}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{f_y}{E}}$$

$$\alpha = \text{imperfection factor (depends on buckling class a, b, c, d)}$$

| Buckling Class | Imperfection Factor α |
|---|---|
| **a** | **0.21** |
| **b** | **0.34** |
| **c** | **0.49** |
| **d** | **0.76** |

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINT THIS PAGE BEFORE EXAM** — these formulas cover ~85% of numerical problems.

| # | Formula Name | Expression | When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = L_e / r$ | Always as a first step | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Euler Critical Load | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^{\,2}$ | Long columns (λ > λ_c) | N |
| 3 | Euler Critical Stress | $f_{cr} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$ | Long columns | N/mm² |
| 4 | Critical Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{E / f_y}$ | To classify column | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Effective Length | $L_e = K \cdot L$ | Always | mm |
| 6 | Radius of Gyration | $r = \sqrt{I / A}$ | Cross-section step | mm |
| 7 | Rankine-Gordon Load | $P_R = P_c / (1 + a \lambda^2)$ | Intermediate columns | N |
| 8 | Crushing Load | $P_c = f_c \cdot A$ | Short columns | N |
| 9 | Nondimensional Slenderness | $\lambda_0 = (\lambda / \pi)\sqrt{f_y / E}$ | IS 800 / Perry-Robertson | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Stress Reduction Factor | $\chi = 1/(\phi_L + \sqrt{\phi_L^{\,2} - \lambda_0^{\,2}})$ | IS 800 design | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Design Compressive Stress | $f_{cd} = \chi \cdot f_y / \gamma_{M_0}$ | IS 800 strength check | N/mm² |
| 12 | Design Strength | $P_d = f_{cd} \cdot A$ | Final answer | N |
| 13 | Perry Auxiliary Function | $\phi_L = 0.5[1 + \alpha(\lambda_0 - 0.2) + \lambda_0^{\,2}]$ | IS 800 / Perry | Dimensionless |

> **Note on Units:** All lengths in mm, area in mm², stress in N/mm² (= MPa), load in N. Convert kN to N (× 1000) when needed.

---

### 2.7 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters in Real Practice

- **Steel Buildings:** Every column in a multi-storey frame (e.g., KTU campus buildings, Infosys towers) is designed using the Perry-Robertson curves of IS 800.
- **Bridges:** Compression members of trusses (top chords) are sized such that λ along the weak axis does not exceed 180 (IS 800 limit).
- **Towers & Masts:** Communication towers use long slender angles — Euler buckling governs.
- **Offshore Jackets:** Tubular braces are designed for both local (d/t ratio) and global Euler buckling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Critical Load (From First Principles)

**Setup:** Consider a pin-ended (hinged-hinged) column of length L, flexural rigidity EI, subjected to an axial compressive load P slightly less than P_cr. The column is in a slightly deflected equilibrium configuration.

Let y(x) be the lateral deflection at distance x from the bottom support.

**Step 1 — Bending Moment Equation at section x:**

The bending moment at any cross-section due to deflection y is:

$$M(x) = -P \cdot y$$

(Negative sign: P creates a moment that tends to increase deflection.)

**Step 2 — Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation:**

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = -P \cdot y$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \frac{P}{EI} \, y = 0$$

**Step 3 — Let k² = P / (EI):**

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 \, y = 0$$

The general solution is:

$$y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions (Hinged-Hinged):**

- At x = 0: y = 0 → A = 0
- At x = L: y = 0 → A cos(kL) + B sin(kL) = 0

Since A = 0: **B sin(kL) = 0**

For non-trivial deflection (B ≠ 0), we need:

$$kL = n\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{n\pi}{L}$$

where n = 1, 2, 3, ... (n = 1 gives the **lowest critical load**).

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$\frac{P}{EI} = k^2 = \left(\frac{\pi}{L}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, EI}{L^2}}$$

For general end conditions, replace L by **L_e = K·L**:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, EI}{(K \cdot L)^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \, EI}{L_e^{\,2}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Effective Length for Fixed-Fixed Column

**Setup:** A column fixed at both ends (no rotation, no translation). When buckling occurs, an inflection point (point of contraflexure) appears at the mid-height, at which the moment is zero. The deflected shape mirrors that of a pin-ended column of half the length.

**Reasoning:**

By symmetry, the midpoint behaves like a pin. The actual column length L thus behaves like two pin-ended columns of length L/2 in series.

Substituting L → L/2 in Euler's formula:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/2)^2} = \frac{4 \pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

Comparing with general formula $P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{(K \cdot L)^2}$:

$$(K \cdot L)^2 = \frac{L^2}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 0.5$$

$$\boxed{L_e = 0.5 \, L \quad \text{for fixed-fixed ends}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:**
A steel column of ISHB 300 @ 0.588 kN/m has the following properties:
- Depth h = 300 mm, flange width b = 250 mm
- Cross-sectional Area A = 7480 mm²
- I_xx = 12519.6 × 10⁴ mm⁴
- I_yy = 4191.6 × 10⁴ mm⁴
- Length L = 4 m
- Both ends effectively pinned
- E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²

**Find:** Euler's critical buckling load.

---

**Solution (Step-by-Step, every transition explained):**

**Step 1 — Identify weak axis (least I):**

$$I_{xx} = 12519.6 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4, \quad I_{yy} = 4191.6 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4$$

Since $I_{yy} < I_{xx}$, the column will buckle about the **y-y axis** (weak axis buckling). Use $I_{yy}$.

$$I_{min} = I_{yy} = 4191.6 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Effective length (both ends pinned):**

$$K = 1.0, \quad L_e = K \cdot L = 1.0 \times 4000 = 4000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's formula:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, I_{min}}{L_e^{\,2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{(3.1416)^2 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (4191.6 \times 10^4)}{(4000)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 4.1916 \times 10^7$$

$$= 9.8696 \times 2 \times 4.1916 \times 10^{12}$$

$$= 82.733 \times 10^{12}$$

$$= 8.2733 \times 10^{13} \text{ N·mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator:**

$$(4000)^2 = 1.6 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Divide:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{8.2733 \times 10^{13}}{1.6 \times 10^7}$$

$$P_{cr} = 5.1708 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 5171 \text{ kN} \approx 5170.8 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 7 — Verification: classify as long or intermediate?**

Slenderness ratio:
$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r_{yy}} = \frac{4000}{\sqrt{I_{yy}/A}} = \frac{4000}{\sqrt{4191.6 \times 10^4 / 7480}}$$

$$= \frac{4000}{\sqrt{5605.35}} = \frac{4000}{74.87} \approx 53.42$$

Critical slenderness for Fe 410: λ_c ≈ 88.86.
Since λ = 53.42 < 88.86 → **Intermediate column** → Euler's result is an over-estimate (Rankine's formula would be more appropriate).

This verification step is what distinguishes a topper from an average student.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Rankine's Formula

**Same column as above.** Take Rankine's constant a = 1/7500 for mild steel.

**Step 1 — Crushing load:**

Assume f_c = f_y = 250 N/mm² (for stocky assumption, full yielding):

$$P_c = f_c \cdot A = 250 \times 7480 = 1,870,000 \text{ N} = 1870 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Slenderness ratio (already computed):**

$$\lambda \approx 53.42$$

**Step 3 — Apply Rankine-Gordon:**

$$P_R = \frac{P_c}{1 + a \lambda^2} = \frac{1,870,000}{1 + (1/7500)(53.42)^2}$$

$$\lambda^2 = 2853.7$$

$$a \lambda^2 = 2853.7 / 7500 = 0.3805$$

$$1 + a\lambda^2 = 1.3805$$

$$P_R = \frac{1,870,000}{1.3805} \approx 1,354,500 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_R \approx 1354.5 \text{ kN}}$$

> Notice how Rankine gives a **realistic, lower value** than Euler (5171 kN) for this intermediate column — this is why IS code uses Rankine-based Perry-Robertson.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Formula

| Given Data | First Check | Then Use |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-section (I, A), L, end conditions | Compute L_e and λ | Proceed |
| λ > λ_c (≈ 89 for Fe 410) | Long column | **Euler** |
| λ < λ_c but λ > ~20 | Intermediate | **Rankine-Gordon** |
| λ very small (< ~20) | Short / stocky | **Crushing (P = f_y × A)** |
| Member with initial bow (imperfection) | Any λ | **Perry-Robertson (IS 800)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Design Procedure Flow for Axially Loaded Compression Member

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Given P, L, End Conditions, Section"] --> B["Compute Effective Length Le = K × L"]
    B --> C["Compute Imin, rmin, Area A"]
    C --> D["Compute Slenderness Ratio λ = Le divided by rmin"]
    D --> E["Compute Critical Slenderness λc = π times sqrt of E over fy"]
    E --> F{"Is λ greater than λc?"}
    F -- "YES" --> G["LONG COLUMN: Apply EULER Pcr = π²EI divided by Le squared"]
    F -- "NO" --> H{"Is λ greater than 20 approximately?"}
    H -- "YES" --> I["INTERMEDIATE COLUMN: Apply RANKINE-GORDON PR = Pc divided by 1 plus a λ²"]
    H -- "NO" --> J["SHORT COLUMN: Crushing governs P = fy × A"]
    G --> K["Apply Safety Factor γm0 = 1.10"]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L["Design Strength Pd = Pcr or PR or Pc divided by γm0"]
    L --> M{"Is Pd greater than or equal to Applied Load P?"}
    M -- "YES" --> N["SAFE: Section is Adequate"]
    M -- "NO" --> O["UNSAFE: Increase Section Size or Add Bracing"]
    O --> B
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Column Buckling Failure Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Axial Compression Load P Applied"] --> S2["Identify Member Geometry and Material"]
    S2 --> S3{"Failure Mode Determined by Slenderness λ"}
    S3 -- "Long slender λ greater than 89" --> S4["FLEXURAL BUCKLING: Sudden Lateral Bowing"]
    S3 -- "Intermediate 20 less than λ less than 89" --> S5["INELASTIC BUCKLING: Combined Material Yield and Instability"]
    S3 -- "Short stocky λ less than 20" --> S6["CRUSHING: Material Yields Compressively"]
    S3 -- "Thin plates in section" --> S7["LOCAL BUCKLING: Plate Element Bows Out"]
    S3 -- "Eccentric load or end moment" --> S8["BEAM-COLUMN FAILURE: Combined Bending and Compression"]
    S4 --> S9["Design Strength governed by EULER"]
    S5 --> S10["Design Strength governed by RANKINE or PERRY"]
    S6 --> S11["Design Strength governed by MATERIAL YIELD"]
    S7 --> S12["Design Strength governed by EFFECTIVE WIDTH METHOD"]
    S8 --> S13["Design Strength governed by SECANT FORMULA or INTERACTION EQUATION"]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic: End Conditions and Effective Length Factor (K)

```
  Both Ends Pinned (Hinged)        Both Ends Fixed
         |  P                            |  P
         v                               v
         |                               |
    -----+-----                    ------+------
         |                               |  <- No rotation, no translation
         |                               |
    -----+-----                    ------+------
         ^                               ^
         |  P                            |  P

   K = 1.0                          K = 0.5
   Le = L                           Le = 0.5 L


  One End Fixed, One End Free       One End Fixed, One End Hinged
       (Cantilever)                       (Propped Cantilever)
         |  P                                |  P
         v                                   v

    -----+------                          --+----------
         |                                  |  <- Fixed at base
         |                                  |
         |                                  |
         ^                                  ^
         |                                  |
                                  ------------+
                                      Fixed

   K = 2.0                          K ≈ 0.7 (≈ 0.65 exact)
   Le = 2.0 L                       Le = 0.7 L
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define the following terms with respect to an axially loaded compression member:
(a) Slenderness ratio
(b) Effective length
(c) Critical buckling load

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Slenderness Ratio (λ):** The ratio of the effective length (L_e) of the compression member to the **least radius of gyration (r_min)** of its cross-section.

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \frac{L_e}{\sqrt{I_{min}/A}}$$

It is a dimensionless parameter that classifies columns as long, intermediate, or short.

(b) **Effective Length (L_e):** The length of an equivalent pin-ended column having the same buckling load as the actual column with its given end conditions. It is expressed as $L_e = K \cdot L$, where K is the effective length factor.

For example: K = 1.0 for both ends hinged, K = 0.5 for both ends fixed, K = 2.0 for a cantilever (one end fixed, other free).

(c) **Critical Buckling Load (P_cr):** The axial compressive load at which a straight, prismatic column, perfectly elastic, will deflect laterally by an indefinite amount under a constant load. Mathematically:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L_e^{\,2}}$$

It represents the **stability limit** of the column, beyond which the member fails by lateral buckling rather than by crushing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

State **Euler's assumptions** in the derivation of the critical load for a long column.

**Model Answer:**

The Euler theory is based on the following assumptions:

1. The column is **initially perfectly straight** and the load is truly axial (concentric).
2. The **cross-section is uniform** throughout the length of the column.
3. The **column material is perfectly elastic**, homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
4. The **self-weight of the column is neglected** in comparison to the applied load.
5. The **end conditions are ideal** — perfectly hinged or perfectly fixed.
6. **Shortening of the column due to direct compression** is negligible compared to the bending deflection.
7. Buckling occurs in the **plane of least flexural rigidity** (weak axis).
8. There are **no initial stresses** (residual stresses, eccentricities, or lateral loads).

> These assumptions limit Euler's theory to **long, slender, perfectly elastic columns**. For real columns with imperfections, the **Perry-Robertson** approach of IS 800 is used.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Format:** Answer ANY ONE of the following:

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2]**

A **hollow circular steel column** of external diameter D = 200 mm and internal diameter d = 150 mm is used as a compression member. The length of the column is **6 m**, and both ends are **effectively held in position but not restrained against rotation** (i.e., both ends hinged).

Take E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² and f_y = 250 N/mm².

**(a)** Calculate the **Euler's critical buckling load** for the column. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Determine the **slenderness ratio** and the **critical slenderness ratio**. Comment on whether Euler's formula is **strictly applicable** in this case. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- D = 200 mm, d = 150 mm, L = 6000 mm
- E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm², f_y = 250 N/mm²
- End conditions: Both ends hinged → K = 1.0 → L_e = L = 6000 mm

---

**(a) Euler's Critical Buckling Load [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area (A):**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d^2\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(200^2 - 150^2\right)$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{4}\left(40000 - 22500\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\times 17500$$

$$\boxed{A = 13744.5 \text{ mm}^2}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia (I):**

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^4 - d^4\right)$$

$$D^4 = (200)^4 = 1.6 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$d^4 = (150)^4 = 0.50625 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\times\left(1.6 - 0.50625\right)\times 10^9$$

$$= \frac{\pi}{64}\times 1.09375 \times 10^9$$

$$= 0.05368 \times 10^9 = 5.368 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\boxed{I = 5.368 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4}$$

**Step 3 — Euler's Critical Load:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^{\,2}} = \frac{(3.1416)^2 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (5.368 \times 10^7)}{(6000)^2}$$

**Numerator:**

$$9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 5.368 \times 10^7$$

$$= 9.8696 \times 1.0736 \times 10^{13}$$

$$= 10.596 \times 10^{13} = 1.0596 \times 10^{14} \text{ N·mm}^2$$

**Denominator:**

$$(6000)^2 = 3.6 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Final:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{1.0596 \times 10^{14}}{3.6 \times 10^7} = 2.9433 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 2943.3 \text{ kN}}$$

---

**(b) Slenderness Ratio and Applicability [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Radius of Gyration (r_min):**

For a circular hollow section, I_xx = I_yy = I (symmetric):

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.368 \times 10^7}{13744.5}}$$

$$= \sqrt{3905.5} \approx 62.49 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Slenderness Ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{6000}{62.49}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 96.02}$$

**Step 3 — Critical Slenderness Ratio:**

$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{f_y}} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{250}}$$

$$= \pi \times \sqrt{800} = \pi \times 28.284 = 88.86$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_c \approx 88.86}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Applicability:**

Since **λ = 96.02 > λ_c = 88.86**, the column is a **long column**. The actual slenderness ratio exceeds the critical slenderness ratio, which means:

- The Euler's critical stress $f_{cr} = \pi^2 E / \lambda^2$ is **less than the yield stress f_y**.
- The column will fail by **elastic flexural buckling** before the material yields.
- **Euler's formula is STRICTLY APPLICABLE** in this case.

$$f_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{2.9433 \times 10^6}{13744.5} = 214.1 \text{ N/mm}^2 < 250 \text{ N/mm}^2 = f_y \quad \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 1]**

A **mild steel column** of section ISMB 400 (properties below) has an **effective length of 4.5 m**. It is subjected to an **axial compressive load of 1200 kN**.

Section Properties (ISMB 400):
- A = 7870 mm²
- r_min = 31.5 mm (about y-y axis)
- f_y = 250 N/mm², E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²
- Rankine's constant a = 1/7500

**(a)** Determine the **strength of the column** using **(i) Euler's formula** and **(ii) Rankine-Gordon formula**. **[8 Marks]**

**(b)** Comment on the **safe or unsafe nature** of the column and recommend a **suitable course of action**. **[6 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- L_e = 4500 mm, A = 7870 mm², r_min = 31.5 mm
- f_y = 250 N/mm², E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm², a = 1/7500
- Applied load P = 1200 kN = 1,200,000 N

---

**(a)(i) Euler's Critical Load [4 Marks]**

**Step 1 — I_min from r_min:**

$$I_{min} = A \cdot r_{min}^2 = 7870 \times (31.5)^2$$

$$= 7870 \times 992.25 = 7.808 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Euler's load:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L_e^{\,2}} = \frac{9.8696 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (7.808 \times 10^6)}{(4500)^2}$$

**Numerator:** $9.8696 \times 2 \times 7.808 \times 10^{11} = 15.412 \times 10^{12} = 1.5412 \times 10^{13}$

**Denominator:** $(4500)^2 = 2.025 \times 10^7$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{1.5412 \times 10^{13}}{2.025 \times 10^7} = 7.611 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 761.1 \text{ kN}}$$

---

**(a)(ii) Rankine-Gordon Critical Load [4 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Crushing load:**

$$P_c = f_c \cdot A = 250 \times 7870 = 1,967,500 \text{ N} = 1967.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \frac{4500}{31.5} \approx 142.86$$

**Step 3 — Rankine's formula:**

$$P_R = \frac{P_c}{1 + a \lambda^2}$$

$$\lambda^2 = 20408.16$$

$$a \lambda^2 = \frac{20408.16}{7500} = 2.721$$

$$1 + a\lambda^2 = 3.721$$

$$P_R = \frac{1967.5}{3.721} = 528.75 \text{ kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_R \approx 528.75 \text{ kN}}$$

---

**(b) Safety Assessment and Recommendation [6 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Design Strength (with partial safety factor γ_m0 = 1.10):**

$$P_{d,\text{Euler}} = \frac{P_{cr}}{\gamma_{m_0}} = \frac{761.1}{1.10} \approx 691.9 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_{d,\text{Rankine}} = \frac{P_R}{\gamma_{m_0}} = \frac{528.75}{1.10} \approx 480.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Applied Load:**

- Applied P = **1200 kN**
- Rankine Design Strength = **480.7 kN** (the governing, lower value)
- Euler Design Strength = 691.9 kN (over-estimate, since column is intermediate)

Since **P_applied (1200 kN) >> P_design (480.7 kN)**, the column is **UNSAFE**.

**Step 3 — Recommendations:**

1. **Increase cross-section:** Try ISMB 450 or higher (larger r_min, larger A).
2. **Reduce effective length:** Provide lateral bracing at mid-height, reducing L_e.
3. **Use encased column:** Concrete encasement increases buckling resistance.
4. **Add cover plates** to flanges to increase I about weak axis.
5. **Upgrade material** to higher grade (f_y = 350 N/mm²) — but slenderness dominates, so this is least effective.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake:** Using actual length L instead of L_e — always read end conditions carefully.
> 2. **Common Mistake:** Using I_xx when I_yy is smaller — column always buckles about the **weakest axis**.
> 3. **Common Mistake:** Applying Euler's formula to intermediate columns without checking λ > λ_c. Always compute λ first.
> 4. **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to convert r in mm when L is also in mm (units cancel in ratio, but check r in same unit as L).
> 5. **Marking Penalty:** Not drawing the FBD or sketching the buckled shape — costs **1 to 2 marks** every time.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before Exam)**

- ☐ **Definition:** A compression member's strength is governed by **buckling**, not just yielding.
- ☐ **Euler's Load:** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ — use **L_e (effective length)**, not L.
- ☐ **Always use I_min** (weak axis moment of inertia) — column buckles about weak axis.
- ☐ **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_e / r$ is **dimensionless**.
- ☐ **Critical slenderness** $\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{E/f_y}$ ≈ 88.86 for Fe 410 (250 N/mm²).
- ☐ **λ > λ_c → Euler applies** (long column); **λ < λ_c → Rankine (intermediate)**; **λ very small → Crushing**.
- ☐ **Effective length factor K:** Pinned-Pinned = 1.0; Fixed-Fixed = 0.5; Cantilever = 2.0; Fixed-Pinned ≈ 0.7.
- ☐ **Rankine's formula:** $P_R = P_c / (1 + a \lambda^2)$; a = 1/7500 for mild steel.
- ☐ **IS 800 uses Perry-Robertson** with buckling classes **a, b, c, d** (α = 0.21, 0.34, 0.49, 0.76).
- ☐ **Partial safety factor γ_m0 = 1.10** for compression member design.
- ☐ **Design strength = χ × f_y × A / γ_m0**, where χ = stress reduction factor.
- ☐ **Units check:** L in mm, A in mm², stress in N/mm², load in N (convert to kN for final answer).
- ☐ **Sketch the buckled shape** in answer sheets — gives extra credit.
- ☐ **Verify Euler's applicability** by computing λ vs λ_c before applying Euler.
- ☐ **Self-weight of column** is typically neglected unless specifically asked.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effective Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

The **Effective Length ($L_e$)** of a compression member (column/strut) is defined as the *length between successive points of contraflexure* of the elastic line in the buckled mode of the member, **OR** equivalently, the distance between successive points of zero bending moment in the deflected shape of the member when it buckles.

Mathematically, the effective length is expressed as:

$$L_e = K \cdot L$$

Where:
- $L$ = Actual unsupported (lateral) length of the compression member
- $K$ = **Effective Length Factor (Rotation Factor)** — a dimensionless coefficient that depends upon the **end conditions** (rotational and translational restraints) provided at the supports of the column

The effective length is *not* a physical dimension of the structure; rather, it is a **conceptual reference length** that allows us to treat a column with complex end restraints as an equivalent pin-ended column of length $L_e$ for the purpose of computing its elastic (Euler) buckling load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim expectation):**
> "Effective length of a compression member is the length of an equivalent pin-ended column having the same load-carrying capacity as the actual member with its given end conditions."

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a **flexible plastic ruler** vertically on a table.

- If you simply **place the ruler upright on the table** (bottom resting, top free), it can wobble a lot — it buckles very easily. This is like a column with one end free (long effective length).

- If you **clamp the bottom firmly with your hand** and gently **pin the top with a finger** (allowing rotation but no sideways movement), the ruler is much stiffer — it takes a lot more force to buckle it. This is like a fixed-pinned column (shorter effective length).

- If you **clamp both ends rigidly** (immovable and cannot rotate), the ruler is the stiffest of all — almost impossible to buckle. This is like a fixed-fixed column (shortest effective length).

So **the more the ends are restrained, the shorter the effective length, and the higher the buckling load**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of $K$:**
> $K$ simply quantifies *"how much more or less stiff is my column compared to a basic pin-ended column of length $L$."*
> - $K = 1.0$ → Same as pin-ended
> - $K = 0.5$ → Twice as stiff (fixed-fixed)
> - $K = 2.0$ → Half as stiff (free-fixed cantilever)

---

## 1.3 Why Effective Length is the Cornerstone of Safe Column Design

The Euler buckling load is given by:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

A small error in estimating $L_e$ leads to a **square-law error** in the safe buckling load. Underestimating $L_e$ is *unsafe*; overestimating is *over-conservative and uneconomical*. The choice of $K$ therefore has **direct safety and economic implications** in structural design, which is why the concept is central to codes such as **IS 800:2007**, **IS 800:2023 (revised)**, **AISC 360**, and **Eurocode 3**.

---

## 1.4 Standard End Conditions and Theoretical K Values (Quick Reference)

| End Condition (Theoretical) | $K$ Value | Buckled Shape |
|---|---|---|
| Both ends pinned (hinged) | $1.0$ | Single half-sine wave |
| One end fixed, other end free (cantilever) | $2.0$ | Quarter sine wave |
| Both ends fixed | $0.5$ | Half of a half-sine wave |
| One end fixed, other end pinned | $0.7$ (≈0.6992) | Asymmetric curve |
| One end fixed, other end guided (sliding) | $1.0$ | Quarter wave |

> [!WARNING]
> These are **THEORETICAL** values assuming *ideal* end conditions. In real framed structures, the joints are neither perfectly pinned nor perfectly rigid, so designers must use **code-recommended effective lengths** (e.g., from alignment charts) that account for the relative stiffness of beams framing into the column.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Derivation Logic for the K Values

The effective length factor $K$ is mathematically derived by solving the **4th-order differential equation of elastic column buckling** (Euler-Bernoulli beam-column theory) with the appropriate boundary conditions for each support case.

The governing differential equation for an axially loaded prismatic column is:

$$EI \frac{d^4 y}{dx^4} + P \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = 0$$

### 2.1.1 Case 1: Both Ends Pinned (Hinged-Hinged)

- Boundary Conditions: $y(0) = 0$, $M(0) = 0$, $y(L) = 0$, $M(L) = 0$
- The buckled shape is a **single half-sine wave**: $y = \delta \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right)$
- Critical load: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$
- Since $L_e = L$, we get $\boxed{K = 1.0}$

### 2.1.2 Case 2: One End Fixed, Other End Free (Cantilever Column)

- Boundary Conditions: $y(0) = 0$, $y'(0) = 0$ (fixed), $M(L) = 0$, $V(L) = 0$ (free)
- The buckled shape is a **quarter sine wave**, equivalent to a pin-ended column of length $2L$.
- $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(2L)^2}$
- Hence $\boxed{K = 2.0}$

### 2.1.3 Case 3: Both Ends Fixed

- Boundary Conditions: $y(0) = 0$, $y'(0) = 0$, $y(L) = 0$, $y'(L) = 0$
- The buckled shape has **points of contraflexure at $L/4$ and $3L/4$**.
- $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/2)^2}$
- Hence $\boxed{K = 0.5}$

### 2.1.4 Case 4: One End Fixed, Other End Pinned

- The transcendental equation: $\tan(kL) = kL$, where $k = \sqrt{P/EI}$
- The smallest non-trivial solution is $kL = 4.4934$
- $P_{cr} = \dfrac{(4.4934)^2 EI}{L^2} = \dfrac{20.19 EI}{L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(0.6992 L)^2}$
- Hence $\boxed{K \approx 0.7}$

### 2.1.5 Case 5: One End Fixed, Other End Guided (Translation prevented, Rotation free)

- Buckled shape: Quarter sine wave of length $L$ (equivalent to pin-pin).
- Hence $\boxed{K = 1.0}$

---

## 2.2 Effective Length in Braced vs Unbraced Frames (IS 800:2007 — Critical KTU Topic)

In real building frames, columns are *not* isolated — they are part of a frame with beams. The IS 800:2007 (and the updated 2023 revision) distinguishes between two extreme cases:

### Case A: Braced Frame (Sway Prevented)
The frame is provided with bracing / shear walls / rigid floor diaphragms so that lateral sway is prevented. The column ends can rotate but cannot translate.

$$K = K_1 \quad (\text{rotational restraint considered})$$

For a braced frame with the column rigidly connected to beams and footing:
- Top end: **restrained against rotation** (by beam stiffness) but **free to translate** (frame doesn't sway). $\rightarrow$ **Effectively pinned-pinned** for the column.
- Bottom end: Similar treatment.

In such frames, $K$ is generally taken between **0.65 and 1.0**, calculated using the **alignment chart (Jackson-Moreland chart)** with parameter $G$.

### Case B: Unbraced Frame (Sway Permitted)
The frame relies on its own bending stiffness to resist lateral loads. The column ends can both rotate **AND** translate (sway).

$$K = K_2 \quad (\text{rotational + translational restraint considered})$$

For an unbraced frame, $K$ is always $\geq 1.0$ and is generally taken between **1.0 and infinity** depending on the relative beam-to-column stiffness ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 800:2007 (Cl. 7.2.2) Design Recommendation:**
> "The effective length $L_e$ of a column in a braced frame shall be taken as $0.8 L$ for normal usage (with $K=0.8$), and for an unbraced frame as $1.2 L$ (with $K=1.2$)."
>
> This is a **simplified code-allowed value** to avoid alignment chart computation.

---

## 2.3 Alignment Chart Methodology (Jackson-Moreland Chart)

The most general method to find $K$ for a column in a frame uses the dimensionless stiffness ratio:

$$G = \frac{\sum \left(\dfrac{E_c I_c}{L_c}\right)}{\sum \left(\dfrac{E_b I_b}{L_b}\right)}$$

- Numerator: Sum of stiffnesses of all columns meeting at the joint
- Denominator: Sum of stiffnesses of all beams meeting at the joint (beams are taken as having far ends pinned — multiply by 1.5 if far end is fixed)

For a **braced frame**, the alignment chart equation (approximate closed form by Julian & Lawrence) is:

$$K = \dfrac{3 G_1 G_2 + 1.4 (G_1 + G_2) + 0.64}{3 G_1 G_2 + 2.0 (G_1 + G_2) + 1.28}$$

For an **unbraced frame**, the approximate closed form is:

$$K = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.6 G_1 G_2 + 4.0 (G_1 + G_2) + 7.5}{G_1 + G_2 + 1.5}}$$

Where $G_1$ and $G_2$ are the $G$ values at the two ends of the column.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_e = K \cdot L$ | Basic definition | 2 |
| 2 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | Euler's elastic buckling load | 3 |
| 3 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{(L_e/r)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | Critical buckling stress (Euler) | 3 |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r}$ | **Slenderness ratio** | 2 |
| 5 | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Radius of gyration (minimum for column buckling) | 1 |
| 6 | $P_d = A_g \cdot f_{cd}$ | Design strength of column using $f_{cd}$ from tables | 3 |
| 7 | $f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda_0^2}} \leq f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | IS 800:2007 design stress (Buckling Class a/b/c/d) | 5 |
| 8 | $\lambda_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{f_y}}$ | Non-dimensional limiting slenderness ($\approx 83.9$ for $f_y=250$ MPa) | 2 |
| 9 | $\phi = 0.5 \left[ 1 + \alpha (\lambda - 0.2) + \lambda^2 \right]$ | Perry-Robertson function (IS 800 form) | 3 |
| 10 | $G = \dfrac{\sum E_c I_c / L_c}{\sum E_b I_b / L_b}$ | Stiffness ratio for alignment chart | 3 |
| 11 | $K_{braced} \approx 0.65 \text{ to } 1.0$ | Code-allowed simplified range (braced) | 2 |
| 12 | $K_{unbraced} \approx 1.0 \text{ to } \infty$ | Code-allowed simplified range (unbraced) | 2 |
| 13 | $L_e = 0.8 L$ (braced), $L_e = 1.2 L$ (unbraced) | **IS 800:2007 simplified defaults** | 2 |
| 14 | $P_d = 0.4 f_y A_g$ | Maximum design strength cap (avoiding $P_d > f_y A_g$) | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units to remember for KTU board exams:** $E$ in MPa (or N/mm²), $I$ in mm⁴, $L$ in mm, $P$ in N, $f_y$ in MPa. Slenderness ratio $\lambda$ is **dimensionless**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel Building Frames**: IS 800 mandates $K$ values (0.8 braced / 1.2 unbraced) for routine designs; alignment charts are used for tall or irregular frames.
- **Transmission Towers & Lattice Masts**: Members with bolted end connections are designed as **pin-ended**, $K = 1.0$.
- **Scaffolding & Formwork Props**: Designed with effective lengths considering joint slip — practically $K \approx 1.0$ to $1.2$.
- **Bridge Piers & Pile Caps**: Cantilever conditions dominate; $K = 2.0$ in extreme cases.
- **Industrial Crane Columns / Monorail Beams**: Combined axial + bending requires careful $K$ selection.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: $K$ for Fixed-Fixed Column (Worked Step-by-Step)

**Problem Setup:** A prismatic column of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, both ends rigidly fixed, subjected to axial compressive load $P$. Find the effective length factor $K$.

### Step 1: Governing Differential Equation

Using the standard Euler-Bernoulli beam-column theory, the moment-curvature relation gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(x) &= -EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} \\
\text{Equilibrium: } M(x) &= P \cdot y(x) + R \cdot x + C
\end{aligned}
$$

For a column buckling in a symmetric mode with both ends fixed, the reactions develop a moment at the ends. By symmetry, the **horizontal reaction is zero**, so the bending moment at any section is simply:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + P \cdot y &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Solution of the Differential Equation

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$. The equation becomes:

$$y'' + k^2 y = 0$$

The general solution is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(x) &= A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx) \\
y'(x) &= Ak \cos(kx) - Bk \sin(kx)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Boundary Conditions (Both Ends Fixed)

**Condition 1:** $y(0) = 0$ (no deflection at bottom)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \sin(0) + B \cos(0) &= 0 \\
B &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Condition 2:** $y'(0) = 0$ (no rotation at bottom)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ak \cos(0) - Bk \sin(0) &= 0 \\
Ak &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For non-trivial deflection ($A \neq 0$), we need the **transcendental equation** to have non-trivial solutions, which requires looking at the OTHER end.

**Condition 3:** $y(L) = 0$ (no deflection at top)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \sin(kL) + B \cos(kL) &= 0 \\
A \sin(kL) &= 0 \quad (\text{since } B = 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Condition 4:** $y'(L) = 0$ (no rotation at top)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ak \cos(kL) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

For $A \neq 0$, we require: $\cos(kL) = 0$

### Step 4: Find the Smallest Non-Zero Root

The roots of $\cos(kL) = 0$ are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
kL &= \dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{3\pi}{2}, \dfrac{5\pi}{2}, \ldots \\
\therefore kL &= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \quad \text{(smallest non-trivial root)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Critical Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
kL &= \dfrac{\pi}{2} \\
\dfrac{P_{cr}}{EI} \cdot L^2 &= \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} \\
P_{cr} &= \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4 L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(2L)^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} \\
\text{Compare with Euler: } P_{cr} &= \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2} \\
\therefore L_e^2 &= 4 L^2 \implies L_e = 2L \\
\therefore K &= \dfrac{L_e}{L} = \dfrac{2L}{L} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — that's the **cantilever (fixed-free) result**, not fixed-fixed! This is because we set the horizontal reaction to zero. For truly fixed-fixed, the end moments provide an additional restraint.

### Step 6: True Fixed-Fixed Case (Corrected)

In a truly fixed-fixed column, the **end restraint moment** adds stiffness. Using the full fourth-order equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI y^{(4)} + P y'' &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Letting $k^2 = P/EI$, the general solution is:

$$y(x) = A \sin(kx) + B \cos(kx) + Cx + D$$

With the four boundary conditions $y(0)=y'(0)=y(L)=y'(L)=0$, the determinant of the coefficient matrix must vanish, leading to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(kL) \cdot (kL) &= 0 \quad \text{or other roots} \\
\therefore kL &= 2\pi \quad \text{(smallest non-trivial root for fixed-fixed)} \\
P_{cr} &= \dfrac{(2\pi)^2 EI}{L^2} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 EI}{L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/2)^2} \\
\therefore L_e &= L/2 \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{K = 0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard textbook answer** for a column with both ends perfectly fixed.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Design of a Steel Column (IS 800:2007)

**Problem:** Design a steel column of length 5 m in a **braced frame** to carry an axial factored load of 1200 kN. Use steel of grade Fe 410 ($f_y = 250$ MPa), $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa. Assume the column is hinged at both ends.

### Step 1: Compute Effective Length

For a hinged-hinged column in a braced frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= 0.8 \text{ (IS 800:2007 simplified value)} \\
L_e &= K \cdot L = 0.8 \times 5000 \text{ mm} = 4000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Assume a Section and Compute Slenderness

Assume an **ISHB 300** section:
- $A_g = 7480 \text{ mm}^2$
- $r_{min} = r_{yy} = 60.1 \text{ mm}$ (weak axis governs)
- $f_y = 250$ MPa, Buckling class 'a' (assuming $h/b < 1.2$)

Slenderness ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \dfrac{4000}{60.1} = 66.56
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute $\lambda_0$ (Limiting Slenderness)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_0 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{f_y}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{250}} \\
&= \sqrt{7895.66} = 88.86
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\lambda = 66.56 < \lambda_0 = 88.86$, the column is in the **inelastic buckling range**.

### Step 4: Compute Imperfection Factor $\alpha$ (Buckling Class 'a')

From IS 800 Table 7, for buckling class 'a': $\alpha = 0.21$

### Step 5: Compute $\phi$ and $f_{cd}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_0^2 &= (88.86)^2 = 7896.5 \\
\lambda^2 &= (66.56)^2 = 4430.2 \\
\phi &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + \alpha(\lambda - 0.2) + \lambda^2 \right] \\
&= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.21 \times (66.56 - 0.2) + 4430.2 \right] \\
&= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 13.93 + 4430.2 \right] \\
&= 0.5 \times 4445.13 = 2222.57
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: The above uses $\lambda$ in non-dimensional form as per IS 800 (i.e., $\lambda = L_e/r$ directly, not the non-dimensional $\bar{\lambda}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{cd} &= \dfrac{f_y / \gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \lambda^2}} \leq \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \\
&= \dfrac{250 / 1.1}{2222.57 + \sqrt{(2222.57)^2 - (4430.2 / 1.0 \text{ careful: this is wrong})}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction (per IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.1.3):**

The design compressive stress uses **non-dimensional slenderness** $\overline{\lambda} = \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{f_{cc}}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda_0}$.

Let me redo this cleanly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\lambda} &= \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda_0} = \dfrac{66.56}{88.86} = 0.749 \\
\phi &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + \alpha(\overline{\lambda} - 0.2) + \overline{\lambda}^2 \right] \\
&= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.21 (0.749 - 0.2) + (0.749)^2 \right] \\
&= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.21 \times 0.549 + 0.561 \right] \\
&= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.1153 + 0.561 \right] = 0.5 \times 1.6763 = 0.8382 \\
\chi &(\text{or } f_{cd}/f_y) = \dfrac{1}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \overline{\lambda}^2}} = \dfrac{1}{0.8382 + \sqrt{(0.8382)^2 - (0.749)^2}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.8382 + \sqrt{0.7026 - 0.561}} = \dfrac{1}{0.8382 + \sqrt{0.1416}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.8382 + 0.3763} = \dfrac{1}{1.2145} = 0.8234 \\
f_{cd} &= \chi \cdot \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = 0.8234 \times \dfrac{250}{1.1} = 0.8234 \times 227.27 = 187.1 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Check Design Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= f_{cd} \times A_g = 187.1 \times 7480 = 1{,}399{,}508 \text{ N} = 1399.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $P_d = 1399.5$ kN $> P_u = 1200$ kN ✅ **The section ISHB 300 is SAFE**.

### Step 7: Utilization Ratio (Quick Check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{UR} &= \dfrac{P_u}{P_d} = \dfrac{1200}{1399.5} = 0.857 = 85.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The design is **safe and reasonably economical** (utilization between 80–95% is ideal for KTU design problems).

---

## 3.3 Sensitivity Analysis: Effect of $K$ on Buckling Load

| $K$ Value | $L_e$ (m) | $P_{cr}$ (kN) for same column | % Change vs $K=1.0$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 (Fixed-Fixed) | 2.5 | 6400 | +300% |
| 0.7 (Fixed-Pinned) | 3.5 | 3265 | +114% |
| 1.0 (Pinned-Pinned) | 5.0 | 1600 | 0% (Reference) |
| 1.2 (Unbraced, code) | 6.0 | 1111 | -30.6% |
| 2.0 (Cantilever) | 10.0 | 400 | -75% |

> [!NOTE]
> This sensitivity table is **favourite KTU Part-B (14 mark) content** — examiners often ask for a discussion on how the choice of end conditions affects the buckling load of a column.
