# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (Conceptual) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the effective length of a compression member. Why is the concept of effective length important in the design of steel columns?**

**Model Answer:**
The effective length of a compression member is defined as the length between successive points of **contraflexure** (points of zero bending moment) of the buckled shape, OR equivalently, the length of an equivalent pin-ended column that has the **same load-carrying capacity** as the actual member with its given end conditions.

Mathematically, $L_e = K \cdot L$, where $K$ is the effective length factor depending on end conditions.

**Importance:** The Euler buckling load varies inversely as the **square of the effective length** ($P_{cr} \propto 1/L_e^2$). Hence, even a small error in estimating $L_e$ leads to a large error in the safe load. The concept allows the designer to account for end restraints without solving complex frame buckling problems. It directly influences the **slenderness ratio** ($\lambda = L_e/r$), which governs the design compressive stress $f_{cd}$ in IS 800:2007. (3 marks)

---

### Question A2 (Numerical) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: A steel column of length 4.5 m is fixed at the base and pinned at the top. Determine its effective length and slenderness ratio if it is an ISHB 200 section ($r_{min} = 49.5$ mm).**

**Model Answer:**

For fixed-pinned end condition: $K = 0.7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_e &= K \cdot L = 0.7 \times 4500 = 3150 \text{ mm} \\
\lambda &= \dfrac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \dfrac{3150}{49.5} = 63.64
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective length = 3150 mm; Slenderness ratio = 63.64 (dimensionless).** (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full Length Design Problem

**Q: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`**

A column in a **braced multi-storey steel frame** has an effective length of 4 m. It is subjected to a factored axial compressive load of 1500 kN. Design a suitable **double-angle back-to-back section** (with gusset plate of 10 mm thickness) using steel of grade **Fe 410** ($f_y = 250$ MPa), $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa. Assume a **single-bolted connection** (slenderness of the built-up section governed by minimum radius of gyration about the axis parallel to the gusset plate).

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Select a trial section and compute the design compressive strength. Check if it is safe.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** If the same column were placed in an **unbraced frame**, how would the design change? Compute the new design strength with a revised $K$ value.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Braced Frame Design (7 marks)

**Step 1: Given Data**
- Factored Load $P_u = 1500$ kN
- $L_e = 4$ m $= 4000$ mm
- $f_y = 250$ MPa
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa
- Assume buckling class 'c' (typical for double-angle sections)

**Step 2: Trial Section**
Try **2 × ISA 100 × 100 × 10 mm** placed back-to-back with 10 mm gusset:
- Area of one angle = $19.24 \text{ cm}^2 = 1924 \text{ mm}^2$
- Total area $A_g = 2 \times 1924 = 3848 \text{ mm}^2$
- $r_{min}$ of single angle (about the weak axis) $= 1.95$ cm $= 19.5$ mm
- $r_{yy}$ of built-up section (about axis parallel to gusset) — design assumption: $r = 30$ mm

**Step 3: Slenderness**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{L_e}{r} = \dfrac{4000}{30} = 133.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Limiting Slenderness $\lambda_0$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_0 &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{f_y}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{250}} = 88.86
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\lambda = 133.3 > \lambda_0 = 88.86$, the column is in the **elastic Euler buckling range**.

**Step 5: Critical Stress (Euler)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{cc} &= \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times 2 \times 10^5}{(133.3)^2} = \dfrac{1{,}973{,}920}{17{,}769} = 111.09 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Design Compressive Stress $f_{cd}$ (IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.1.3)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{cd} &= \dfrac{f_{cc}}{\gamma_{m0}} \times \chi \quad \text{(using the stress-based form)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the **standard form with $\phi$ and $\overline{\lambda}$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\lambda} &= \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda_0} = \dfrac{133.3}{88.86} = 1.50 \\
\phi &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + \alpha(\overline{\lambda} - 0.2) + \overline{\lambda}^2 \right] \\
&= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.49 (1.50 - 0.2) + (1.50)^2 \right] \quad \text{(class 'c', } \alpha = 0.49\text{)} \\
&= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.637 + 2.25 \right] = 0.5 \times 3.887 = 1.944 \\
\chi &= \dfrac{1}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \overline{\lambda}^2}} = \dfrac{1}{1.944 + \sqrt{(1.944)^2 - (1.50)^2}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1.944 + \sqrt{3.779 - 2.25}} = \dfrac{1}{1.944 + \sqrt{1.529}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1.944 + 1.237} = \dfrac{1}{3.181} = 0.3144 \\
f_{cd} &= \chi \cdot \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = 0.3144 \times \dfrac{250}{1.1} = 0.3144 \times 227.27 = 71.45 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Design Capacity Check**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_d &= f_{cd} \times A_g = 71.45 \times 3848 = 274{,}940 \text{ N} \approx 275 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**❌ The trial section FAILS.** $P_d = 275$ kN $\ll$ $P_u = 1500$ kN. The column is too slender.

**Step 8: Revise Section**
Since the column failed, we need a **larger area and/or larger $r$**. Try a heavier section such as **2 × ISA 150 × 150 × 15 mm** or a **rolled I-section like ISHB 400** with $A_g = 10470 \text{ mm}^2$, $r_{min} = 49.5$ mm.

For ISHB 400: $A_g = 10470 \text{ mm}^2$, $r_{min} = 49.5$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{4000}{49.5} = 80.8 \\
\overline{\lambda} &= \dfrac{80.8}{88.86} = 0.909
\end{aligned}
$$

For class 'a' ($\alpha = 0.21$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.21(0.909 - 0.2) + (0.909)^2 \right] = 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.149 + 0.826 \right] = 0.987 \\
\chi &= \dfrac{1}{0.987 + \sqrt{0.974 - 0.826}} = \dfrac{1}{0.987 + 0.385} = 0.728 \\
f_{cd} &= 0.728 \times 227.27 = 165.4 \text{ MPa} \\
P_d &= 165.4 \times 10470 = 1{,}731{,}720 \text{ N} \approx 1732 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**✅ The section ISHB 400 is SAFE.** $P_d = 1732$ kN $> P_u = 1500$ kN.

**Utilization ratio = $1500/1732 = 86.6\%$** — Economical and safe. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Unbraced Frame Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1: New Effective Length**

For an unbraced frame, IS 800:2007 recommends $K = 1.2$ (simplified):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_e^{new} &= 1.2 \times 4000 = 4800 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Recompute Slenderness for ISHB 400**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda^{new} &= \dfrac{4800}{49.5} = 96.97 \\
\overline{\lambda}^{new} &= \dfrac{96.97}{88.86} = 1.091
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Recompute $f_{cd}$ and $P_d$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi^{new} &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.21(1.091 - 0.2) + (1.091)^2 \right] = 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.187 + 1.190 \right] = 1.189 \\
\chi^{new} &= \dfrac{1}{1.189 + \sqrt{1.414 - 1.190}} = \dfrac{1}{1.189 + 0.473} = 0.602 \\
f_{cd}^{new} &= 0.602 \times 227.27 = 136.8 \text{ MPa} \\
P_d^{new} &= 136.8 \times 10470 = 1{,}432{,}296 \text{ N} \approx 1432 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comparison**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{P_d^{unbraced}}{P_d^{braced}} &= \dfrac{1432}{1732} = 0.827 \quad (\text{a drop of } 17.3\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**❌ The ISHB 400 is now UNSAFE** in the unbraced frame ($P_d = 1432$ kN $< P_u = 1500$ kN).

**Step 5: Revised Design**
A heavier section such as **ISHB 450** ($A_g = 11840 \text{ mm}^2$, $r_{min} = 54.5$ mm) is required:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{4800}{54.5} = 88.07 \\
\overline{\lambda} &= 0.991 \\
\phi &= 0.5[1 + 0.21(0.991-0.2) + 0.982] = 0.5[1 + 0.166 + 0.982] = 1.074 \\
\chi &= \dfrac{1}{1.074 + \sqrt{1.153 - 0.982}} = \dfrac{1}{1.074 + 0.414} = 0.672 \\
f_{cd} &= 0.672 \times 227.27 = 152.7 \text{ MPa} \\
P_d &= 152.7 \times 11840 = 1{,}808{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 1808 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**✅ ISHB 450 is SAFE in unbraced frame.** Utilization = $1500/1808 = 83\%$.

**Conclusion:** Moving from a braced to an unbraced frame **decreases the load capacity by ~17%** for the same section, and a **heavier section (ISHB 450)** is required for the unbraced case, increasing the steel consumption and project cost. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Conceptual + Numerical)

**Q: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]`**

**(a)** [7 marks] With neat sketches, explain the **buckled shapes and effective length factors** for a compression member with the following end conditions:
1. Both ends hinged
2. Both ends fixed
3. One end fixed, other end free
4. One end fixed, other end hinged

**(b)** [7 marks] A steel column of length 6 m in an industrial building carries a **factored axial load of 800 kN**. The column is **fixed at the base and free at the top** (a flagpole condition). Design a suitable **tubular section** using steel grade Fe 410 ($f_y = 250$ MPa). Assume the buckling class is 'a'.

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Theoretical End Conditions (7 marks)

The four cases are tabulated with their buckled shapes:

| End Condition | $K$ | $L_e$ | Buckled Shape Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hinged-Hinged** | 1.0 | $L$ | Single half-sine wave with zero moment at both ends. Deflection max at mid-height. |
| **Fixed-Fixed** | 0.5 | $L/2$ | Symmetric double curvature with **points of contraflexure at $L/4$ and $3L/4$**. |
| **Fixed-Free (Cantilever)** | 2.0 | $2L$ | Quarter-sine wave; deflection max at the free end. |
| **Fixed-Hinged** | 0.7 | $0.7L$ | Asymmetric single curvature; contraflexure at $0.3L$ from fixed end. |

**Critical loads comparison (for same section and length):**
- Hinged-Hinged: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L^2$ (Reference)
- Fixed-Fixed: $P_{cr} = 4 \pi^2 EI / L^2$ (4× higher)
- Fixed-Hinged: $P_{cr} \approx 2.05 \pi^2 EI / L^2$ (2× higher)
- Fixed-Free: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (2L)^2 = \pi^2 EI / 4L^2$ (4× lower)

**Physical insight:** Restraint against rotation and/or translation increases buckling load. The most efficient end condition is fixed-fixed, while the weakest is the cantilever. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Cantilever Column Design (7 marks)

**Step 1: Effective Length**

For fixed-free condition: $K = 2.0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_e &= 2.0 \times 6000 = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Trial Tubular Section**

Try a **circular hollow section (CHS) 168.3 mm OD × 6.3 mm thick**:
- $A_g = 32.1 \text{ cm}^2 = 3210 \text{ mm}^2$
- $r = 58.0$ mm (outer radius for thin tube)
- Buckling class 'a' → $\alpha = 0.21$

**Step 3: Slenderness and Critical Stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{12{,}000}{58.0} = 206.9 \\
\lambda_0 &= 88.86 \\
\overline{\lambda} &= \dfrac{206.9}{88.86} = 2.328
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: $\phi$ and $\chi$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.21(2.328 - 0.2) + (2.328)^2 \right] \\
&= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.447 + 5.420 \right] = 3.434 \\
\chi &= \dfrac{1}{3.434 + \sqrt{(3.434)^2 - (2.328)^2}} = \dfrac{1}{3.434 + \sqrt{11.79 - 5.42}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{3.434 + \sqrt{6.37}} = \dfrac{1}{3.434 + 2.524} = \dfrac{1}{5.958} = 0.1678
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Design Stress and Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{cd} &= 0.1678 \times 227.27 = 38.14 \text{ MPa} \\
P_d &= 38.14 \times 3210 = 122{,}400 \text{ N} = 122.4 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**❌ Trial section FAILS** ($P_d = 122$ kN $\ll$ $P_u = 800$ kN). The cantilever is too slender.

**Step 6: Revise to a Larger Section**
Try **CHS 244.5 mm OD × 8.0 mm thick**:
- $A_g = 59.4 \text{ cm}^2 = 5940 \text{ mm}^2$
- $r = 84.5$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{12{,}000}{84.5} = 142.0 \\
\overline{\lambda} &= \dfrac{142.0}{88.86} = 1.598 \\
\phi &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.21(1.598 - 0.2) + (1.598)^2 \right] = 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.294 + 2.554 \right] = 1.924 \\
\chi &= \dfrac{1}{1.924 + \sqrt{3.702 - 2.554}} = \dfrac{1}{1.924 + 1.072} = 0.334 \\
f_{cd} &= 0.334 \times 227.27 = 75.91 \text{ MPa} \\
P_d &= 75.91 \times 5940 = 450{,}920 \text{ N} \approx 451 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**❌ Still FAILS.** Try a heavier tube: **CHS 323.9 mm OD × 10.0 mm thick**:
- $A_g = 98.6 \text{ cm}^2 = 9860 \text{ mm}^2$
- $r = 112$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \dfrac{12{,}000}{112} = 107.1 \\
\overline{\lambda} &= \dfrac{107.1}{88.86} = 1.205 \\
\phi &= 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.21(1.205 - 0.2) + (1.205)^2 \right] = 0.5 \left[ 1 + 0.211 + 1.452 \right] = 1.332 \\
\chi &= \dfrac{1}{1.332 + \sqrt{1.774 - 1.452}} = \dfrac{1}{1.332 + 0.567} = 0.527 \\
f_{cd} &= 0.527 \times 227.27 = 119.8 \text{ MPa} \\
P_d &= 119.8 \times 9860 = 1{,}181{,}200 \text{ N} \approx 1181 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**✅ CHS 323.9 × 10 mm is SAFE.** $P_d = 1181$ kN $> P_u = 800$ kN.

**Utilization = $800/1181 = 67.7\%$** — Slightly over-designed, but acceptable for KTU.

**Alternative practical advice:** Cantilever columns with $K = 2.0$ are **highly inefficient** and uneconomical. In real industrial buildings, providing even a **light bracing** at the top (e.g., a knee brace) changes the effective length to nearly $L/2$ and drastically reduces the steel required. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $L$ in m vs mm — slenderness ratio errors of 1000×. Always work in **N and mm** for IS 800 problems.
> 2. **Using $r$ about the strong axis when weak axis governs:** The radius of gyration used in $\lambda = L_e/r$ **must be the minimum** ($r_{min}$ or $r_{yy}$ whichever is smaller).
> 3. **Confusing $\lambda$ (geometric) with $\overline{\lambda}$ (non-dimensional) in the $\phi$ formula:** The Perry-Robertson $\phi$ formula in IS 800 uses $\overline{\lambda} = \lambda / \lambda_0$, **NOT** the raw $\lambda$. This is a 3-mark error.
> 4. **Wrong buckling class:** Using class 'a' (α=0.21) for a slender section that should be class 'c' (α=0.49). Always consult IS 800 Table 7.
> 5. **Ignoring the cap $P_d \leq 0.4 f_y A_g$:** For very stocky columns, this cap applies.
> 6. **Choosing $K = 1.0$ by default for "pin-ended":** In a frame, even a "pinned" base might be partially restrained. Use the **code-allowed simplified $K = 0.8$ (braced) or $1.2$ (unbraced)** unless the alignment chart is explicitly required.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — Effective Length (Module 4)**

- **Definition**: $L_e = K \cdot L$, where $K$ depends on end restraints and frame sway condition.
- **Five canonical K values** (THEORETICAL):
  - Hinged-Hinged → $K = 1.0$
  - Fixed-Fixed → $K = 0.5$
  - Fixed-Free (Cantilever) → $K = 2.0$
  - Fixed-Hinged → $K = 0.7$ (exactly $1/\sqrt{2.046}$)
  - Fixed-Guided → $K = 1.0$
- **IS 800:2007 simplified values** (for routine design):
  - Braced frame: $K = 0.8$ → $L_e = 0.8L$
  - Unbraced frame: $K = 1.2$ → $L_e = 1.2L$
- **Slenderness ratio**: $\lambda = L_e / r_{min}$ — use **minimum radius of gyration**.
- **Limiting slenderness** for $f_y = 250$ MPa: $\lambda_0 \approx 88.86$.
- **Euler's critical load**: $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_e^2$ (valid only for $\lambda \geq \lambda_0$).
- **Perry-Robertson** (IS 800 form): $f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y/\gamma_{m0}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^2 - \overline{\lambda}^2}}$ with $\phi = 0.5[1 + \alpha(\overline{\lambda} - 0.2) + \overline{\lambda}^2]$.
- **Buckling classes** (IS 800 Table 7) with imperfection factors: a (0.21), b (0.34), c (0.49), d (0.76).
- **Stiffness ratio** for alignment charts: $G = \dfrac{\sum E_c I_c / L_c}{\sum E_b I_b / L_b}$.
- **Braced frame**: Sway prevented → $K$ in range $[0.65, 1.0]$.
- **Unbraced frame**: Sway permitted → $K \geq 1.0$, can be very large.
- **Sensitivity**: $P_{cr} \propto 1/L_e^2$ — squaring the effective length **quadruples** its effect.
- **Always check weak axis**: For I-sections, the buckling about the minor axis ($y$-$y$) often governs, so use $r_{yy}$.
- **Practical tip**: Cantilever columns ($K=2.0$) are uneconomical — provide bracing wherever feasible.
- **IS 800 cap**: $P_d \leq 0.4 f_y A_g$ to prevent over-estimation of capacity for very stocky members.
- **Partial safety factor** $\gamma_{m0} = 1.1$ for steel (always in the denominator).
- **For partial safety factor $\gamma_{m1}$**: Use 1.25 for factory/shop fabrication, 1.30 for field/site fabrication (only for stress calculations involving ultimate stress $f_u$).
- **Units discipline**: All work in N and mm; $E$ in MPa = N/mm²; $f_y$ in MPa.
- **Alignment chart alternatives**: Newmark's chart, Julian-Lawrence equations (closed form) — preferred in computer analysis.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4: EFFECTIVE LENGTH**
> *Best of luck for your KTU University Examinations!*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Slenderness Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maximum Slenderness Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — MAXIMUM SLENDERNESS RATIO

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slenderness Ratio (λ — lambda)** is defined as the ratio of the *effective length* of a compression member to its *least radius of gyration*.
>
> $$\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$$

where,

- $L_{eff}$ = Effective length of the member (depends on end conditions)
- $r_{min}$ = Least radius of gyration of the cross-section
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area
- $I_{min}$ = Minimum second moment of area about the centroidal axis

The **Maximum Slenderness Ratio (λ_max)** is the *upper codal limit* prescribed by Indian Standards (and global codes) beyond which a structural member — especially a column, strut, or compression member — is **not permitted** to be used in design. It acts as a **safety threshold**: any member exceeding this value is deemed excessively slender, susceptible to premature *elastic (Euler) buckling*, and is rejected on safety grounds.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Pencil vs. Steel Rod" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine trying to break a short, fat **candle (small length, large thickness)** versus a long, thin **broomstick**. Apply equal axial force at the ends:
> - The **candle** resists — it is *stocky*, low slenderness ratio, fails by crushing.
> - The **broomstick** suddenly **bows sideways** and snaps — it is *slender*, high slenderness ratio, fails by buckling.
>
> The **Maximum Slenderness Ratio** is the engineering community's "rule" that says: *"Do not let your columns behave like broomsticks — keep them within a safe slender-to-stocky window, otherwise buckling happens before the material even realises it is loaded."*

### Key Physical Metric

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| $\lambda$ | Slenderness Ratio | **Dimensionless** |
| $L_{eff}$ | Effective Length | mm |
| $r_{min}$ | Least Radius of Gyration | mm |
| $\sigma_{cr}$ | Critical Buckling Stress | N/mm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why λ_max matters in SAFETY design:**
> When $\lambda > \lambda_{max}$, the Euler critical stress $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^{2}E/\lambda^{2}$ drops below the **yield stress** of the material. This means the member **buckles elastically** before it can mobilise its full strength — a catastrophic, brittle-type failure with no warning. Hence λ_max is a *fundamental safety check* before any strength calculation.

---

## 1.2 What is the Least Radius of Gyration?

$$r_{min} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}}$$

For common sections:

| Section Type | $r_{min}$ (minimum) |
|--------------|--------------------|
| Solid rectangular ($b \times d$, $b < d$) | $\dfrac{b}{\sqrt{12}}$ |
| Solid circular (diameter $D$) | $\dfrac{D}{4}$ |
| Hollow circular (outer $D$, inner $d$) | $\dfrac{\sqrt{D^{2} + d^{2}}}{4}$ |
| I-Section / Channel | Controlled by web/flange — taken as the smaller of $r_{xx}, r_{yy}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Slenderness Ratio Governs Buckling — The Physics

The strength of a column is governed by **two distinct failure modes**:

1. **Crushing (Material failure)** — when $\lambda$ is small (stocky column).
2. **Buckling (Geometric instability failure)** — when $\lambda$ is large (slender column).

The transition is governed by **Euler's critical stress**:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} \, E}{\lambda^{2}}$$

- As $\lambda \uparrow$, $\sigma_{cr} \downarrow$ rapidly (inverse-square relationship).
- Design codes therefore **cap** $\lambda$ to ensure $\sigma_{cr} \geq f_y$ (or a permissible working stress).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A column with $\lambda = 180$ in structural steel has $\sigma_{cr}$ roughly $\dfrac{1}{32400}$ times $E$. For $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², this is about **6.1 N/mm²** — far below the yield stress of mild steel (~250 N/mm²). Hence the code sets 180 as a **safety ceiling** to guarantee the column does not buckle before yielding.

---

## 2.2 Stepwise Logic of the Slenderness Concept

- **Step 1:** Identify the member as *compression* (column, strut, bracing) or *tension*.
- **Step 2:** Determine **end fixity conditions** to compute $L_{eff} = k \cdot L$, where $k$ depends on restraint.
- **Step 3:** Compute the **least radius of gyration** $r_{min}$ from the cross-sectional geometry.
- **Step 4:** Calculate $\lambda = L_{eff} / r_{min}$.
- **Step 5:** Compare against **codal λ_max**.
- **Step 6:** If $\lambda > \lambda_{max}$, **reject the section** or **redesign** (increase $r_{min}$ by enlarging section, or reduce $L_{eff}$ by adding bracing).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE — All formulas, limits and units you must memorise for the KTU exam.**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|-----------|----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = L_{eff} / r_{min}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Radius of Gyration | $r_{min} = \sqrt{I_{min}/A}$ | mm |
| 3 | Effective Length | $L_{eff} = k \cdot L$ | mm |
| 4 | Euler Critical Load | $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / L_{eff}^{2}$ | N |
| 5 | Euler Critical Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^{2} E / \lambda^{2}$ | N/mm² |
| 6 | Rankine Critical Load | $P_{R} = \dfrac{P_{cr} \cdot f_y \cdot A}{f_y \cdot A + P_{cr}}$ | N |
| 7 | Rectangular section | $r_{min} = b / \sqrt{12}$ | $b$ = smaller side |
| 8 | Solid circular section | $r_{min} = D/4$ | $D$ = diameter |
| 9 | λ_max (Steel column, IS 800) | **180** | IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.2.2 |
| 10 | λ_max (Steel beam in erection) | **250** | IS 800:2007 |
| 11 | λ_max (Steel tension member) | **350** | IS 800:2007 |
| 12 | λ_max (RCC short column, braced) | ≤ **12** (L/b ratio) | IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.1.2 |
| 13 | λ_max (RCC short column, unbraced) | ≤ **10** (L/b ratio) | IS 456:2000 |
| 14 | End condition factor $k$ | 1.0 (fixed-free), 0.5 (fixed-fixed), 0.7 (fixed-pinned), 1.0 (pinned-pinned), 2.0 (fixed-guided) | — |

---

## 2.4 IS 800:2007 Provisions (Steel) — Detailed Classification

For a **compression member**:

$$\lambda_{max} \;=\; 180 \quad \text{(IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.2.2)}$$

For a **tension member** (subjected to reversal of stress or for aesthetic rigidity):

$$\lambda_{max} \;=\; 350$$

For a **member normally acting as a tie but subjected to possible reversal of stresses**:

$$\lambda_{max} \;=\; 250$$

For **purlins**:

$$\lambda_{max} \;=\; 180$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Codal Cross-Reference for KTU:** Students must quote clause numbers (e.g., *IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.2.2*) for full marks in Part B questions. Examiners award 1–2 marks for correct clause citation.

---

## 2.5 IS 456:2000 Provisions (RCC) — Slenderness Check

A concrete column is classified by the ratio:

$$\text{Slenderness}_{\text{RCC}} \;=\; \dfrac{L_{u}}{b}$$

where $L_{u}$ = unsupported length and $b$ = lateral dimension.

| Condition | Classification |
|-----------|----------------|
| $\leq 12$ for braced / $\leq 10$ for unbraced | **Short column** — design by crushing only |
| Greater than the above | **Long (slender) column** — design by crushing + buckling (additional moments per SP-16) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design office check:** Every column must pass $\lambda \leq \lambda_{max}$ before its strength is even computed. It is the **first safety filter**.
- **Industrial structures:** Crane gantry columns must satisfy low λ because lateral sway and vibration dominate.
- **Bridge piers:** Tall piers of flyovers in Kerala are *always* controlled by slenderness checks — the box-section's outer dimensions are dictated by λ_max before reinforcement detailing begins.
- **Steel roof trusses:** Long compression chords are often governed by λ_max, leading to compound section detailing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Slenderness Ratio and Euler Critical Stress

### Starting Point — Euler's Buckling Theory

Consider a straight, prismatic, axially loaded pin-ended column. The differential equation of the deflected shape under axial compressive load $P$ is:

$$E I \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -P \, y$$

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; k^{2} \, y \;=\; 0 \qquad \text{where} \quad k^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{EI}$$

The general solution is:

$$y \;=\; A \sin(kx) \;+\; B \cos(kx)$$

### Applying Boundary Conditions

For a pin-ended column:

- At $x = 0$, deflection $y = 0$ → $B = 0$
- At $x = L$, deflection $y = 0$ → $A \sin(kL) = 0$

For a non-trivial solution ($A \neq 0$):

$$kL \;=\; n\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad k \;=\; \dfrac{n\pi}{L}$$

### Deriving the Critical Load

$$P_{cr} \;=\; k^{2} \, EI \;=\; \dfrac{n^{2} \pi^{2} EI}{L^{2}}$$

Taking the lowest mode ($n = 1$) — buckling is governed by the first mode:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} EI}{L^{2}}$$

### Converting to Effective Length (General End Conditions)

For arbitrary end conditions, replace $L$ with $L_{eff}$:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} EI}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$

### Deriving the Critical Stress

Dividing both sides by cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} EI}{A \, L_{eff}^{2}}$$

Recognising that $r^{2} = I/A$ (radius of gyration squared):

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E \, r^{2}}{L_{eff}^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\left( L_{eff} / r \right)^{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** This equation is the foundation of *all* column-buckling safety codes. It shows that buckling stress **decreases with the square** of slenderness — making slender columns extremely unsafe.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why λ_max is Capped at 180 for Steel

The condition to be enforced by the code is:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;\geq\; \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m}}$$

In working-stress philosophy (older IS 800), it is ensured that even at $\lambda_{max}$, the column does not buckle before yielding. Using $f_y = 250$ N/mm² and $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm²:

$$\dfrac{\pi^{2} \cdot 2 \times 10^{5}}{\lambda_{max}^{2}} \;\geq\; 250$$

$$\lambda_{max}^{2} \;\leq\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} \cdot 2 \times 10^{5}}{250} \;=\; 7895.7$$

$$\lambda_{max} \;\leq\; 88.9 \quad \text{(pure Euler limit)}$$

But because:

- Real columns have **initial imperfections** (out-of-straightness),
- **Residual stresses** from rolling exist,
- **Load eccentricity** is inevitable,

the actual permissible $\sigma_{cr}$ is far lower. Empirical **column curves** (Perry-Robertson, IS 800 Table 9) calibrate the safe limit, leading to a **practical cap of 180** to provide a wide safety margin while permitting geometric flexibility in design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a high-yield KTU Part B answer.** It earns full marks (7/7) when written in stepwise form with explanation of why 180 (not 89) is the codal value.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A steel column of effective length 4.5 m consists of an ISHB 300 section.
- Area, $A = 7480$ mm²
- $I_{yy} = 48.1 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴
- $r_{yy} = 25.4$ mm

Check whether the column satisfies the **maximum slenderness ratio** provision of IS 800:2007.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify minimum radius of gyration.**

Since $r_{yy} = 25.4$ mm is given, compare with $r_{xx}$. For typical ISHB sections, $r_{yy}$ is generally the minimum.

$$r_{min} \;=\; 25.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute slenderness ratio.**

$$\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}} \;=\; \dfrac{4500}{25.4} \;=\; 177.16$$

**Step 3 — Compare with λ_max = 180.**

$$\lambda = 177.16 \;\leq\; 180 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SAFE} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Conclude with safety margin.**

$$\text{Utilisation ratio} \;=\; \dfrac{177.16}{180} \times 100\% \;=\; 98.42\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The column *just* satisfies the codal limit; in practice, designers prefer to keep the utilisation below 85–90% to leave room for detailing tolerances. Consider a slightly heavier section if practical.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effective Length Coefficients for Different End Conditions

| End Condition | $k$ | $L_{eff} = k \cdot L$ | Description |
|---------------|-----|----------------------|-------------|
| Both ends pinned | 1.0 | $L$ | Hinged-hinged (theoretical column) |
| Both ends fixed | 0.5 | $L/2$ | Highest restraint |
| One end fixed, other pinned | 0.7 | $0.7L$ | Common in portal frames |
| One end fixed, other free | 2.0 | $2L$ | Cantilever column (worst case) |
| One end fixed, other guided | 1.0 | $L$ | Sliding translation allowed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Classification of Compression Members by Slenderness Ratio

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNodeA["Compression Member Loaded Axially"] --> branchA1{"Compute Slenderness Ratio lambda"}
    branchA1 -->|"lambda less than 12 RCC braced"| groupShort["SHORT COLUMN: Failure by Crushing Only"]
    branchA1 -->|"lambda between 12 and 80"| groupMed["MEDIUM COLUMN: Euler Perry Robertson Regime"]
    branchA1 -->|"lambda greater than 80"| groupLong["LONG COLUMN: Elastic Buckling Dominant"]
    groupShort --> checkShort["Design Strength equals Compressive Strength of Material"]
    groupMed --> checkMed["Use IS 800 Table 9 Column Curve to find fcd"]
    groupLong --> checkLong["Use Euler Equation fcd equals pi square E divided by lambda square"]
    branchA1 -->|"lambda exceeds 180 Steel OR 12 RCC braced"| rejectNode["REJECT SECTION: Violates Maximum Slenderness Limit of Code"]
    rejectNode --> actionNode["ACTION: Increase cross section OR reduce effective length"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Decision Flowchart for Codal Compliance (KTU Exam Hot Favourite)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputStart["Identify Member Type: Column, Strut, Tie, Purlin, Beam"] --> codePick{"Which Code Applies"}
    codePick -->|"Steel Member"| is800Node["Apply IS 800 Cl 7.2.2"]
    codePick -->|"Concrete Member"| is456Node["Apply IS 456 Cl 25.1.2"]
    is800Node --> steelCheck{"Member Type"}
    steelCheck -->|"Compression Member"| compNode["Lambda Max equals 180"]
    steelCheck -->|"Tension Member"| tenNode["Lambda Max equals 350"]
    steelCheck -->|"Tie with Stress Reversal"| revNode["Lambda Max equals 250"]
    is456Node --> rccCheck{"Building Bracing"}
    rccCheck -->|"Braced Frame"| bracedNode["Limit equals 12 using L over b"]
    rccCheck -->|"Unbraced Frame"| unbrNode["Limit equals 10 using L over b"]
    compNode --> finalComp{"Actual lambda less than or equal to 180"}
    finalComp -->|"YES"| passComp["PASS: Member is Acceptable"]
    finalComp -->|"NO"| failComp["FAIL: Redesign Required"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — End Conditions & Effective Length Multipliers

```mermaid
graph LR
    condA["Pin Pin"] -->|k equals 1.0| effA["L eff equals L"]
    condB["Fix Fix"] -->|k equals 0.5| effB["L eff equals 0.5 L"]
    condC["Fix Pin"] -->|k equals 0.7| effC["L eff equals 0.7 L"]
    condD["Fix Free"] -->|k equals 2.0| effD["L eff equals 2.0 L"]
    condE["Fix Guide"] -->|k equals 1.0| effE["L eff equals L"]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Function Map: Where λ_max Sits in the Design Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1A["Step 1: Load Estimation from IS 875"] --> step2A["Step 2: Architectural Layout of Column Grid"]
    step2A --> step3A["Step 3: Preliminary Section Selection from Steel Table"]
    step3A --> step4A{"Step 4: Compute Effective Length L eff from End Restraints"}
    step4A --> step5A["Step 5: Compute Least Radius of Gyration r min"]
    step5A --> step6A["Step 6: Calculate Slenderness Ratio lambda"]
    step6A --> step7A{"Step 7: SLENDERNESS CHECK Lambda versus Lambda max 180"}
    step7A -->|"PASS"| step8A["Step 8: Strength Check from IS 800 Table 9 fcd"]
    step7A -->|"FAIL"| step9A["REJECT: Increase Section OR Add Lateral Bracing"]
    step8A --> step10A["Step 9: Detailing and Connection Design"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

*Define slenderness ratio. State the maximum permissible values of slenderness ratio as per IS 800:2007 for (i) a compression member, (ii) a tension member, and (iii) a member subjected to reversal of stress.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The slenderness ratio $\lambda$ of a compression member is defined as the ratio of its effective length to the least radius of gyration of its cross-section:
$$\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$$

**Codal limits (2 marks, ½ each):**

| Member Type | λ_max |
|-------------|-------|
| Compression member | **180** |
| Tension member | **350** |
| Member with stress reversal | **250** |

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

*Explain the physical significance of slenderness ratio in the design of compression members. Why is a maximum limit prescribed by codes?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Physical significance (2 marks):**
The slenderness ratio is a **dimensionless geometric parameter** that quantifies the relative slenderness of a compression member. It directly controls the **mode of failure**:
- For low $\lambda$ (short/stocky members), failure occurs by **crushing/yielding** of material.
- For high $\lambda$ (slender members), failure occurs by **elastic buckling** (Euler-type instability).
- The Euler critical stress $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^{2}E/\lambda^{2}$ decreases as $\lambda^{2}$, making buckling the governing failure mode for slender members.

**Why a maximum limit is prescribed (1 mark):**
Beyond $\lambda_{max}$, the elastic buckling stress drops below the safe working stress of the material, causing premature, sudden, brittle-type failure without warning. Hence codes prescribe $\lambda_{max}$ as a **fundamental safety threshold** for human life protection.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ **Question Choice A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** *Derive the expression for Euler's critical load and critical stress for a long column. Hence, explain how the slenderness ratio governs the buckling behaviour of columns.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A steel column of effective length 5.0 m is built up of two ISMC 250 channels placed back-to-back with a clear gap of 200 mm between webs. Compute the slenderness ratio about both axes and verify whether it satisfies IS 800:2007 limit. Properties of ISMC 250: A = 3810 mm², $I_{xx} = 4380 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴, $I_{yy} = 222 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴, $C_{yy} = 23.0$ mm, $r_{xx} = 107.2$ mm, $r_{yy} = 24.1$ mm.* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State Euler's basic differential equation:
$$EI \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + Py = 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Solve with boundary conditions for pin-ended column, giving general solution and $kL = \pi$, hence:
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} EI}{L^{2}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Generalise to any end condition using $L_{eff} = kL$:
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} EI}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Divide by area A and substitute $r^{2} = I/A$:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Discuss transition between crushing and buckling regimes using the slenderness ratio.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Identify three regimes (short, medium, long) and the role of $\lambda_{max} = 180$ for steel.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclude with practical safety significance: high $\lambda$ leads to brittle elastic buckling without warning — hence the codal cap of 180.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify $L_{eff} = 5.0$ m = 5000 mm.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Slenderness about xx-axis (channels facing the bending plane about their own strong axis):

$$\lambda_{xx} = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{xx}} = \dfrac{5000}{107.2} = 46.6$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** For yy-axis, since two channels are placed **back-to-back** with a gap of 200 mm, the centroid shifts outward by $C_{yy} = 23.0$ mm per channel. Combined centroidal distance $y = C_{yy} + (200/2) = 23.0 + 100 = 123$ mm.

Using parallel axis theorem:

$$I_{yy,combined} = 2 \left[ I_{yy} + A \cdot y^{2} \right] = 2 \left[ 222 \times 10^{4} + 3810 \times (123)^{2} \right]$$
$$= 2 \left[ 222 \times 10^{4} + 576.5 \times 10^{4} \right] = 2 \times 798.5 \times 10^{4} = 1597 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

Combined area $A_{total} = 2 \times 3810 = 7620$ mm²:

$$r_{yy,combined} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1597 \times 10^{4}}{7620}} = \sqrt{20958.0} = 144.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):**

$$\lambda_{yy} = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{yy,combined}} = \dfrac{5000}{144.8} = 34.5$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Identify governing slenderness — the **maximum** governs:
$$\lambda_{max} = \max(46.6, 34.5) = 46.6$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compare with codal limit 180:
$$\lambda = 46.6 \;\ll\; 180 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{SAFE} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The built-up section has adequate slenderness safety margin for the given column length.

---

### ✅ **Question Choice B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** *What do you understand by the "effective length" of a compression member? With neat sketches, explain the five classical end conditions and state the corresponding effective length coefficients.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A circular hollow steel column has an outer diameter of 200 mm and inner diameter of 150 mm. Its effective length is 6 m. Find the slenderness ratio. If the yield stress of steel is 250 N/mm² and $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², calculate the Euler critical load and the critical stress. Comment on whether the column will yield before buckling.* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define effective length as the length between successive points of zero bending moment (inflection points) in the buckled shape, or equivalently $L_{eff} = k \cdot L$.

**Step 2 (5 marks — 1 mark each):** Tabulate five end conditions:

| End Condition | $k$ | $L_{eff}$ | Sketch Description |
|---------------|-----|-----------|---------------------|
| Pin–Pin | 1.0 | $L$ | Both ends hinged, free to rotate |
| Fixed–Fixed | 0.5 | $0.5L$ | Both ends restrained, no rotation or translation |
| Fixed–Pinned | 0.7 | $0.7L$ | One end fully fixed, other pinned |
| Fixed–Free | 2.0 | $2L$ | Cantilever — base fixed, top free |
| Fixed–Guided | 1.0 | $L$ | Translation allowed but no rotation |

**Step 3 (1 mark):** State that real structures fall between these ideal cases; IS 800 uses recommended $k$ values per Table 11.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute cross-sectional properties for hollow circular section:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2}) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(200^{2} - 150^{2}) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(40000 - 22500) = 13744.5 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^{4} - d^{4}) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(200^{4} - 150^{4}) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(1.6 \times 10^{9} - 5.0625 \times 10^{8}) = 5.367 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute radius of gyration:

$$r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{5.367 \times 10^{7}}{13744.5}} = \sqrt{3905.6} = 62.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute slenderness ratio:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r} = \dfrac{6000}{62.5} = 96.0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Euler critical load:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} EI}{L_{eff}^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 5.367 \times 10^{7}}{(6000)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{1.0594 \times 10^{14}}{3.6 \times 10^{7}} = 2.943 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \;=\; 2943 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Critical stress:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{2.943 \times 10^{6}}{13744.5} = 214.1 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Yielding load:

$$P_y = f_y \times A = 250 \times 13744.5 = 3.436 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \;=\; 3436 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Comparison & conclusion:

$$\sigma_{cr} = 214.1 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \;<\; f_y = 250 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\text{Since } \sigma_{cr} < f_y, \text{ the column will BUCKLE before yielding.}$$

Also check $\lambda_{max}$: $\lambda = 96 \ll 180$ → slenderness limit is satisfied, but the failure mode is elastic buckling, not yielding.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE MARKS on slenderness ratio questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the codal clause number** — always write *"as per IS 800:2007, Cl. 7.2.2"* alongside the value 180. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for omission.
> 2. **Using wrong $r$ for built-up sections** — for back-to-back channels, you MUST apply the **parallel axis theorem**. Simply doubling $I_{yy}$ without shifting the centroid yields a WRONG slenderness (overestimated safety) → significant marks lost.
> 3. **Confusing $r_{min}$ with $r_{max}$** — always take the **least** radius of gyration; otherwise the slenderness is *underestimated* and the design appears falsely safe.
> 4. **Mixing up units** — $L_{eff}$ must be in mm when $r$ is in mm; mixing metres with mm gives a wrong answer 1000× off.
> 5. **Omitting the conclusion statement** — end every problem with *"Since $\lambda \leq 180$, the member is safe as per IS 800"* or *"FAIL — redesign required"* for full marks.
> 6. **Forgetting effective length coefficients** — for fixed–free cantilever columns, students commonly use $k = 1$ instead of $k = 2$, leading to grossly unsafe design.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Slenderness Ratio** $\lambda = L_{eff} / r_{min}$ is a **dimensionless** geometric parameter.
- ✅ **Maximum Slenderness Ratio** is a **codal safety cap**, not a design optimisation.
- ✅ **IS 800:2007 λ_max = 180** for steel compression members (Cl. 7.2.2).
- ✅ **IS 800:2007 λ_max = 350** for steel tension members.
- ✅ **IS 800:2007 λ_max = 250** for ties with possible stress reversal.
- ✅ **IS 456:2000 limit = 12 (braced) / 10 (unbraced)** for short RCC columns (using L/b ratio).
- ✅ Euler critical stress $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^{2}E/\lambda^{2}$ decreases with the **square** of slenderness.
- ✅ The governing slenderness is the **maximum** of $\lambda_{xx}$ and $\lambda_{yy}$.
- ✅ Effective length coefficients: 1.0 (pin-pin), 0.5 (fix-fix), 0.7 (fix-pin), 2.0 (fix-free), 1.0 (fix-guide).
- ✅ For built-up sections, **parallel axis theorem is mandatory** to compute the combined $r_{min}$.
- ✅ A column **buckles before yielding** when $\sigma_{cr} < f_y$ — this is the safety-critical condition.
- ✅ Always **state the codal clause** alongside numerical limits in KTU answers.
- ✅ Always **end with a clear safety verdict** — PASS / FAIL / REDESIGN.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beams: Design Procedure for Laterally Supported and Unsupported Beams
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Beams: Design Procedure for Laterally Supported and Unsupported Beams

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 800:2007 Terminology)

A **flexural member (beam)** is a structural member subjected predominantly to transverse loads and/or bending moments. According to **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel – Limit State Method)**, beams are categorized based on the **lateral restraint provided to the compression flange**:

- **Laterally Supported Beam**: A beam in which the compression flange is laterally restrained against lateral-torsional buckling (LTB) at sufficiently close intervals. The lateral restraint is typically provided by the floor system, bracing, or secondary members.
- **Laterally Unsupported Beam**: A beam in which the compression flange is **NOT laterally supported** along its length, or is supported at intervals too large to prevent lateral-torsional buckling. Such beams must be designed accounting for **Lateral-Torsional Buckling (LTB)** failure mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The classification of a beam as "supported" or "unsupported" is **NOT** based on whether the compression flange is in contact with something — it is based on the **unbraced length ($L_{LT}$)** relative to the limiting values prescribed in IS 800:2007.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine holding a **wooden ruler (yardstick)** vertically on your desk and pressing down on the top with your palm. The ruler bends easily — this is simple bending. Now, instead of pressing straight down, apply the same force while slightly tilting the ruler sideways. The ruler will **suddenly twist and snap sideways** before it can bend significantly. This sideways twisting failure is exactly what **lateral-torsional buckling (LTB)** is.

- **Laterally Supported Beam** = Ruler held firmly at the top AND bottom by clamps (your hands) — the compression edge cannot move sideways, so it just bends normally.
- **Laterally Unsupported Beam** = Ruler held only at the bottom — the top (compression) edge is free to move sideways and twist → it fails by LTB before reaching its full bending capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering example**: Roof purlins supporting a corrugated metal sheet provide lateral support to the top flange of a roof beam. In a crane girder, however, the top flange is often unsupported between columns, making it a classic **laterally unsupported beam**.

### 1.3 Key Design Parameters

The following design parameters (all in **bold** as per KTU marking convention) govern the design:

- **Design Bending Moment ($M_d$)**: Factored bending moment to be resisted.
- **Design Shear Force ($V_d$)**: Factored shear force to be resisted.
- **Section Modulus ($Z_{pz}, Z_{ez}$)**: Plastic and elastic section moduli.
- **Unsupported Length ($L_{LT}$)**: Length between effective lateral restraints.
- **Elastic Critical Moment ($M_{cr}$)**: The moment at which LTB occurs elastically.
- **Design Bending Strength ($M_{d,limit}$)**: Limiting bending strength from IS 800 Table 14/15.
- **Buckling Strength of Plate Girders / Rolled Beams ($M_d$)**: Reduced strength for LTB.

### 1.4 Section Classification (Recap from IS 800:2007 Table 2)

Before designing any beam, the cross-section must be classified based on width-to-thickness ratios of its elements:

| Class | Behavior | Rotation Capacity | Design Bending Strength |
|-------|----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Plastic (Class 1)** | Can develop full plastic hinge | High | $M_{d} = Z_p \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ |
| **Compact (Class 2)** | Can develop yield but not plastic hinge | Limited | $M_{d} = Z_p \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ |
| **Semi-compact (Class 3)** | Yield at extreme fiber only | None | $M_{d} = Z_e \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ |
| **Slender (Class 4)** | Local buckling before yield | None | Effective section properties used |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: For a beam to be designed by **plastic design** (using $Z_p$), the section MUST be **Class 1 (Plastic)**. If Class 2 or 3, design strength reduces accordingly.

### 1.5 Partial Safety Factors (IS 800:2007 Table 5)

- $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ — for failure due to yielding
- $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$ — for failure due to ultimate stress
- $\gamma_{mb} = 1.10$ — for failure due to buckling (LTB)
- $f_y$ = yield stress of steel (e.g., **250 MPa** for Fe 410, **350 MPa** for Fe 540)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Design Philosophy

Both laterally supported and unsupported beams are designed using the **Limit State Method of Design** as per IS 800:2007. The fundamental requirement is:

$$\text{Design Action (Factored Load)} \leq \text{Design Resistance (Strength)}$$

For beams, two primary limit states govern:

1. **Limit State of Strength (ULS)**: Bending, Shear, Bearing, Lateral-Torsional Buckling
2. **Limit State of Serviceability (SLS)**: Deflection, Vibration

### 2.2 Design Procedure: Laterally Supported Beams (LTB prevented)

A laterally supported beam fails only by **moment capacity** and **shear capacity** — buckling is not considered. The procedure is:

- **Step 1**: Compute factored design loads ($w_u = 1.5 w$, $M_u = 1.5 M$, $V_u = 1.5 V$).
- **Step 2**: Classify the cross-section (Class 1, 2, 3, or 4).
- **Step 3**: Compute the **design bending strength** $M_d$ from the appropriate table of IS 800.
- **Step 4**: Compute the **design shear strength** $V_d$ from IS 800 Cl. 8.4.
- **Step 5**: Check that $M_u \leq M_d$ and $V_u \leq V_d$.
- **Step 6**: Check for **Web Bearing and Buckling** (under concentrated loads).
- **Step 7**: Check **deflection** under service loads.

### 2.3 Design Procedure: Laterally Unsupported Beams (LTB must be checked)

When a beam is laterally unsupported, the compression flange tends to buckle sideways (laterally) and twist the cross-section. The elastic critical moment $M_{cr}$ is the moment at which this happens. The procedure is:

- **Step 1–3**: Same as supported beams.
- **Step 4**: Compute the **elastic critical moment** $M_{cr}$ using the appropriate formula based on support conditions and cross-section.
- **Step 5**: Compute the **non-dimensional slenderness ratio** $\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y / M_{cr}}$.
- **Step 6**: Compute the **permissible bending stress** $f_{bd}$ from Table 14/15 of IS 800.
- **Step 7**: Design bending strength $M_d = f_{bd} \cdot Z_p$ (or $f_{bd} \cdot Z_e$ for semi-compact).
- **Step 8**: Check shear, web buckling, and deflection.
- **Step 9**: Check **bending-shear interaction** (if $0.6 V_d < V_u \leq V_d$).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (IS 800:2007 Compliant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE**: The vertical bar symbol is rendered as `\vert` in the following table to preserve markdown formatting. Do NOT use `|` inside table cells.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applicable For | Reference (IS 800:2007) |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Design Bending Strength (Plastic/Compact) | $M_d = \beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Class 1 & 2 | Cl. 8.2.1.2 |
| 2 | Design Bending Strength (Semi-compact) | $M_d = Z_e \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Class 3 | Cl. 8.2.1.2 |
| 3 | Design Shear Strength (Plastic/Compact) | $V_d = f_{yw} \cdot A_v / \gamma_{m0}$ | All beams | Cl. 8.4.1 |
| 4 | Shear Area ($A_v$) — I/H Section | $A_v = h \cdot t_w$ | I, H, Channel | Cl. 8.4.1 |
| 5 | Shear Area ($A_v$) — Rect. Hollow | $A_v = 2 \cdot h \cdot t$ for major axis bending | RHS/SHS | Cl. 8.4.1 |
| 6 | Elastic Critical Moment (LTB) | $M_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I_y \cdot h_f}{2 \cdot L_{LT}^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{4 \cdot G \cdot J \cdot L_{LT}^2}{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I_y \cdot h_f^2}}$ | LTB | Cl. 8.2.2.1 |
| 7 | Simplified $M_{cr}$ for I/H | $M_{cr} = \frac{\pi \cdot \sqrt{E \cdot I_y \cdot G \cdot J}}{L_{LT}} \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I_y}{L_{LT}^2 \cdot G \cdot J}\right]^{0.5}$ | I, H | App. F |
| 8 | Non-dimensional slenderness | $\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\frac{\beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y}{M_{cr}}}$ | LTB check | Cl. 8.2.2.1 |
| 9 | Permissible bending stress $f_{bd}$ | From Table 14 (rolled sections) or 15 (welded) | LTB | Table 14/15 |
| 10 | Deflection limit (Beams) | $\delta \leq L / 300$ (general) | SLS | Table 6 |
| 11 | Deflection limit (Purlins/Crane) | $\delta \leq L / 200$ (purlins) or $L / 750$ (cranes) | SLS | Table 6 |
| 12 | Web Bearing Stress | $F_{w} = b_1 \cdot t_w$ (stiff bearing) | Concentrated load | Cl. 8.5.1 |
| 13 | Shear Buckling (thin web) | $V_{cr} = A_v \cdot \tau_b$ where $\tau_b$ from Cl. 8.4.2 | Slender webs | Cl. 8.4.2 |
| 14 | Effective Length Factor | $L_{LT} = K_L \cdot L$ (depending on end conditions) | LTB | Table 13 |
| 15 | $\beta_b$ factor (moment gradient) | From Table 12 (1.0 for uniform moment, 1.2 for parabolic) | LTB | Table 12 |

Where:
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **MPa** (Young's Modulus of steel)
- $G = 0.769 \times 10^5$ **MPa** (Shear Modulus)
- $I_y$ = Second moment of area about minor axis
- $J$ = Torsional constant ($J \approx \sum b_i t_i^3 / 3$ for open sections)
- $h_f$ = Distance between shear centers of flanges $\approx h - t_f$
- $L_{LT}$ = Effective length for LTB
- $\beta_b$ = Moment factor depending on loading pattern
- $b_1$ = Effective stiff bearing length

### 2.5 Classification of Beams — Quick Decision Flowchart Logic

The decision of whether a beam is "laterally supported" or "unsupported" depends on:

1. **$\lambda_{LT} \leq 0.4$**: Treat as laterally supported, no LTB check needed → $M_d = Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$.
2. **$0.4 < \lambda_{LT} \leq \pi\sqrt{2}$**: Elastic LTB — use Table 14/15 to find $f_{bd}$.
3. **$\lambda_{LT} > \pi\sqrt{2}$**: No useful strength for LTB in elastic regime.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

The design procedure for laterally supported vs. unsupported beams is critical for:

- **Roof trusses and purlins** (lateral support from sheeting)
- **Crane girders** (unsupported top flange → critical design)
- **Bridge girders** (intermediate cross-frames provide discrete lateral support)
- **Skylight supports and gable columns**
- **Floor beams in composite construction** (shear studs provide full lateral restraint)
- **Purlins in industrial buildings** — design governed by LTB because they receive support only from roof sheeting at discrete points

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world insight**: In **portal frame buildings**, the rafter beam is often considered laterally supported at purlin locations. If purlin spacing is, say, 1.5 m, the unsupported length is 1.5 m, and the rafter is designed as a laterally supported beam. However, the rafter is also subject to axial compression from the portal action → it becomes a **beam-column**, requiring interaction equations.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Elastic Critical Moment $M_{cr}$ for I-Section

The elastic critical moment is derived by **classical stability theory** considering a simply supported beam under uniform moment with lateral restraint only at the ends.

Consider a thin-walled open section (I-section) with:
- Minor axis moment of inertia: $I_y$
- St. Venant's torsion constant: $J$
- Warping constant: $I_w$ (for I-sections: $I_w = I_y \cdot h_f^2 / 4$)
- Distance between flange centroids: $h_f$

The total potential energy (Lagrangian) considering bending about the strong axis and lateral-torsional deformation is:

$$\Pi = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^L \left[ E I_y \left(\frac{d^2 u}{dz^2}\right)^2 + E I_w \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dz^2}\right)^2 + G J \left(\frac{d\phi}{dz}\right)^2 - M \phi \frac{d^2 u}{dz^2} \right] dz$$

Where $u$ is the lateral displacement and $\phi$ is the twist angle.

Applying the **principle of stationary potential energy** and assuming sinusoidal buckling mode:

$$u = A \sin\left(\frac{\pi z}{L}\right), \quad \phi = B \sin\left(\frac{\pi z}{L}\right)$$

Substituting and minimizing with respect to $A$ and $B$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial A} &= 0 \Rightarrow \left(\frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L^2}\right) A - \left(\frac{M \pi^2}{L^2}\right) B = 0 \\
\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial B} &= 0 \Rightarrow -\left(\frac{M \pi^2}{L^2}\right) A + \left(G J + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L^2}\right) B = 0
\end{aligned}$$

For non-trivial solution, the **determinant** must equal zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L^2}\right)\left(G J + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L^2}\right) - \left(\frac{M_{cr} \pi^2}{L^2}\right)^2 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for $M_{cr}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr}^2 &= \left(\frac{\pi^2 E I_y}{L^2}\right) \cdot \left(G J + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L^2}\right) \\
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi}{L} \sqrt{E I_y \cdot G J} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_w}{L^2 G J}}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $I_w = I_y h_f^2 / 4$ for a doubly symmetric I-section:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi}{L} \sqrt{E I_y G J} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_y h_f^2}{4 L^2 G J}} \\
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi \sqrt{E I_y G J}}{2 L} \cdot \sqrt{4 + \frac{\pi^2 E I_y h_f^2}{L^2 G J}} \\
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi^2 E I_y h_f}{2 L^2} \sqrt{1 + \frac{4 G J L^2}{\pi^2 E I_y h_f^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **IS 800:2007 expression** given in Cl. 8.2.2.1:

$$\boxed{M_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I_y h_f}{2 L_{LT}^2} \sqrt{1 + \frac{4 G J L_{LT}^2}{\pi^2 E I_y h_f^2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation**: The first term under the square root represents **St. Venant's torsion resistance** ($GJ$). The second term represents **warping torsion resistance** ($EI_w$). For long beams, $GJ$ dominates; for short beams, $EI_w$ dominates.

### 3.2 Derivation of Permissible Bending Stress $f_{bd}$ for LTB

The non-dimensional slenderness parameter is defined as:

$$\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\frac{\beta_b Z_p f_y}{M_{cr}}}$$

This compares the **squash capacity** ($\beta_b Z_p f_y$) to the **elastic buckling capacity** ($M_{cr}$). When $\lambda_{LT} \leq 0.4$, LTB does not govern and the full plastic moment is available.

The design bending stress $f_{bd}$ for a rolled I-section (Table 14 of IS 800) is given by the **Perry-Robertson formula**:

$$f_{bd} = \frac{\chi_{LT} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{mb}}$$

Where the **reduction factor** $\chi_{LT}$ is computed as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{\phi_{LT} + \sqrt{\phi_{LT}^2 - \lambda_{LT}^2}} \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[1 + \alpha_{LT} (\lambda_{LT} - 0.2) + \lambda_{LT}^2\right] \\
\alpha_{LT} &= 0.21 \text{ (for rolled sections)} \\
\alpha_{LT} &= 0.49 \text{ (for welded sections)}
\end{aligned}$$

The design bending strength is then:

$$M_d = f_{bd} \cdot Z_p = \frac{\chi_{LT} \cdot f_y \cdot Z_p}{\gamma_{mb}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$\gamma_{mb} = 1.10$** is used for buckling checks (NOT $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$ — both happen to be 1.10 but represent different limit states).

### 3.3 Worked Example: Laterally Unsupported Beam Design (Full Derivation Walkthrough)

**Problem**: Design a laterally unsupported simply supported beam of span 6 m subjected to a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m (service) and a central concentrated load of 60 kN (service). Use **Fe 410** steel ($f_y = 250$ MPa), $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa. Use ISMB 400 section ($Z_p = 1206.34 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3$, $Z_e = 935.6 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3$, $I_y = 622.6 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^4$, $J = 290.3 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^4$, $h = 400$ mm, $h_f = 372$ mm, $b_f = 140$ mm, $t_f = 16$ mm, $t_w = 8.9$ mm).

**Step 1: Factored Loads**

$$\begin{aligned}
w_u &= 1.5 \times 30 = 45 \text{ kN/m} \\
P_u &= 1.5 \times 60 = 90 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Maximum Bending Moment (Factored)**

Maximum moment occurs at mid-span due to UDL + Point Load:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_u &= \frac{w_u L^2}{8} + \frac{P_u L}{4} \\
M_u &= \frac{45 \times 6^2}{8} + \frac{90 \times 6}{4} \\
M_u &= 202.5 + 135 = 337.5 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Shear Force (Factored)**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_u &= \frac{w_u L}{2} + \frac{P_u}{2} = \frac{45 \times 6}{2} + \frac{90}{2} = 135 + 45 = 180 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Section Classification (assumed Class 1 Plastic)**

Check the outstand of compression flange:
$$\begin{aligned}
b/t_f &= 70/16 = 4.375 \\
\epsilon &= \sqrt{250/f_y} = 1.0 \text{ (for Fe 410)} \\
(b/t_f)_{limit, Class 1} &= 9.4 \epsilon = 9.4
\end{aligned}$$

Since $4.375 < 9.4$, the flange is **Class 1**. Web also typically Class 1 for standard rolled sections.

**Step 5: Design Bending Strength (Full Plastic)**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{d,plastic} &= \frac{Z_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \\
M_{d,plastic} &= \frac{1206.34 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} \\
M_{d,plastic} &= 274.17 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 274.17 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Compute Elastic Critical Moment $M_{cr}$**

Since beam is laterally unsupported, $L_{LT} = 6$ m = 6000 mm.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi^2 E I_y h_f}{2 L_{LT}^2} \sqrt{1 + \frac{4 G J L_{LT}^2}{\pi^2 E I_y h_f^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting values:
- $\pi^2 = 9.8696$
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $I_y = 622.6 \times 10^4$ mm⁴
- $h_f = 372$ mm
- $L_{LT} = 6000$ mm
- $G = 0.769 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $J = 290.3 \times 10^3$ mm⁴

Compute first term:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\pi^2 E I_y h_f}{2 L_{LT}^2} &= \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 622.6 \times 10^4 \times 372}{2 \times (6000)^2} \\
&= \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 622.6 \times 10^4 \times 372}{72 \times 10^6}
\end{aligned}$$

Numerator:
$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 622.6 \times 10^4 \times 372 \\
&= 9.8696 \times 2 \times 622.6 \times 372 \times 10^9 \\
&= 9.8696 \times 463214.4 \times 10^9 \\
&= 4571.36 \times 10^{12}
\end{aligned}$$

First term:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{4571.36 \times 10^{12}}{72 \times 10^6} = 63.49 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute ratio inside square root:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{4 G J L_{LT}^2}{\pi^2 E I_y h_f^2} &= \frac{4 \times 0.769 \times 10^5 \times 290.3 \times 10^3 \times (6000)^2}{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 622.6 \times 10^4 \times (372)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Numerator of ratio:
$$\begin{aligned}
N_2 &= 4 \times 0.769 \times 10^5 \times 290.3 \times 10^3 \times 36 \times 10^6 \\
&= 4 \times 0.769 \times 290.3 \times 36 \times 10^{14} \\
&= 32131.0 \times 10^{14} = 3.213 \times 10^{18}
\end{aligned}$$

Denominator of ratio:
$$\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= 9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 622.6 \times 10^4 \times 138384 \\
&= 9.8696 \times 2 \times 622.6 \times 138384 \times 10^9 \\
&= 1702.4 \times 10^{15} = 1.702 \times 10^{18}
\end{aligned}$$

Ratio:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{N_2}{D_2} &= \frac{3.213 \times 10^{18}}{1.702 \times 10^{18}} = 1.888
\end{aligned}$$

So inside square root: $1 + 1.888 = 2.888$, $\sqrt{2.888} = 1.6995$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= 63.49 \times 10^6 \times 1.6995 \\
M_{cr} &= 107.89 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 107.89 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Non-dimensional Slenderness**

For a simply supported beam with UDL + central point load, $\beta_b \approx 1.07$ (interpolated from Table 12 of IS 800).

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{\frac{\beta_b Z_p f_y}{M_{cr}}} \\
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{\frac{1.07 \times 1206.34 \times 10^3 \times 250}{107.89 \times 10^6}} \\
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{\frac{322.69 \times 10^6}{107.89 \times 10^6}} \\
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{2.991} = 1.729
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8: Check Lateral Support Status**

Since $\lambda_{LT} = 1.729 > 0.4$, the beam is **LATERALLY UNSUPPORTED** — LTB check required.

**Step 9: Permissible Stress from Table 14**

For $\lambda_{LT} = 1.729$ (rolled section, $\alpha_{LT} = 0.21$):
Using the Perry-Robertson formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[1 + 0.21 (1.729 - 0.2) + (1.729)^2\right] \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[1 + 0.21 \times 1.529 + 2.989\right] \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \left[1 + 0.321 + 2.989\right] \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 \times 4.310 = 2.155
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{2.155 + \sqrt{2.155^2 - 1.729^2}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{2.155 + \sqrt{4.644 - 2.989}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{2.155 + \sqrt{1.655}} \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{2.155 + 1.287} = \frac{1}{3.442} = 0.2905
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{bd} &= \frac{\chi_{LT} \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{0.2905 \times 250}{1.10} = 66.02 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 10: Design Bending Strength with LTB**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_d &= f_{bd} \cdot Z_p = 66.02 \times 1206.34 \times 10^3 \\
M_d &= 79.65 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 79.65 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 11: Bending Check**

$$M_u = 337.5 \text{ kN·m} \gg M_d = 79.65 \text{ kN·m}$$

❌ **ISMB 400 is NOT ADEQUATE** for the given loading as a laterally unsupported beam. We need a heavier section (e.g., ISMB 500 or ISMB 600).

**Step 12: Iterative Re-design**

Try **ISMB 600** ($Z_p = 3060 \times 10^3$ mm³, $I_y = 1893 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $J = 950 \times 10^3$ mm⁴, $h = 600$ mm, $h_f = 568$ mm):

Recompute $M_{cr}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 1893 \times 10^4 \times 568}{72 \times 10^6} \\
&= 9.8696 \times 2 \times 1893 \times 568 \times 10^3 / 72 \\
&= 21216.6 \times 10^3 / 72 \times 10^6 \to \text{(re-compute carefully)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &\approx 295 \text{ kN·m} \quad \text{(after recalculation)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{\frac{1.07 \times 3060 \times 10^3 \times 250}{295 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{2.776} = 1.666 \\
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 [1 + 0.21(1.666 - 0.2) + 1.666^2] = 0.5 [1 + 0.308 + 2.776] = 2.042 \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{2.042 + \sqrt{2.042^2 - 1.666^2}} = \frac{1}{2.042 + 1.293} = 0.300 \\
M_d &= 0.300 \times 250 \times 3060 \times 10^3 / 1.10 = 208.6 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

Still inadequate. **ISMB 600 with intermediate lateral bracing** is recommended.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Shear Strength Verification (IS 800:2007 Cl. 8.4)

For rolled I-sections:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{f_{yw} \cdot A_v}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{f_{yw} \cdot h \cdot t_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} \quad \text{(if shear yielding is the mode)}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $f_{yw}$ is the yield stress of web (for rolled sections, $f_{yw} = f_y$).

**Check** for ISMB 400:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{250 \times 400 \times 8.9}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10} = \frac{890000}{1.905} = 467.2 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $V_u = 180$ kN $\leq V_d = 467.2$ kN ✅ — Shear is **OK**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Shear capacity of rolled I-sections is rarely critical compared to bending. However, **web crippling** (bearing failure under concentrated loads) often is critical at support points. Always check both.

### 3.5 Deflection Check (Limit State of Serviceability)

For simply supported beam with UDL:
$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{max} &= \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E I_x} + \frac{P L^3}{48 E I_x} \leq \frac{L}{300}
\end{aligned}$$

Allowable deflection: $L/300 = 6000/300 = 20$ mm.

For ISMB 400 ($I_x = 20462 \times 10^4$ mm⁴):
$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{UDL} &= \frac{5 \times 30 \times (6000)^4}{384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 20462 \times 10^4} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 30 \times 1.296 \times 10^{18}}{1.572 \times 10^{16}} \\
&= 12.37 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{Point} &= \frac{60 \times (6000)^3}{48 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 20462 \times 10^4} = \frac{60 \times 2.16 \times 10^{14}}{1.964 \times 10^{15}} = 6.60 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Total deflection: $\delta = 12.37 + 6.60 = 18.97$ mm $\leq 20$ mm ✅
