## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Beam Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Beam Design Problem] --> B[Compute Factored Loads: wu, Mu, Vu]
    B --> C[Assume Trial Section from ISMB Table]
    C --> D[Classify Section: Class 1, 2, 3, or 4]
    D --> E{Is Compression Flange Laterally Supported?}
    E -- YES --> F[Laterally Supported Path]
    E -- NO --> G[Laterally Unsupported Path]

    F --> F1[Compute Md from Zp * fy / gamma_m0]
    F1 --> F2[Check Mu ≤ Md]
    F2 --> F3[Check Vd from IS 800 Cl 8.4]
    F3 --> F4[Check Web Bearing and Buckling]
    F4 --> F5[Deflection Check L/300]
    F5 --> Z1[Design Safe Laterally Supported]

    G --> G1[Compute LLT and KL factor]
    G1 --> G2[Compute Mcr from IS 800 Cl 8.2.2.1]
    G2 --> G3[Compute lambda_LT = sqrt beta_b * Zp * fy / Mcr]
    G3 --> G4{lambda_LT > 0.4?}
    G4 -- NO --> F
    G4 -- YES --> G5[Compute fbd from Table 14/15 Perry-Robertson]
    G5 --> G6[Compute Md = fbd * Zp]
    G6 --> G7[Check Mu ≤ Md]
    G7 --> G8[Check Shear and Bending-Shear Interaction]
    G8 --> G9[Deflection Check]
    G9 --> G10[Final Section Safe]
    Z1 --> END[END]
    G10 --> END
```

### 4.2 Lateral-Torsional Buckling: Physical Mechanism Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cross[Cross-Section View: I-Beam]
        direction TB
        CF[Compression Flange TOP] --> BW[Web] --> TF[Tension Flange BOTTOM]
    end

    subgraph Side[Side View: Beam Bending]
        direction LR
        L1[Left Support] --> M[Moment Applied] --> R1[Right Support]
    end

    subgraph Def[Deformed Shape: LTB Mode]
        direction LR
        DS[Beam Twists and Bows Sideways]
    end

    Cross --> Side
    Side --> Def
    Def --> Loss[Capacity Loss: M_cr less than M_p]
```

### 4.3 Design Decision Matrix: Supported vs. Unsupported

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Beam Type Identification] --> Q1{Is floor system or<br>sheeting connected to<br>compression flange?}
    Q1 -- YES, Continuous --> SUP[Laterally Supported]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is LLT less than<br>critical unbraced length<br>from IS 800?}
    Q2 -- YES --> SUP
    Q2 -- NO --> UNSUP[Laterally Unsupported]
    SUP --> P1[Use Md = Zp fy / 1.10]
    UNSUP --> P2[Use Md = fbd * Zp from Table 14/15]
    P1 --> END[Design Complete]
    P2 --> END
```

### 4.4 Section Classification Flow (Web and Flange)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SC[Start: Classify Section] --> S1[Compute b/t ratios<br>for flange and web]
    S1 --> S2{Is flange outstand<br>b/tf less than 9.4 epsilon?}
    S2 -- YES --> F1[Flange: Class 1]
    S2 -- NO --> S3{Is b/tf less than 10.5 epsilon?}
    S3 -- YES --> F2[Flange: Class 2]
    S3 -- NO --> S4{Is b/tf less than 15.7 epsilon?}
    S4 -- YES --> F3[Flange: Class 3]
    S4 -- NO --> F4[Flange: Class 4 Slender]

    F1 --> W1[Check Web: d/tw ratios]
    F2 --> W1
    F3 --> W1
    F4 --> W4[Section is Class 4 overall]

    W1 --> W2{Is web ratio less than 84 epsilon?}
    W2 -- YES --> C1[Web: Class 1]
    W2 -- NO --> W3{Is web ratio less than 105 epsilon?}
    W3 -- YES --> C2[Web: Class 2]
    W3 -- NO --> C3[Web: Class 3]

    C1 --> OK1[Section Overall: Worst Case = Class 1]
    C2 --> OK2[Section Overall: Worst Case = Class 2]
    C3 --> OK3[Section Overall: Worst Case = Class 3]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Matrix Table (Roll of LTB in Beam Design)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Beam Parameters] --> B[Plastic Moment Mp]
    A --> C[Elastic Critical Moment Mcr]
    B --> D[Ratio lambda_LT]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Lambda_LT Zone}
    E -- Zone 1: le 0.4 --> R1[Full Mp Available]
    E -- Zone 2: 0.4 to 1.0 --> R2[Reduced Mb Buckling]
    E -- Zone 3: 1.0 to 2.5 --> R3[Significant Reduction]
    E -- Zone 4: gt 2.5 --> R4[Elastic LTB Dominates]
```

### 4.6 Mermaid Block: Moment Capacity Curve (Buckling Curve Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CP[Capacity Curve]
    direction LR
        P1[Plastic Capacity Mp] --> P2[Reduced Mb Zone]
        P2 --> P3[Elastic LTB Zone]
        P3 --> P4[Very Low Capacity]
    end
    P1 --> X1[lambda_LT less than 0.4]
    P2 --> X2[lambda_LT 0.4 to 1.0]
    P3 --> X3[lambda_LT 1.0 to 2.5]
    P4 --> X4[lambda_LT greater than 2.5]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define lateral-torsional buckling (LTB) in a beam. Under what conditions is a beam considered laterally supported?**

**Model Answer**:
Lateral-torsional buckling (LTB) is a mode of instability in which the compression flange of a beam, which is not laterally restrained, buckles sideways accompanied by twisting of the cross-section, leading to a sudden loss of load-carrying capacity before reaching its full plastic moment.

A beam is considered **laterally supported** when:
1. The compression flange is restrained against lateral movement at intervals such that the **non-dimensional slenderness $\lambda_{LT} \leq 0.4$**, OR
2. It is effectively restrained by floor systems, purlins, bracings, or sheeting that prevents lateral displacement AND twisting of the compression flange.

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List the factors that affect the elastic critical moment $M_{cr}$ of a laterally unsupported beam.**

**Model Answer**:
The elastic critical moment $M_{cr}$ depends on:
1. **Unsupported length ($L_{LT}$)** — longer length decreases $M_{cr}$ (inversely with $L^2$).
2. **Minor axis moment of inertia ($I_y$)** — larger $I_y$ increases $M_{cr}$.
3. **Torsional constant ($J$)** — larger $J$ increases $M_{cr}$.
4. **Warping constant ($I_w$)** — larger $I_w$ increases $M_{cr}$.
5. **Distance between flange centroids ($h_f$)** — larger $h_f$ increases $M_{cr}$.
6. **Modulus of elasticity ($E$)** and **Shear modulus ($G$)** of steel.
7. **Loading pattern** (uniform moment, point load, UDL) via the $\beta_b$ factor.
8. **End conditions** (effective length factor $K_L$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (14 Marks)

**A simply supported beam of effective span 5 m is subjected to a uniformly distributed load of 40 kN/m (service) and a concentrated load of 50 kN (service) at mid-span. The beam is laterally UNSUPPORTED. Design the beam using Fe 410 steel. Check the adequacy of ISMB 450 section. Assume:**

- ISMB 450: $h = 450$ mm, $b_f = 150$ mm, $t_f = 17.4$ mm, $t_w = 9.4$ mm
- $Z_p = 1533.4 \times 10^3$ mm³, $Z_e = 1192.6 \times 10^3$ mm³
- $I_x = 30390.8 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, $I_y = 834.0 \times 10^4$ mm⁴
- $J = 400 \times 10^3$ mm⁴, $h_f = 432.6$ mm
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $G = 0.769 \times 10^5$ MPa
- $\beta_b = 1.13$ for this loading pattern

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Bending Strength and LTB Check**

**Step 1: Factored Loads and Moments**

$$\begin{aligned}
w_u &= 1.5 \times 40 = 60 \text{ kN/m} \\
P_u &= 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \text{ kN} \\
M_u &= \frac{w_u L^2}{8} + \frac{P_u L}{4} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 5^2}{8} + \frac{75 \times 5}{4} = 187.5 + 93.75 = 281.25 \text{ kN·m} \\
V_u &= \frac{w_u L}{2} + \frac{P_u}{2} = 150 + 37.5 = 187.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Elastic Critical Moment $M_{cr}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= \frac{\pi^2 E I_y h_f}{2 L_{LT}^2} \sqrt{1 + \frac{4 G J L_{LT}^2}{\pi^2 E I_y h_f^2}} \\
L_{LT} &= 5000 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 432.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

First term calculation:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 834 \times 10^4 \times 432.6}{2 \times (5000)^2} \\
&= \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 834 \times 432.6 \times 10^9}{50 \times 10^6} \\
&= \frac{7124.7 \times 10^{12}}{50 \times 10^6} = 142.49 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Second term inside square root:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{4 \times 0.769 \times 10^5 \times 400 \times 10^3 \times (5000)^2}{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 834 \times 10^4 \times (432.6)^2} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 0.769 \times 400 \times 25 \times 10^6 \times 10^5}{9.8696 \times 2 \times 834 \times 187146 \times 10^8} \\
&= \frac{30760 \times 10^{11}}{3.078 \times 10^{18}} = 0.999
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$\begin{aligned}
M_{cr} &= 142.49 \times 10^6 \times \sqrt{1 + 0.999} = 142.49 \times 10^6 \times \sqrt{1.999} \\
&= 142.49 \times 10^6 \times 1.4142 = 201.49 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} \\
M_{cr} &= 201.49 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Non-dimensional Slenderness**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{LT} &= \sqrt{\frac{\beta_b Z_p f_y}{M_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.13 \times 1533.4 \times 10^3 \times 250}{201.49 \times 10^6}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{433.18 \times 10^6}{201.49 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{2.150} = 1.466
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\lambda_{LT} = 1.466 > 0.4$, LTB governs.

**Step 4: Permissible Bending Stress (Table 14, Perry-Robertson)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{LT} &= 0.5 [1 + 0.21 (1.466 - 0.2) + 1.466^2] \\
&= 0.5 [1 + 0.266 + 2.149] = 1.708 \\
\chi_{LT} &= \frac{1}{1.708 + \sqrt{1.708^2 - 1.466^2}} = \frac{1}{1.708 + \sqrt{2.917 - 2.149}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.708 + \sqrt{0.768}} = \frac{1}{1.708 + 0.876} = \frac{1}{2.584} = 0.387
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{bd} &= \frac{\chi_{LT} f_y}{\gamma_{mb}} = \frac{0.387 \times 250}{1.10} = 87.95 \text{ MPa} \\
M_d &= f_{bd} \times Z_p = 87.95 \times 1533.4 \times 10^3 \\
&= 134.86 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 134.86 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Check**: $M_u = 281.25$ kN·m $> M_d = 134.86$ kN·m

❌ **ISMB 450 is INADEQUATE**. Need a heavier section or lateral bracing.

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Shear, Web Buckling, and Deflection Checks**

**Step 1: Design Shear Strength (IS 800 Cl. 8.4)**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{f_{yw} h t_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} = \frac{250 \times 450 \times 9.4}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10} \\
&= \frac{1057500}{1.905} = 555.12 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $V_u = 187.5$ kN $< V_d = 555.12$ kN ✅ — Shear is **OK**.

**Step 2: Web Bearing Check (IS 800 Cl. 8.5.1)**

For bearing under concentrated load:
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{w,design} &= \frac{f_{yw} \cdot t_w \cdot b_1}{\gamma_{m0}}
\end{aligned}$$

With $b_1$ = effective bearing length (assume 100 mm stiff bearing):
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{w,design} &= \frac{250 \times 9.4 \times 100}{1.10} = 213.6 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Applied bearing load: $P_u/2 = 37.5$ kN $< 213.6$ kN ✅

**Step 3: Web Buckling Check (IS 800 Cl. 8.5.2)**

For unstiffened web:
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{wb} &= \frac{1000 t_w^3}{d} \cdot \frac{f_{yw}}{\gamma_{m1}} \text{ (kN) if } d/t_w \text{ large}
\end{aligned}$$

For rolled sections, this is rarely critical. Web is Class 1, so OK.

**Step 4: Deflection Check**

Allowable: $L/300 = 5000/300 = 16.67$ mm

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{UDL} &= \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E I_x} = \frac{5 \times 40 \times (5000)^4}{384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 30390.8 \times 10^4} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 40 \times 6.25 \times 10^{14}}{2.334 \times 10^{16}} = 5.36 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{Point} &= \frac{P L^3}{48 E I_x} = \frac{50 \times (5000)^3}{48 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 30390.8 \times 10^4} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 1.25 \times 10^{14}}{2.918 \times 10^{15}} = 2.14 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Total: $\delta = 5.36 + 2.14 = 7.50$ mm $< 16.67$ mm ✅

**Conclusion**: ISMB 450 fails in bending due to LTB but passes all other checks. The designer should either:
- (i) Provide intermediate lateral bracing (reduces $L_{LT}$)
- (ii) Select a heavier section (ISMB 500 or higher)

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks)

**Design a laterally SUPPORTED beam of effective span 4.5 m subjected to a uniformly distributed load of 50 kN/m (service) over the entire span. Use Fe 410 steel. Check the adequacy of ISMB 400.**

- ISMB 400: $h = 400$ mm, $t_w = 8.9$ mm, $t_f = 16$ mm, $b_f = 140$ mm
- $Z_p = 1206.34 \times 10^3$ mm³, $Z_e = 935.6 \times 10^3$ mm³
- $I_x = 20462 \times 10^4$ mm⁴
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $f_y = 250$ MPa

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Section Classification and Bending Strength**

**Step 1: Factored Loads**

$$\begin{aligned}
w_u &= 1.5 \times 50 = 75 \text{ kN/m} \\
M_u &= \frac{w_u L^2}{8} = \frac{75 \times 4.5^2}{8} = \frac{75 \times 20.25}{8} = 189.84 \text{ kN·m} \\
V_u &= \frac{w_u L}{2} = \frac{75 \times 4.5}{2} = 168.75 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Section Classification**

$$\begin{aligned}
\epsilon &= \sqrt{250/f_y} = 1.0 \\
(b/t_f)_{flange} &= 70/16 = 4.375
\end{aligned}$$

Limits for flange outstand (Table 2 of IS 800):
- Class 1: $b/t_f \leq 9.4\epsilon = 9.4$ ✅
- Class 2: $b/t_f \leq 10.5\epsilon = 10.5$ ✅
- Class 3: $b/t_f \leq 15.7\epsilon = 15.7$ ✅

Flange is **Class 1**. Web is also Class 1 for rolled sections. **Section is Plastic (Class 1).**

**Step 3: Design Bending Strength**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_d &= \frac{\beta_b \cdot Z_p \cdot f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} = \frac{1.0 \times 1206.34 \times 10^3 \times 250}{1.10} \\
&= 274.17 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

For UDL, $\beta_b = 1.0$ (simply supported, UDL on entire span).

**Check**: $M_u = 189.84$ kN·m $< M_d = 274.17$ kN·m ✅

**Step 4: Bending Strength Utilization Ratio**

$$\text{UR} = M_u / M_d = 189.84 / 274.17 = 0.692 = 69.2\%$$

This is **economical** (between 70-90% is ideal, but 69% is acceptable).

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Shear, Deflection, and Serviceability Checks**

**Step 1: Design Shear Strength**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{f_{yw} \cdot h \cdot t_w}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{m0}} = \frac{250 \times 400 \times 8.9}{\sqrt{3} \times 1.10} = 467.20 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Check: $V_u = 168.75$ kN $\leq V_d = 467.20$ kN ✅

**Step 2: Deflection Check (Service Load)**

Allowable: $L/300 = 4500/300 = 15$ mm

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{max} &= \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E I_x} = \frac{5 \times 50 \times (4500)^4}{384 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 20462 \times 10^4} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 50 \times 4.106 \times 10^{14}}{1.572 \times 10^{16}} = 6.53 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Check: $\delta = 6.53$ mm $< 15$ mm ✅

**Step 3: Web Bearing at Supports**

For 100 mm bearing length:
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{w,design} &= \frac{250 \times 8.9 \times 100}{1.10} = 202.27 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Reaction = $w_u L / 2 = 168.75$ kN $< 202.27$ kN ✅

**Step 4: Final Summary**

| Check | Applied | Capacity | Status |
|-------|---------|----------|--------|
| Bending | 189.84 kN·m | 274.17 kN·m | ✅ OK (UR = 69%) |
| Shear | 168.75 kN | 467.20 kN | ✅ OK |
| Deflection | 6.53 mm | 15.00 mm | ✅ OK |
| Web Bearing | 168.75 kN | 202.27 kN | ✅ OK |

**Conclusion**: **ISMB 400 is ADEQUATE** as a laterally supported beam for the given loading.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Exam Pitfalls** — Where students lose marks:
> 
> 1. **Forgetting $\beta_b$ factor**: Many students compute $\lambda_{LT}$ using $Z_p f_y / M_{cr}$ (without $\beta_b$). This is wrong. **$\beta_b$ comes from Table 12 and depends on the moment gradient.**
> 
> 2. **Using $\gamma_{m0}$ instead of $\gamma_{mb}$**: For LTB, use $\gamma_{mb} = 1.10$ (not 1.10 for yielding). **Some students use 1.25 incorrectly.**
> 
> 3. **Confusing $M_d$ formulas**: For supported beam → $M_d = Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$. For unsupported → $M_d = f_{bd} Z_p$ where $f_{bd}$ comes from Table 14/15.
> 
> 4. **Wrong section classification**: If a student assumes a section is Class 1 (plastic) but it is Class 3, the entire design is wrong.
> 
> 5. **Not checking serviceability**: Bending strength may pass, but deflection may fail. **Always check both ULS and SLS.**
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to include self-weight of beam**: Add self-weight to service loads before factoring.
> 
> 7. **Wrong units in $M_{cr}$ calculation**: $I_y$ should be in mm⁴, $L$ in mm, $E$ in MPa. Final $M_{cr}$ in N·mm, convert to kN·m by dividing by $10^6$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**:
> 
> **1. Key Definitions**:
> - Lateral-torsional buckling (LTB): Lateral deflection + twisting of compression flange
> - Laterally supported beam: $\lambda_{LT} \leq 0.4$ or compression flange continuously restrained
> - Section classification: Plastic (Class 1), Compact (Class 2), Semi-compact (Class 3), Slender (Class 4)
> 
> **2. Critical Formulas to Memorize**:
> - Plastic bending strength: $M_d = Z_p f_y / \gamma_{m0}$
> - Elastic critical moment: $M_{cr} = (\pi^2 E I_y h_f / 2 L_{LT}^2) \cdot \sqrt{1 + 4 G J L_{LT}^2 / (\pi^2 E I_y h_f^2)}$
> - Slenderness ratio: $\lambda_{LT} = \sqrt{\beta_b Z_p f_y / M_{cr}}$
> - Shear strength: $V_d = f_{yw} h t_w / (\sqrt{3} \gamma_{m0})$
> - Deflection limit: $\delta \leq L / 300$
> 
> **3. Material Constants**:
> - $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa, $G = 0.769 \times 10^5$ MPa
> - $\gamma_{m0} = \gamma_{mb} = 1.10$, $\gamma_{m1} = 1.25$
> - $f_y = 250$ MPa (Fe 410), 350 MPa (Fe 540)
> 
> **4. Design Procedure Summary**:
> - Step 1: Compute factored loads ($1.5 \times$ service loads)
> - Step 2: Classify section
> - Step 3: Check lateral support status ($\lambda_{LT}$)
> - Step 4: Compute design bending strength $M_d$
> - Step 5: Check $M_u \leq M_d$
> - Step 6: Check shear, web buckling, bearing
> - Step 7: Check deflection (SLS)
> 
> **5. Common ISMB Sections and Properties** (must memorize for KTU exam):
> - ISMB 250, ISMB 300, ISMB 350, ISMB 400, ISMB 450, ISMB 500, ISMB 600
> - Properties: $h$, $b_f$, $t_f$, $t_w$, $I_x$, $I_y$, $Z_p$, $Z_e$, $J$
> 
> **6. Key Differences** (most asked in KTU 2-markers):
> - Laterally supported: $M_d = Z_p f_y / 1.10$ (full plastic)
> - Laterally unsupported: $M_d = f_{bd} Z_p$ (reduced by LTB)
> - $\lambda_{LT} \leq 0.4$: Treated as supported
> - $\lambda_{LT} > 0.4$: Full LTB analysis needed
> 
> **7. Common Mistakes to Avoid**:
> - Forgetting $\beta_b$ in $\lambda_{LT}$ calculation
> - Using $\gamma_{m1}$ instead of $\gamma_{mb}$ for LTB
> - Neglecting self-weight
> - Skipping deflection check
> - Not converting units consistently (N-mm or kN-m)

---

**End of Module 4 Notes: Beams: Design Procedure for Laterally Supported and Unsupported Beams**

*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: PESFT754 | PESFT754 - Safety in Design of Structures | Module 4*

topics.title: Plate Thickness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT754 - SAFETY IN DESIGN OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plate Thickness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of **Safety in Design of Structures**, **Plate Thickness** refers to the dimensional parameter governing the *local buckling behaviour*, *cross-sectional classification*, and *post-buckling reserve strength* of flat plate elements (webs, flanges, cover plates, gusset plates) that constitute built-up steel sections such as plate girders, box girders, and stiffened panels. The *unsupported width-to-thickness ratio* (commonly denoted as **b/t** or **d/t**) is the governing non-dimensional slenderness parameter that determines whether a plate element behaves as a **stocky (compact)**, **non-compact**, or **slender** element under the action of in-plane compression, shear, or flexure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Plate thickness is examined under the umbrella of *limit state design of plate elements*, where the student's ability to **classify** cross-sections (per IS 800:2007 / IS 800:2024), compute **effective width**, and check **local buckling safety** is directly tested.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Paper vs. Cardboard" Intuition

Imagine holding a **rectangular sheet of stiff cardboard** (say, a postcard) flat between your thumbs. Push inward from the short edges (applying compression). The card resists because it is **thick enough** relative to its width. Now replace it with a **thin A4 sheet of paper** of the same plan dimensions. The very moment you apply the same compressive force, the paper visibly ripples, wrinkles, and buckles out-of-plane, even though the *material strength* of paper is essentially identical to that of cardboard.

The **ratio of width to thickness (b/t)** is the engineering translator of this everyday observation:
- **Low b/t** → plate is *stocky* → fails by yielding → **full cross-section is effective**.
- **High b/t** → plate is *slender* → fails by elastic local buckling → **only a reduced "effective width" carries load**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Plate thickness does not appear in strength formulas in isolation — it enters the design world **only through the slenderness ratio (b/t)**. A *thicker* plate for the *same width* is always *safer* against local buckling, but adds self-weight and material cost — the essence of **safety-economy optimisation** in structural steel design.

### Governing Physical Metric — Width-to-Thickness Ratio (b/t)

The fundamental parameter is:

$$\lambda_{p} = \frac{b}{t}$$

where:
- $b$ = clear width of the plate element between supporting stiffeners or edges (in **mm**)
- $t$ = thickness of the plate element (in **mm**)
- $\lambda_p$ = width-to-thickness slenderness ratio (**dimensionless**)

> [!TIP]
> Always report b/t as a **dimensionless number**. KTU valuation penalises students who carry units of mm/mm or mistakenly report it as "mm".

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cross-Sectional Classification of Plate Elements (IS 800 Framework)

Every plate element in a steel section is classified based on its b/t ratio into one of **three** categories. The boundaries are codified as $\lambda_{pd}$ (compact/non-compact limit) and $\lambda_{pd}^{lim}$ (non-compact/slender limit), both **yield-stress dependent** because the elastic buckling stress $f_{cr}$ is a function of the modulus of elasticity $E = \textbf{2.0} \times \textbf{10}^{\textbf{5}}$ **N/mm²** and the yield stress $f_y$.

**Classification Logic (Step-wise):**

- **Step 1 — Compute actual slenderness:**
Compute $\lambda_p = b/t$ for the plate element under the loading of interest (compression, shear, or flexure).

- **Step 2 — Compute limiting slenderness ratios:**
These are derived from the condition that the **elastic local buckling stress** equals the **yield stress** (for the compact limit) or a fraction of yield (for the slender limit).

- **Step 3 — Apply comparison logic:**
  - If $\lambda_p \le \lambda_{p,\text{compact}}$ → **Plastic (Class 1)** or **Compact (Class 2)** design section.
  - If $\lambda_{p,\text{compact}} < \lambda_p \le \lambda_{p,\text{slender}}$ → **Semi-compact (Class 3)**.
  - If $\lambda_p > \lambda_{p,\text{slender}}$ → **Slender (Class 4)** — design using **effective width**.

- **Step 4 — Decide design philosophy:**
Plastic design is permitted only for Class 1 sections; elastic design with effective width is mandatory for Class 4.

### 2.2 Critical Buckling Stress for Plates (Bryan-Richardson Concept)

For a **simply supported rectangular plate** of width $b$, thickness $t$, loaded by uniform in-plane compression $f$ per unit length on the two short edges, the **elastic critical buckling stress** is given by the classical **Bryan-Richardson formula**:

$$f_{cr} = \frac{k_{c} \, \pi^{2} \, E}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2}) \, (b/t)^{2}}$$

where:
- $k_c$ = **buckling coefficient** (depends on edge support and load type)
- $E$ = Young's modulus = $\textbf{2.0} \times \textbf{10}^{\textbf{5}}$ **N/mm²**
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio for steel = **0.3** (dimensionless)
- $b/t$ = width-to-thickness slenderness ratio
- $12(1-\nu^{2}) = 12(1-0.09) = 11.02$ (numerical constant for steel)

For simply supported edges with uniform compression: $k_c = 4.0$ (for long plates, $a/b \ge 1$).

### 2.3 Effective Width Concept (von Karman / Winter)

For **slender plates** (Class 4), the post-buckling reserve is mobilised by considering only a reduced "effective width" $b_{eff}$ on each side of the plate that carries the stress equal to yield. **Winter's formula** (adopted in IS 800) gives:

$$b_{eff} = \rho \, \frac{b}{1 - \psi} \quad \text{for stress gradient } \psi = \sigma_{2}/\sigma_{1}$$

For uniform compression ($\psi = 1$):

$$\frac{b_{eff}}{b} = \rho = \frac{1}{\bar{\lambda}_{p}} - \frac{0.22}{\bar{\lambda}_{p}^{2}} \quad \text{but} \quad \rho \le 1.0$$

where the **non-dimensional slenderness** is:

$$\bar{\lambda}_{p} = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{f_{cr}}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application / Limiting Use |
|---|--------------------|------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Width-to-thickness ratio | $\lambda_p = b/t$ | Primary slenderness parameter |
| 2 | Elastic critical stress (Bryan) | $f_{cr} = k_c \pi^{2} E / [12(1-\nu^{2})(b/t)^{2}]$ | Local buckling of plates |
| 3 | Compact limit (outstand) | $\lambda_{pd} = 9.4 \varepsilon$ | IS 800 — projecting element |
| 4 | Slender limit (outstand) | $\lambda_{pd}^{lim} = 13.6 \varepsilon$ | IS 800 — projecting element |
| 5 | Compact limit (internal) | $\lambda_{pd} = 29.3 \varepsilon$ | IS 800 — internal element |
| 6 | Slender limit (internal) | $\lambda_{pd}^{lim} = 40.8 \varepsilon$ | IS 800 — internal element |
| 7 | $\varepsilon$ factor | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250 / f_y}$ | Material adjustment for $f_y$ in MPa |
| 8 | Effective width factor | $\rho = (1/\bar{\lambda}_p) - (0.22/\bar{\lambda}_p^{2}) \le 1.0$ | Winter's formula, uniform compression |
| 9 | Non-dimensional slenderness | $\bar{\lambda}_p = \sqrt{f_y / f_{cr}}$ | Plate classification parameter |
| 10 | Effective design stress | $f_d = f_y / (\gamma_{m0}) \times (b_{eff}/b)$ | Reduced strength for slender plates |

where $\gamma_{m0} = \textbf{1.10}$ is the partial safety factor for material (yielding).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Plate thickness considerations govern the safety and economy of:
- **Plate girders** in highway and railway bridges (web and flange thickness)
- **Box girder decks** of cable-stayed and suspension bridges
- **Industrial storage tanks** (shell plate thickness under hydrostatic pressure)
- **Ship hull plating** under combined in-plane loads
- **Cold-formed steel sections** in light-gauge construction
- **High-rise steel building** coupling beams and outrigger trusses

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **post-buckling reserve** of slender plates is the cornerstone of modern plate-girder design. Ignoring it (a common student mistake) leads to uneconomical and excessively thick webs. The challenge is to use the *correct effective width* formula for the *correct stress distribution* — uniform compression, bending, or shear.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Elastic Critical Buckling Stress for a Plate

We derive the classical Bryan-Richardson plate-buckling formula step by step. Consider a rectangular plate, simply supported on all four edges, of length $a$, width $b$, and thickness $t$, subjected to in-plane compressive force $N_x$ per unit length on the two short edges (along $x$-axis).

**Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation**

The classical plate-buckling equation for a flat plate under in-plane load $N_x$ is derived from the principle of minimum potential energy, giving the **von Karman plate-buckling differential equation**:

$$D \left( \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + 2 \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2} \partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}} \right) + N_x \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} = 0$$

where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity per unit width** of the plate:

$$D = \frac{E \, t^{3}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2})}$$

and $w(x,y)$ is the small lateral out-of-plane deflection of the plate.

**Step 2 — Deflection Function (Navier-type Solution)**

For a simply supported plate, the displacement function satisfying the boundary conditions $w=0$ and $\partial^{2}w/\partial y^{2}=0$ at $y=0$ and $y=b$ is taken as a double Fourier series. For the **fundamental buckling mode** (single half-wave in $x$ and one half-wave in $y$ for the most critical case):

$$w(x,y) = w_{0} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{a} \right) \sin \left( \frac{\pi y}{b} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Governing Equation**

Substituting $w(x,y)$ into the differential equation, compute each term:

$$\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} = w_{0} \, \left( \frac{\pi}{a} \right)^{4} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{a} \right) \sin \left( \frac{\pi y}{b} \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}} = w_{0} \, \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{4} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{a} \right) \sin \left( \frac{\pi y}{b} \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2} \partial y^{2}} = w_{0} \, \left( \frac{\pi}{a} \right)^{2} \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{a} \right) \sin \left( \frac{\pi y}{b} \right)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} = -w_{0} \, \left( \frac{\pi}{a} \right)^{2} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{a} \right) \sin \left( \frac{\pi y}{b} \right)$$

Substituting all four terms into the governing equation and factoring out the common sinusoidal terms (which are non-zero in the interior), we obtain:

$$D \left[ \left( \frac{\pi}{a} \right)^{4} + 2 \left( \frac{\pi}{a} \right)^{2} \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{2} + \left( \frac{\pi}{b} \right)^{4} \right] - N_x \left( \frac{\pi}{a} \right)^{2} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Critical Load $N_x^{cr}$**

Solving for $N_x$:

$$N_x^{cr} = D \, \pi^{2} \left[ \frac{1}{a^{2}} + \frac{2}{b^{2}} + \frac{a^{2}}{b^{4}} \right]$$

To find the **minimum** (most critical) load, treat $a$ as variable and minimise $N_x^{cr}$ with respect to $a$. Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d N_x^{cr}}{d a} = D \pi^{2} \left[ -\frac{2}{a^{3}} + \frac{2a}{b^{4}} \right] = 0$$

This gives $a^{2} = b^{2}$, i.e., $a = b$ (the most critical aspect ratio is unity for the simply supported case with uniform compression).

Substituting $a = b$ into the expression for $N_x^{cr}$:

$$N_x^{cr} = D \, \pi^{2} \, \frac{4}{b^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Critical Stress**

The average compressive stress at buckling is the force per unit area:

$$f_{cr} = \frac{N_x^{cr}}{t} = \frac{4 D \pi^{2}}{b^{2} \, t} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} \, E \, t^{2}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2}) \, b^{2} \, t} = \frac{4 \pi^{2} E}{12 (1-\nu^{2}) (b/t)^{2}}$$

This is the **Bryan-Richardson formula** for simply supported plates under uniform compression with buckling coefficient $k_c = 4.0$.

**Step 6 — General Form with Buckling Coefficient $k_c$**

For different boundary conditions and load types, the coefficient of 4 in the numerator is replaced by a generalised $k_c$:

$$\boxed{f_{cr} = \frac{k_{c} \, \pi^{2} \, E}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2}) \, (b/t)^{2}}}$$

Common $k_c$ values:
- Uniform compression, simply supported: $k_c = 4.0$
- Uniform compression, one edge clamped: $k_c = 5.42$
- Pure shear, simply supported: $k_c = 5.35$ (for $a/b \to \infty$)
- Pure bending, simply supported: $k_c = 23.9$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Limiting b/t for Compact Sections (Internal Element)

We derive the **compact limit slenderness ratio** for an internal plate element (web of an I-section) under pure bending.

**Step 1 — Condition for the Compact Limit**

At the compact/semi-compact boundary, the **plate just reaches yield** at the extreme fibre **before** elastic local buckling occurs. Equating $f_{cr} = f_y$:

$$f_y = \frac{k_{c} \, \pi^{2} \, E}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2}) \, (b/t)^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Limiting b/t**

$$(b/t)_{lim} = \pi \sqrt{ \frac{k_c \, E}{12 (1-\nu^{2}) f_y} }$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Constants**

With $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$, $k_c = 23.9$ (for pure bending of internal element, simply supported):

$$(b/t)_{lim} = \pi \sqrt{ \frac{23.9 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12 \times 0.91 \times f_y} } = \pi \sqrt{ \frac{4.378 \times 10^{6}}{f_y} }$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the $\varepsilon$ Factor**

Define $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250 / f_y}$ (where $f_y$ is in MPa). Then:

$$(b/t)_{lim} = \pi \sqrt{ \frac{4.378 \times 10^{6}}{250} } \times \sqrt{ \frac{250}{f_y} } = \pi \sqrt{ 17512 } \times \varepsilon$$

Computing $\pi \sqrt{17512} = \pi \times 132.3 = 415.6$ ... hmm, this is the *unsupported* limit. For a **semi-compact** web under bending, the codified IS 800 value (Class 2 → Class 3 boundary) is:

$$\lambda_{pd} = 105 \, \varepsilon \quad \text{(web of I-section, pure bending)}$$

The slightly different constant arises from the chosen code calibration including the residual stress and initial imperfection factors. The full IS 800 derivation includes **modified buckling coefficient** $k_c^{mod}$ accounting for stress gradient and edge restraint.

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration**

For $f_y = 250$ MPa: $\varepsilon = 1.0$, hence $\lambda_{pd} = 105.0$
For $f_y = 350$ MPa: $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/350} = 0.845$, hence $\lambda_{pd} = 88.7$

This shows that **higher yield steel requires thicker plates** for the same classification — a direct safety-economy consequence.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Width Reduction Factor $\rho$ (Winter's Formula)

**Step 1 — Non-dimensional Slenderness**

$$\bar{\lambda}_{p} = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{f_{cr}}} = \frac{(b/t)}{(b/t)_{lim}}$$

**Step 2 — Winter's Empirical Post-Buckling Reduction**

For uniformly compressed slender plates with initial imperfections, von Kárman proposed the effective width concept, later refined by Winter as:

$$\rho = \frac{b_{eff}}{b} = \frac{1}{\bar{\lambda}_{p}} - \frac{0.22}{\bar{\lambda}_{p}^{2}} \le 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example**

Given: $b = 400$ mm, $t = 6$ mm, $f_y = 250$ MPa, $f_{cr} = 120$ MPa.

Compute slenderness ratio:
$$b/t = 400/6 = 66.67$$

Compute non-dimensional slenderness:
$$\bar{\lambda}_{p} = \sqrt{250/120} = \sqrt{2.083} = 1.443$$

Compute reduction factor:
$$\rho = \frac{1}{1.443} - \frac{0.22}{1.443^{2}} = 0.693 - 0.1057 = 0.587$$

Compute effective width:
$$b_{eff} = 0.587 \times 400 = 234.8 \text{ mm}$$

Reduction in effective area = $(1 - 0.587) \times 100 = 41.3\%$

This is the **magnitude of post-buckling loss** the design engineer must accommodate via the reduced design strength:

$$f_d = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \times \frac{b_{eff}}{b} = \frac{250}{1.10} \times 0.587 = 133.4 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Slenderness vs. Behaviour

| b/t Range | Classification | Failure Mode | Design Strength | Effective Width |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| b/t $\le \lambda_{pd}$ | Class 1 (Plastic) | Full yielding, plastic hinge | $f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | $b_{eff} = b$ |
| $\lambda_{pd} <$ b/t $\le \lambda_{pd}^{lim}$ | Class 3 (Semi-compact) | Yielding at extreme fibre | $f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | $b_{eff} = b$ |
| b/t $> \lambda_{pd}^{lim}$ | Class 4 (Slender) | Elastic local buckling | $\rho \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | $b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plate Classification Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plate Element with b and t] --> B[Compute actual slenderness ratio: b divide t]
    B --> C{b greater than t by threshold}
    C -- Compute eps --> D[eps equals sqrt 250 divided by fy]
    D --> E[lambda pd equals 9.4 eps for outstand]
    D --> F[lambda pd lim equals 13.6 eps for outstand]
    D --> G[lambda pd equals 29.3 eps for internal]
    D --> H[lambda pd lim equals 40.8 eps for internal]
    E --> I{Compare b by t with limits}
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -- b/t less than equal to lambda pd --> J[Class 1 or 2: Plastic or Compact]
    I -- lambda pd less than b/t less than equal to lambda pd lim --> K[Class 3: Semi Compact]
    I -- b/t greater than lambda pd lim --> L[Class 4: Slender]
    J --> M[Use full area: fy by gamma m0]
    K --> N[Use full area but limit strain]
    L --> O[Apply effective width: rho times b]
    O --> P[Reduced strength: rho times fy by gamma m0]
    M --> Q[End: Section Safe]
    N --> Q
    P --> Q
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define the term **width-to-thickness ratio (b/t)** of a plate element. How does it influence the classification of a steel cross-section per IS 800?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **width-to-thickness ratio** of a plate element is defined as the ratio of the clear unsupported width $b$ of the plate (measured between adjacent stiffeners, webs, or free edges as appropriate) to its thickness $t$:

$$\lambda_p = \frac{b}{t} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

It is the **governing slenderness parameter** that determines whether a plate element can develop its full plastic moment (Class 1), full yield (Class 2/3), or only an effective reduced width (Class 4) before local buckling intervenes. **Lower b/t values** indicate a *stocky* plate that yields before buckling, while **higher b/t values** indicate a *slender* plate that buckles elastically with reduced effective width. Per IS 800, the limiting values $\lambda_{pd}$ and $\lambda_{pd}^{lim}$ depend on the yield stress through the factor $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/f_y}$ and the element type (internal or outstand, compression, bending, or shear).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supple Set]**

State **Winter's formula** for effective width of a slender plate. Why is the 0.22 constant significant?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Winter's formula gives the reduction factor $\rho$ for the effective width of a uniformly compressed slender plate:

$$\rho = \frac{1}{\bar{\lambda}_p} - \frac{0.22}{\bar{\lambda}_p^{2}} \le 1.0$$

where $\bar{\lambda}_p = \sqrt{f_y / f_{cr}}$ is the non-dimensional slenderness.

The **constant 0.22** is an empirical correction that accounts for **initial geometric imperfections** (out-of-flatness) and **residual stresses** present in real fabricated plates. The original von Kárman (1929) elastic theory predicted $\rho = 1/\bar{\lambda}_p$, but experiments on cold-formed sections by Winter, Walker, and others showed systematic deviation. The 0.22 term **calibrates the elastic theory to test data** with a 95% lower-bound confidence — it is therefore a **safety factor embedded in the effective width formula** itself, distinct from the partial safety factor $\gamma_{m0}$. This is a classic example of how empirical constants in design codes represent *measured reality* rather than pure theory.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

(a) Derive the **elastic critical buckling stress** of a simply supported rectangular plate under uniform in-plane compression. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)

(b) An **outstand element** of a hot-rolled I-section has a clear width of $b = 95$ mm and thickness $t = 10$ mm. The steel grade is **Fe 410** ($f_y = 250$ MPa). Classify the outstand element per IS 800 limits and compute the **effective design stress** if it falls in Class 4. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The full derivation as presented in **Section 3.1 above** carries 7 marks distributed as:

- Statement of governing differential equation (1 mark)
- Deflection function satisfying boundary conditions (1 mark)
- Substitution and algebraic simplification (2 marks)
- Minimisation with respect to aspect ratio (1 mark)
- Final formula with $k_c$ generalisation and constants (2 marks)

**Final answer to be stated clearly:**

$$\boxed{f_{cr} = \frac{k_{c} \, \pi^{2} \, E}{12 \, (1 - \nu^{2}) \, (b/t)^{2}}}$$

with $k_c = 4.0$ for simply supported, uniformly compressed long plates, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$.

**(b) Classification and Effective Design Stress (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute actual slenderness:**
$$b/t = 95/10 = 9.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\varepsilon$ factor:**
$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{250}{f_y}} = \sqrt{\frac{250}{250}} = 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute limiting slenderness ratios (outstand element, per IS 800):**

For an **outstand of an I-section flange** under uniform compression (or as part of a flexural compression element):
$$\lambda_{pd} = 9.4 \, \varepsilon = 9.4 \times 1.0 = 9.4$$
$$\lambda_{pd}^{lim} = 13.6 \, \varepsilon = 13.6 \times 1.0 = 13.6$$

**Step 4 — Apply classification:**

Since $b/t = 9.5 > \lambda_{pd} = 9.4$ but $b/t = 9.5 < \lambda_{pd}^{lim} = 13.6$, the element is in the range $9.4 < 9.5 \le 13.6$, hence classified as **Class 3 (Semi-Compact)**.

(Note: This is *just* outside the compact zone — a classic exam illustration of borderline classification.)

**Step 5 — Compute effective design stress (if it were Class 4):**

If the b/t had exceeded 13.6, we would have proceeded as follows.

Compute critical buckling stress using $k_c = 0.425$ (outstand flange, simply supported with one free edge):
$$f_{cr} = \frac{0.425 \times \pi^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12 \times 0.91 \times 9.5^{2}} = \frac{0.425 \times 9.8696 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{10.92 \times 90.25}$$

$$f_{cr} = \frac{8.388 \times 10^{5}}{985.5} = 851.1 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Non-dimensional slenderness:**
$$\bar{\lambda}_p = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{f_{cr}}} = \sqrt{\frac{250}{851.1}} = \sqrt{0.2937} = 0.542$$

**Step 7 — Reduction factor (Winter's formula, but capped at 1.0 for $\bar{\lambda}_p \le 0.673$):**

Since $\bar{\lambda}_p = 0.542 < 0.673$, we take $\rho = 1.0$ (plate is not slender enough for reduction to be effective).

**Step 8 — Effective design stress:**
$$f_d = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_{m0}} \times \rho = \frac{250}{1.10} \times 1.0 = 227.3 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Conclusion:** For this borderline element, **no reduction** is required because $\bar{\lambda}_p < 0.673$ — the Winter formula caps $\rho$ at 1.0. The element is governed by **yielding**, not buckling.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice]**

(a) Explain the **three classes of plate element cross-sections** (Plastic, Compact, Semi-compact) per IS 800. State the **limiting b/t ratios** for an internal element of a beam web in pure bending. (7 marks)

(b) A plate girder web is $d = 1500$ mm deep and $t = 12$ mm thick. The flanges are $b_f = 400$ mm and $t_f = 25$ mm. Steel is **Fe 410** ($f_y = 250$ MPa). Check whether the **web** can be classified as Class 3 (semi-compact) under pure bending. If not, compute the **effective depth** of the web. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Classes of Plate Elements (7 marks):**

| Class | Behaviour | Design Strength | Design Method |
|-------|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| **Class 1 (Plastic)** | Full plastic hinge rotation capacity, $b/t \le \lambda_{pd}$ | $f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Plastic analysis |
| **Class 2 (Compact)** | Yields but limited plastic rotation | $f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Elastic with $M_p$ limit |
| **Class 3 (Semi-compact)** | Extreme fibre yields, no local buckling | $f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Elastic, no $M_p$ |
| **Class 4 (Slender)** | Local buckling before yield | $\rho f_y / \gamma_{m0}$ | Effective width |

**Limiting b/t for internal element of beam web under pure bending (IS 800):**

$$\lambda_{pd} = 105 \, \varepsilon \quad \text{(Class 2 → Class 3 boundary)}$$

$$\lambda_{pd}^{lim} = 126 \, \varepsilon \quad \text{(Class 3 → Class 4 boundary)}$$

where $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250 / f_y}$.

**(b) Web Classification Check (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute actual slenderness of web:**
$$d/t = 1500/12 = 125.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\varepsilon$ factor:**
$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/250} = 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute limiting slenderness for internal web (pure bending):**
$$\lambda_{pd} = 105 \times 1.0 = 105.0$$
$$\lambda_{pd}^{lim} = 126 \times 1.0 = 126.0$$

**Step 4 — Classification:**

Since $105.0 < d/t = 125.0 < 126.0$, the web is **just inside Class 3 (semi-compact)** — *barely* avoiding the Class 4 (slender) zone.

**Step 5 — For safety, also verify by computing effective depth (if it were Class 4):**

The plate is *just* under the Class 3 limit, so it is acceptable as Class 3 with **no reduction**. However, to demonstrate the procedure, assume a hypothetical case where $d/t = 130$ (i.e., exceeding $\lambda_{pd}^{lim}=126$):

Compute critical stress (web in bending, $k_c = 23.9$, simply supported, with effective width factor for stress gradient):

For internal web under bending with stress gradient $\psi = -1$ (pure bending):
$$f_{cr} = \frac{23.9 \times \pi^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{12 \times 0.91 \times 130^{2}} = \frac{4.718 \times 10^{7}}{1.844 \times 10^{5}} = 255.9 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\bar{\lambda}_p = \sqrt{250/255.9} = 0.988$$

$$\rho = \frac{1}{0.988} - \frac{0.22}{0.988^{2}} = 1.012 - 0.225 = 0.787$$

$$b_{eff} = 0.787 \times 1500 = 1180.5 \text{ mm}$$

Reduction = $6.7\%$.

**Conclusion for given problem:** The actual web with $d/t = 125$ is **Class 3 — semi-compact** and may be designed using **full elastic capacity** $f_y / \gamma_{m0} = 250/1.10 = 227.3$ N/mm² with no reduction in cross-section. The designer should consider **increasing web thickness to 13 mm** to provide a safety margin above the Class 3/4 boundary.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Plate Thickness Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon$ factor:** A student who writes $\lambda_{pd} = 9.4$ instead of $9.4\varepsilon$ for $f_y = 350$ MPa loses **2 marks**. The $\varepsilon$ factor is *non-negotiable* in IS 800.
>
> 2. **Using wrong $k_c$:** For an **internal web under pure bending** with simply supported edges, $k_c = 23.9$. For a web under **pure shear**, $k_c = 5.35$ (as $a/b \to \infty$). Mixing these up changes $f_{cr}$ by a factor of $\sim 4.5$.
>
> 3. **Reporting b/t with units:** A value like "$b/t = 95$ mm" is **wrong** and loses a mark. Always state it as a *dimensionless ratio*.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the 0.22 in Winter's formula:** Writing $\rho = 1/\bar{\lambda}_p$ without the second term forfeits 1 mark — that 0.22 is the **safety calibration** Winter introduced over von Kárman.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Poisson's ratio constant:** $12(1-\nu^{2}) = 11.02$ for steel, NOT 12. Examiners often give partial credit for writing 12(1-$\nu^2$) symbolically and full credit for substituting 11.02.
>
> 6. **Skipping the unit check in final answer:** Always state effective width in **mm** and design stress in **N/mm²** — silent unit assumptions are penalised.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Plate Thickness — Module 4):**

- The **single governing parameter** is the width-to-thickness ratio $\lambda_p = b/t$ (dimensionless).
- IS 800 classification has **three boundaries**: Class 1/2 (plastic/compact), Class 2/3 (compact/semi-compact), Class 3/4 (semi-compact/slender).
- Limiting slenderness ratios are **yield-stress dependent** through $\varepsilon = \sqrt{250/f_y}$.
- **Outstand element limits**: $\lambda_{pd} = 9.4\varepsilon$, $\lambda_{pd}^{lim} = 13.6\varepsilon$ (compression).
- **Internal element limits**: $\lambda_{pd} = 29.3\varepsilon$ (uniform compression), $40.8\varepsilon$ (slender), $105\varepsilon$ (web bending compact), $126\varepsilon$ (web bending slender).
- **Bryan-Richardson formula**: $f_{cr} = k_c \pi^2 E / [12(1-\nu^{2})(b/t)^2]$ — must be memorised.
- **Material constants**: $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$, $\gamma_{m0} = 1.10$.
- **Winter's effective width formula**: $\rho = 1/\bar{\lambda}_p - 0.22/\bar{\lambda}_p^2 \le 1.0$.
- **Post-buckling reserve** is the engineering justification for using thin webs in plate girders.
- **Buckling coefficient $k_c$** depends on load type (compression: 4.0; bending: 23.9; shear: 5.35) and edge support.
- **Numerical workflow**: Compute b/t → Compute $\varepsilon$ → Compare with $\lambda_{pd}$ and $\lambda_{pd}^{lim}$ → Classify → If Class 4, compute $\bar{\lambda}_p$, $\rho$, $b_{eff}$, $f_d$.
- For **stability against local buckling**, *increasing plate thickness* is the most direct design lever; alternatively, *adding intermediate stiffeners* reduces the effective unsupported width $b$.
- The **safe design stress** for any slender plate is $f_d = \rho \cdot f_y / \gamma_{m0}$, never greater than $f_y / \gamma_{m0}$.
